### User *(2026-03-20T21:59:24.148Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→# GeMeA — GND Werk Linking: Title Extraction
     2→
     3→Script: `scripts/link_gnd_works.py`
     4→Pipeline position: after rdf2jsonld, before mocho
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## The problem
     9→
    10→DDB `dc:title` strings are not clean bibliographic titles — they follow **ISBD punctuation conventions** and pack multiple data elements into a single string:
    11→
    12→```
    13→Faust / von Johann Wolfgang von Goethe. - 3. Aufl. - Weimar : Böhlau, 1887
    14→```
    15→
    16→This single `dc:title` value contains: title, statement of responsibility, edition, place of publication, publisher, and year. Sending the raw string to the local GND instance Werk lookup fails or produces low-precision results.
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## Step 1 — Rule-based ISBD parser (primary)
    21→
    22→ISBD uses standardized punctuation as area separators. Split on these markers in order:
    23→
    24→| Marker | Splits off |
    25→|--------|-----------|
    26→| ` / ` | Statement of responsibility (everything after) |
    27→| ` : ` | Subtitle (keep) or publisher (drop if preceded by place) |
    28→| `. - ` | New ISBD area (edition, publication info, etc.) |
    29→
    30→**Algorithm:**
    31→
    32→```python
    33→def extract_title_isbd(raw: str) -> str | None:
    34→    # Split on statement of responsibility
    35→    if " / " in raw:
    36→        title_part = raw.split(" / ")[0].strip()
    37→    else:
    38→        title_part = raw
    39→    # Drop anything after ". -" (edition/publication area)
    40→    if ". - " in title_part:
    41→        title_part = title_part.split(". - ")[0].strip()
    42→    # Keep subtitles joined by " : " (strip trailing punctuation)
    43→    return title_part.rstrip(".,;:/").strip() or None
    44→```
    45→
    46→`extraction_method = "isbd"` when this produces a non-empty result shorter than the raw string.
    47→
    48→---
    49→
    50→## Step 2 — NER fallback (secondary)
    51→
    52→For records without ISBD markers (~71% of DDB records based on Goethe-Faust sample analysis — NER is the majority path, not a small fallback), use a NER model to label spans. See [ner-bibliographic.md](ner-bibliographic.md) for model selection; current recommendation is NuNER Zero (`numind/NuNER_Zero`) evaluated first, with `xlm-roberta-base` fine-tuning if needed.
    53→
    54→Label set:
    55→
    56→| Label | Example |
    57→|-------|---------|
    58→| `TITLE` | "Faust" |
    59→| `PERSON` | "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe" |
    60→| `PUBLISHER` | "Böhlau" |
    61→| `YEAR` | "1887" |
    62→| `EDITION` | "3. Aufl." |
    63→
    64→Extract the `TITLE` span(s). If multiple `TITLE` spans: join with ` : ` (main title + subtitle).
    65→
    66→`extraction_method = "ner"` for these records. NER is slower — only invoke when ISBD parsing fails.
    67→
    68→---
    69→
    70→## Step 3 — Author GND URI cross-reference
    71→
    72→The extracted title alone is often ambiguous (many works share the same title). Cross-referencing with the author's GND URI narrows the local GND instance Werk result set.
    73→
    74→If the `edm:ProvidedCHO` links to an `edm:Agent` that already has a `d-nb.info/gnd/` URI (from Phase 1b pre-population or the source data), pass it to the local GND instance query:
    75→
    76→```
    77→GET /gnd/search?q=label:"{title}" AND creator:{gnd_person_uri}&filter=type:Work
    78→```
    79→
    80→Without an author GND URI, fall back to title-only search:
    81→
    82→```
    83→GET /gnd/search?q=label:"{title}"&filter=type:Work
    84→```
    85→
    86→---
    87→
    88→## Step 4 — local GND instance Werk lookup and scoring
    89→
    90→Local GND instance — query endpoint TBD (see infrastructure setup).
    91→
    92→```
    93→GET /gnd/search?q=label:"{extracted_title}"&filter=type:Work&size=5
    94→```
    95→
    96→Score the top candidates:
    97→
    98→| Condition | Match type | Predicate |
    99→|-----------|-----------|-----------|
   100→| Exact label match (case-insensitive) | `exact` | `owl:sameAs` |
   101→| Normalized match (diacritics, punctuation stripped) | `normalized` | `owl:sameAs` |
   102→| Fuzzy match (Levenshtein ≤ 2) | `fuzzy` | `skos:closeMatch` |
   103→| No candidate above threshold | `unresolved` | — (no triple written) |
   104→
   105→---
   106→
   107→## Scale: deduplication
   108→
   109→65M ProvidedCHOs, but many share identical `dc:title` strings (multiple copies of the same work across institutions). Deduplicate before calling local GND instance:
   110→
   111→```
   112→unique titles ≈ 5–10M (estimated; actual number computed during run)
   113→```
   114→
   115→**Strategy:**
   116→1. First pass: collect all `(raw_title, author_gnd_uri_if_available)` pairs from rdf2jsonld output
   117→2. Deduplicate by pair; run local GND instance lookups on unique pairs only
   118→3. Second pass: join results back to all ProvidedCHOs sharing the same pair
   119→
   120→This reduces API calls from ~65M to ~5–10M and respects local GND instance rate limits.
   121→
   122→---
   123→
   124→## Output format
   125→
   126→Per-ProvidedCHO JSON record (written to `data/raw/gnd-works/`):
   127→
   128→```json
   129→{
   130→  "cho_uri": "https://www.deutsche-digitale-bibliothek.de/item/ABC123",
   131→  "raw_title": "Faust / von Johann Wolfgang von Goethe. - 3. Aufl. - Weimar : Böhlau, 1887",
   132→  "extracted_title": "Faust",
   133→  "extraction_method": "isbd",
   134→  "author_gnd_uri": "https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238",
   135→  "gnd_werk_uri": "https://d-nb.info/gnd/118607359",
   136→  "match_type": "exact",
   137→  "match_confidence": 1.0
   138→}
   139→```
   140→
   141→Unresolved records omit `gnd_werk_uri`, `match_type`, `match_confidence`.
   142→
   143→The `extraction_method` field enables paper §4 quality reporting: how many records were resolved by rule vs. NER, and what the overall Werk linking coverage is.
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→## Integration with mocho
   148→
   149→mocho reads both the rdf2jsonld output and the GND Werk triples produced by `link_gnd_works.py`. The GND Werk URI is the key mocho uses to group `edm:ProvidedCHO` instances into `mocho:Work` entities. ProvidedCHOs with `gnd_werk_uri = null` (unresolved) remain as standalone Manifestations without a parent Work.
   150→
   151→See `notes/mocho-alignment.md` for mocho internals.
   152→
   153→---
   154→
   155→## Paper §4 quality metrics
   156→
   157→- **Title extraction coverage**: % of ProvidedCHOs where ISBD parsing succeeded vs. NER fallback vs. unresolved
   158→- **GND Werk linking rate**: % of ProvidedCHOs with a confirmed GND Werk URI
   159→- **Work grouping size distribution**: how many ProvidedCHOs per `mocho:Work` (distribution; median; max)
   160→- **Precision sample**: manual spot-check of 100 linked pairs
   161→
   162→Target: ≥ 70% Werk linking rate (condition for enabling `/work/{id}` API and WEMI pages).
   163→



```

### User *(2026-03-20T21:59:24.551Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→# GeMeA — NER for Bibliographic Title Extraction
     2→
     3→Context: NER is the **fallback** in `link_gnd_works.py` for records without ISBD markers. The primary extractor is the rule-based ISBD parser. NER only runs when that fails.
     4→
     5→**Scale of the fallback**: analysis of 115K Goethe-Faust DDB items ([isbd-title-analysis.md](../../goethe-faust/notes/isbd-title-analysis.md)) shows only **~29% of titles carry any ISBD pattern**, meaning NER applies to ~71% of records — the majority, not a small edge case. The dominant ISBD signal is ` :` (18%); the ` / ` split used by the parser appears in only 2.1% of titles. These figures are from one DDB provider subset and may vary across the full corpus.
     6→
     7→Target label set: `TITLE`, `PERSON`, `PUBLISHER`, `YEAR`, `EDITION`.
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Historical language scope
    12→
    13→DDB objects span several centuries. Title content may be in modern German, Early Modern German, Middle High German, Latin, or other languages. This affects model choice.
    14→
    15→**Key distinction**: the NER task is structural, not semantic. The model identifies *which span is the title* within a catalog string — it does not need to understand the title. Catalog records follow modern ISBD conventions regardless of object era, so:
    16→
    17→- **ISBD parsing (primary)** — unaffected; punctuation-based, language-agnostic
    18→- **Silver labeling** — unaffected; labels derive from ISBD structure, not title content
    19→- **NER fallback** — affected for the ~71% of records without ISBD markers
    20→
    21→For the fallback, historical language introduces real risk:
    22→
    23→| Scenario | Risk | Notes |
    24→|---|---|---|
    25→| Modern German catalog record, historical title | Low | Surrounding catalog text is modern; only the title span is archaic |
    26→| Early Modern / Middle High German title | Medium | Tokenization degrades; subword vocab misses historical orthography |
    27→| Latin titles | Medium | Common for pre-18th century scholarly works; monolingual German models fail |
    28→| Mixed-language catalog strings | Medium | Some DDB providers mix German and Latin in descriptions |
    29→
    30→---
    31→
    32→## Model options
    33→
    34→| Model | Type | Fine-tune needed? | TITLE OOB | PERSON OOB | Historical/Latin | Notes |
    35→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
    36→| `xlm-roberta-large` | Transformer | Yes | No | No | Good | **Recommended base for fine-tuning** — multilingual training covers Latin and historical text better than monolingual alternatives |
    37→| spaCy `de_core_news_lg` | Transformer | No | No | Yes | Poor | Trained on modern German news; degrades on historical orthography and Latin |
    38→| `flair/ner-german-large` | Stacked embeddings | No | No | Yes (PER) | Poor | CoNLL-2003 labels; same limitation as spaCy |
    39→| `deepset/gbert-large` | BERT-large | Yes | No | No | Poor | Monolingual modern German; no off-the-shelf bibliographic NER |
    40→| LLM (GPT-4o, Claude, Llama 3) | Generative | No | Yes | Yes | Good | Handles Latin and historical German well; too slow at 5–10M records but strong as a one-time labeler |
    41→| **NuNER Zero** (`numind/NuNER_Zero`) | Zero-shot NER | No | Yes | Yes | Moderate | +3.1% F1 over GLiNER-large-v2.1; token classifier (handles arbitrarily long entities); same size, local CPU; **current SOTA in this category** |
    42→| GLiNER (`gliner_multi-v2.1`) | Zero-shot NER | No | Yes | Yes | Moderate | NAACL 2024; span-based with configurable width; multilingual; slightly behind NuNER Zero on benchmarks |
    43→| GROBID | Rule-based + CRF | No | Partial | Partial | Poor | Trained on scientific citations, not ISBD/library catalog — different domain; not recommended |
    44→
    45→**Note on gbert-large**: was listed as the original plan but was never properly justified. Monolingual modern German is the wrong choice for a historically diverse corpus.
    46→
    47→---
    48→
    49→## NuNER Zero — zero-shot NER (current recommendation)
    50→
    51→[NuNER Zero](https://huggingface.co/numind/NuNER_Zero) (NuMind, 2024) is the current SOTA compact zero-shot NER model, outperforming GLiNER-large-v2.1 by +3.1% F1. Unlike GLiNER, it is a token classifier rather than span-based, so it handles arbitrarily long entities — relevant for verbose DDB title strings.
    52→
    53→```python
    54→from gliner import GLiNER
    55→
    56→model = GLiNER.from_pretrained("numind/NuNerZero")
    57→
    58→# Labels must be lower-cased
    59→labels = ["title", "person", "publisher", "year", "edition"]
    60→
    61→text = "Faust drittes Buch von Goethe erschienen Weimar"
    62→entities = model.predict_entities(text, labels)
    63→
    64→# Merge adjacent tokens with the same label (token classifier output)
    65→def merge_entities(entities, text):
    66→    if not entities:
    67→        return []
    68→    merged = []
    69→    current = entities[0]
    70→    for nxt in entities[1:]:
    71→        if nxt["label"] == current["label"] and nxt["start"] <= current["end"] + 1:
    72→            current["text"] = text[current["start"]:nxt["end"]].strip()
    73→            current["end"] = nxt["end"]
    74→        else:
    75→            merged.append(current)
    76→            current = nxt
    77→    merged.append(current)
    78→    return merged
    79→
    80→entities = merge_entities(entities, text)
    81→# → [{"text": "Faust drittes Buch", "label": "title", "score": 0.91}, ...]
    82→```
    83→
    84→BERT-sized — runs on CPU, same deployment footprint as GLiNER. Zero-shot precision on DDB strings is unknown; **evaluate on ~500 stratified fallback records before committing**.
    85→
    86→---
    87→
    88→## LLM options at inference time
    89→
    90→NER applies to ~71% of records (~3.5–7M unique pairs after deduplication). LLM API at inference is expensive at this scale; local options are strongly preferred.
    91→
    92→| Approach | Scale | Cost | Historical/Latin | Effort |
    93→|---|---|---|---|---|
    94→| **NuNER Zero** (local) | All NER records | Free | Moderate | Low — deploy and evaluate |
    95→| **Local LLM** (Llama 3.1 8B) | All NER records | GPU infra only | Moderate | Medium |
    96→| **LLM one-time labeler → fine-tune xlm-roberta** | Generate labels once | Low one-time API cost | Good | Medium |
    97→| **Fine-tuned xlm-roberta-base** | All NER records | Cheap at inference | Good (if trained on historical) | High upfront |
    98→| **LLM API at inference** (GPT-4o, Claude) | All NER records | ~$1,500–3,500 at full scale | Good | Low — but costly |
    99→
   100→**Recommended path**: start with NuNER Zero on 500 stratified NER records. If precision is acceptable, done. If not, use an LLM to label a few thousand records and fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on that output.
   101→
   102→---
   103→
   104→## If no labeled training data is available
   105→
   106→**1. Silver labeling from the ISBD pipeline**
   107→The ~29% of records where ISBD parsing succeeds become auto-labeled training examples (a useful but minority source):
   108→- Pre-`/` segment → `TITLE`
   109→- Post-`/` segment → `PERSON` (statement of responsibility)
   110→- Post-`. -` segments → `EDITION`, `PUBLISHER`, `YEAR` by position
   111→
   112→Language-agnostic — works for Latin and historical German titles. Fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on these labels. Evaluate on a manually checked gold set of ~500 records including historical and Latin examples.
   113→
   114→**2. LLM as a one-time labeler**
   115→Use GPT-4o or Claude to label a few thousand records. Handles Latin and historical German well. Fine-tune a smaller model on those outputs. Expensive per call but a one-time cost.
   116→
   117→**Distant supervision (supplementary)**
   118→For records where a GND Werk URI was confirmed via the local GND instance, the extracted title string is a positive `TITLE` example. Linked person GND URIs supply `PERSON` labels. Useful to augment silver labels, not sufficient alone.
   119→
   120→---
   121→
   122→## Available datasets for fine-tuning
   123→
   124→| Dataset | Labels | Language | Size | Relevance |
   125→|---|---|---|---|---|
   126→| [empathyai/books-ner-dataset](https://huggingface.co/datasets/empathyai/books-ner-dataset) | TITLE, AUTHOR | English | Project Gutenberg catalogues | Closest label match; English only — use for domain-transfer pretraining |
   127→| [HIPE-2022 (ajmc)](https://github.com/hipe-eval/HIPE-2022-data) | Fine-grained bibliographic refs | German, French, English | ~10K tokens | Historical document NER with bibliographic references; German included — more relevant given historical scope |
   128→| [CLEF-HIPE-2020](https://zenodo.org/record/3836029) | PER, ORG, LOC, PROD (work titles) | German, French, English | Historical newspapers | PROD label covers work titles; 19th-century orthography — relevant for historical German |
   129→| [GermEval 2014](https://sites.google.com/site/germeval2014ner/) | PER, ORG, LOC, OTH | German | ~31K sentences | Modern German only; useful only for PERSON transfer |
   130→
   131→**Recommended fine-tuning path**:
   132→1. Pretrain on `empathyai/books-ner-dataset` for TITLE/AUTHOR signal (domain transfer)
   133→2. Fine-tune on silver-labeled DDB records
   134→3. Supplement with HIPE-2022 ajmc and CLEF-HIPE-2020 for historical German and Latin signal
   135→4. Evaluate on a gold set stratified by era (modern, 19th c., pre-1800, Latin)
   136→
   137→---
   138→
   139→## Decision
   140→
   141→1. **Try NuNER Zero first** — current SOTA compact zero-shot NER, no training data, runs locally, handles arbitrarily long spans. Evaluate on 500 stratified fallback records.
   142→2. **If NuNER Zero precision is insufficient**, use an LLM to label those same records and fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on that output.
   143→3. **Silver labeling** (ISBD-derived) augments any fine-tuning — language-agnostic, large volume, no annotation cost.
   144→4. Use `xlm-roberta` over any monolingual German model — the historical and Latin scope makes multilingual pretraining essential.
   145→5. **GROBID**: trained on scientific citations, not library catalog records — not recommended.
   146→
   147→---
   148→
   149→## Open questions
   150→
   151→- [ ] Is TITLE extraction sufficient, or are PUBLISHER/YEAR/EDITION labels needed for the paper's quality metrics?
   152→- [ ] Silver label quality: how clean are the ISBD-derived annotations for training, especially for historical records?
   153→- [x] ISBD coverage varies by provider — measure across more DDB subsets before assuming the 29%/71% split holds corpus-wide → **DF_DE_TITLES (4.47M)** confirms 28.4% ISBD coverage (excl. trailing `.`); 29% estimate holds corpus-wide
   154→- [ ] What share of non-ISBD records have Latin or historical German titles? (determines how much historical signal is needed)
   155→- [ ] Gold set composition: sample should be stratified by era to catch historical degradation early
   156→- [ ] ISBD parser uses ` /` as primary split, but ` /` appears in only 0.8% of DF_DE_TITLES (vs 2.1% in Goethe-Faust subset); ` :` at 20.3% is the dominant signal — verify parser prioritizes ` :` for silver label generation
   157→- [ ] Trailing period (17.5% of corpus) is noisy — fires on abbreviations (`Hrsg.`, `Bd.`) and ordinals; strip known German abbreviations before using as silver label signal, or require co-occurrence with another ISBD marker
   158→



```

### User *(2026-03-20T22:00:07.663Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→# GeMeA — Architecture
     2→
     3→## Data Flow
     4→
     5→```
     6→DDB JSON-LD files
     7→      │
     8→      ▼  rdf2jsonld (parallel conversion)
     9→RDF/JSON (W3C serialization)
    10→      │
    11→      ▼  scripts/link_gnd_works.py  (Phase 0: title → GND Werk URI, pre-mocho)
    12→RDF/JSON + GND Werk triples
    13→      │
    14→      ▼  mocho (EDM → mocho:Work grouping + RDA normalization)
    15→      │  ⚠ mocho.owl is WIP — pipeline blocked until stable
    16→Normalized RDF triples (N-Triples / Turtle)
    17→      │
    18→      ├──▶  ingest/load_qlever.py ──▶  QLever
    19→      │                                (named graphs per provider)
    20→      │                                        │
    21→      │                                        ▼  Phase 1b
    22→      │                              ingest/link_gnd_agents.py
    23→      │                                  (graph/gnd-enrichment)
    24→      │                                        │
    25→      └──▶  ingest/index_es.py ◀──────────────┘  Elasticsearch
    26→                                         (one doc per ProvidedCHO)
    27→                                                │
    28→                                                ▼
    29→                                         FastAPI (api/)
    30→                                         ├── /search       → Elasticsearch
    31→                                         ├── /item/{id}    → QLever SPARQL
    32→                                         ├── /sparql       → QLever proxy
    33→                                         └── /suggest      → Elasticsearch
    34→                                                │
    35→                                                ▼
    36→                                         Next.js (frontend/)
    37→                                         ├── /             Search home
    38→                                         ├── /search       Results + facets
    39→                                         ├── /item/[id]    Object detail
    40→                                         ├── /agent/[id]   Agent page
    41→                                         ├── /place/[id]   Place page
    42→                                         ├── /timespan/[id] TimeSpan page
    43→                                         ├── /map          Map view
    44→                                         └── /explore      Timeline + graph entry
    45→```
    46→
    47→---
    48→
    49→## Components
    50→
    51→### Ingest (`ingest/`)
    52→
    53→| Script | Responsibility |
    54→|--------|---------------|
    55→| `scripts/link_gnd_works.py` | **Pre-mocho (Phase 0)**: link `dc:title` strings → GND Werk URIs via lobid-gnd; output triples feed mocho for `mocho:Work` grouping |
    56→| `load_qlever.py` | Build QLever index from mocho N-Triples via `qlever index`; assign named graphs per provider |
    57→| `index_es.py` | Build Elasticsearch documents from mocho RDF; one doc per ProvidedCHO |
    58→| `build_docs.py` | Assemble ES document fields from SPARQL query over QLever |
    59→| `validate.py` | Post-ingest integrity checks (triple count, missing required fields) |
    60→
    61→**mocho:Work:** mocho creates `mocho:Work` entities (mocho-specific class, not standard `frbr:Work`) by grouping `edm:ProvidedCHO` instances that share the same GND Werk URI. The GND Werk link — produced by `link_gnd_works.py` — is the key mocho uses to determine which ProvidedCHOs belong to the same Work. mocho.owl is **currently WIP**; the `/work/{id}` API and WEMI hierarchy are blocked on its stabilization.
    62→
    63→**Named graph strategy:** `http://gemea.ddb.de/graph/{provider-id}` — allows per-provider reload without touching the full dataset.
    64→
    65→**ES document shape (per ProvidedCHO):**
    66→```json
    67→{
    68→  "id": "http://www.deutsche-digitale-bibliothek.de/item/ABC123",
    69→  "title": ["Faust", "Faust: Der Tragödie erster Teil"],
    70→  "type": "literature",
    71→  "sector": "bibliothek",
    72→  "agent": [{"uri": "http://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238", "label": "Goethe, Johann Wolfgang von"}],
    73→  "place": [{"uri": "http://sws.geonames.org/2925533", "label": "Frankfurt am Main", "lat": 50.11, "lon": 8.68}],
    74→  "timespan": {"earliest": 1808, "latest": 1808},
    75→  "provider": {"id": "...", "label": "Freies Deutsches Hochstift"},
    76→  "thumbnail": "https://...",
    77→  "isShownAt": "https://..."
    78→}
    79→```
    80→
    81→`type` is normalized at index time via `ingest/type_mapping.json` (maps raw `dc:type` strings → controlled vocabulary). `sector` is derived from provider metadata.
    82→
    83→---
    84→
    85→### API (`api/`)
    86→
    87→**Framework:** FastAPI (async)
    88→
    89→| Endpoint | Backend | Notes |
    90→|----------|---------|-------|
    91→| `GET /search` | Elasticsearch | params: `q`, `type`, `sector`, `place`, `from`, `to`, `institution`, `page`, `size`; `type` is a controlled vocabulary (arts/culture branches, see below) |
    92→| `GET /sectors` | Elasticsearch | list of DDB institutional sectors with object counts |
    93→| `GET /suggest` | Elasticsearch | autocomplete on title + agent + place labels |
    94→| `GET /item/{id}` | QLever SPARQL | full entity description + 1-hop neighbors |
    95→| `GET /agent/{id}` | QLever SPARQL | agent description + linked objects |
    96→| `GET /place/{id}` | QLever SPARQL | place description + linked objects |
    97→| `GET /work/{id}` | QLever SPARQL | FRBR Work: title, agents, expressions, all manifestations; **conditional** on mocho outputting `frbr:Work` nodes |
    98→| `GET /expression/{id}` | QLever SPARQL | FRBR Expression: language, date, parent Work, manifestations; **conditional** on mocho output |
    99→| `GET /sparql` | QLever proxy | read-only pass-through; rate-limited |
   100→| `POST /graphql` | QLever SPARQL | GraphQL schema over RDA/EDM entities; easier frontend consumption than SPARQL |
   101→| `GET /health` | — | service health check |
   102→
   103→**`type` — controlled vocabulary (arts and culture branches):**
   104→
   105→The `type` filter is restricted to a curated set of top-level domain labels, not raw RDF type URIs. These map to `dc:type` values in the EDM data and are normalized at index time.
   106→
   107→| `type` value | Maps to (EDM `dc:type` / GND genre) |
   108→|---|---|
   109→| `literature` | Handschrift, Buch, Manuskript, Textdokument, Werktitel |
   110→| `music` | Musikhandschrift, Notendruck, Tonträger, Musikwerk |
   111→| `theater` | Theaterzettel, Bühnenbildentwurf, Regiebuch |
   112→| `film` | Film, Filmdokument, Kinoplakat |
   113→| `painting` | Gemälde, Aquarell, Zeichnung |
   114→| `graphic` | Druckgrafik, Radierung, Holzschnitt, Lithografie |
   115→| `photography` | Fotografie, Fotodokument, Lichtbild |
   116→| `architecture` | Architekturzeichnung, Bauplan, Modell |
   117→| `sculpture` | Skulptur, Plastik, Objekt |
   118→| `map` | Karte, Kartografisches Dokument |
   119→| `object` | Kunstgewerbe, Objekt, Gebrauchsgegenstand |
   120→
   121→Unmapped `dc:type` values are indexed under `object` as fallback. The mapping table is maintained in `ingest/type_mapping.json`.
   122→
   123→**`sector` — DDB institutional sectors:**
   124→
   125→The DDB organizes providers by institutional type. `sector` is a first-class filter and facet.
   126→
   127→| `sector` value | DDB institution type |
   128→|---|---|
   129→| `bibliothek` | Public and research libraries |
   130→| `archiv` | State, municipal, and private archives |
   131→| `museum` | Museums and collections |
   132→| `mediathek` | Media libraries (audio, video, film) |
   133→| `denkmal` | Monument and heritage sites |
   134→
   135→**Caching:** Redis; TTL 5 min for search results, 1 hour for entity pages.
   136→
   137→**Security (OWASP):**
   138→- SPARQL injection: parameterized query templates; QLever is read-only by default; block UPDATE/INSERT/DELETE at proxy
   139→- Input sanitization: strip HTML/script from all query params
   140→- Rate limiting: token bucket on `/sparql` (10 req/s) and `/search` (50 req/s)
   141→- CORS: configured whitelist; defaults to same-origin
   142→- Security headers: via Nginx (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy)
   143→- Elasticsearch not exposed publicly; behind API only
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→### Frontend (`frontend/`)
   148→
   149→**Framework:** Next.js 14+ (App Router), Tailwind CSS
   150→
   151→| Page | Route | Key components |
   152→|------|-------|---------------|
   153→| Search home | `/` | `SearchBar`, `FeaturedItems` |
   154→| Search results | `/search` | `SearchBar`, `FacetSidebar`, `ResultList`, `Pagination` |
   155→| Object detail | `/item/[id]` | `MetadataPanel`, `GraphViz` (Cytoscape.js), `ThumbnailGallery` |
   156→| Agent page | `/agent/[id]` | `EntityHeader`, `LinkedObjects` |
   157→| Place page | `/place/[id]` | `EntityHeader`, `MapView` (Leaflet), `LinkedObjects` |
   158→| TimeSpan page | `/timespan/[id]` | `EntityHeader`, `Timeline` (D3), `LinkedObjects` |
   159→| Work page _(conditional)_ | `/work/[id]` | `WEMIHierarchy` (Work → Expressions → Manifestations), `LinkedObjects` |
   160→| Map | `/map` | `MapView` (Leaflet), cluster markers, `SearchBar` |
   161→| Explore | `/explore` | `Timeline` (D3 histogram), `GraphViz` entry |
   162→
   163→**Content negotiation** (Linked Data): `/item/{id}` serves HTML to browsers, Turtle/JSON-LD to RDF clients via `Accept` header (handled in API layer).
   164→
   165→---
   166→
   167→### Docker / Infrastructure (`docker/`)
   168→
   169→**Services (Docker Compose):**
   170→
   171→| Service | Image | Port | Notes |
   172→|---------|-------|------|-------|
   173→| `qlever` | `adfreiburg/qlever` | 7001 (internal) | persistent volume for QLever index |
   174→| `elasticsearch` | `elasticsearch:8.x` | 9200 (internal) | single-node |
   175→| `redis` | `redis:alpine` | 6379 (internal) | API response cache |
   176→| `api` | custom FastAPI | 8000 (internal) | |
   177→| `frontend` | custom Next.js | 3000 (internal) | |
   178→| `nginx` | `nginx:alpine` | 80, 443 | reverse proxy; TLS termination; security headers |
   179→
   180→**Deployment (v1):** Single hosted instance operated by the team. Self-hosting (lite + full mode Docker Compose for external users) is v2 future work.
   181→
   182→---
   183→
   184→---
   185→
   186→## Future Extensions (v2+)
   187→
   188→These are not in v1 but the architecture is designed to accommodate them without structural changes.
   189→
   190→| Extension | Component | Trigger |
   191→|-----------|-----------|---------|
   192→| **Text2SPARQL** | API: `POST /nl-search` | LLM translates natural language → SPARQL; 2-stage entity resolution (ArtKB pattern: Mistral → label lookup). Removes SPARQL expertise barrier for non-technical users. |
   193→| **Visual search** | Vector DB: Qdrant | Embed DDB thumbnails (via `edm:isShownAt`) with CLIP/SwinV2; `POST /search/image`. Adds image-to-image and text-to-image retrieval. |
   194→| **Object storage** | MinIO | Cache DDB thumbnails locally for reliability and controlled access; replaces dependency on external URLs. |
   195→| **ODRL rights** | Ingest + triplestore | Model per-item licensing (CC0, CC BY, rights reserved) as ODRL expressions. Strengthens FAIR "Reusable" dimension; relevant for institutional reuse. |
   196→| **Wikidata enrichment** | Ingest or API | Link GND agents/places to Wikidata entities; surface additional labels, images, DBpedia abstracts. |
   197→
   198→---
   199→
   200→## API Contracts
   201→
   202→### `GET /search`
   203→```
   204→Query params:
   205→  q           string   keyword query (required)
   206→  type        string   arts/culture branch (controlled vocabulary; see type table above)
   207→  sector      string   DDB institutional sector (bibliothek | archiv | museum | mediathek | denkmal)
   208→  place       string   place URI or label
   209→  from        int      earliest year
   210→  to          int      latest year
   211→  institution string   provider institution URI
   212→  page        int      default 1
   213→  size        int      default 20, max 100
   214→
   215→Response 200:
   216→{
   217→  "total": 12345,
   218→  "page": 1,
   219→  "results": [
   220→    {
   221→      "id": "...",
   222→      "title": "...",
   223→      "type": "painting",
   224→      "sector": "museum",
   225→      "thumbnail": "...",
   226→      "highlight": {"title": ["..."]},
   227→      "score": 1.23
   228→    }
   229→  ],
   230→  "facets": {
   231→    "type": [{"value": "painting", "label": "Gemälde / Painting", "count": 42}],
   232→    "sector": [{"value": "museum", "label": "Museum", "count": 18}],
   233→    "place": [...],
   234→    "institution": [...],
   235→    "year": [{"year": 1808, "count": 7}]
   236→  }
   237→}
   238→```
   239→
   240→### `GET /sectors`
   241→```
   242→Response 200:
   243→[
   244→  {"value": "bibliothek", "label": "Bibliothek",  "count": 28400000},
   245→  {"value": "archiv",     "label": "Archiv",      "count": 14200000},
   246→  {"value": "museum",     "label": "Museum",      "count": 12100000},
   247→  {"value": "mediathek",  "label": "Mediathek",   "count":  8900000},
   248→  {"value": "denkmal",    "label": "Denkmal",     "count":  1400000}
   249→]
   250→```
   251→
   252→### `GET /item/{id}`
   253→```
   254→Response 200:
   255→{
   256→  "id": "...",
   257→  "types": [...],
   258→  "titles": [{"value": "...", "lang": "de"}],
   259→  "agents": [{"uri": "...", "label": "...", "role": "..."}],
   260→  "places": [{"uri": "...", "label": "...", "lat": ..., "lon": ...}],
   261→  "timespan": {"label": "...", "earliest": 1808, "latest": 1808},
   262→  "provider": {"uri": "...", "label": "..."},

   265→  "neighbors": [{"uri": "...", "label": "...", "relation": "..."}],

   267→}
   268→```
   269→
   270→### `GET /work/{id}` _(conditional on mocho frbr:Work output + Phase 1b coverage)_
   271→```
   272→Response 200:

   285→      ]

### Assistant *(2026-03-20T22:01:30.969Z)*

Good context. The notes currently say "local GND instance — query endpoint TBD" and assume a lobid-style REST API. The QLever endpoint replaces all of that. Here's the plan.

---

## Plan: QLever DNB GND endpoint for Werk entity linking

**Endpoint:** `https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd`  
**HTTP API:** `POST` with body `query=<URL-encoded SPARQL>`, `Accept: application/sparql-results+json`

---

### Step 0 — Recon queries (run once before implementation)

Three things to verify before writing production queries:

**1. Work type coverage** — how many Works exist, what GND subclasses to include:
```sparql
PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
SELECT ?type (COUNT(?w) AS ?n) WHERE {
  ?w a ?type .
  FILTER(STRSTARTS(STR(?type), "https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#"))
}
GROUP BY ?type ORDER BY DESC(?n) LIMIT 20
```
Expected: `gndo:Work`, `gndo:MusicalWork`, `gndo:SeriesOfConferencePublications` etc. — record which to include in `VALUES`.

**2. Confirm text index covers `preferredNameForTheWork`:**
```sparql
PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
SELECT ?w ?label ?score WHERE {
  ?w a gndo:Work .
  ?w gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?label .
  ?label contains-word "faust"
  BIND(score(?label) AS ?score)
}
ORDER BY DESC(?score) LIMIT 5
```
If this returns results, the predicate is indexed — use pattern A below. If not, fall back to FILTER REGEX (pattern B).

**3. Confirm author predicate name** — GND uses `gndo:firstAuthor` or `gndo:creator` depending on entity type; verify:
```sparql
PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
SELECT ?pred WHERE {
  <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118607359> ?pred ?obj .
  FILTER(STRSTARTS(STR(?obj), "https://d-nb.info/gnd/"))
} LIMIT 10
```

---

### Step 1 — Query patterns

**Pattern A: title + author, text-indexed (primary)**

Used when: author GND URI is available AND `contains-word` is confirmed.

```sparql
PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>

SELECT ?work ?prefLabel ?score WHERE {
  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork }
  ?work a ?wtype .
  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
  ?work gndo:firstAuthor <{author_gnd_uri}> .
  ?prefLabel contains-word "{title_tokens}"
  BIND(score(?prefLabel) AS ?score)
}
ORDER BY DESC(?score)
TEXTLIMIT 5
LIMIT 10
```

`TEXTLIMIT 5` caps QLever's internal text candidates; LIMIT 10 caps rows. Both recommended by QLever docs for performance.

**Pattern B: title only, text-indexed**

Used when: no author GND URI.

```sparql
PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>

SELECT ?work ?prefLabel ?varLabel ?score WHERE {
  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork }
  ?work a ?wtype .
  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
  {
    ?prefLabel contains-word "{title_tokens}"
    BIND(?prefLabel AS ?varLabel)
    BIND(score(?prefLabel) AS ?score)
  } UNION {
    ?work gndo:variantNameForTheWork ?alt .
    ?alt contains-word "{title_tokens}"
    BIND(?alt AS ?varLabel)
    BIND(score(?alt) AS ?score)
  }
}
ORDER BY DESC(?score)
TEXTLIMIT 5
LIMIT 10
```

The UNION catches cases where the DDB title matches a GND variant name rather than the preferred form.

**Pattern C: FILTER fallback** (if `contains-word` is not indexed for the predicate)

```sparql
PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>

SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork }
  ?work a ?wtype .
  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "{normalized_title}")
}
LIMIT 10
```

This is exact-match only; use normalized title (diacritics stripped, lowercased) for maximum recall.

---

### Step 2 — Title token preparation

QLever `contains-word` is word-based; multi-word titles need special handling:

- **Short titles (1–3 tokens):** pass all tokens: `contains-word "faust"` or separate triple patterns per word if AND semantics are needed
- **Long titles:** pass the 2–3 most distinctive tokens (skip stopwords: *der, die, das, ein, von, und*)
- **Phrase search:** QLever supports `contains-word "faust tragödie"` to require both words; use this for disambiguation

Normalize before tokenizing:
1. Unicode NFC normalize
2. Strip diacritics for the `contains-word` string (QLever's text index may or may not handle Umlauts depending on configuration — test both `"faust"` and `"fauſt"`)
3. Lowercase

---

### Step 3 — Post-retrieval scoring (unchanged logic, new source)

After QLever returns up to 10 candidates with QLever relevance scores:

| Condition | `match_type` | Triple predicate |
|---|---|---|
| `prefLabel == extracted_title` (case-insensitive) | `exact` | `owl:sameAs` |
| Normalized match (strip diacritics + punctuation) | `normalized` | `owl:sameAs` |
| Levenshtein distance ≤ 2 to prefLabel or any variantLabel | `fuzzy` | `skos:closeMatch` |
| No candidate above threshold | `unresolved` | — |

The QLever score is used only to rank candidates before this comparison, not as `match_confidence`. Use normalized edit distance to prefLabel as `match_confidence`.

---

### Step 4 — Changes to `link_gnd_works.py`

**Replace** the lobid-gnd REST call (Step 3–4 in the current note) with:

```python
import httpx

QLEVER_ENDPOINT = "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd"

async def query_gnd_werk(extracted_title: str, author_gnd_uri: str | None) -> list[dict]:
    tokens = tokenize_for_qlever(extracted_title)  # stopword-filtered, lowercased
    if author_gnd_uri:
        sparql = QUERY_A.format(
            title_tokens=tokens,
            author_gnd_uri=author_gnd_uri
        )
    else:
        sparql = QUERY_B.format(title_tokens=tokens)
    
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10.0) as client:
        r = await client.post(
            QLEVER_ENDPOINT,
            data={"query": sparql},
            headers={"Accept": "application/sparql-results+json"}
        )
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()["results"]["bindings"]
```

Key parameter: use `httpx.AsyncClient` with a concurrency limit (e.g. `asyncio.Semaphore(20)`) — QLever handles concurrent queries but the public endpoint will have rate limits. Start at 10 concurrent, tune up.

---

### Step 5 — What this replaces

| Old plan | New plan |
|---|---|
| `GET /gnd/search?q=label:"{title}"&filter=type:Work` (lobid REST) | SPARQL POST to QLever endpoint |
| Endpoint TBD / infrastructure setup required | Public DNB endpoint, no setup |
| No text relevance ranking | QLever `score()` for ranking |
| Title-only or title+author_uri string | Same; `gndo:firstAuthor` predicate name TBD via recon |

---

### Step 6 — Update notes

Two files need updating:

- [gnd-title-extraction.md](gemea/notes/gnd-title-extraction.md) — Steps 3–4: replace lobid REST pattern with the SPARQL patterns above; update "endpoint TBD" to the QLever URL
- [architecture.md](gemea/notes/architecture.md) — `link_gnd_works.py` row: note it calls `https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd` directly (no local GND infra required)

---

### Open questions to resolve first

1. **Is `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` in the text index?** → Run recon query 2. Determines whether to use Pattern A/B or fall back to Pattern C.
2. **Exact predicate for author link** (`gndo:firstAuthor` vs `gndo:creator`)? → Run recon query 3.
3. **Does QLever at DNB support `contains-word` on German Umlauts natively**, or do queries need diacritic-normalized tokens?
4. **Rate limits on the public endpoint** — no SLA published; test with `TEXTLIMIT 5 LIMIT 10` queries and measure latency before settling on concurrency level.

The recon queries are fast to run and answer all four. I'd do those before touching `link_gnd_works.py`.

## Approved Plan:
# Plan: Write GND linking plan to notes file

## Context

During the previous conversation turn, a detailed plan was produced for using the QLever DNB GND endpoint (`https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd`) for Werk entity linking in `link_gnd_works.py`. This replaces the lobid-gnd REST approach that was previously documented with "endpoint TBD." The user wants this plan persisted as a notes file in the GeMeA project.

## Task

Create `gemea/notes/gnd-linking-plan.md` with the full plan content from the planning discussion.

## File to create

**Path:** `/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/gnd-linking-plan.md`

## Content to write

The file should contain exactly the plan developed in the prior turn, structured as:

1. **Endpoint and HTTP API** — `https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd`, POST with `application/sparql-results+json`
2. **Step 0 — Recon queries** (3 queries: Work type coverage, confirm text index on `preferredNameForTheWork`, confirm author predicate name)
3. **Step 1 — Query patterns** (Pattern A: title+author text-indexed; Pattern B: title-only with UNION for variant names; Pattern C: FILTER fallback if text index not available)
4. **Step 2 — Title token preparation** (tokenization, stopword removal, multi-word handling, diacritic normalization)
5. **Step 3 — Post-retrieval scoring** (exact / normalized / fuzzy match type assignment, unchanged logic)
6. **Step 4 — Changes to `link_gnd_works.py`** (replace lobid REST with async httpx POST, concurrency semaphore)
7. **Step 5 — What this replaces** (comparison table: old lobid plan vs new QLever plan)
8. **Open questions** (4 items: text index coverage, author predicate, Umlaut handling, rate limits)

## No other files to modify

This is a new notes file only. `gnd-title-extraction.md` and `architecture.md` updates (noted as needed) are out of scope for this task — the user can decide whether to apply those separately.

## Verification

After writing, confirm the file exists and is readable at the expected path.

```

{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/gnd-linking-plan.md",
  "content": "# GeMeA — GND Werk Linking via QLever DNB Endpoint\n\nScript: `scripts/link_gnd_works.py`\nReplaces: lobid-gnd REST API approach (previously planned but endpoint was TBD)\n\n---\n\n## Endpoint and HTTP API\n\n**Endpoint:** `https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd`\n**Method:** `POST`\n**Body:** `query=<URL-encoded SPARQL>` (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`)\n**Accept:** `application/sparql-results+json`\n\nQLever instance settings:\n- Languages: `en`\n- Default max rows: 100\n- Default mode: 3 — SPARQL & context-sensitive entities\n\nQLever-specific keywords used: `contains-word`, `textlimit`, `score()`\n\n---\n\n## Step 0 — Recon queries (run once before implementation)\n\nThree things to verify before writing production queries.\n\n### 0.1 Work type coverage\n\nDetermine which GND classes to include in `VALUES ?wtype`:\n\n```sparql\nSELECT ?type (COUNT(?w) AS ?n) WHERE {\n  ?w a ?type .\n  FILTER(STRSTARTS(STR(?type), \"https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#\"))\n}\nGROUP BY ?type ORDER BY DESC(?n) LIMIT 20\n```\n\nExpected results include `gndo:Work`, `gndo:MusicalWork`, `gndo:SeriesOfConferencePublications`. Record which to include.\n\n### 0.2 Confirm text index covers `preferredNameForTheWork`\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\nSELECT ?w ?label ?score WHERE {\n  ?w a gndo:Work .\n  ?w gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?label .\n  ?label contains-word \"faust\"\n  BIND(score(?label) AS ?score)\n}\nORDER BY DESC(?score) LIMIT 5\n```\n\nIf this returns results → use Pattern A/B (text-indexed).\nIf QLever errors or returns empty → fall back to Pattern C (FILTER REGEX).\n\n### 0.3 Confirm author predicate name\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\nSELECT ?pred WHERE {\n  <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118607359> ?pred ?obj .\n  FILTER(STRSTARTS(STR(?obj), \"https://d-nb.info/gnd/\"))\n}\nLIMIT 10\n```\n\nDetermines whether to use `gndo:firstAuthor` or `gndo:creator` in Pattern A.\n\n---\n\n## Step 1 — Query patterns\n\n### Pattern A — Title + author, text-indexed (primary)\n\nUsed when: author GND URI is available AND `contains-word` is confirmed (recon 0.2).\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\n\nSELECT ?work ?prefLabel ?score WHERE {\n  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork }\n  ?work a ?wtype .\n  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .\n  ?work gndo:firstAuthor <{author_gnd_uri}> .\n  ?prefLabel contains-word \"{title_tokens}\"\n  BIND(score(?prefLabel) AS ?score)\n}\nORDER BY DESC(?score)\nTEXTLIMIT 5\nLIMIT 10\n```\n\nReplace `gndo:firstAuthor` with the predicate confirmed in recon 0.3.\n`TEXTLIMIT 5` caps QLever's internal text candidates for performance; `LIMIT 10` caps output rows.\n\n### Pattern B — Title only, text-indexed\n\nUsed when: no author GND URI is available.\nUNION catches cases where the DDB title matches a GND variant name rather than the preferred form.\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\n\nSELECT ?work ?prefLabel ?varLabel ?score WHERE {\n  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork }\n  ?work a ?wtype .\n  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .\n  {\n    ?prefLabel contains-word \"{title_tokens}\"\n    BIND(?prefLabel AS ?varLabel)\n    BIND(score(?prefLabel) AS ?score)\n  } UNION {\n    ?work gndo:variantNameForTheWork ?alt .\n    ?alt contains-word \"{title_tokens}\"\n    BIND(?alt AS ?varLabel)\n    BIND(score(?alt) AS ?score)\n  }\n}\nORDER BY DESC(?score)\nTEXTLIMIT 5\nLIMIT 10\n```\n\n### Pattern C — FILTER fallback (if `contains-word` not available on the predicate)\n\nExact-match only; normalized title (diacritics stripped, lowercased) maximizes recall.\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\n\nSELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {\n  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork }\n  ?work a ?wtype .\n  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .\n  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = \"{normalized_title}\")\n}\nLIMIT 10\n```\n\n---\n\n## Step 2 — Title token preparation\n\nQLever `contains-word` is word-based, not phrase-based by default.\n\n**Tokenization rules:**\n1. Unicode NFC normalize\n2. Lowercase\n3. Strip diacritics (test both forms — QLever may or may not handle Umlauts natively; see open questions)\n4. Remove stopwords: `der, die, das, ein, eine, von, und, zu, im, in, an, auf, für, mit, bei, dem, den`\n5. For multi-word titles: use 2–3 most distinctive tokens\n\n**Multi-word handling:**\n- Short (1–3 tokens): pass all tokens in one `contains-word` value — QLever treats this as AND\n- Long titles: pass 2–3 most distinctive (non-stopword) tokens\n- High-ambiguity titles (single common word like *Briefe*, *Gedichte*): require author constraint (Pattern A); skip title-only search if no author URI\n\n---\n\n## Step 3 — Post-retrieval scoring\n\nQLever's `score()` is used only to rank candidates before string comparison. Match type is assigned in Python:\n\n| Condition | `match_type` | Triple predicate |\n|---|---|---|\n| `prefLabel == extracted_title` (case-insensitive) | `exact` | `owl:sameAs` |\n| Normalized match (strip diacritics + punctuation) | `normalized` | `owl:sameAs` |\n| Levenshtein distance ≤ 2 to `prefLabel` or any `variantNameForTheWork` | `fuzzy` | `skos:closeMatch` |\n| No candidate above threshold | `unresolved` | — |\n\n`match_confidence` = normalized edit similarity to `prefLabel` (not QLever score).\n\nThis logic is unchanged from the original plan in `gnd-title-extraction.md`.\n\n---\n\n## Step 4 — Changes to `link_gnd_works.py`\n\nReplace the lobid-gnd REST call (Steps 3–4 in `gnd-title-extraction.md`) with:\n\n```python\nimport httpx\nimport asyncio\n\nQLEVER_ENDPOINT = \"https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd\"\nCONCURRENCY = 10  # tune up after rate-limit testing\n\n_semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(CONCURRENCY)\n\nasync def query_gnd_werk(\n    extracted_title: str,\n    author_gnd_uri: str | None\n) -> list[dict]:\n    tokens = tokenize_for_qlever(extracted_title)\n    sparql = (\n        QUERY_A.format(title_tokens=tokens, author_gnd_uri=author_gnd_uri)\n        if author_gnd_uri\n        else QUERY_B.format(title_tokens=tokens)\n    )\n    async with _semaphore:\n        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10.0) as client:\n            r = await client.post(\n                QLEVER_ENDPOINT,\n                data={\"query\": sparql},\n                headers={\"Accept\": \"application/sparql-results+json\"}\n            )\n    r.raise_for_status()\n    return r.json()[\"results\"][\"bindings\"]\n```\n\nPattern selection:\n- Pattern A if `author_gnd_uri` is set\n- Pattern B otherwise\n- Pattern C as fallback if recon 0.2 shows `contains-word` is unavailable\n\n---\n\n## Step 5 — What this replaces\n\n| Old plan | New plan |\n|---|---|\n| `GET /gnd/search?q=label:\"{title}\"&filter=type:Work` (lobid REST) | SPARQL POST to QLever endpoint |\n| Endpoint TBD / local infrastructure required | Public DNB endpoint, no setup |\n| No text relevance ranking | QLever `score()` ranks candidates |\n| `link_gnd_works.py` depended on lobid API availability | Depends on `sparql.dnb.de` uptime (DNB-operated) |\n| Title-only or title + author_uri via query param | Same semantics; `gndo:firstAuthor` predicate (verify in recon 0.3) |\n\n---\n\n## Open questions\n\n- [ ] **Is `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` in the text index?** Run recon 0.2. Determines Pattern A/B vs Pattern C.\n- [ ] **Exact predicate for author link** — `gndo:firstAuthor` or `gndo:creator`? Run recon 0.3.\n- [ ] **Umlaut handling in `contains-word`** — does the QLever text index at DNB normalize Umlauts, or must queries use diacritic-stripped tokens? Test `\"goethe\"` vs `\"göthe\"` against a known record.\n- [ ] **Rate limits on the public endpoint** — no published SLA; start at `CONCURRENCY = 10`, measure latency, tune up cautiously.\n"
}

### User *(2026-03-20T22:33:36.495Z)*

in Step 2, what are the criteria in selecting "distinctive tokens" and why?

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-20T22:34:10.859Z)*

Two criteria, one underlying principle.

**The principle:** `contains-word` with multiple tokens uses AND semantics in QLever — each additional token makes the query *more* restrictive. The candidate retrieval stage should maximize recall (get the right GND Work somewhere in the top-10); precision is handled by post-retrieval string scoring. So the goal is the minimum set of tokens that uniquely identifies the work without over-constraining.

**Criterion 1 — Semantic centrality**

Use tokens that are the *essential content* of the title, not modifiers or structural words. In German bibliographic titles this means:

- **Keep:** nouns, proper nouns (place/person names embedded in titles), unique adjectives
- **Drop:** articles (*der, die, das*), prepositions (*von, zu, in, auf*), coordinating conjunctions (*und, oder*), ordinals (*erster, zweiter*), format/edition terms (*Band, Teil, Heft, Auflage, Bd., Nr.*)

Example: *"Die Leiden des jungen Werthers"* → tokens `leiden werthers` (or just `werthers` — the proper noun alone is sufficient and distinctive)

**Criterion 2 — Low collision frequency**

Prefer tokens that appear in few GND Works — high information value in the index. Rough heuristics:

- **High-collision terms to avoid even if not stopwords:** *Briefe, Gedichte, Werke, Schriften, Geschichte, Bericht, Katalog* — these appear in thousands of Works and contribute almost no discriminating power
- **Low-collision terms to prefer:** proper nouns, compound nouns specific to the subject, unusual words

Example: *"Gedichte"* alone as a query would return thousands of GND Works. *"Gedichte faust"* is a meaningful constraint. *"faust"* alone probably suffices if there's an author URI.

**Why 2–3 tokens specifically**

- 1 token: usually sufficient with Pattern A (author constraint already narrows heavily); sometimes sufficient for highly distinctive titles in Pattern B
- 2–3 tokens: the sweet spot for Pattern B (no author) — enough to discriminate, low risk of AND-excluding the right Work due to GND/DDB title variation
- >3 tokens: increasing risk of false negatives when GND's preferred form differs from DDB's form (e.g., GND has *"Faust. Der Tragödie erster Teil"* but DDB has *"Faust: Erster Teil"* — 4+ tokens from the DDB form likely miss the GND entry)

**A concrete decision rule for the code:**

```python
GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS = {
    "briefe", "gedichte", "werke", "schriften", "geschichte",
    "bericht", "katalog", "sammlung", "aufsätze", "texte",
    "band", "teil", "heft", "nummer", "jahrgang",
}

def select_tokens(tokens: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    # After stopword removal
    distinctive = [t for t in tokens if t not in GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS and len(t) > 3]
    return distinctive[:3] if len(distinctive) >= 2 else tokens[:3]
```

The `len(t) > 3` filter also drops short high-frequency fragments (*"am", "des", "zur"* if not caught by the stopword list). `GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS` needs to be expanded empirically by running the recon query in Step 0.1 and inspecting high-frequency title words across GND Works.

Worth noting in the open questions: the `GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS` list is corpus-specific and should be derived from a frequency query against the endpoint, not hardcoded blindly.

### User *(2026-03-20T22:34:40.924Z)*

Step 3: provide citations on the match types and triple predicates

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-20T22:35:33.491Z)*

**`owl:sameAs`**

W3C OWL 2 Web Ontology Language Primer (2012), §4.2: defines `owl:sameAs` as asserting that two URIs denote the same individual in the world. The semantics are strong — any statement true of one URI is entailed to be true of the other under OWL semantics.

> Bock et al., *OWL 2 Web Ontology Language Primer*, W3C Recommendation, 2012. https://www.w3.org/TR/owl2-primer/

**`skos:closeMatch`**

W3C SKOS Reference (2009), §10 (Mapping Properties): `skos:closeMatch` links two concepts that are "sufficiently similar that they can be used interchangeably in some information retrieval applications." The spec explicitly distinguishes it from `skos:exactMatch` (safe for use in all contexts) and notes `closeMatch` is the right predicate when approximate equivalence cannot be asserted with confidence.

> Miles & Bechhofer, *SKOS Simple Knowledge Organization System Reference*, W3C Recommendation, 2009. https://www.w3.org/TR/skos-reference/#mapping

**Why `owl:sameAs` for exact/normalized but `skos:closeMatch` for fuzzy**

The key paper is:

> Halpin, Hayes, McCusker, McGuinness & Thompson, *When owl:sameAs Isn't the Same: An Analysis of Identity in Linked Data*, ISWC 2010. DOI: 10.1007/978-3-642-17746-0_16

Halpin et al. show that `owl:sameAs` is routinely misused in linked data to express approximate or uncertain identity, which causes unwanted OWL entailments downstream. Their recommendation: reserve `owl:sameAs` for *verified* identity; use `skos:closeMatch` for uncertain or approximate matches. This directly motivates the exact/normalized → `owl:sameAs`, fuzzy → `skos:closeMatch` split in Step 3.

**Match type taxonomy (exact / normalized / fuzzy)**

The three-level taxonomy is standard in entity linking surveys:

> Shen, Wang & Han, *Entity Linking with a Knowledge Base: Issues, Techniques, and Solutions*, IEEE TKDE 27(2), 2015. DOI: 10.1109/TKDE.2014.2327028

Section 3.1 covers mention-to-entity string matching and distinguishes exact string match, surface-form normalization (case, diacritics, punctuation), and approximate/fuzzy matching as the standard candidate generation ladder.

**Levenshtein distance ≤ 2 specifically**

> Levenshtein, *Binary Codes Capable of Correcting Deletions, Insertions, and Reversals*, Soviet Physics Doklady 10(8), 1966.

The threshold of ≤ 2 is a common empirical choice in bibliographic entity linking but is not derived from first principles — it should be validated against a gold set (as already planned in `gnd-title-extraction.md` §Paper §4 quality metrics). The ISWC 2010 GND / authority record linking literature doesn't converge on a single threshold.

---

**One flag for the plan:** `owl:sameAs` asserts identity between *individuals* under OWL semantics. A DDB ProvidedCHO and a GND Werk URI are arguably not the same individual — the CHO is a catalog record for a manifestation, the Werk URI is an authority record for an abstract work. `skos:exactMatch` would be semantically safer for exact/normalized matches too, since it asserts equivalence for retrieval purposes without triggering OWL identity entailments. Worth deciding explicitly before triple generation; the Halpin paper and the SKOS §10 spec are the relevant sources for that decision.

Remove stopwords: `der, die, das, ein, eine, von, und, zu, im, in, an, auf, für, mit, bei, dem, den`\n5. For multi-word titles: use 2–3 most distinctive tokens\n\n**Multi-word handling:**\n- Short (1–3 tokens): pass all tokens in one `contains-word` value — QLever treats this as AND\n- Long titles: pass 2–3 most distinctive (non-stopword) tokens\n- High-ambiguity titles (single common word like *Briefe*, *Gedichte*): require author constraint (Pattern A); skip title-only search if no author URI",
  "new_string": "## Step 2 — Title token preparation\n\nQLever `contains-word` is word-based, not phrase-based by default.\n\n**Tokenization rules:**\n1. Unicode NFC normalize\n2. Lowercase\n3. Strip diacritics (test both forms — QLever may or may not handle Umlauts natively; see open questions)\n4. Remove stopwords: `der, die, das, ein, eine, von, und, zu, im, in, an, auf, für, mit, bei, dem, den`\n5. For multi-word titles: select 2–3 distinctive tokens (see below)\n\n### Distinctive token selection\n\n**Underlying principle:** `contains-word` with multiple tokens is AND semantics — each additional token makes the query more restrictive. Candidate retrieval should maximize recall (get the right GND Work somewhere in the top-10); precision is handled by post-retrieval string scoring in Step 3. The goal is the minimum set of tokens that identifies the work without over-constraining.\n\n**Criterion 1 — Semantic centrality.** Use tokens that are the essential content of the title, not modifiers or structural words:\n\n- **Keep:** nouns, proper nouns (place/person names embedded in titles), semantically unique adjectives\n- **Drop even if not in the stopword list:** articles, prepositions, ordinals (*erster, zweiter*), format/edition terms (*Band, Teil, Heft, Auflage, Bd., Nr.*)\n\nExample: *\"Die Leiden des jungen Werthers\"* → tokens `leiden werthers`; the proper noun alone (`werthers`) is sufficient with Pattern A.\n\n**Criterion 2 — Low collision frequency.** Prefer tokens that appear in few GND Works. Some words are not stopwords but are so common in bibliographic titles that they add almost no discriminating power:\n\n```python\nGENERIC_TITLE_WORDS = {\n    \"briefe\", \"gedichte\", \"werke\", \"schriften\", \"geschichte\",\n    \"bericht\", \"katalog\", \"sammlung\", \"aufsätze\", \"texte\",\n    \"band\", \"teil\", \"heft\", \"nummer\", \"jahrgang\",\n}\n```\n\nExample: *\"Gedichte\"* alone returns thousands of GND Works. *\"faust\"* alone is sufficient with an author URI.\n\nThis list should be derived empirically from a frequency query against the endpoint, not hardcoded blindly — run after recon 0.1.\n\n**Decision rule:**\n\n```python\ndef select_tokens(tokens: list[str]) -> list[str]:\n    # tokens: already stopword-filtered, lowercased, diacritics-stripped\n    distinctive = [t for t in tokens if t not in GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS and len(t) > 3]\n    return distinctive[:3] if len(distinctive) >= 2 else tokens[:3]\n```\n\n`len(t) > 3` also drops short high-frequency fragments not caught by the stopword list.\n\n**Why 2–3 tokens specifically:**\n\n- 1 token: usually sufficient with Pattern A (author constraint already narrows heavily); sometimes sufficient for highly distinctive titles in Pattern B\n- 2–3 tokens: the sweet spot for Pattern B — enough to discriminate, low risk of AND-excluding the right Work due to GND/DDB title variation\n- >3 tokens: increasing risk of false negatives when GND's preferred form differs from DDB's (e.g., GND has *\"Faust. Der Tragödie erster Teil\"* but DDB has *\"Faust: Erster Teil\"* — four tokens from the DDB form likely miss the GND entry)\n\n**Multi-word handling:**\n- Short (1–3 tokens after filtering): pass all remaining tokens\n- Long titles: apply `select_tokens()` above\n- High-ambiguity titles (single generic word like *Briefe*, *Gedichte* with no distinctive co-tokens): require author constraint (Pattern A); skip title-only search (Pattern B) if no author URI is available"
}
```

```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/gnd-linking-plan.md",
  "old_string": "## Step 3 — Post-retrieval scoring\n\nQLever's `score()` is used only to rank candidates before string comparison. Match type is assigned in Python:\n\n| Condition | `match_type` | Triple predicate |\n|---|---|---|\n| `prefLabel == extracted_title` (case-insensitive) | `exact` | `owl:sameAs` |\n| Normalized match (strip diacritics + punctuation) | `normalized` | `owl:sameAs` |\n| Levenshtein distance ≤ 2 to `prefLabel` or any `variantNameForTheWork` | `fuzzy` | `skos:closeMatch` |\n| No candidate above threshold | `unresolved` | — |\n\n`match_confidence` = normalized edit similarity to `prefLabel` (not QLever score).\n\nThis logic is unchanged from the original plan in `gnd-title-extraction.md`.",
  "new_string": "## Step 3 — Post-retrieval scoring\n\nQLever's `score()` is used only to rank candidates before string comparison. Match type is assigned in Python:\n\n| Condition | `match_type` | Triple predicate |\n|---|---|---|\n| `prefLabel == extracted_title` (case-insensitive) | `exact` | `owl:sameAs` |\n| Normalized match (strip diacritics + punctuation) | `normalized` | `owl:sameAs` |\n| Levenshtein distance ≤ 2 to `prefLabel` or any `variantNameForTheWork` | `fuzzy` | `skos:closeMatch` |\n| No candidate above threshold | `unresolved` | — |\n\n`match_confidence` = normalized edit similarity to `prefLabel` (not QLever score).\n\nThis logic is unchanged from the original plan in `gnd-title-extraction.md`.\n\n### Predicate justification\n\n**`owl:sameAs`** (exact and normalized matches) — asserts that two URIs denote the same individual; any statement true of one is entailed true of the other under OWL semantics (Bock et al., *OWL 2 Web Ontology Language Primer*, W3C Recommendation, 2012, §4.2).\n\n**`skos:closeMatch`** (fuzzy matches) — links two concepts \"sufficiently similar that they can be used interchangeably in some information retrieval applications\" but stops short of asserting full identity (Miles & Bechhofer, *SKOS Simple Knowledge Organization System Reference*, W3C Recommendation, 2009, §10).\n\n**Why `owl:sameAs` for certain matches and `skos:closeMatch` for uncertain:** Halpin et al. show that `owl:sameAs` is routinely misused in linked data to express approximate identity, producing unwanted OWL entailments downstream. Their recommendation: reserve `owl:sameAs` for verified identity; use `skos:closeMatch` for uncertain or approximate matches (Halpin, Hayes, McCusker, McGuinness & Thompson, *When owl:sameAs Isn't the Same: An Analysis of Identity in Linked Data*, ISWC 2010, DOI: 10.1007/978-3-642-17746-0_16).\n\n**Match type taxonomy** (exact / normalized / fuzzy) follows the standard candidate generation ladder in entity linking (Shen, Wang & Han, *Entity Linking with a Knowledge Base: Issues, Techniques, and Solutions*, IEEE TKDE 27(2), 2015, §3.1, DOI: 10.1109/TKDE.2014.2327028).\n\n**Levenshtein distance** (Levenshtein, *Binary Codes Capable of Correcting Deletions, Insertions, and Reversals*, Soviet Physics Doklady 10(8), 1966). The threshold of ≤ 2 is a common empirical choice; validate against the gold set planned in `gnd-title-extraction.md` §Paper §4 quality metrics.\n\n### Open predicate question\n\n`owl:sameAs` asserts identity between *individuals*. A DDB ProvidedCHO and a GND Werk URI are arguably not the same individual — the CHO is a catalog record for a manifestation, the Werk URI is an authority record for an abstract work. `skos:exactMatch` (same concept for retrieval purposes, without OWL identity entailments; SKOS Reference §10) may be semantically safer for exact and normalized matches too. Decide before triple generation; the Halpin paper and SKOS §10 are the relevant sources."
}
```

### User *(2026-03-20T22:42:40.768Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→# ISBD Punctuation Analysis — DF_DE_TITLES
     2→
     3→**Dataset:** `data/DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl` — 4,477,780 rows, column `title`
     4→- Source: German titles from "content" objects.
     5→
     6→**Script:** `scripts/check_isbd_titles.py`
     7→**Date:** 2026-03-17
     8→
     9→## Summary
    10→
    11→| Metric | Count | % |
    12→|---|---|---|
    13→| Rows total | 4,477,780 | — |
    14→| Empty / null titles | 0 | 0% |
    15→| Titles with ≥1 ISBD pattern (incl. trailing `.`) | 1,993,553 | **44.5%** |
    16→| Titles with ≥1 ISBD pattern (excl. trailing `.`) | 1,272,718 | **28.4%** |
    17→
    18→## Pattern Breakdown
    19→
    20→| Pattern | Signal | Count | % |
    21→|---|---|---|---|
    22→| ` :` | Other title information | 909,869 | 20.3% |
    23→| trailing `.` | Area-end period | 783,752 | 17.5% |
    24→| `[ ]` | Supplied / inferred data | 315,263 | 7.0% |
    25→| `…` / `...` | Ellipsis / truncation | 246,370 | 5.5% |
    26→| ` ;` | Subsequent SoR / series | 174,813 | 3.9% |
    27→| ` /` | Statement of responsibility | 33,907 | 0.8% |
    28→| ` =` | Parallel title | 26,264 | 0.6% |
    29→
    30→## Notes on the Trailing Period
    31→
    32→The trailing period (`.` at end of string) is an **ISBD area-closing mark** — it closes the title area when the field doesn't already end with `?`, `!`, or `)`. Examples:
    33→
    34→- `Harzreise im Winter.` — closes title area
    35→- `Goethes Briefe, Bd. 2.` — closes title + numbering
    36→- `Der neue Amadis.` — simple title close
    37→
    38→**Why it's noisy.** The regex `[^.]\.$` (last char is `.`, preceded by a non-dot) also fires on:
    39→
    40→- **Abbreviations as last token**: `Hrsg.`, `Bd.`, `Nr.`, `u.a.` — very common in German bibliographic data
    41→- **Ordinals**: `4.`, `Bd. 3.`
    42→- **Incomplete titles ending in an abbreviation**: `... et al.`
    43→
    44→The 17.5% / 783k figure is therefore an **upper bound**. True ISBD area-closing periods are a subset. To get a cleaner signal, one would need to either:
    45→1. Strip known German abbreviations before applying the pattern, or
    46→2. Use a more conservative heuristic (e.g., require the title to also contain another ISBD marker, suggesting it was catalogued with full ISBD punctuation).
    47→
    48→For the purposes of GeMeA title extraction, ` :` (20.3%) is the most reliable single ISBD indicator — it almost always signals an `other title information` segment that should be split off from the main title.
    49→



```

**Tool call:** `Write`
```json
{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/paper-tpdl-gnd-linking.md",
  "content": "# Paper Idea — TPDL: Linking DDB Titles to GND Works\n\n**Venue:** International Conference on Theory and Practice of Digital Libraries (TPDL)\n**Typical deadline:** ~April–May; conference ~September\n**Format:** Full paper 12–15 pp. LNCS or short paper 6–8 pp.\n**Status:** Idea — not started\n\n---\n\n## Pitch\n\n65 million objects in the Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek carry raw `dc:title` strings in ISBD punctuation format. We describe a pipeline to extract clean titles from these strings — using a rule-based ISBD parser and a zero-shot NER model — and link them to GND Werktitel authority records via a public SPARQL endpoint. A key contribution is the silver-labeled NER training corpus, automatically derived from the ISBD-parsed subset, which we release as the first large-scale German bibliographic NER dataset. We report linking coverage and precision across title types and extraction methods, and quantify how much GND authority enrichment changes downstream retrieval quality in the GeMeA knowledge graph.\n\n---\n\n## Core contributions\n\n1. **Silver NER dataset** — automatically labeled from 4.5M DDB titles (DF_DE_TITLES) using the ISBD parser as a distant supervisor. First large-scale German bibliographic NER dataset with `TITLE`, `PERSON`, `PUBLISHER`, `YEAR`, `EDITION` labels. Released publicly.\n\n2. **Entity linking pipeline** — ISBD rule-based extraction + NuNER Zero zero-shot NER fallback → QLever SPARQL lookup against the DNB GND endpoint (`https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd`) with three-tier match scoring (exact / normalized / fuzzy). Deduplication reduces 65M lookups to ~5–10M unique pairs.\n\n3. **Evaluation at scale** — linking coverage and precision reported by extraction method (ISBD vs. NER), title language (modern German / historical German / Latin), and match type. Manual gold set of ~500 records stratified by era.\n\n4. **Downstream impact** — before/after comparison in GeMeA: how many ProvidedCHOs gain a `mocho:Work` parent; change in WEMI grouping depth; effect on `/work/{id}` page coverage.\n\n---\n\n## Research questions\n\n1. What fraction of DDB `dc:title` strings can be resolved to a GND Werktitel, and how does that vary by extraction method?\n2. How clean are silver labels derived from ISBD parsing? Can they train a NER model that generalizes to the non-ISBD majority (71%)?\n3. How does NuNER Zero zero-shot NER compare to an ISBD-fine-tuned model on bibliographic title extraction?\n4. What proportion of historical German and Latin titles remain unresolved, and why?\n\n---\n\n## Paper structure (sketch)\n\n1. **Introduction** — scale of the DDB, motivation for GND Werk linking, gap: no existing pipeline or labeled data\n2. **Background** — GND authority data, ISBD punctuation conventions, GeMeA architecture (brief)\n3. **Dataset: DF_DE_TITLES** — 4.47M titles, ISBD coverage analysis (28.4% excl. trailing period), pattern breakdown (`:` 20.3%, `/` 0.8%, etc.); source: `isbd-title-analysis.md`\n4. **Title extraction**\n   - Rule-based ISBD parser (primary; 28.4% coverage)\n   - Silver labeling procedure (ISBD → auto-annotation → dataset release)\n   - NuNER Zero zero-shot NER (fallback; 71.6% of records)\n   - Evaluation: gold set of 500 stratified records\n5. **Entity linking via QLever GND**\n   - Endpoint and query design (Pattern A/B/C; `contains-word`; `gndo:firstAuthor` cross-reference)\n   - Distinctive token selection for `contains-word`\n   - Post-retrieval scoring: exact / normalized / fuzzy; predicate assignment (`owl:sameAs` / `skos:closeMatch`)\n   - Deduplication strategy (65M → 5–10M unique pairs)\n6. **Results**\n   - Linking coverage by extraction method and title language\n   - Precision sample (100 manually checked linked pairs)\n   - Work grouping size distribution\n   - Downstream GeMeA impact\n7. **Discussion** — failure modes, historical/Latin titles, ambiguous titles without author URI\n8. **Conclusion and dataset release**\n\n---\n\n## Key numbers to report (from existing analysis)\n\n| Metric | Value | Source |\n|---|---|---|\n| Total DDB objects | ~65M | GeMeA project |\n| DF_DE_TITLES rows | 4,477,780 | `isbd-title-analysis.md` |\n| ISBD coverage (excl. trailing `.`) | 28.4% | `isbd-title-analysis.md` |\n| NER fallback scope | ~71.6% | `isbd-title-analysis.md` |\n| ` :` pattern (most reliable ISBD signal) | 20.3% | `isbd-title-analysis.md` |\n| ` /` pattern (SoR split) | 0.8% | `isbd-title-analysis.md` |\n| Unique title pairs (estimated) | 5–10M | `gnd-title-extraction.md` |\n| Target Werk linking rate | ≥70% | `gnd-title-extraction.md` |\n\n---\n\n## Differentiators from the ISWC GeMeA paper\n\n| | ISWC GeMeA paper | This TPDL paper |\n|---|---|---|\n| Focus | Full KG architecture (GeMeA system) | Title extraction + GND linking methodology |\n| Audience | Semantic Web / KG | Digital libraries / information science |\n| Contribution | System + resource | Dataset + pipeline + evaluation |\n| NER detail | Brief (one paragraph) | Central (§4) |\n| Linking evaluation | Quality metrics for paper §4 | Core results (§6) |\n| Dataset release | Not planned | Yes — silver NER corpus |\n\nThe two papers cite each other but are self-contained. This paper feeds the GeMeA pipeline; ISWC describes the broader system.\n\n---\n\n## Related work hooks\n\n- Bibliographic entity linking: VIAF, OCLC WorldCat reconciliation\n- German NER: GermEval 2014, CLEF-HIPE-2020, HIPE-2022 (ajmc)\n- Zero-shot NER: NuNER Zero (NuMind 2024), GLiNER (NAACL 2024)\n- ISBD parsing: no prior automated approach at this scale (gap to claim)\n- Silver labeling via distant supervision: Mintz et al. 2009 (standard citation)\n- QLever for large-scale SPARQL: Bast et al. (university of Freiburg)\n\n---\n\n## Open questions before committing\n\n- [ ] Does TPDL accept dataset/resource papers, or must the focus be a methodology paper? Check recent proceedings.\n- [ ] Is the silver corpus large enough to be a standalone contribution, or does it need NuNER Zero evaluation results first?\n- [ ] Overlap with ISWC GeMeA paper: are §4 quality metrics in the ISWC paper sufficient to preclude a standalone TPDL paper, or is the NER + linking detail clearly outside ISWC scope?\n- [ ] Do we have the DF_DE_TITLES for the full DDB corpus, or only the 4.47M German content subset? Coverage numbers change significantly.\n"
}
```

Decide before triple generation; the Halpin paper and SKOS §10 are the relevant sources.",
  "new_string": "**`skos:exactMatch`** (exact and normalized matches) — asserts two concepts are \"an exact match\" and can be used interchangeably for all retrieval purposes, without the OWL identity entailments of `owl:sameAs` (Miles & Bechhofer, *SKOS Simple Knowledge Organization System Reference*, W3C Recommendation, 2009, §10). Semantically appropriate here: a DDB ProvidedCHO and a GND Werk URI are not the same individual (CHO = catalog record for a manifestation; Werk URI = authority record for an abstract work), so asserting OWL individual identity would be incorrect.\n\n**`skos:closeMatch`** (fuzzy matches) — links two concepts \"sufficiently similar that they can be used interchangeably in some information retrieval applications\" but stops short of asserting full equivalence (Miles & Bechhofer, 2009, §10).\n\n**Why not `owl:sameAs`:** Halpin et al. show that `owl:sameAs` is routinely misused in linked data to express approximate or cross-type identity, producing unwanted OWL entailments downstream. Their recommendation: reserve `owl:sameAs` for verified same-individual identity; use SKOS mapping properties for concept-level matching (Halpin, Hayes, McCusker, McGuinness & Thompson, *When owl:sameAs Isn't the Same: An Analysis of Identity in Linked Data*, ISWC 2010, DOI: 10.1007/978-3-642-17746-0_16).\n\n**Match type taxonomy** (exact / normalized / fuzzy) follows the standard candidate generation ladder in entity linking (Shen, Wang & Han, *Entity Linking with a Knowledge Base: Issues, Techniques, and Solutions*, IEEE TKDE 27(2), 2015, §3.1, DOI: 10.1109/TKDE.2014.2327028).\n\n**Levenshtein distance** (Levenshtein, *Binary Codes Capable of Correcting Deletions, Insertions, and Reversals*, Soviet Physics Doklady 10(8), 1966). The threshold of ≤ 2 is a common empirical choice; validate against the gold set planned in `gnd-title-extraction.md` §Paper §4 quality metrics."
}
```

### User *(2026-03-20T22:47:21.800Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→# Paper Idea — TPDL: Linking DDB Titles to GND Works
     2→
     3→**Venue:** International Conference on Theory and Practice of Digital Libraries (TPDL)
     4→**Typical deadline:** ~April–May; conference ~September
     5→**Format:** Full paper 12–15 pp. LNCS or short paper 6–8 pp.
     6→**Status:** Idea — not started
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## Pitch
    11→
    12→65 million objects in the Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek carry raw `dc:title` strings in ISBD punctuation format. We describe a pipeline to extract clean titles from these strings — using a rule-based ISBD parser and a zero-shot NER model — and link them to GND Werktitel authority records via a public SPARQL endpoint. A key contribution is the silver-labeled NER training corpus, automatically derived from the ISBD-parsed subset, which we release as the first large-scale German bibliographic NER dataset. We report linking coverage and precision across title types and extraction methods, and quantify how much GND authority enrichment changes downstream retrieval quality in the GeMeA knowledge graph.
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Core contributions
    17→
    18→1. **Silver NER dataset** — automatically labeled from 4.5M DDB titles (DF_DE_TITLES) using the ISBD parser as a distant supervisor. First large-scale German bibliographic NER dataset with `TITLE`, `PERSON`, `PUBLISHER`, `YEAR`, `EDITION` labels. Released publicly.
    19→
    20→2. **Entity linking pipeline** — ISBD rule-based extraction + NuNER Zero zero-shot NER fallback → QLever SPARQL lookup against the DNB GND endpoint (`https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd`) with three-tier match scoring (exact / normalized / fuzzy). Deduplication reduces 65M lookups to ~5–10M unique pairs.



```

### Assistant *(2026-03-20T22:47:44.005Z)*

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/paper-tpdl-gnd-linking.md",
  "old_string": "---\n\n## Key numbers to report (from existing analysis)",
  "new_string": "---\n\n## Design decisions\n\n### `skos:exactMatch` over `owl:sameAs` for confirmed links\n\nThe linking pipeline uses `skos:exactMatch` (exact and normalized matches) and `skos:closeMatch` (fuzzy matches) rather than `owl:sameAs`. The choice is worth one sentence in the paper because reviewers from a Semantic Web background will ask.\n\n**The argument:** `owl:sameAs` asserts that two URIs denote the same individual — any triple true of one is entailed true of the other under OWL semantics. A DDB ProvidedCHO and a GND Werk URI are not the same individual: the CHO is a catalog record for a physical manifestation; the Werk URI is an authority record for an abstract work. Asserting `owl:sameAs` between them would be ontologically incorrect and would propagate unintended inferences through any reasoner that consumes the data.\n\n`skos:exactMatch` asserts that two concepts \"can be used interchangeably for all retrieval purposes\" (Miles & Bechhofer, *SKOS Reference*, W3C 2009, §10) — appropriate for confirmed title-to-Werk links without the OWL identity burden. `skos:closeMatch` handles fuzzy matches where interchangeability holds only in some retrieval contexts.\n\nSupporting citation: Halpin et al. (*When owl:sameAs Isn't the Same*, ISWC 2010) document systematic misuse of `owl:sameAs` in linked data for approximate and cross-type identity, and recommend SKOS mapping properties as the correct alternative.\n\n---\n\n## Key numbers to report (from existing analysis)"
}
```
