endpoint does not normalize)**\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\nSELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {\n  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }\n  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }\n  ?work a ?wtype .\n  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .\n  ?work ?authorPred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .\n  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = \"gotz von berlichingen\")\n}\nLIMIT 10\n```\n\n```bash\ncurl -s -X POST \"https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd\" \\\n  -H \"Accept: application/sparql-results+json\" \\\n  -H \"Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded\" \\\n  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\nSELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {\n  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }\n  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }\n  ?work a ?wtype .\n  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .\n  ?work ?authorPred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .\n  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = \"gotz von berlichingen\")\n}\nLIMIT 10'\n```\n\n**Interpretation:**\n\n| Query A result | Query B result | Conclusion | Impact on FR-05 |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Results returned | No results | Endpoint does **not** normalize Umlauts — FILTER is byte-exact | Strip diacritics from **query string only if GND stores stripped forms**; otherwise keep Umlauts; send both forms via UNION |\n| Results returned | Results returned | Endpoint normalizes Umlauts (accent-insensitive collation) | Stripping is safe; normalized form sufficient for FILTER |\n| No results | No results | Title not stored as `preferredNameForTheWork` in this form | Re-check with `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` (OQ-03) |\n\n---\n\n## Knowledge base index\n\n**Description:** DNB Bibliography and GND (Gemeinsame Normdatei) (`authorities-gnd_lds`, `dnb-all_lds` version 23.02.2026) — updated monthly\n\n| Metric | Value |\n|---|---|\n| Triples | 1,242,168,767 |\n| Subjects | 186,556,644 |\n| Predicates | 591 |\n| Objects | 457,761,162 |\n\n---\n\n## Endpoint and HTTP API\n\n**Endpoint:** `https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd`\n**Method:** `POST`\n**Body:** `query=<URL-encoded SPARQL>` (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`)\n**Accept:** `application/sparql-results+json`\n\nQLever instance settings:\n- Languages: `en`\n- Default max rows: 100\n- Default mode: 3 — SPARQL & context-sensitive entities\n\nQLever-specific keywords used: `contains-word`, `textlimit`, `score()`\n\n---\n\n## Step 0 — Recon queries (run once before implementation)\n\nThree things to verify before writing production queries.\n\n### 0.1 Work type coverage\n\nIFLA LRM defines a Work (LRM-E2) as \"a distinct intellectual or artistic creation\" — the abstract entity at the top of the WEMI hierarchy, independent of any particular realization or carrier. The GND ontology (`gnd_20251218.ttl`) models six subclasses of `gndo:Work`. Only three correspond to the IFLA LRM Work concept:\n\n| GND class | Label (en / de) | MARC21 | IFLA LRM | Include in queries |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `gndo:Work` | Work / Werk | `079 $v=wit` | LRM-E2 Work — direct match | **Yes** |\n| `gndo:MusicalWork` | Musical work / Werk der Musik | `079 $v=wim` | LRM-E2 Work — a musical composition as distinct creative entity | **Yes** |\n| `gndo:Manuscript` | Manuscript / Schriftdenkmal | `079 $v=wis` | LRM-E2 Work — a unique historical manuscript (e.g. Nibelungenlied) is the distinct intellectual creation itself | **Yes** |\n| `gndo:Expression` | Expression / Expression | `079 $v=wie` | LRM-E3 Expression — the realization of a Work in a specific linguistic or notational form; one level below Work in WEMI | No |\n| `gndo:VersionOfAMusicalWork` | Version of a musical work / Fassung eines Werks der Musik | `079 $v=wif` | LRM-E3 Expression — a specific arrangement or version is a realization of the Work, not a new Work (unless substantially transformed) | No |\n| `gndo:CollectiveManuscript` | Collective manuscript / Sammelhandschrift | `079 $v=wil` | LRM-E4 Manifestation — a bound collection of multiple works; the container, not the intellectual creation | No |\n| `gndo:ProvenanceCharacteristic` | Provenance characteristic / Provenienzmerkmal | `079 $v=wip` | No WEMI equivalent — ownership marks and traces left in/on physical items; not a creative entity | No |\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\nSELECT ?type (COUNT(?w) AS ?n) WHERE {\n  VALUES ?type {\n    gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript\n    gndo:Expression gndo:VersionOfAMusicalWork\n    gndo:CollectiveManuscript gndo:ProvenanceCharacteristic\n  }\n  ?w a ?type .\n}\nGROUP BY ?type ORDER BY DESC(?n)\n```\n\nConstraining with `VALUES` avoids a full dataset type scan and lets the endpoint use per-type indexes. The original open-ended query caused a 504 Gateway Timeout.\n\n**Results:**\n\n| ?type | ?n |\n|---|---|\n| MusicalWork | 330,818 |\n| Work | 242,333 |\n| ProvenanceCharacteristic | 10,080 |\n| Manuscript | 7,133 |\n| Expression | 5,766 |\n| VersionOfAMusicalWork | 2,905 |\n| CollectiveManuscript | — (no results; zero entities in dataset) |\n\n**Conclusion:** `VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }` is confirmed. The three IFLA LRM Work-equivalent classes cover 580,284 entities (242,333 + 330,818 + 7,133). The four excluded classes are either IFLA Expression-level (`Expression`, `VersionOfAMusicalWork`), have no WEMI equivalent (`ProvenanceCharacteristic`), or are absent from this dataset (`CollectiveManuscript`).\n\n### 0.2 Confirm text index covers `preferredNameForTheWork`\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\nSELECT ?w ?label ?score WHERE {\n  ?w a gndo:Work .\n  ?w gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?label .\n  ?label contains-word \"faust\"\n  BIND(score(?label) AS ?score)\n}\nORDER BY DESC(?score) LIMIT 5\n```\n\n**Test (curl):**\n\n```bash\ncurl -s -X POST \"https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd\" \\\n  -H \"Accept: application/sparql-results+json\" \\\n  -H \"Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded\" \\\n  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\nSELECT ?w ?label WHERE {\n  ?w a gndo:Work .\n  ?w gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?label .\n  ?label contains-word \"faust\"\n}\nLIMIT 5'\n```\n\n**Result:**\n\n```json\n{\n  \"exception\": \"Invalid SPARQL query: Token \\\"contains-word\\\": mismatched input 'contains-word' expecting {'(', 'a', '^', '!', IRI_REF, PNAME_NS, PNAME_LN, VAR1, VAR2, PREFIX_LANGTAG}\",\n  \"metadata\": { \"line\": 5, \"positionInLine\": 9, \"startIndex\": 155, \"stopIndex\": 167 },\n  \"status\": \"ERROR\"\n}\n```\n\nThe parser fails at line 5, position 9 — exactly where `contains-word` appears as predicate. The endpoint enforces SPARQL 1.1 grammar; `contains-word` is QLever-specific syntax and is not available here. **Patterns A and B do not apply. Pattern C is the active query path.**\n\n### 0.3 Confirm author predicate name\n\nQuery Works that link to Goethe (`https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238`) to find the predicate used:\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\nSELECT ?work ?pred WHERE {\n  ?work ?pred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .\n  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }\n  ?work a ?wtype .\n}\nLIMIT 10\n```\n\n**Results:**\n\n| ?work | ?pred |\n|---|---|\n| 4427937-1 | author |\n| 134483180X | relatedPerson |\n| 1367943906 | author |\n| 1345022085 | author |\n| 1367911087 | author |\n| 134558573X | author |\n| 1345758731 | poet |\n| 1346874786 | author |\n| 1347120017 | author |\n| 1347121307 | author |\n\n**Conclusion:** The dominant predicate is `gndo:author` (8/10 results). `gndo:poet` appears as a role-specific variant (Goethe wrote lyrics for musical works); `gndo:relatedPerson` is too broad for author linking.\n\nFrom `gnd_20251218.ttl`, the relevant author-role property hierarchy is:\n\n| Property | Label (en/de) | `rdfs:subPropertyOf` | MARC21 | Scope |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `gndo:author` | Author / Verfasser | RDA `P60434` | `$4=auta` | Textual works; primary creator |\n| `gndo:firstAuthor` | First author / Erste Verfasserschaft | **`gndo:author`**, RDA `P60434` | `$4=aut1` | Subproperty of `author`; single/lead author |\n| `gndo:creator` | Creator / Urheber | — | `$4=urhe` | General; no subproperty relation to `author` |\n| `gndo:poet` | Poet / Dichter | RDA `P60477` | `$4=dich` | Words of non-dramatic musical works |\n| `gndo:composer` | Composer / Komponist | RDA `P60426` | `$4=koma` | Musical works |\n\n**SPARQL does not infer `rdfs:subPropertyOf` without a reasoner.** A query constraining `gndo:author` will miss triples that use `gndo:firstAuthor`, even though `firstAuthor` is a subproperty of `author`. Pattern A must explicitly cover both using `VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor }`. `gndo:poet` and `gndo:composer` should be added for coverage of MusicalWork records.\n\n### 0.4 Confirm literal format of `preferredNameForTheWork`\n\nDetermines whether VALUES-based queries must include `@de` language-tagged variants.\n\n```bash\ncurl -s -X POST \"https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd\" \\\n  -H \"Accept: application/sparql-results+json\" \\\n  -H \"Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded\" \\\n  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\nSELECT ?l (lang(?l) AS ?tag) WHERE {\n  <https://d-nb.info/gnd/4427937-1> gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?l .\n}\nLIMIT 5'\n```\n\n**Result:**\n\n```json\n{\n  \"l\": { \"type\": \"literal\", \"value\": \"Faust I / Walpurgisnacht / Berlin / Staatsbibliothek Berlin / Ms. germ. qu. 527\" },\n  \"tag\": { \"type\": \"literal\", \"value\": \"\" }\n}\n```\n\n**Conclusions:**\n1. `preferredNameForTheWork` literals have **no language tag** — `@de` variants are not needed in VALUES clauses.\n2. GND preferred names are **not clean titles** — this Manuscript entry carries a full ISBD-style string including location and shelfmark, not just the work title. Pattern C's exact FILTER match will not resolve titles like \"Faust\" against such records; those can only be matched via `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` or through a separate normalized-title query.\n\n---\n\n## Step 1 — Query patterns\n\n| Query pattern | Fields | Justification |\n|---|---|---|\n| **A** — Title + author | `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` (text search); `gndo:author`, `gndo:firstAuthor`, `gndo:poet`, `gndo:composer` (constraint via VALUES) | Author URI narrows the candidate set before text matching; highest precision. Use when author GND URI is available and text index is confirmed (recon 0.2–0.3). |\n| **B** — Title only | `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` (preferred); `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` (UNION fallback) | No author URI available. UNION over variant names increases recall for cases where the DDB title matches a GND alternative form rather than the preferred label. Use when text index is confirmed but no author URI. |\n| **C** — FILTER fallback | `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` (exact string match) | `contains-word` unavailable or not indexed for the predicate (determined by recon 0.2). Exact/normalized string comparison only; no relevance ranking. Lower recall than A/B. |\n\n### Pattern A — Title + author, text-indexed (primary)\n\nUsed when: author GND URI is available AND `contains-word` is confirmed (recon 0.2).\n\n`gndo:firstAuthor` is a subproperty of `gndo:author` but SPARQL does not infer this without a reasoner — both must be listed explicitly. `gndo:poet` and `gndo:composer` cover musical and lyrical works.\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\n\nSELECT ?work ?prefLabel ?score WHERE {\n  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }\n  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }\n  ?work a ?wtype .\n  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .\n  ?work ?authorPred <{author_gnd_uri}> .\n  ?prefLabel contains-word \"{title_tokens}\"\n  BIND(score(?prefLabel) AS ?score)\n}\nORDER BY DESC(?score)\nTEXTLIMIT 5\nLIMIT 10\n```\n\n`TEXTLIMIT 5` caps QLever's internal text candidates for performance; `LIMIT 10` caps output rows.\n\n### Pattern B — Title only, text-indexed\n\nUsed when: no author GND URI is available.\nUNION catches cases where the DDB title matches a GND variant name rather than the preferred form.\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\n\nSELECT ?work ?prefLabel ?varLabel ?score WHERE {\n  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }\n  ?work a ?wtype .\n  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .\n  {\n    ?prefLabel contains-word \"{title_tokens}\"\n    BIND(?prefLabel AS ?varLabel)\n    BIND(score(?prefLabel) AS ?score)\n  } UNION {\n    ?work gndo:variantNameForTheWork ?alt .\n    ?alt contains-word \"{title_tokens}\"\n    BIND(?alt AS ?varLabel)\n    BIND(score(?alt) AS ?score)\n  }\n}\nORDER BY DESC(?score)\nTEXTLIMIT 5\nLIMIT 10\n```\n\n### Pattern C — FILTER (active query path)\n\nExact-match only; normalized title (diacritics stripped, lowercased) maximizes recall.\n\n```sparql\nPREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>\n\nSELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {\n  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }\n  ?work a ?wtype .\n  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .\n  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = \"{normalized_title}\")\n}\nLIMIT 10\n```\n\n### Conclusion from Step 0\n\n| Finding | Source | Impact on Step 1 |\n|---|---|---|\n| `contains-word` raises a SPARQL parse error | Step 0.2 | Patterns A and B are not available. **Pattern C is the sole active query path.** |\n| `VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }` covers 580,284 entities (242k + 331k + 7k) | Step 0.1 | Confirmed — use in all patterns |\n| Dominant author predicate is `gndo:author`; `gndo:firstAuthor` is a subproperty but SPARQL does not infer it | Step 0.3 | Pattern A must use `VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }` |\n| `gndo:poet` appears for lyrical/musical works (Goethe as lyricist) | Step 0.3 | Include `gndo:poet` in `VALUES ?authorPred` |\n| `CollectiveManuscript` has zero entities in this dataset | Step 0.1 | Confirmed exclusion; no impact on queries |\n\nPattern C with author constraint (when author GND URI is available) becomes the effective Pattern A: same `VALUES ?authorPred` constraint, FILTER instead of `contains-word`.\n\n---\n\n## Step 2 — Title token preparation\n\nQLever `contains-word` is word-based, not phrase-based by default. Title preparation has three distinct phases: normalization, tokenization, and filtering.\n\n### Normalization (pre-tokenization)\n\n1. **Unicode NFC normalize** — Unicode Standard Annex #15, Normalization Form C: Canonical Decomposition followed by Canonical Composition. Ensures consistent code-point representation before any string operation. Example: `a` + combining diaeresis (two code points, NFD) → `ä` (one code point, NFC). Required so that lowercasing, diacritic stripping, and string comparison behave predictably regardless of source encoding.\n2. **Lowercase** — case-fold the NFC-normalized string.\n3. **Strip diacritics** — decompose (NFD) then remove combining characters (`unicodedata.category(c) == 'Mn'`). Maps `ä→a`, `ü→u`, `ö→o`, `ß→ss`. Note: test whether the GND endpoint requires this — if the text index is accent-insensitive, stripping may reduce rather than increase recall.\n\n### Tokenization\n\n4. **Split on whitespace and punctuation** — split the normalized string into individual word tokens.\n\n### Filtering\n\n5. **Remove stopwords:** `der, die, das, ein, eine, von, und, zu, im, in, an, auf, für, mit, bei, dem, den`\n6. **Select 2–3 distinctive tokens** (see below)\n\n### Distinctive token selection\n\n**Underlying principle:** `contains-word` with multiple tokens is AND semantics — each additional token makes the query more restrictive. Candidate retrieval should maximize recall (get the right GND Work somewhere in the top-10); precision is handled by post-retrieval string scoring in Step 3. The goal is the minimum set of tokens that identifies the work without over-constraining.\n\n**Criterion 1 — Semantic centrality.** Use tokens that are the essential content of the title, not modifiers or structural words:\n\n- **Keep:** nouns, proper nouns (place/person names embedded in titles), semantically unique adjectives\n- **Drop even if not in the stopword list:** articles, prepositions, ordinals (*erster, zweiter*), format/edition terms (*Band, Teil, Heft, Auflage, Bd., Nr.*)\n\nExample: *\"Die Leiden des jungen Werthers\"* → tokens `leiden werthers`; the proper noun alone (`werthers`) is sufficient with Pattern A.\n\n**Criterion 2 — Low collision frequency.** Prefer tokens that appear in few GND Works. Some words are not stopwords but are so common in bibliographic titles that they add almost no discriminating power:\n\n```python\nGENERIC_TITLE_WORDS = {\n    \"briefe\", \"gedichte\", \"werke\", \"schriften\", \"geschichte\",\n    \"bericht\", \"katalog\", \"sammlung\", \"aufsätze\", \"texte\",\n    \"band\", \"teil\", \"heft\", \"nummer\", \"jahrgang\",\n}\n```\n\nExample: *\"Gedichte\"* alone returns thousands of GND Works. *\"faust\"* alone is sufficient with an author URI.\n\nThis list should be derived empirically from a frequency query against the endpoint, not hardcoded blindly — run after recon 0.1.\n\n**Script:** `scripts/check_generic_title_words.py` — queries the DNB endpoint per word using exact VALUE matching on capitalized German forms (plain literals, no `@de` tag — confirmed by recon 0.4). Run with `python scripts/check_generic_title_words.py`.\n\n**Results** (counts = GND Works whose full `preferredNameForTheWork` is exactly this word or common article+word variants; threshold ≥100 = drop):\n\n| Word | Count | Keep in list? |\n|---|---|---|\n| briefe | 74 | Yes (low collision) |\n| gedichte | 90 | Yes (low collision) |\n| werke | 3,212 | **No — drop from list** |\n| schriften | 1 | Yes (low collision) |\n| geschichte | 5 | Yes (low collision) |\n| bericht | 2 | Yes (low collision) |\n| katalog | 0 | Yes (low collision) |\n| sammlung | 0 | Yes (low collision) |\n| aufsätze | 0 | Yes (low collision) |\n| texte | 2 | Yes (low collision) |\n| band | 0 | Yes (low collision) |\n| teil | 1 | Yes (low collision) |\n| heft | 0 | Yes (low collision) |\n| nummer | 0 | Yes (low collision) |\n| jahrgang | 0 | Yes (low collision) |\n\n**Conclusion:** Only `werke` (3,212) exceeds the threshold and is genuinely high-collision as a standalone title. All other words are low-frequency as full titles — GND preferred names are typically qualified strings (ISBD-style, see recon 0.4), not bare single-word titles. `GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS` should be revised to contain only `werke`; the others do not need filtering for Pattern C exact-match queries.\n\n**Decision rule:**\n\n```python\ndef select_tokens(tokens: list[str]) -> list[str]:\n    # tokens: already stopword-filtered, lowercased, diacritics-stripped\n    distinctive = [t for t in tokens if t not in GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS and len(t) > 3]\n    return distinctive[:3] if len(distinctive) >= 2 else tokens[:3]\n```\n\n`len(t) > 3` also drops short high-frequency fragments not caught by the stopword list.\n\n**Why 2–3 tokens specifically:**\n\n- 1 token: usually sufficient with Pattern A (author constraint already narrows heavily); sometimes sufficient for highly distinctive titles in Pattern B\n- 2–3 tokens: the sweet spot for Pattern B — enough to discriminate, low risk of AND-excluding the right Work due to GND/DDB title variation\n- >3 tokens: increasing risk of false negatives when GND's preferred form differs from DDB's (e.g., GND has *\"Faust. Der Tragödie erster Teil\"* but DDB has *\"Faust: Erster Teil\"* — four tokens from the DDB form likely miss the GND entry)\n\n**Multi-word handling:**\n- Short (1–3 tokens after filtering): pass all remaining tokens\n- Long titles: apply `select_tokens()` above\n- High-ambiguity titles (single generic word like *Briefe*, *Gedichte* with no distinctive co-tokens): require author constraint (Pattern A); skip title-only search (Pattern B) if no author URI is available\n\n---\n\n## Step 3 — Post-retrieval scoring\n\nQLever's `score()` is used only to rank candidates before string comparison. Match type is assigned in Python:\n\n| Condition | `match_type` | Triple predicate |\n|---|---|---|\n| `prefLabel == extracted_title` (case-insensitive) | `exact` | `skos:exactMatch` |\n| Normalized match (strip diacritics + punctuation) | `normalized` | `skos:exactMatch` |\n| Levenshtein distance ≤ 2 to `prefLabel` or any `variantNameForTheWork` | `fuzzy` | `skos:closeMatch` |\n| No candidate above threshold | `unresolved` | — |\n\n`match_confidence` = normalized edit similarity to `prefLabel` (not QLever score).\n\nThis logic is unchanged from the original plan in `gnd-title-extraction.md`.\n\n### Predicate justification\n\n**`skos:exactMatch`** (exact and normalized matches) — asserts two concepts are \"an exact match\" and can be used interchangeably for all retrieval purposes, without the OWL identity entailments of `owl:sameAs` (Miles & Bechhofer, *SKOS Simple Knowledge Organization System Reference*, W3C Recommendation, 2009, §10). Semantically appropriate here: a DDB ProvidedCHO and a GND Werk URI are not the same individual (CHO = catalog record for a manifestation; Werk URI = authority record for an abstract work), so asserting OWL individual identity would be incorrect.\n\n**`skos:closeMatch`** (fuzzy matches) — links two concepts \"sufficiently similar that they can be used interchangeably in some information retrieval applications\" but stops short of asserting full equivalence (Miles & Bechhofer, 2009, §10).\n\n**Why not `owl:sameAs`:** Halpin et al. show that `owl:sameAs` is routinely misused in linked data to express approximate or cross-type identity, producing unwanted OWL entailments downstream. Their recommendation: reserve `owl:sameAs` for verified same-individual identity; use SKOS mapping properties for concept-level matching (Halpin, Hayes, McCusker, McGuinness & Thompson, *When owl:sameAs Isn't the Same: An Analysis of Identity in Linked Data*, ISWC 2010, DOI: 10.1007/978-3-642-17746-0_16).\n\n**Match type taxonomy** (exact / normalized / fuzzy) follows the standard candidate generation ladder in entity linking (Shen, Wang & Han, *Entity Linking with a Knowledge Base: Issues, Techniques, and Solutions*, IEEE TKDE 27(2), 2015, §3.1, DOI: 10.1109/TKDE.2014.2327028).\n\n**Levenshtein distance** (Levenshtein, *Binary Codes Capable of Correcting Deletions, Insertions, and Reversals*, Soviet Physics Doklady 10(8), 1966). The threshold of ≤ 2 is a common empirical choice; validate against the gold set planned in `gnd-title-extraction.md` §Paper §4 quality metrics.\n\n---\n\n## Step 4 — Changes to `link_gnd_works.py`\n\nReplace the lobid-gnd REST call (Steps 3–4 in `gnd-title-extraction.md`) with:\n\n```python\nimport httpx\nimport asyncio\n\nQLEVER_ENDPOINT = \"https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd\"\nCONCURRENCY = 10  # tune up after rate-limit testing\n\n_semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(CONCURRENCY)\n\nasync def query_gnd_werk(\n    extracted_title: str,\n    author_gnd_uri: str | None\n) -> list[dict]:\n    tokens = tokenize_for_qlever(extracted_title)\n    sparql = (\n        QUERY_A.format(title_tokens=tokens, author_gnd_uri=author_gnd_uri)\n        if author_gnd_uri\n        else QUERY_B.format(title_tokens=tokens)\n    )\n    async with _semaphore:\n        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10.0) as client:\n            r = await client.post(\n                QLEVER_ENDPOINT,\n                data={\"query\": sparql},\n                headers={\"Accept\": \"application/sparql-results+json\"}\n            )\n    r.raise_for_status()\n    return r.json()[\"results\"][\"bindings\"]\n```\n\nPattern selection:\n- Pattern A if `author_gnd_uri` is set\n- Pattern B otherwise\n- Pattern C as fallback if recon 0.2 shows `contains-word` is unavailable\n\n---\n\n## Step 5 — What this replaces\n\n| Old plan | New plan |\n|---|---|\n| `GET /gnd/search?q=label:\"{title}\"&filter=type:Work` (lobid REST) | SPARQL POST to QLever endpoint |\n| Endpoint TBD / local infrastructure required | Public DNB endpoint, no setup |\n| No text relevance ranking | QLever `score()` ranks candidates |\n| `link_gnd_works.py` depended on lobid API availability | Depends on `sparql.dnb.de` uptime (DNB-operated) |\n| Title-only or title + author_uri via query param | Same semantics; `gndo:firstAuthor` predicate (verify in recon 0.3) |\n"
}
```

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T09:17:42.523Z)*

It doesn't. NER is not mentioned anywhere in `gnd-linking-plan.md`.

NER appears in the spec (`gnd-linking-spec.md`) under FR-01 — title extraction is described as a two-stage process: ISBD rule-based parsing first, NER fallback for records without ISBD markers (~71.6% of corpus, using NuNER Zero). But that's in the spec, not the plan.

The plan jumps straight from "extracted title" to token preparation (Step 2) — it assumes the title has already been extracted and doesn't describe how. The extraction pipeline (ISBD + NER) lives in the older `notes/gnd-title-extraction.md`, which the plan references in Step 3 and Step 4 but doesn't incorporate.

  "old_string": "## Step 1 — Query patterns",
  "new_string": "## Step 1 — Title extraction\n\nRaw `dc:title` strings in DDB ProvidedCHOs are ISBD-encoded: they concatenate title, statement of responsibility, edition, publisher, place, and year into a single field using ISBD punctuation. Sending raw strings to GND produces low-precision matches. Extraction runs before token preparation (Step 3) and GND querying (Step 2).\n\n### Method: two-stage pipeline\n\n| Stage | Trigger | Method |\n|---|---|---|\n| **Primary — ISBD rule-based parser** | Record contains ISBD punctuation markers | Split on ` / `, `. - `, ` : ` per ISBD conventions; take the leading segment as the title |\n| **Secondary — NER fallback** | No ISBD markers detected (~71.6% of corpus) | Zero-shot NER with NuNER Zero; extract spans tagged as work titles |\n\nThe ISBD parser handles the minority of records that use structured punctuation. NuNER Zero (zero-shot) handles the majority without ISBD markers, where rule-based splitting would either produce nothing or split on the wrong boundary.\n\n### ISBD split rules\n\n| Separator | Meaning in ISBD | Action |\n|---|---|---|\n| ` / ` | Statement of responsibility | Take everything before as title |\n| `. - ` | New ISBD area (edition, publication, etc.) | Take everything before as title |\n| ` : ` | Subtitle or other title information | Optional split; retain subtitle if short |\n\nExample: `\"Faust : eine Tragödie / von Goethe. - Neue Ausg.\"` → extracted title: `\"Faust : eine Tragödie\"` (or `\"Faust\"` if subtitle is stripped).\n\n### Output\n\n`extracted_title` — a clean title string passed to Step 3 (token preparation) and used as the FILTER value in Pattern C queries.\n`extraction_method` — `\"isbd\"` or `\"ner\"`, recorded in the output JSON (FR-07).\n\nFull extraction design and NER model selection are documented in `notes/gnd-title-extraction.md`.\n\n---\n\n## Step 2 — Query patterns"
}
```

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T09:37:38.653Z)*

Three distinct uses:

- **Line 4** (header): identifies lobid as what this plan replaces — intentional provenance note.
- **Lines 21–22** (summary table): describes Steps 5 and 6 as being about replacing lobid — these are accurate descriptions of what those steps cover.
- **Lines 550, 593, 596** (Steps 5–6): the actual migration content — the replacement rationale and comparison table in Step 6 is the entire point of that section.

All occurrences are in the "what this replaces" context, not as an active dependency. So lobid isn't being treated as a current approach — it's explicitly framed as the old plan throughout. Is there a specific mention that feels out of place, or would you prefer to remove the historical comparison entirely from the document?

### User *(2026-03-23T09:46:40.789Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→# GeMeA — NER for Bibliographic Title Extraction
     2→
     3→Context: NER is the **fallback** in `link_gnd_works.py` for records without ISBD markers. The primary extractor is the rule-based ISBD parser. NER only runs when that fails.
     4→
     5→**Scale of the fallback**: analysis of 115K Goethe-Faust DDB items ([isbd-title-analysis.md](../../goethe-faust/notes/isbd-title-analysis.md)) shows only **~29% of titles carry any ISBD pattern**, meaning NER applies to ~71% of records — the majority, not a small edge case. The dominant ISBD signal is ` :` (18%); the ` / ` split used by the parser appears in only 2.1% of titles. These figures are from one DDB provider subset and may vary across the full corpus.
     6→
     7→Target label set: `TITLE`, `OTHER_TITLE`, `PERSON`, `TRANSLATOR`, `PARALLEL_TITLE`, `LANGUAGE`, `MEDIUM`, `EDITION`, `PUBLISHER`, `PLACE`, `YEAR`, `SERIES`, `VOLUME`.
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## FRBR label scope
    12→
    13→Labels are organised by FRBR level. The goal is to extract enough structure to distinguish Works, Expressions, and Manifestations in the KG.
    14→
    15→**Work** — the abstract intellectual creation
    16→
    17→| Label | What it marks | Typical ISBD / catalog cue |
    18→|---|---|---|
    19→| `TITLE` | Proper title | Before ` :` or ` /`; primary string |
    20→| `OTHER_TITLE` | Subtitle / other title information | After ` :` |
    21→| `PERSON` | Author / creator | After ` /`; "von", "hrsg. von", "ed. by" |
    22→
    23→**Expression** — a particular realization of the Work (language, translator, medium)
    24→
    25→| Label | What it marks | Typical ISBD / catalog cue |
    26→|---|---|---|
    27→| `TRANSLATOR` | Translator name | After ` /`; "übersetzt von", "Übers.:", "transl. by" |
    28→| `TRANSLATION` | Translation source phrase | "Aus dem Englischen", "in deutscher Übersetzung", "traduit de" |
    29→| `PARALLEL_TITLE` | Title in another language | After ` =` |
    30→| `LANGUAGE` | Language of this expression | Explicit language name when stated in the title field |
    31→| `MEDIUM` | Notational / physical medium | "Klavierauszug", "Partitur", "Textbuch", "Hörbuch" |
    32→
    33→**Manifestation** — a specific published edition
    34→
    35→| Label | What it marks | Typical ISBD / catalog cue |
    36→|---|---|---|
    37→| `EDITION` | Edition statement | "2. Aufl.", "Neuausg.", "rev. ed.", "erw. Fassung" |
    38→| `PLACE` | Place of publication | Before `:` in `. - Place : Publisher` imprint block |
    39→| `PUBLISHER` | Publisher name | After `:` in imprint block; "Verlag", known publisher names |
    40→| `YEAR` | Year of publication | Trailing 4-digit year; after `,` in imprint |
    41→| `SERIES` | Series title | Inside `( )` after imprint |
    42→| `VOLUME` | Volume / part number | "Bd.", "Teil", "Vol.", "Heft", "Nr." |
    43→
    44→**Notes on Expression vs. Manifestation boundary.** A title string rarely contains explicit language codes (those live in MARC 041); `LANGUAGE` and `TRANSLATION` are therefore sparse but high-value when present. `PARALLEL_TITLE` (` =` cue) and `TRANSLATOR` are more common and are the primary Expression-distinguishing signals available in the title field. `MEDIUM` is relevant for music scores and audio items in the DDB.
    45→
    46→---
    47→
    48→## Historical language scope
    49→
    50→DDB objects span several centuries. Title content may be in modern German, Early Modern German, Middle High German, Latin, or other languages. This affects model choice.
    51→
    52→**Key distinction**: the NER task is structural, not semantic. The model identifies *which span is the title* within a catalog string — it does not need to understand the title. Catalog records follow modern ISBD conventions regardless of object era, so:
    53→
    54→- **ISBD parsing (primary)** — unaffected; punctuation-based, language-agnostic
    55→- **Silver labeling** — unaffected; labels derive from ISBD structure, not title content
    56→- **NER fallback** — affected for the ~71% of records without ISBD markers
    57→
    58→For the fallback, historical language introduces real risk:
    59→
    60→| Scenario | Risk | Notes |
    61→|---|---|---|
    62→| Modern German catalog record, historical title | Low | Surrounding catalog text is modern; only the title span is archaic |
    63→| Early Modern / Middle High German title | Medium | Tokenization degrades; subword vocab misses historical orthography |
    64→| Latin titles | Medium | Common for pre-18th century scholarly works; monolingual German models fail |
    65→| Mixed-language catalog strings | Medium | Some DDB providers mix German and Latin in descriptions |
    66→
    67→---
    68→
    69→## Model options
    70→
    71→| Model | Type | Fine-tune needed? | TITLE OOB | PERSON OOB | Historical/Latin | Notes |
    72→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
    73→| `xlm-roberta-large` | Transformer | Yes | No | No | Good | **Recommended base for fine-tuning** — multilingual training covers Latin and historical text better than monolingual alternatives |
    74→| spaCy `de_core_news_lg` | Transformer | No | No | Yes | Poor | Trained on modern German news; degrades on historical orthography and Latin |
    75→| `flair/ner-german-large` | Stacked embeddings | No | No | Yes (PER) | Poor | CoNLL-2003 labels; same limitation as spaCy |
    76→| `deepset/gbert-large` | BERT-large | Yes | No | No | Poor | Monolingual modern German; no off-the-shelf bibliographic NER |
    77→| LLM (GPT-4o, Claude, Llama 3) | Generative | No | Yes | Yes | Good | Handles Latin and historical German well; too slow at 5–10M records but strong as a one-time labeler |
    78→| **NuNER Zero** (`numind/NuNER_Zero`) | Zero-shot NER | No | Yes | Yes | Moderate | +3.1% F1 over GLiNER-large-v2.1; token classifier (handles arbitrarily long entities); same size, local CPU; **current SOTA in this category** |
    79→| GLiNER (`gliner_multi-v2.1`) | Zero-shot NER | No | Yes | Yes | Moderate | NAACL 2024; span-based with configurable width; multilingual; slightly behind NuNER Zero on benchmarks |
    80→| GROBID | Rule-based + CRF | No | Partial | Partial | Poor | Trained on scientific citations, not ISBD/library catalog — different domain; not recommended |
    81→
    82→**Note on gbert-large**: was listed as the original plan but was never properly justified. Monolingual modern German is the wrong choice for a historically diverse corpus.
    83→
    84→---
    85→
    86→## NuNER Zero — zero-shot NER (current recommendation)
    87→
    88→[NuNER Zero](https://huggingface.co/numind/NuNER_Zero) (NuMind, 2024) is the current SOTA compact zero-shot NER model, outperforming GLiNER-large-v2.1 by +3.1% F1. Unlike GLiNER, it is a token classifier rather than span-based, so it handles arbitrarily long entities — relevant for verbose DDB title strings.
    89→
    90→```python
    91→from gliner import GLiNER
    92→
    93→model = GLiNER.from_pretrained("numind/NuNerZero")
    94→
    95→# Labels must be lower-cased
    96→labels = [
    97→    # Work
    98→    "title", "other title", "person",
    99→    # Expression
   100→    "translator", "translation", "parallel title", "language", "medium",
   101→    # Manifestation
   102→    "edition", "publisher", "place", "year", "series", "volume",
   103→]
   104→
   105→text = "Faust drittes Buch von Goethe erschienen Weimar"
   106→entities = model.predict_entities(text, labels)
   107→
   108→# Merge adjacent tokens with the same label (token classifier output)
   109→def merge_entities(entities, text):
   110→    if not entities:
   111→        return []
   112→    merged = []
   113→    current = entities[0]
   114→    for nxt in entities[1:]:
   115→        if nxt["label"] == current["label"] and nxt["start"] <= current["end"] + 1:
   116→            current["text"] = text[current["start"]:nxt["end"]].strip()
   117→            current["end"] = nxt["end"]
   118→        else:
   119→            merged.append(current)
   120→            current = nxt
   121→    merged.append(current)
   122→    return merged
   123→
   124→entities = merge_entities(entities, text)
   125→# → [{"text": "Faust drittes Buch", "label": "title", "score": 0.91}, ...]
   126→```
   127→
   128→BERT-sized — runs on CPU, same deployment footprint as GLiNER. Zero-shot precision on DDB strings is unknown; **evaluate on ~500 stratified fallback records before committing**.
   129→
   130→---
   131→
   132→## LLM options at inference time
   133→
   134→NER applies to ~71% of records (~3.5–7M unique pairs after deduplication). LLM API at inference is expensive at this scale; local options are strongly preferred.
   135→
   136→| Approach | Scale | Cost | Historical/Latin | Effort |
   137→|---|---|---|---|---|
   138→| **NuNER Zero** (local) | All NER records | Free | Moderate | Low — deploy and evaluate |
   139→| **Local LLM** (Llama 3.1 8B) | All NER records | GPU infra only | Moderate | Medium |
   140→| **LLM one-time labeler → fine-tune xlm-roberta** | Generate labels once | Low one-time API cost | Good | Medium |
   141→| **Fine-tuned xlm-roberta-base** | All NER records | Cheap at inference | Good (if trained on historical) | High upfront |
   142→| **LLM API at inference** (GPT-4o, Claude) | All NER records | ~$1,500–3,500 at full scale | Good | Low — but costly |
   143→
   144→**Recommended path**: start with NuNER Zero on 500 stratified NER records. If precision is acceptable, done. If not, use an LLM to label a few thousand records and fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on that output.
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→## If no labeled training data is available
   149→
   150→**1. Silver labeling from the ISBD pipeline**
   151→The ~28% of records where ISBD area structure is present (`has_dot_dash`) become auto-labeled training examples. Silver candidate selection is handled by `scripts/rate_isbd_fields.py` → `data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv`:
   152→
   153→- **Silver tier 2** (primary): `has_dot_dash AND f_person AND ≥1 manifestation field` — structural annotation, multi-field spans; use as primary training set
   154→- **Silver tier 1** (augmentation): `n_fields ≥ 3` or `(f_person AND f_year)` — partial annotation; validate ~200 records before use
   155→
   156→See [isbd-field-rating.md](isbd-field-rating.md) for the full detection spec and [isbd-field-rating-adr.md](isbd-field-rating-adr.md) for design decisions.
   157→
   158→ISBD pattern → NER label mapping across FRBR levels:
   159→
   160→| ISBD pattern | Label(s) | Notes |
   161→|---|---|---|
   162→| Before ` :` or ` /` | `TITLE` | Primary title string |
   163→| After ` :` | `OTHER_TITLE` | Subtitle / other title info |
   164→| After ` =` | `PARALLEL_TITLE` | Expression-level; often a translation |
   165→| After ` /`; no "übersetzt" keyword | `PERSON` | Statement of responsibility |
   166→| After ` /`; "übersetzt von", "Übers.:", "transl. by" | `TRANSLATOR` | Expression-level |
   167→| Span matching "Aus dem …", "in … Übersetzung", "traduit de" | `TRANSLATION` | Expression-level; sparse |
   168→| Medium keyword ("Klavierauszug", "Partitur", "Textbuch", "Hörbuch") | `MEDIUM` | Expression-level; relevant for music/audio |
   169→| Edition keyword before `. -` block | `EDITION` | "Aufl.", "Neuausg.", "rev. ed." |
   170→| `. - Place :` block, first token(s) before `:` | `PLACE` | Manifestation-level |
   171→| `. - Place :` block, after `:` up to `,` | `PUBLISHER` | Manifestation-level |
   172→| Trailing 4-digit year or year after `,` | `YEAR` | Manifestation-level |
   173→| `( Series ; N )` block, title part | `SERIES` | Manifestation-level |
   174→| "Bd.", "Teil", "Vol.", "Heft", "Nr." + number | `VOLUME` | Manifestation-level |
   175→
   176→TRANSLATOR disambiguation is the main ambiguity: the ` /` cue fires for both `PERSON` and `TRANSLATOR`; keyword matching ("übersetzt", "Übers.", "transl.") is sufficient for a first-pass split. Language-agnostic structurally — works for Latin and historical German titles. Fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on these labels. Evaluate on a manually checked gold set of ~500 records including historical and Latin examples.
   177→
   178→**2. LLM as a one-time labeler**
   179→Use GPT-4o or Claude to label a few thousand records. Handles Latin and historical German well. Fine-tune a smaller model on those outputs. Expensive per call but a one-time cost.
   180→
   181→**Distant supervision (supplementary)**
   182→For records where a GND Werk URI was confirmed via the local GND instance, the extracted title string is a positive `TITLE` example. Linked person GND URIs supply `PERSON` labels. Useful to augment silver labels, not sufficient alone.
   183→
   184→---
   185→
   186→## Available datasets for fine-tuning
   187→
   188→| Dataset | Labels | Language | Size | Relevance |
   189→|---|---|---|---|---|
   190→| [empathyai/books-ner-dataset](https://huggingface.co/datasets/empathyai/books-ner-dataset) | TITLE, AUTHOR | English | Project Gutenberg catalogues | Closest label match; English only — use for domain-transfer pretraining |
   191→| [HIPE-2022 (ajmc)](https://github.com/hipe-eval/HIPE-2022-data) | Fine-grained bibliographic refs | German, French, English | ~10K tokens | Historical document NER with bibliographic references; German included — more relevant given historical scope |
   192→| [CLEF-HIPE-2020](https://zenodo.org/record/3836029) | PER, ORG, LOC, PROD (work titles) | German, French, English | Historical newspapers | PROD label covers work titles; 19th-century orthography — relevant for historical German |
   193→| [GermEval 2014](https://sites.google.com/site/germeval2014ner/) | PER, ORG, LOC, OTH | German | ~31K sentences | Modern German only; useful only for PERSON transfer |
   194→
   195→**Recommended fine-tuning path**:
   196→1. Pretrain on `empathyai/books-ner-dataset` for TITLE/AUTHOR signal (domain transfer)
   197→2. Fine-tune on silver-labeled DDB records
   198→3. Supplement with HIPE-2022 ajmc and CLEF-HIPE-2020 for historical German and Latin signal
   199→4. Evaluate on a gold set stratified by era (modern, 19th c., pre-1800, Latin)
   200→
   201→---
   202→
   203→## Decision
   204→
   205→1. **Try NuNER Zero first** — current SOTA compact zero-shot NER, no training data, runs locally, handles arbitrarily long spans. Evaluate on 500 stratified fallback records.
   206→2. **If NuNER Zero precision is insufficient**, use an LLM to label those same records and fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on that output.
   207→3. **Silver labeling** (ISBD-derived) augments any fine-tuning — language-agnostic, large volume, no annotation cost.
   208→4. Use `xlm-roberta` over any monolingual German model — the historical and Latin scope makes multilingual pretraining essential.
   209→5. **GROBID**: trained on scientific citations, not library catalog records — not recommended.
   210→
   211→---
   212→
   213→## Open questions
   214→
   215→| ID | Title | Status | Blocks |
   216→|---|---|---|---|
   217→| SR-01 | ISBD signal coverage (corpus-wide) | ✅ Resolved | — |
   218→| SR-02 | ISBD parser split priority | ✅ Resolved | — |
   219→| SR-03 | Silver label quality and false positive rate | 🔲 Open | SR-08 |
   220→| SR-04 | TRANSLATOR / PERSON disambiguation | 🔲 Open | SR-07 |
   221→| SR-05 | Trailing period noise | 🔲 Open | — |
   222→| SR-06 | Historical and Latin title scope | 🔲 Open | SR-07 |
   223→| SR-07 | Gold set composition | 🔲 Open | SR-08 |
   224→| SR-08 | NuNER Zero evaluation | 🔲 Open — blocked on SR-07 | — |
   225→| SR-09 | FRBR metric scope for paper | 🔲 Open | SR-07 |
   226→| SR-10 | DF_DE_TITLES source and title-length scope | 🔲 Open | — |
   227→
   228→---
   229→
   230→### SR-01 — ISBD signal coverage (corpus-wide)
   231→**Status:** Resolved — [isbd-field-rating.md](isbd-field-rating.md)
   232→- DF_DE_TITLES (4.47M): 20.2% have ` :`, 0.8% have ` /`, 14.6% have a year, 3.6% have an edition keyword
   233→- Area separator `. -` present in only **1.2%** of records (53k) — structural tier is limited; heuristic tier carries 99% of silver candidates
   234→- Tier 2 silver (structural, multi-field): **4,613 records** (0.1%)
   235→- Tier 1 silver (heuristic, partial): **335,524 records** (7.5%)
   236→
   237→### SR-02 — ISBD parser split priority
   238→**Status:** Resolved
   239→- ` /` (SoR) appears in only 0.8% of titles; ` :` (subtitle) at 20.2% is the dominant title-area signal
   240→- Parser must prioritise ` :` splitting for `OTHER_TITLE` / `TITLE` boundary, not ` /`
   241→
   242→### SR-03 — Silver label quality and false positive rate
   243→**Status:** Open — blocked on validation
   244→- Tier-2 labels are structurally derived (`. -` area separator present) — expected high precision
   245→- Tier-1 labels use heuristic whole-string patterns — false positive rate unknown for ` :`, ` /`, YEAR
   246→- **Action:** run `scripts/validate_heuristic_fields.py` (200-record stratified sample); accept tier-1 for augmentation only if false positive rate < 15% per field
   247→
   248→### SR-04 — TRANSLATOR / PERSON disambiguation
   249→**Status:** Open
   250→- ` /` fires for both PERSON (author) and TRANSLATOR; keyword matching ("übersetzt von", "Übers.:", "transl.") provides first-pass split
   251→- **Action:** validate keyword heuristic precision on 100-record sample of ` /`-flagged titles before using TRANSLATOR as a distinct silver label
   252→
   253→### SR-05 — Trailing period noise

accept tier-1 for augmentation only if false positive rate < 15% per field\n\n### 2.4 SR-04 — TRANSLATOR / PERSON disambiguation\n**Status:** Open\n- ` /` fires for both PERSON (author) and TRANSLATOR; keyword matching (\"übersetzt von\", \"Übers.:\", \"transl.\") provides first-pass split\n- **Action:** validate keyword heuristic precision on 100-record sample of ` /`-flagged titles before using TRANSLATOR as a distinct silver label\n\n### 2.5 SR-05 — Trailing period noise\n**Status:** Open\n- Trailing `.` fires in 17.5% of corpus but also hits abbreviations (`Hrsg.`, `Bd.`) and ordinals — upper bound, not a clean signal\n- **Action:** before using as silver label signal, require co-occurrence with another ISBD marker, or strip a curated German abbreviation list\n\n### 2.6 SR-06 — Historical and Latin title scope\n**Status:** Open\n- 92.4% of records (tier 0) have no ISBD signals and fall to NER fallback — unknown share are Latin or pre-modern German\n- **Action:** sample 200 records from `dc_type` = Leichenpredigt / pre-1800 Monografie; estimate Latin / Early Modern German proportion to determine how much historical signal is needed in training\n\n### 2.7 SR-07 — Gold set composition\n**Status:** Open — blocks SR-08\n- **Requirement:** ~500 manually annotated records stratified by: era (modern / 19th c. / pre-1800 / Latin), silver tier (2 / 1 / 0), and `dc_type`\n- Must cover tier-0 fallback records (the NER majority path) not just ISBD-structured ones\n\n### 2.8 SR-08 — NuNER Zero evaluation\n**Status:** Open — blocked on SR-07\n- **Requirement:** run NuNER Zero zero-shot on 500 stratified fallback records; assess TITLE and PERSON F1 on gold set\n- **Decision gate:** precision ≥ threshold → use zero-shot; else → LLM labeling + fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on silver + LLM-labeled set\n\n### 2.9 SR-09 — FRBR metric scope for paper\n**Status:** Open\n- **Requirement:** confirm which FRBR levels the paper's quality metrics cover — Work (TITLE, PERSON) only, or also Expression (TRANSLATOR, PARALLEL_TITLE, MEDIUM) and Manifestation (PUBLISHER, PLACE, YEAR, EDITION, SERIES, VOLUME)\n- Determines which label types must appear in the gold set and which NER labels are in scope for the evaluation section\n\n### 2.10 SR-10 — DF_DE_TITLES source and title-length scope\n**Status:** Open\n- **Question 1:** Confirm the exact source of `DF_DE_TITLES` — is it derived from a specific DDB facet, dc_type filter, or the full object dump? Understanding the selection criteria affects generalizability claims in the paper.\n- **Question 2:** The current corpus skews toward long ISBD strings. Short titles (single-token or bare proper-title strings with no punctuation signals) represent the NER-fallback majority and may need explicit representation in the silver and gold sets to avoid training on a length-biased sample.\n- **Action:** Check DF_DE_TITLES provenance in pipeline notes / download script; plot title-length distribution (token count); decide whether to stratify sampling by length (e.g. short ≤ 5 tokens, medium 6–20, long > 20)\n\n---\n\n## 3. FRBR label scope\n\nLabels are organised by FRBR level. The goal is to extract enough structure to distinguish Works, Expressions, and Manifestations in the KG.\n\n**Work** — the abstract intellectual creation\n\n| Label | What it marks | Typical ISBD / catalog cue |\n|---|---|---|\n| `TITLE` | Proper title | Before ` :` or ` /`; primary string |\n| `OTHER_TITLE` | Subtitle / other title information | After ` :` |\n| `PERSON` | Author / creator | After ` /`; \"von\", \"hrsg. von\", \"ed. by\" |\n\n**Expression** — a particular realization of the Work (language, translator, medium)\n\n| Label | What it marks | Typical ISBD / catalog cue |\n|---|---|---|\n| `TRANSLATOR` | Translator name | After ` /`; \"übersetzt von\", \"Übers.:\", \"transl. by\" |\n| `TRANSLATION` | Translation source phrase | \"Aus dem Englischen\", \"in deutscher Übersetzung\", \"traduit de\" |\n| `PARALLEL_TITLE` | Title in another language | After ` =` |\n| `LANGUAGE` | Language of this expression | Explicit language name when stated in the title field |\n| `MEDIUM` | Notational / physical medium | \"Klavierauszug\", \"Partitur\", \"Textbuch\", \"Hörbuch\" |\n\n**Manifestation** — a specific published edition\n\n| Label | What it marks | Typical ISBD / catalog cue |\n|---|---|---|\n| `EDITION` | Edition statement | \"2. Aufl.\", \"Neuausg.\", \"rev. ed.\", \"erw. Fassung\" |\n| `PLACE` | Place of publication | Before `:` in `. - Place : Publisher` imprint block |\n| `PUBLISHER` | Publisher name | After `:` in imprint block; \"Verlag\", known publisher names |\n| `YEAR` | Year of publication | Trailing 4-digit year; after `,` in imprint |\n| `SERIES` | Series title | Inside `( )` after imprint |\n| `VOLUME` | Volume / part number | \"Bd.\", \"Teil\", \"Vol.\", \"Heft\", \"Nr.\" |\n\n**Notes on Expression vs. Manifestation boundary.** A title string rarely contains explicit language codes (those live in MARC 041); `LANGUAGE` and `TRANSLATION` are therefore sparse but high-value when present. `PARALLEL_TITLE` (` =` cue) and `TRANSLATOR` are more common and are the primary Expression-distinguishing signals available in the title field. `MEDIUM` is relevant for music scores and audio items in the DDB.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Historical language scope\n\nDDB objects span several centuries. Title content may be in modern German, Early Modern German, Middle High German, Latin, or other languages. This affects model choice.\n\n**Key distinction**: the NER task is structural, not semantic. The model identifies *which span is the title* within a catalog string — it does not need to understand the title. Catalog records follow modern ISBD conventions regardless of object era, so:\n\n- **ISBD parsing (primary)** — unaffected; punctuation-based, language-agnostic\n- **Silver labeling** — unaffected; labels derive from ISBD structure, not title content\n- **NER fallback** — affected for the ~71% of records without ISBD markers\n\nFor the fallback, historical language introduces real risk:\n\n| Scenario | Risk | Notes |\n|---|---|---|\n| Modern German catalog record, historical title | Low | Surrounding catalog text is modern; only the title span is archaic |\n| Early Modern / Middle High German title | Medium | Tokenization degrades; subword vocab misses historical orthography |\n| Latin titles | Medium | Common for pre-18th century scholarly works; monolingual German models fail |\n| Mixed-language catalog strings | Medium | Some DDB providers mix German and Latin in descriptions |\n\n---\n\n## 5. Model options\n\n| Model | Type | Fine-tune needed? | TITLE OOB | PERSON OOB | Historical/Latin | Notes |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `xlm-roberta-large` | Transformer | Yes | No | No | Good | **Recommended base for fine-tuning** — multilingual training covers Latin and historical text better than monolingual alternatives |\n| spaCy `de_core_news_lg` | Transformer | No | No | Yes | Poor | Trained on modern German news; degrades on historical orthography and Latin |\n| `flair/ner-german-large` | Stacked embeddings | No | No | Yes (PER) | Poor | CoNLL-2003 labels; same limitation as spaCy |\n| `deepset/gbert-large` | BERT-large | Yes | No | No | Poor | Monolingual modern German; no off-the-shelf bibliographic NER |\n| LLM (GPT-4o, Claude, Llama 3) | Generative | No | Yes | Yes | Good | Handles Latin and historical German well; too slow at 5–10M records but strong as a one-time labeler |\n| **NuNER Zero** (`numind/NuNER_Zero`) | Zero-shot NER | No | Yes | Yes | Moderate | +3.1% F1 over GLiNER-large-v2.1; token classifier (handles arbitrarily long entities); same size, local CPU; **current SOTA in this category** |\n| GLiNER (`gliner_multi-v2.1`) | Zero-shot NER | No | Yes | Yes | Moderate | NAACL 2024; span-based with configurable width; multilingual; slightly behind NuNER Zero on benchmarks |\n| GROBID | Rule-based + CRF | No | Partial | Partial | Poor | Trained on scientific citations, not ISBD/library catalog — different domain; not recommended |\n\n**Note on gbert-large**: was listed as the original plan but was never properly justified. Monolingual modern German is the wrong choice for a historically diverse corpus.\n\n---\n\n## 6. NuNER Zero — zero-shot NER (current recommendation)\n\n[NuNER Zero](https://huggingface.co/numind/NuNER_Zero) (NuMind, 2024) is the current SOTA compact zero-shot NER model, outperforming GLiNER-large-v2.1 by +3.1% F1. Unlike GLiNER, it is a token classifier rather than span-based, so it handles arbitrarily long entities — relevant for verbose DDB title strings.\n\n```python\nfrom gliner import GLiNER\n\nmodel = GLiNER.from_pretrained(\"numind/NuNerZero\")\n\n# Labels must be lower-cased\nlabels = [\n    # Work\n    \"title\", \"other title\", \"person\",\n    # Expression\n    \"translator\", \"translation\", \"parallel title\", \"language\", \"medium\",\n    # Manifestation\n    \"edition\", \"publisher\", \"place\", \"year\", \"series\", \"volume\",\n]\n\ntext = \"Faust drittes Buch von Goethe erschienen Weimar\"\nentities = model.predict_entities(text, labels)\n\n# Merge adjacent tokens with the same label (token classifier output)\ndef merge_entities(entities, text):\n    if not entities:\n        return []\n    merged = []\n    current = entities[0]\n    for nxt in entities[1:]:\n        if nxt[\"label\"] == current[\"label\"] and nxt[\"start\"] <= current[\"end\"] + 1:\n            current[\"text\"] = text[current[\"start\"]:nxt[\"end\"]].strip()\n            current[\"end\"] = nxt[\"end\"]\n        else:\n            merged.append(current)\n            current = nxt\n    merged.append(current)\n    return merged\n\nentities = merge_entities(entities, text)\n# → [{\"text\": \"Faust drittes Buch\", \"label\": \"title\", \"score\": 0.91}, ...]\n```\n\nBERT-sized — runs on CPU, same deployment footprint as GLiNER. Zero-shot precision on DDB strings is unknown; **evaluate on ~500 stratified fallback records before committing** (see [SR-08](#28-sr-08--nunner-zero-evaluation)).\n\n---\n\n## 7. LLM options at inference time\n\nNER applies to ~71% of records (~3.5–7M unique pairs after deduplication). LLM API at inference is expensive at this scale; local options are strongly preferred.\n\n| Approach | Scale | Cost | Historical/Latin | Effort |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| **NuNER Zero** (local) | All NER records | Free | Moderate | Low — deploy and evaluate |\n| **Local LLM** (Llama 3.1 8B) | All NER records | GPU infra only | Moderate | Medium |\n| **LLM one-time labeler → fine-tune xlm-roberta** | Generate labels once | Low one-time API cost | Good | Medium |\n| **Fine-tuned xlm-roberta-base** | All NER records | Cheap at inference | Good (if trained on historical) | High upfront |\n| **LLM API at inference** (GPT-4o, Claude) | All NER records | ~$1,500–3,500 at full scale | Good | Low — but costly |\n\n**Recommended path**: start with NuNER Zero on 500 stratified NER records. If precision is acceptable, done. If not, use an LLM to label a few thousand records and fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on that output.\n\n---\n\n## 8. If no labeled training data is available\n\n**8.1 Silver labeling from the ISBD pipeline**\n\nThe ~28% of records where ISBD area structure is present (`has_dot_dash`) become auto-labeled training examples. Silver candidate selection is handled by `scripts/rate_isbd_fields.py` → `data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv`:\n\n- **Silver tier 2** (primary): `has_dot_dash AND f_person AND ≥1 manifestation field` — structural annotation, multi-field spans; use as primary training set\n- **Silver tier 1** (augmentation): `n_fields ≥ 3` or `(f_person AND f_year)` — partial annotation; validate ~200 records before use\n\nSee [isbd-field-rating.md](isbd-field-rating.md) for the full detection spec and [isbd-field-rating-adr.md](isbd-field-rating-adr.md) for design decisions.\n\nISBD pattern → NER label mapping across FRBR levels:\n\n| ISBD pattern | Label(s) | Notes |\n|---|---|---|\n| Before ` :` or ` /` | `TITLE` | Primary title string |\n| After ` :` | `OTHER_TITLE` | Subtitle / other title info |\n| After ` =` | `PARALLEL_TITLE` | Expression-level; often a translation |\n| After ` /`; no \"übersetzt\" keyword | `PERSON` | Statement of responsibility |\n| After ` /`; \"übersetzt von\", \"Übers.:\", \"transl. by\" | `TRANSLATOR` | Expression-level |\n| Span matching \"Aus dem …\", \"in … Übersetzung\", \"traduit de\" | `TRANSLATION` | Expression-level; sparse |\n| Medium keyword (\"Klavierauszug\", \"Partitur\", \"Textbuch\", \"Hörbuch\") | `MEDIUM` | Expression-level; relevant for music/audio |\n| Edition keyword before `. -` block | `EDITION` | \"Aufl.\", \"Neuausg.\", \"rev. ed.\" |\n| `. - Place :` block, first token(s) before `:` | `PLACE` | Manifestation-level |\n| `. - Place :` block, after `:` up to `,` | `PUBLISHER` | Manifestation-level |\n| Trailing 4-digit year or year after `,` | `YEAR` | Manifestation-level |\n| `( Series ; N )` block, title part | `SERIES` | Manifestation-level |\n| \"Bd.\", \"Teil\", \"Vol.\", \"Heft\", \"Nr.\" + number | `VOLUME` | Manifestation-level |\n\nTRANSLATOR disambiguation is the main ambiguity: the ` /` cue fires for both `PERSON` and `TRANSLATOR`; keyword matching (\"übersetzt\", \"Übers.\", \"transl.\") is sufficient for a first-pass split. Language-agnostic structurally — works for Latin and historical German titles. Fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on these labels. Evaluate on a manually checked gold set of ~500 records including historical and Latin examples.\n\n**8.2 LLM as a one-time labeler**\n\nUse GPT-4o or Claude to label a few thousand records. Handles Latin and historical German well. Fine-tune a smaller model on those outputs. Expensive per call but a one-time cost.\n\n**8.3 Distant supervision (supplementary)**\n\nFor records where a GND Werk URI was confirmed via the local GND instance, the extracted title string is a positive `TITLE` example. Linked person GND URIs supply `PERSON` labels. Useful to augment silver labels, not sufficient alone.\n\n---\n\n## 9. Available datasets for fine-tuning\n\n| Dataset | Labels | Language | Size | Relevance |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| [empathyai/books-ner-dataset](https://huggingface.co/datasets/empathyai/books-ner-dataset) | TITLE, AUTHOR | English | Project Gutenberg catalogues | Closest label match; English only — use for domain-transfer pretraining |\n| [HIPE-2022 (ajmc)](https://github.com/hipe-eval/HIPE-2022-data) | Fine-grained bibliographic refs | German, French, English | ~10K tokens | Historical document NER with bibliographic references; German included — more relevant given historical scope |\n| [CLEF-HIPE-2020](https://zenodo.org/record/3836029) | PER, ORG, LOC, PROD (work titles) | German, French, English | Historical newspapers | PROD label covers work titles; 19th-century orthography — relevant for historical German |\n| [GermEval 2014](https://sites.google.com/site/germeval2014ner/) | PER, ORG, LOC, OTH | German | ~31K sentences | Modern German only; useful only for PERSON transfer |\n\n**Recommended fine-tuning path**:\n1. Pretrain on `empathyai/books-ner-dataset` for TITLE/AUTHOR signal (domain transfer)\n2. Fine-tune on silver-labeled DDB records\n3. Supplement with HIPE-2022 ajmc and CLEF-HIPE-2020 for historical German and Latin signal\n4. Evaluate on a gold set stratified by era (modern, 19th c., pre-1800, Latin)\n\n---\n\n## 10. Decision\n\n1. **Try NuNER Zero first** — current SOTA compact zero-shot NER, no training data, runs locally, handles arbitrarily long spans. Evaluate on 500 stratified fallback records (see [SR-08](#28-sr-08--nunner-zero-evaluation)).\n2. **If NuNER Zero precision is insufficient**, use an LLM to label those same records and fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on that output.\n3. **Silver labeling** (ISBD-derived) augments any fine-tuning — language-agnostic, large volume, no annotation cost.\n4. Use `xlm-roberta` over any monolingual German model — the historical and Latin scope makes multilingual pretraining essential.\n5. **GROBID**: trained on scientific citations, not library catalog records — not recommended.\n"
}
```

### User *(2026-03-23T14:24:51.656Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2→"""
     3→analyse_years.py
     4→================
     5→Extract creation years from the DDB Goethe-Faust item dataset using the
     6→edm:TimeSpan begin/end fields, determine the optimal year bucket size, and
     7→produce a bar chart saved to output/fig_years.png.
     8→
     9→Year source
    10→-----------
    11→edm.RDF.TimeSpan.begin  — preferred; represents the start of the date range
    12→edm.RDF.TimeSpan.end    — fallback when begin is absent
    13→
    14→Bucketing logic
    15→---------------
    16→The script inspects the full year range and per-bucket record density, then
    17→automatically selects the most readable bucket size:
    18→  - 5 years   : range ≤ 100 years  OR  the 5-year bins each average ≥ 50 records
    19→  - 10 years  : range ≤ 300 years  OR  the decade bins each average ≥ 50 records
    20→  - 25 years  : otherwise (very long range or sparse data)
    21→
    22→Output
    23→------
    24→  output/fig_years.png        — bar chart of record counts per year bucket
    25→  output/years-analysis.json  — raw year counts and bucketed counts
    26→
    27→Usage
    28→-----
    29→    python scripts/analyse_years.py
    30→"""
    31→
    32→import json
    33→import re
    34→import math
    35→import matplotlib
    36→matplotlib.use("Agg")
    37→import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
    38→import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
    39→from collections import Counter
    40→from pathlib import Path
    41→
    42→PROJECT  = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
    43→IN_PATH  = PROJECT / "data" / "items-all-goethe-faust.json"
    44→OUT_PNG  = PROJECT / "output" / "fig_years.png"
    45→OUT_JSON = PROJECT / "output" / "years-analysis.json"
    46→
    47→# Two alternatives:
    48→#  1. year surrounded by non-digits (ISO, free-text)
    49→#  2. year followed by exactly 4 more digits (YYYYMMDD, e.g. "18300213")
    50→YEAR_RE = re.compile(
    51→    r'(?<!\d)(1[0-9]{3}|20[0-2][0-9])(?!\d)'
    52→    r'|(?<!\d)(1[0-9]{3}|20[0-2][0-9])(?=\d{4}(?!\d))'
    53→)
    54→
    55→
    56→# ── helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    57→
    58→def extract_year(val):
    59→    """Return the first 4-digit year (1000–2029) found in val, or None."""
    60→    if not val:
    61→        return None
    62→    if isinstance(val, dict):
    63→        val = val.get("$") or ""
    64→    if isinstance(val, list):
    65→        val = " ".join(str(v) for v in val)
    66→    m = YEAR_RE.search(str(val))
    67→    return int(m.group(1) or m.group(2)) if m else None
    68→
    69→
    70→MAX_BINS = 45  # maximum non-empty bins before trying a wider bucket
    71→
    72→def bucket_counts(year_counts, size, year_min, year_max):
    73→    """Aggregate year_counts into buckets of width `size`."""
    74→    start = (year_min // size) * size
    75→    end   = ((year_max // size) + 1) * size
    76→    bins  = {}
    77→    for b in range(start, end, size):
    78→        label = f"{b}–{b + size - 1}"
    79→        bins[label] = sum(year_counts.get(y, 0) for y in range(b, b + size))
    80→    return bins
    81→
    82→
    83→MAX_BINS = 45  # maximum non-empty bins before trying a wider bucket
    84→
    85→def choose_bucket(year_min, year_max, year_counts):
    86→    """Return the smallest bucket size that produces ≤ MAX_BINS non-empty bins."""
    87→    for size in (5, 10, 25, 50, 100):
    88→        bins = bucket_counts(year_counts, size, year_min, year_max)
    89→        non_empty = sum(1 for v in bins.values() if v > 0)
    90→        if non_empty <= MAX_BINS:
    91→            return size
    92→    return 100
    93→
    94→
    95→# ── scan dataset ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    96→
    97→print("Scanning dataset ...")
    98→year_counts  = Counter()   # year (int) → record count
    99→missing      = 0
   100→total        = 0
   101→
   102→with open(IN_PATH) as f:
   103→    for line in f:
   104→        line = line.strip()
   105→        if not line:
   106→            continue
   107→        try:
   108→            rec = json.loads(line)
   109→        except json.JSONDecodeError:
   110→            continue
   111→        total += 1
   112→
   113→        ts = rec.get("edm", {}).get("RDF", {}).get("TimeSpan")
   114→        if not isinstance(ts, dict):
   115→            missing += 1
   116→            continue
   117→
   118→        year = extract_year(ts.get("begin")) or extract_year(ts.get("end"))
   119→        if year:
   120→            year_counts[year] += 1
   121→        else:
   122→            missing += 1
   123→
   124→has_year = sum(year_counts.values())
   125→year_min = min(year_counts)
   126→year_max = max(year_counts)
   127→
   128→print(f"Total records    : {total:,}")
   129→print(f"Has year         : {has_year:,}  ({100*has_year/total:.1f}%)")
   130→print(f"Missing year     : {missing:,}")
   131→print(f"Year range       : {year_min} – {year_max}  (span {year_max - year_min} years)")
   132→
   133→# ── choose bucket size ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   134→
   135→size   = choose_bucket(year_min, year_max, year_counts)
   136→bins   = bucket_counts(year_counts, size, year_min, year_max)
   137→# Drop empty bins; restrict to 1600+ for a compact chart (pre-1600 is very sparse)
   138→non_empty = [(k, v) for k, v in bins.items()
   139→             if v > 0 and int(k.split("–")[0]) >= 1600]
   140→labels    = [k for k, _ in non_empty]
   141→counts    = [v for _, v in non_empty]
   142→
   143→n_pre1600 = sum(v for k, v in bins.items()
   144→                if v > 0 and int(k.split("–")[0]) < 1600)
   145→print(f"\nSelected bucket size: {size} years  ({len(labels)} non-empty bins from 1600+)")
   146→print(f"  Pre-1600 records omitted from chart: {n_pre1600:,}")
   147→
   148→# ── save JSON ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   149→
   150→output = {
   151→    "total_records":  total,
   152→    "records_with_year": has_year,
   153→    "records_missing_year": missing,
   154→    "year_range": [year_min, year_max],
   155→    "bucket_size": size,
   156→    "raw_year_counts": {str(y): c for y, c in sorted(year_counts.items())},
   157→    "bucketed_counts": dict(non_empty),
   158→}
   159→with open(OUT_JSON, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
   160→    json.dump(output, f, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)
   161→print(f"Saved JSON  : {OUT_JSON}")
   162→
   163→# ── plot ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   164→
   165→plt.rcParams.update({
   166→    "font.family": "sans-serif",
   167→    "axes.spines.top":   False,
   168→    "axes.spines.right": False,
   169→    "figure.dpi": 150,
   170→})
   171→
   172→fig_w = max(10, len(labels) * 0.62)
   173→fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(fig_w, 6))
   174→
   175→# Colour bars: highlight the 19th-century Goethe-era buckets
   176→colors = []
   177→for lbl in labels:
   178→    start_yr = int(lbl.split("–")[0])
   179→    if 1749 <= start_yr < 1850:
   180→        colors.append("#C44E52")   # Goethe era (1749–1832): red
   181→    else:
   182→        colors.append("#4C72B0")   # default blue
   183→
   184→bars = ax.bar(range(len(labels)), counts, color=colors, width=0.78, linewidth=0)
   185→
   186→# X-axis: show only the start year of each bucket to avoid crowding
   187→ax.set_xticks(range(len(labels)))
   188→ax.set_xticklabels([lbl.split("–")[0] for lbl in labels],
   189→                   rotation=60, ha="right", fontsize=8.5)
   190→ax.set_ylabel("Number of records", fontsize=10)
   191→ax.set_title(
   192→    f"DDB Goethe-Faust Records by Year of Creation"
   193→    f"  ·  {size}-year buckets  ·  N={sum(counts):,}  (1600–present)",
   194→    fontsize=12, fontweight="bold", pad=10,
   195→)
   196→ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.35)
   197→ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"{int(x):,}"))
   198→
   199→# Annotate bars: counts inside tall bars (white), above short bars (dark).
   200→vmax = max(counts)
   201→for bar, val in zip(bars, counts):
   202→    if val == 0:
   203→        continue
   204→    if val >= 0.07 * vmax:
   205→        # Tall enough: centre text inside the bar in white
   206→        ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, val / 2,
   207→                f"{val:,}", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=6.5,
   208→                rotation=90, color="white", fontweight="bold")
   209→    else:
   210→        # Short bar: place count just above, in the bar's own colour
   211→        ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, val + vmax * 0.012,
   212→                f"{val:,}", ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=6,
   213→                rotation=90, color=bar.get_facecolor())
   214→
   215→# Legend patch for highlighted era
   216→from matplotlib.patches import Patch
   217→legend_elements = [
   218→    Patch(facecolor="#C44E52", label="Goethe era (1749–1832)"),
   219→    Patch(facecolor="#4C72B0", label="Other"),
   220→]
   221→ax.legend(handles=legend_elements, frameon=False, fontsize=9, loc="upper left")
   222→
   223→fig.tight_layout()
   224→fig.savefig(OUT_PNG, bbox_inches="tight")
   225→plt.close(fig)
   226→print(f"Saved chart : {OUT_PNG}")
   227→



```

     1→#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2→"""
     3→Purpose:  Load data/DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl, extract title column(s),
     4→          and report how many titles contain ISBD punctuation marks.
     5→Usage:    python3 check_isbd_titles.py [--data PATH]
     6→Inputs:   DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl — pandas DataFrame
     7→Outputs:  Console summary with pattern breakdown and sample titles
     8→Dependencies: pandas
     9→Assumptions:  DataFrame has a column containing title strings;
    10→              script auto-detects the title column by name heuristic.
    11→"""
    12→
    13→import re
    14→import argparse
    15→import pickle
    16→from collections import Counter
    17→from pathlib import Path
    18→
    19→import pandas as pd
    20→
    21→DATA_DEFAULT = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "data" / "DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl"
    22→
    23→# ISBD punctuation patterns
    24→# Each tuple: (label, signal, compiled regex)
    25→ISBD_PATTERNS = [
    26→    ("space-slash",   "Statement of responsibility ( / )", re.compile(r" /")),
    27→    ("space-colon",   "Other title information ( : )",     re.compile(r" :")),
    28→    ("space-equals",  "Parallel title ( = )",              re.compile(r" =")),
    29→    ("space-semi",    "Subsequent SoR / series ( ; )",     re.compile(r" ;")),
    30→    ("ellipsis",      "Ellipsis / truncation (… or ...)",  re.compile(r"\.\.\.|…")),
    31→    ("sq-brackets",   "Supplied / inferred data ([ ])",    re.compile(r"\[.+?\]")),
    32→    ("trailing-dot",  "Area-end period (.)",               re.compile(r"[^.]\.$")),
    33→]
    34→
    35→
    36→def detect_title_column(df: pd.DataFrame) -> str:
    37→    """Return the first column whose name looks like a title field."""
    38→    candidates = [c for c in df.columns if re.search(r"title|titel|name", c, re.I)]
    39→    if candidates:
    40→        return candidates[0]
    41→    raise ValueError(
    42→        f"Cannot detect title column. Available columns: {list(df.columns)}"
    43→    )
    44→
    45→
    46→def check_isbd(title: str) -> list[str]:
    47→    """Return list of ISBD pattern labels found in title."""
    48→    if not isinstance(title, str):
    49→        return []
    50→    return [label for label, _, pat in ISBD_PATTERNS if pat.search(title)]
    51→
    52→
    53→def main(data_path: Path) -> None:
    54→    print(f"Loading {data_path} ...")
    55→    with open(data_path, "rb") as f:
    56→        df = pickle.load(f)
    57→
    58→    if not isinstance(df, pd.DataFrame):
    59→        raise TypeError(f"Expected DataFrame, got {type(df)}")
    60→
    61→    print(f"Shape: {df.shape}")
    62→    print(f"Columns: {list(df.columns)}\n")
    63→
    64→    col = detect_title_column(df)
    65→    print(f"Using title column: '{col}'\n")
    66→
    67→    titles = df[col].dropna().astype(str).tolist()
    68→    total_titles = len(titles)
    69→    empty = (df[col].isna() | (df[col].astype(str).str.strip() == "")).sum()
    70→
    71→    # Analyse
    72→    pattern_counts: Counter = Counter()
    73→    flagged: list[tuple[str, list[str]]] = []
    74→
    75→    for title in titles:
    76→        hits = check_isbd(title)
    77→        if hits:
    78→            flagged.append((title, hits))
    79→            pattern_counts.update(hits)
    80→
    81→    n_flagged = len(flagged)
    82→    pct = n_flagged / total_titles * 100 if total_titles else 0
    83→
    84→    print(f"Rows total:           {len(df):>8}")
    85→    print(f"Empty / null titles:  {empty:>8}")
    86→    print(f"Titles analysed:      {total_titles:>8}")
    87→    print(f"Titles with ISBD:     {n_flagged:>8}  ({pct:.1f}%)")
    88→    print()
    89→    print("Pattern breakdown:")
    90→    print(f"  {'Pattern':<16}  {'Signal':<40}  {'Count':>7}  {'%':>6}")
    91→    print("  " + "-" * 74)
    92→    for label, signal, _ in ISBD_PATTERNS:
    93→        n = pattern_counts[label]
    94→        p = n / total_titles * 100 if total_titles else 0
    95→        print(f"  {label:<16}  {signal:<40}  {n:>7}  {p:>5.1f}%")
    96→
    97→    print()
    98→    print("Sample flagged titles (up to 20):")
    99→    for title, hits in flagged[:20]:
   100→        print(f"  [{', '.join(hits)}]  {title!r}")
   101→
   102→
   103→if __name__ == "__main__":
   104→    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
   105→        description="Check ISBD punctuation in DF_DE_TITLES pkl."
   106→    )
   107→    parser.add_argument("--data", type=Path, default=DATA_DEFAULT)
   108→    args = parser.parse_args()
   109→    main(args.data)
   110→



```

     1→#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2→# Purpose:      Rate each DDB title string for the likely presence of bibliographic fields
     3→#               (TITLE, OTHER_TITLE, PERSON, PARALLEL_TITLE, EDITION, PUBLISHER, PLACE,
     4→#               YEAR, SERIES, VOLUME) using ISBD punctuation rules. Assigns a silver_tier
     5→#               to each record for NER training data selection. Optionally writes N
     6→#               concrete examples per ISBD pattern for documentation.
     7→# Usage:        python3 scripts/rate_isbd_fields.py [--data PATH] [--output PATH]
     8→#                   [--examples N] [--batch-size N]
     9→# Inputs:       data/DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl — DataFrame with obj_id + title columns
    10→# Outputs:      data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv — field flags + silver_tier per record
    11→#               data/processed/isbd_examples.csv       — N examples per pattern (--examples)
    12→# Dependencies: pandas
    13→# Assumptions:  DataFrame has 'obj_id' and 'title' columns.
    14→#               data/processed/ directory will be created if absent.
    15→
    16→import re
    17→import argparse
    18→import pickle
    19→from pathlib import Path
    20→
    21→import pandas as pd
    22→
    23→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    24→# Paths
    25→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    26→
    27→ROOT = Path(__file__).parent.parent
    28→DATA_DEFAULT = ROOT / "data" / "DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl"
    29→OUTPUT_DEFAULT = ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "isbd_field_ratings.csv"
    30→EXAMPLES_DEFAULT = ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "isbd_examples.csv"
    31→DDB_ITEM_URL = "https://ddb.de/item/{}"
    32→
    33→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    34→# Pre-compiled regex patterns
    35→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    36→
    37→# ISBD area separator ". -" (space · period · space · hyphen · space)
    38→RE_AREA_SEP = re.compile(r"\. - ")
    39→
    40→# Title-area signals (applied to area[0] for structural; whole string for heuristic)
    41→RE_OTHER_TITLE = re.compile(r" :")   # subtitle / other title information
    42→RE_PERSON      = re.compile(r" /")   # statement of responsibility
    43→RE_PARALLEL    = re.compile(r" =")   # parallel title (often a translation)
    44→
    45→# Edition keywords (case-insensitive)
    46→RE_EDITION = re.compile(
    47→    r"(?i)\b(?:\d+\.?\s*)?"
    48→    r"(?:Aufl(?:age)?|Ausg(?:abe)?|[Ee]d(?:ition)?|[Rr]ev(?:ised)?|"
    49→    r"überarb(?:eitet)?|[Ee]rw(?:eiterte)?|[Vv]erb(?:esserte)?|"
    50→    r"Neuausg(?:abe)?|neu\s*bearb)"
    51→    r"\b"
    52→)
    53→
    54→# Year: 1400–2029 (broad range for historical DDB items)
    55→RE_YEAR = re.compile(r"\b(?:1[4-9]\d{2}|20[012]\d)\b")
    56→
    57→# Publication imprint: " : " separating place from publisher
    58→RE_IMPRINT = re.compile(r"\w.+ : \w")
    59→
    60→# Publisher hint for heuristic tier (whole-string)



```
