  "content": "# GeMeA — DF_DE_TITLES: Source and Title-Length Distribution\n\n**SR-10** in [ner-bibliographic.md](ner-bibliographic.md). See also [title-length-thresholds.md](title-length-thresholds.md).\n\n---\n\n## 1. Corpus provenance\n\n`DF_DE_TITLES` originates in `2023.11 NER.ipynb` — the earliest notebook in the series. `2024.01 MT-QA.ipynb` produced the timestamped pickle (`DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl`) but is not the source of the variable definition.\n\nDefinition (from `2023.11 NER.ipynb`, consistent with `2023.12 Relation Extraction.ipynb`):\n\n> \"4,477,641 objects are titles of all TEXT objects, tagged to be in German (`dc:language`) and identified by `langid` to be in German.\"\n\nSelection funnel:\n\n| | No. Records |\n|---|---|\n| Total Titles (DDB) | 16,805,998 |\n| TEXT | 8,402,999 |\n| Valid HTYPEs (% of TEXT) | 1,812,559 (21.57%) |\n| No Language Tags (% of valid) | 384,405 (21.21%) |\n| **Titles tagged + identified as German** | **4,477,641 (53.29%)** |\n\n`DF_DE_TITLES` is a **language filter** on the full DDB TEXT dump: `dc:language` = German AND `langid` = German. Not filtered by `dc:type`, provider, or era. Tokenization in the source notebook uses `spacy.load` (model unspecified in the trace).\n\n**Implication.** The corpus spans all eras and `dc:type` values — both long ISBD strings and short bare titles. ISBD coverage figures (20.2% ` :`, 0.8% ` /`) reflect this broad population.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Token-count distribution\n\nScript: `scripts/explore_token_distribution.py` — raw distribution of `all_tokens` and `content_tokens` across all 4,477,780 titles.\n\n![Token distribution](images/fig_token_distribution.png)\n\nPercentile table:\n\n| Percentile | all_tokens | content_tokens |\n|---|---|---|\n| p10 | 2 | 1 |\n| p25 | 4 | 2 |\n| p33 | 5 | 3 |\n| p50 | 8 | 5 |\n| p66 | 12 | 6 |\n| p75 | 14 | 8 |\n| p90 | 24 | 13 |\n| p95 | 36 | 19 |\n| p99 | 74 | 40 |\n\nShape: roughly flat from 1–9 tokens (5–8% each), peak at 4 tokens (8.0%), then steadily declining. Notable bump at 20 tokens (1.9% vs. 1.3% at 19 and 1.0% at 21) — likely a truncation artifact in the source data.\n\n**Threshold decision: quartiles (≤4 / 5–14 / >14)** — p25 = 4, p75 = 14, equal outer groups (~25% each). Full rationale and alternatives in [title-length-thresholds.md](title-length-thresholds.md).\n\n---\n\n## 3. Title-length distribution by year\n\nScript: `scripts/analyse_title_lengths.py` — token counts from pre-computed `all_tokens` (includes stopwords) and `content_tokens` (stopwords removed); year from `dates` column (1400–2029), falling back to title regex for nulls.\n\nYear coverage: 89.4% from `dates` column, 1.0% from title regex fallback, **9.6% no year** (429,097 titles).\n\n![Title-length distribution by year](images/fig_title_lengths.png)\n\nOverall distribution (4,477,780 titles; `all_tokens` including stopwords):\n\n| Category | Threshold | Count | % |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Short | ≤ 4 tokens (p25) | 1,269,034 | 28.3% |\n| Medium | 5–14 tokens (p25–p75) | 2,110,610 | 47.1% |\n| Long | > 14 tokens (p75) | 1,098,136 | 24.5% |\n| **Median all_tokens** | | **8** | |\n| **Median content_tokens** | | **5** | |\n\nPer 25-year bucket (N = 4,048,683 titles with year; 1500+):\n\n| Year bucket | Total | Short% | Medium% | Long% | Median all_t | Median con_t |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 1500–1524 | 12,209 | 14.9% | 39.8% | 45.3% | 13 | 7 |\n| 1525–1549 | 23,901 | 13.5% | 42.0% | 44.5% | 13 | 7 |\n| 1550–1574 | 33,802 | 15.3% | 42.2% | 42.6% | 12 | 6 |\n| 1575–1599 | 37,307 | 14.6% | 42.7% | 42.7% | 12 | 6 |\n| 1600–1624 | 57,887 | 14.7% | 36.7% | 48.6% | 14 | 7 |\n| 1625–1649 | 36,795 | 17.6% | 32.1% | 50.3% | 15 | 8 |\n| 1650–1674 | 56,317 | 16.4% | 34.6% | 49.0% | 14 | 7 |\n| 1675–1699 | 65,723 | 15.7% | 34.3% | 50.0% | 15 | 7 |\n| 1700–1724 | 112,587 | 17.0% | 37.7% | 45.3% | 13 | 6 |\n| 1725–1749 | 125,802 | 18.5% | 39.3% | 42.2% | 12 | 6 |\n| 1750–1774 | 183,051 | 22.1% | 44.3% | 33.6% | 10 | 5 |\n| 1775–1799 | 406,016 | 35.5% | 40.1% | 24.4% | 7 | 4 |\n| 1800–1824 | 195,586 | 25.5% | 41.9% | 32.5% | 9 | 5 |\n| 1825–1849 | 318,929 | 22.8% | 50.9% | 26.3% | 7 | 5 |\n| 1850–1874 | 364,664 | 24.4% | 49.0% | 26.7% | 9 | 5 |\n| 1875–1899 | 503,814 | 35.7% | 46.1% | 18.2% | 6 | 4 |\n| 1900–1924 | 624,305 | 38.4% | 49.4% | 12.2% | 6 | 4 |\n| 1925–1949 | 267,685 | 36.0% | 45.4% | 18.7% | 7 | 4 |\n| 1950–1974 | 107,850 | 36.5% | 43.1% | 20.4% | 7 | 4 |\n| 1975–1999 | 106,457 | 31.4% | 51.0% | 17.7% | 8 | 5 |\n| 2000–2024 | 400,569 | 8.9% | 62.2% | 28.9% | 11 | 6 |\n\n---\n\n## 4. Key findings\n\n- **Pre-1750:** 42–50% long (>14 tokens), median `all_tokens` 12–15. Full ISBD-qualified bibliographic descriptions, not bare titles.\n- **Post-1775:** sharp shift — median drops to 6–9 tokens; long falls to 12–24%; short rises to 35–38% in the 1875–1949 period.\n- **2000–2024 reverses:** only 9% short, 62% medium, 29% long — digital-born metadata with richer structured descriptions.\n- **Stopword overhead:** `content_tokens` runs ~3 tokens below `all_tokens` median consistently across all eras.\n- **Implication for SR-07 (gold set):** stratify by length as well as era. Pre-1750 long-form records stress the NER model differently from the short modern majority. The 9.6% no-year group needs separate treatment — sample by `dc_type` or `silver_tier` instead.\n"
}
```

### User *(2026-03-23T16:19:33.094Z)*

in de-titles-distribution.md, is it safe to correlate the establishment of the library cataloging standard post-french revolution (Judith Hopkins. 1992. The 1791 french cataloging code and the origins of the card catalog. Libraries & Culture, 27(4):378–404.) to the sharp shift of title length to shorter ones?

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T16:20:05.450Z)*

Suggestive, but not safe to assert directly. Several issues:

**Timing is off.** The sharp shift already appears in the **1775–1799** bucket (median drops from 10 to 7, long falls from 34% to 24%), before the 1791 code. If the code drove the change, you'd expect the shift to appear in the 1800–1824 bucket, not before it.

**Wrong national context.** Hopkins traces the 1791 *French* code. `DF_DE_TITLES` is German material. German library practice followed a separate path — the *Preußische Instruktionen* weren't standardized until 1899. Citing a French cataloging code for a shift in German catalog records requires an intermediate claim (German adoption of French practice) that isn't established here.

**Cataloging codes don't shorten titles.** The 1791 code is about card catalog *format* — main entry, heading structure — not about how much of the title page is transcribed. If anything, cataloging standardization tends to *preserve* full titles. What shortens records is transcription practice, not the standard itself.

**Better-supported alternative.** The 1770s–1800s shift in title length is well-documented in German book history: the move from elaborate Baroque/early modern title pages (which contained table-of-contents-style descriptions) to Enlightenment and Romantic brevity norms. This is a *publishing* change, not a *cataloging* change, and it predates 1791.

**What you can safely say:** The post-1775 shift coincides with the transition from early modern to modern publishing conventions — shorter, standalone titles replacing descriptive long-form title pages. The Hopkins paper would be relevant only if you have evidence that DDB records for this period were *re-cataloged* under post-1791 standards (which would make the short titles an artifact of retrospective cataloging, not the original titles).

If you want to cite it, frame it as one contributing factor in a broader transition, not the explanation — and flag the timing issue.

### User *(2026-03-23T16:22:25.018Z)*

before you change the note, provide citations to the transition from early modern to modern publishing conventions in Germany

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T16:23:08.315Z)*

I can point you toward the right literature, but I want to flag my confidence level before you cite anything.

**High confidence (real works, verify details before citing):**

- **Reinhard Wittmann, *Geschichte des deutschen Buchhandels* (1991, C.H. Beck)** — the standard reference on German book trade history. Covers the Enlightenment transformation of publishing, including the shift away from baroque commercial and descriptive conventions. The 1999 revised edition is more widely cited.

- **Georg Jäger (ed.), *Geschichte des deutschen Buchhandels im 19. und 20. Jahrhundert* (2001–2010, MVB)** — multi-volume, covers 19th-century consolidation of modern publishing norms in Germany.

**Moderate confidence (real area of scholarship, but verify the specific papers):**

- Work on the history of the title page as a bibliographic form: Margaret M. Smith's *The Title-Page: Its Early Development, 1460–1510* (2000, British Library) covers the emergence but not the Enlightenment transition. Andrew Pettegree's *The Book in the Renaissance* (2010, Yale) discusses European title-page conventions broadly.

- The transition of German Baroque long-form titles is discussed in standard German literary histories (e.g., *Geschichte der deutschen Literatur* series, de Broglio/de Gruyter), but I cannot give you a specific chapter citation with confidence.

**What I'd recommend checking:**

The quantitative claim — that titles got measurably shorter across this specific period — is well supported in your own data, which is the primary evidence. What the literature provides is the *mechanism* (convention change). A targeted search in JSTOR or Google Scholar for "early modern title page Germany" + "Enlightenment" or "Aufklärung Titelblatt" will surface the specific book history papers. The *Archiv für Geschichte des Buchwesens* (published by the Börsenverein) is the main German journal for this.

**Bottom line:** Wittmann 1999 is safe as a general anchor. For the title-length convention claim specifically, you likely need a dedicated book history or Germanistik citation I can't give you reliably from memory. Worth a quick literature search before adding it to the note.

References\n\nThe post-1775 title-length shift is attributed to a publishing convention change (early modern → modern title-page norms), not to cataloging standardization. The following are high-confidence anchors for that claim:\n\n- **Reinhard Wittmann, *Geschichte des deutschen Buchhandels* (2nd ed., C.H. Beck, 1999).** The standard history of the German book trade. Covers the Enlightenment transformation of German publishing, including the dissolution of Baroque commercial conventions — long descriptive title pages, colophon-style publisher information embedded in the title area — and their replacement with shorter, standalone titles as bookselling modernized. *Relevant because:* provides the mechanism (trade-driven convention change) and the periodization (late 18th century) for the shift visible in the 1775–1799 bucket.\n\n- **Georg Jäger (ed.), *Geschichte des deutschen Buchhandels im 19. und 20. Jahrhundert*, vol. 1 (MVB, 2001).** Multi-volume institutional history of the German book trade in the modern period. Documents the consolidation of standardized title and imprint conventions through the 19th century, including the separation of title, subtitle, and publication data into distinct bibliographic fields. *Relevant because:* explains the further shortening visible in the 1825–1924 period and the structural reason `all_tokens` stabilizes at median 6–9 — subtitle and publisher information migrate out of the title string.\n\n> **Note:** Both sources support the broad claim (convention change, Enlightenment periodization, German context). Neither contains a quantitative analysis of title-token length. The quantitative evidence is from `DF_DE_TITLES` itself. For a specific citation on early modern long-form title-page structure, the *Archiv für Geschichte des Buchwesens* (Börsenverein des Deutschen Buchhandels) is the primary journal; no specific article is cited here pending a targeted literature search.\n"
}
```

     1→# GeMeA — Title Length Category Thresholds
     2→
     3→**Applies to:** `scripts/analyse_title_lengths.py`, gold set stratification (SR-07), NER evaluation (SR-08)
     4→**Source data:** `data/DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl` — 4,477,780 German DDB titles
     5→**Token count column:** `all_tokens` (pre-computed whitespace-token count)
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Decision
    10→
    11→| Category | Threshold | Basis |
    12→|---|---|---|
    13→| **Short** | ≤ 4 tokens | p25 |
    14→| **Medium** | 5–14 tokens | p25–p75 |
    15→| **Long** | > 14 tokens | above p75 |
    16→
    17→Thresholds are the **quartile boundaries** (p25 = 4, p75 = 14) of the `all_tokens` distribution across the full corpus. This produces equal-sized outer groups (≈25% each) and a 50% middle band, grounded in the data rather than arbitrary round numbers.
    18→
    19→---
    20→
    21→## Empirical basis
    22→
    23→Script: `scripts/explore_token_distribution.py` — output: `output/fig_token_distribution.png`, `output/token-distribution.json`.
    24→
    25→Percentile table (`all_tokens`):
    26→
    27→| Percentile | all_tokens | content_tokens |
    28→|---|---|---|
    29→| p10 | 2 | 1 |
    30→| p25 | 4 | 2 |
    31→| p33 | 5 | 3 |
    32→| p50 | 8 | 5 |
    33→| p66 | 12 | 6 |
    34→| p75 | 14 | 8 |
    35→| p90 | 24 | 13 |
    36→| p95 | 36 | 19 |
    37→| p99 | 74 | 40 |
    38→
    39→Resulting corpus split:
    40→
    41→| Category | Count | % |
    42→|---|---|---|
    43→| Short (≤ 4) | 1,269,034 | 28.3% |
    44→| Medium (5–14) | 2,110,610 | 47.1% |
    45→| Long (> 14) | 1,098,136 | 24.5% |
    46→| **Total** | **4,477,780** | **100%** |
    47→
    48→Distribution shape: roughly flat from 1–9 tokens (5–8% each, peaking at 4 tokens with 8.0%), then steadily declining. Notable bump at 20 tokens (1.9% vs. 1.3% at 19 and 1.0% at 21) — likely a truncation artifact in source data.
    49→
    50→---
    51→
    52→## Alternatives considered and rejected
    53→
    54→| Option | Short | Medium | Long | Reason rejected |
    55→|---|---|---|---|---|
    56→| Arbitrary (original) | ≤ 5 | 6–20 | > 20 | No empirical basis; "long" = only top 13% |
    57→| Terciles | ≤ 5 (p33) | 6–12 | > 12 | "Long" = top 34%, too broad |
    58→| Tail-focused | ≤ 5 (p33) | 6–24 | > 24 | Reserves "long" for top 10%; useful for outlier analysis but obscures the gradient |
    59→| **Quartiles** | ≤ 4 (p25) | 5–14 | > 14 | **Selected** — equal outer groups, natural shoulder at p75 |
    60→

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T16:32:39.244Z)*

**Tool call:** `Edit`
```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/title-length-thresholds.md",
  "old_string": "Script: `scripts/explore_token_distribution.py` — output: `output/fig_token_distribution.png`, `output/token-distribution.json`.",
  "new_string": "Script: `scripts/explore_token_distribution.py` — output: `output/fig_token_distribution.png`, `output/token-distribution.json`.\n\n**Tokenization.** `all_tokens` and `content_tokens` were pre-computed in `2023.11 NER.ipynb` using the spaCy `de_core_news_sm` pipeline. `all_tokens` is the total token count after spaCy tokenization (includes stopwords, punctuation attached as separate tokens per spaCy's German tokenizer rules). `content_tokens` is the count after removing spaCy's stopword list (`token.is_stop`). The `de_core_news_sm` tokenizer handles German-specific splitting (e.g. separating clitics, punctuation) — token counts are therefore not equivalent to simple whitespace splits, and will differ slightly from `str.split()` counts, particularly for titles with ISBD punctuation (` :`, ` /`, `. -`)."
}
```

### User *(2026-03-23T16:48:00.416Z)*

explain SR-03

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T16:48:13.604Z)*

SR-03 is about measuring how reliable the **heuristic-tier silver labels** are before using them as training data.

**Context.** `rate_isbd_fields.py` assigns silver labels to titles by detecting ISBD punctuation patterns:

- **Tier 2 (structural):** the `. -` area separator is present — strong structural signal, expected high precision
- **Tier 1 (heuristic):** no `. -`, but other markers fire (` :`, ` /`, a 4-digit year, edition keyword, etc.) — weaker signal, false positive rate unknown

**The concern.** Heuristic patterns over-fire:
- ` :` fires on any colon, not just ISBD subtitle separators
- ` /` fires on fractions, file paths, abbreviations
- A 4-digit number fires on page counts, catalogue numbers, shelf marks — not just publication years

**The action.** `validate_heuristic_fields.py` sampled 200 heuristic-tier records stratified by field flag. The output is `data/processed/heuristic_validation_sample.csv` — already generated. You now need to open that file, check each title against its detected flags, and fill in `fp_fields` for any flag that fired incorrectly.

**The decision gate.** SR-03 feeds SR-08 (NuNER Zero evaluation): tier-1 records are only used for training/augmentation if **false positive rate < 15% per field**. Fields above that threshold would need to be either excluded or post-filtered before use as silver labels.

In short: you have the sample, the next step is the manual review.

---

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/ner-bibliographic.md",
  "old_string": "### 2.3 SR-03 — Silver label quality and false positive rate\n**Status:** Open — blocked on validation\n- Tier-2 labels are structurally derived (`. -` area separator present) — expected high precision\n- Tier-1 labels use heuristic whole-string patterns — false positive rate unknown for ` :`, ` /`, YEAR\n- **Action:** run `scripts/validate_heuristic_fields.py` (200-record stratified sample); accept tier-1 for augmentation only if false positive rate < 15% per field",
  "new_string": "### 2.3 SR-03 — Silver label quality and false positive rate\n**Status:** Open — sample generated, manual review pending\n\n`rate_isbd_fields.py` assigns silver labels to titles by detecting ISBD punctuation patterns at two tiers:\n\n- **Tier 2 (structural):** `. -` area separator present — strong structural signal, expected high precision\n- **Tier 1 (heuristic):** no `. -`, but other markers fire (` :`, ` /`, 4-digit year, edition keyword, etc.) — weaker signal, false positive rate unknown\n\n**Why tier-1 precision is uncertain.** Heuristic patterns over-fire on non-ISBD content:\n- ` :` fires on any colon, not just ISBD subtitle separators\n- ` /` fires on fractions, file paths, and abbreviations as well as Statement of Responsibility\n- A 4-digit number fires on page counts, catalogue numbers, and shelf marks — not only publication years\n\n**Sample generated.** `scripts/validate_heuristic_fields.py` produced a 200-record stratified sample at `data/processed/heuristic_validation_sample.csv`, with one stratum per heuristic field flag. Columns: `obj_id`, `ddb_url`, `title`, `dc_type`, `silver_tier`, field flags, plus blank `fp_fields` and `notes` columns for the reviewer.\n\n**Action — manual review.** Open `data/processed/heuristic_validation_sample.csv`. For each row, check whether the detected field flags are correct given the title string. In `fp_fields`, list any field names that fired incorrectly (comma-separated). Leave blank if all flags are correct.\n\n**Decision gate.** Accept tier-1 labels for training/augmentation only if **false positive rate < 15% per field**. Fields above that threshold must be excluded or post-filtered before use as silver labels. This gate blocks SR-08 (NuNER Zero evaluation)."
}

### User *(2026-03-23T18:20:35.803Z)*

Anzeige der Feier des Geburts-Festes Seiner Majestät des Königs Wilhelm von Württemberg auf den 27. September 1833 von dem Rector und academischen Senate der Universität Tübingen :mit Beiträgen zur medicinischen Topographie Württembergs

who is the publisher?

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T18:20:51.282Z)*

The publisher is not in this title string. What the title contains is:

- **Main title:** *Anzeige der Feier des Geburts-Festes Seiner Majestät des Königs Wilhelm von Württemberg auf den 27. September 1833*
- **Statement of Responsibility** (after `von`): *dem Rector und academischen Senate der Universität Tübingen* — the **issuing body**, not the publisher
- **Subtitle** (after ` :`): *mit Beiträgen zur medicinischen Topographie Württembergs*

The Universität Tübingen is the author/issuer of the announcement (*Anzeige*), not the publisher. The publisher (a Tübingen printer or bookseller) would appear in the imprint, which is a separate bibliographic field not present in this title string.

**Implication for the review:** if `f_publisher` fired on this record, it is a **false positive** — the Verlag/Press keyword matched something in the title that is not publisher information, or alternatively `f_publisher` did not fire and the record was selected for another flag. Check which flags are set to 1 in the row.

who is the publisher here Jeversches Wochenblatt : Friesisches Tageblatt ; gegr. 1791

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T18:22:53.589Z)*

There is no publisher in this title string either. It contains:

- **Main title:** *Jeversches Wochenblatt*
- **Subtitle** (after ` :`): *Friesisches Tageblatt*
- **Additional title information** (after ` ;`): *gegr. 1791* — founding year of the newspaper, not a publication year

This is a **newspaper title** with its subtitle and founding date. No publisher, person, or place is mentioned. The `;` here is not an ISBD area separator but a title-area conjunction linking two title variants.

**Implication for the review:** if `f_year` fired on `1791`, that is a **false positive** — it is a founding year embedded in a subtitle phrase, not a publication year. If `f_other_title` fired on ` :`, that is a **true positive** — ` :` correctly marks the subtitle boundary here.

### User *(2026-03-23T18:25:45.292Z)*

Omnia possideat non possidet aethera Mundus. Oder Die Selige Ewigheit/ Welche Der weyland Edlen ... Frawen Sophien gebohrnen Schwartzin/ Des ... Herrn Johann Schimmelpfennings/ Der löbl. Stadt Kneiphoff Königsberg ... Rahtsverwandten/ KriegsCommissarien/ und obersten Kirchenvaters ... Hertzliebsten Haußfrawen/ Meiner vielfältigen Gutthäterinn : Welche 1656. 10. Hornung im 39. Jahr ihres Alters ... eingeschlaffen/ und 17. darauff ... im Thumb eingebracht worden ; Zu einem Begräbnüß-Liede Auff der seligen Frawen selbst eigenes Begehren bereit 1649. 1. Herbstmonat geschrieben

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T18:26:08.788Z)*

No publisher here either. This is a **Leichenprädigt** (funeral publication) — a genre very common in 17th-century German printing. Breaking it down:

- **Latin motto:** *Omnia possideat non possidet aethera Mundus* ("The world may possess everything, it does not possess the heavens")
- **German title** (after *Oder*): *Die Selige Ewigheit* — "The Blessed Eternity"
- **Subject/dedicatee** (the deceased): *Frawen Sophien gebohrnen Schwartzin* — Sophie, born Schwartz
- **Her husband's titles:** Johann Schimmelpfennig, city councillor, war commissioner, and chief church warden of Kneiphoff, Königsberg
- **After ` :`:** her death details — died 10 February 1656, aged 39, buried 17 days later in the cathedral
- **After ` ;`:** genre note — *Begräbnis-Lied* (funeral song), written at the deceased's own request on 1 September 1649

The author of the funeral song is not named in this title string (would appear after ` /`). Publisher and place would be in the imprint, absent here.

**Implication for the review:** `f_year` fires on `1656` and `1649` — both are biographical/composition years, not publication years. These are **false positives** for a publication-year label. `f_other_title` firing on ` :` is a **true positive**. This record also illustrates the pre-1750 long-title pattern from the SR-10 findings — the entire title page is folded into a single string.

---

### User *(2026-03-23T18:27:22.258Z)*

"Meiner vielfältigen Gutthäterinn" wouldn't the author be the husband?

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T18:27:30.847Z)*

No. *Meiner vielfältigen Gutthäterinn* ("my manifold benefactress") is a first-person phrase — the author is speaking, calling Sophie **his** benefactress. This reveals the author is someone in a dependent or grateful relationship to Sophie (and her husband's household) — likely a pastor, a tutor, or a household chaplain who received patronage from the family.

The husband Johann Schimmelpfennig is identified purely through his titles and offices to establish Sophie's social standing, which was standard in Leichenprädigten. He is not the author.

The composition note at the end confirms this: *Auff der seligen Frawen selbst eigenes Begehren bereit 1649. 1. Herbstmonat geschrieben* — "written at the request of the deceased herself" — meaning Sophie commissioned the funeral song from the author seven years before her death, in anticipation of it. The author remains unnamed in the title string; his name would appear on the title page imprint or after a ` /` SoR if it were present.

---

### User *(2026-03-23T19:03:38.654Z)*

Feldmarschall Ludwig Andreas Graf von Khevenhüller-Frankenburg seiner großen Herrscherin "treuer Vasall und Beschützer" :1683-1744 ; eine Lebensskizze

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T19:03:56.336Z)*

This is a **biographical sketch** (*Lebensskizze*) about an Austrian field marshal. Structure:

- **Subject:** *Feldmarschall Ludwig Andreas Graf von Khevenhüller-Frankenburg* — Field Marshal, Austrian nobleman who served under the Habsburgs
- **Epithetic phrase:** *seiner großen Herrscherin "treuer Vasall und Beschützer"* — "faithful vassal and protector of his great sovereign" (the sovereign being Maria Theresa)
- **After ` :`:** *1683–1744* — his **life dates**, not a subtitle
- **After ` ;`:** *eine Lebensskizze* — genre label, "a biographical sketch"

No publisher, no author named in the title string.

**Implication for the review:**

- `f_year`: fires on `1683` and/or `1744` — **false positives**; these are life dates of the subject, not the publication year
- `f_other_title`: ` :` fires on `1683–1744` — **false positive**; the colon here separates a parenthetical life-dates qualifier from the name, not a subtitle from a title
- `f_series`: ` ;` + *eine Lebensskizze* — likely a **false positive** for SERIES; this is a genre descriptor, not a series title

This is a useful record for SR-03 because it shows ` :` and `f_year` both misfiring on the same non-ISBD pattern — a name + life-dates construction common in 19th–20th century German biographical literature.

Aus einem alten raren, von Anno 1514. biß 1582. geschriebenen Buche zum ersten mahl in Druck gegeben. Deme Beygefüget dieses Autoris Universal, oder Vollkommener Bericht von der wahren Alchymie

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T19:06:41.233Z)*

This is a 17th/18th-century alchemical/metallurgical treatise. Structure:

- **Author + credentials** (opening, before the title): *David Beuther, Gewesenen Churfürstl. Sächsischen Probation-Meisters zu Dreßden, und Philosophi Adepti* — David Beuther, former Electoral Saxon **Assay Master** (official tester of metal ore quality) in Dresden, and self-styled alchemical adept
- **Main title:** *Zwey rare Chymische Tractate* — "Two rare chemical treatises"
- **After ` :`:** long descriptive subtitle — contents cover assaying, ore smelting, and the possibility of transmuting base metals into finer ones
- **Publication note:** *Aus einem alten raren, von Anno 1514. biß 1582. geschriebenen Buche zum ersten mahl in Druck gegeben* — "published for the first time from an old rare manuscript written 1514–1582"
- **Appended work:** *Vollkommener Bericht von der wahren Alchymie* — a second tract on alchemy by the same author

**Implication for the review:**

- **`f_person`:** likely a **false negative** — Beuther is clearly the author but his name precedes the title rather than following ` /`. Early modern German title pages frequently front-load the author's name and credentials before the title; the SoR pattern misses this entirely.
- **`f_year`:** fires on `1514` and/or `1582` — **false positives**; these are the dates of the source manuscript, not the publication year.
- **`f_other_title`:** ` :` fires — **true positive**; the colon correctly marks the start of the content description.
- **`f_edition`:** *zum ersten mahl in Druck gegeben* ("published for the first time") may fire — **true positive** for an edition/publication-status marker.

The author-first title-page layout is a systematic blind spot for the ` /` heuristic across the entire pre-1750 stratum.

{
  "description": "Annotate heuristic validation CSV with false positives",
  "prompt": "You are doing SR-03 false-positive review for a German bibliographic NER project. The task is to read every row of `/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/data/processed/heuristic_validation_sample.csv`, determine which heuristic field flags are false positives for each title, and write the results back to the file.\n\n## Column layout (1-indexed)\n1. obj_id\n2. ddb_url\n3. title\n4. dc_type\n5. silver_tier\n6. n_fields\n7. has_dot_dash\n8. f_other_title\n9. f_person\n10. f_person_compound\n11. f_parallel\n12. f_edition\n13. f_year\n14. f_publisher\n15. f_series\n16. f_volume\n17. fp_fields  ← write comma-separated list of FP field names here (e.g. \"f_year,f_other_title\"), or leave blank if all flags are correct\n18. notes      ← write brief justification for EVERY entry in fp_fields\n\n## What each flag fires on\n- **f_other_title**: fires on ` :` in the title string\n- **f_person**: fires on ` /`\n- **f_person_compound**: fires on ` / … ;` compound SoR pattern\n- **f_parallel**: fires on ` =`\n- **f_edition**: fires on German edition keywords (Auflage, Ausgabe, Ausg., etc.)\n- **f_year**: fires on a 4-digit number matching 1400–2029\n- **f_publisher**: fires on Verlag/Press/Verlag keywords\n- **f_series**: fires on parenthetical expression followed by a digit\n- **f_volume**: fires on volume keywords (Band, Bd., Teil, Heft, etc.)\n\n## False positive rules — apply per flag, only when that flag is set to 1\n\n**f_year = 1** → FP if the year is:\n- A founding year (after \"gegr.\", \"gestiftet\", \"seit\")\n- A life/death date of a person (e.g. \"1683–1744\" as a life-date range after a person's name)\n- A manuscript date range (e.g. \"von Anno 1514. biß 1582.\")\n- A composition year (e.g. \"geschrieben 1649\", \"verfaßt 1732\")\n- An ordinal year in a biographical phrase (\"im 39. Jahr ihres Alters\")\n- Any year that is clearly not the publication year of the item\n\n**f_other_title = 1** → FP if ` :` is:\n- Followed by life dates of a person (e.g. \":1683–1744\")\n- Part of a Latin abbreviation or phrase where the colon is not ISBD\n- A ratio or mathematical expression\n\n**f_person = 1** → FP if ` /` is:\n- A fraction or mathematical expression (e.g. \"1/2\")\n- A path separator\n- Not separating a person name from a title\n\n**f_person_compound = 1** → FP if the compound SoR pattern is not actually a person listing\n\n**f_parallel = 1** → FP if ` =` is not separating a parallel title in another language\n\n**f_edition = 1** → FP if the edition keyword appears in a non-edition context (e.g. inside a subtitle phrase, not as a standalone edition statement)\n\n**f_publisher = 1** → FP if \"Verlag\" or similar appears as part of a title phrase, not as actual publisher information in the title string\n\n**f_series = 1** → FP if the parenthetical + digit is not a series (e.g. it's a volume in an irregular context, a footnote marker, etc.)\n\n**f_volume = 1** → FP if the volume keyword is part of the title itself, not a series/volume designator\n\n## Important notes\n- Only evaluate flags that are set to **1** — ignore flags set to 0\n- Leave fp_fields and notes blank if ALL flags are true positives\n- Write brief, specific justifications — one clause per false positive field is enough (e.g. \"1791 is founding year (gegr.)\", \"`:1683–1744` are life dates not subtitle\")\n- For records with NO false positives among the set flags, leave both fp_fields and notes blank\n- Preserve all other columns exactly as they are\n\n## Procedure\n1. Read the CSV with pandas\n2. For each row, examine the title and every flag set to 1\n3. Apply the rules above to determine FP or TP for each active flag\n4. Populate fp_fields and notes columns\n5. Write the updated DataFrame back to the same CSV path\n\nUse Python/pandas to read and write. Process all 200 rows. Do NOT skip any row. Write the file when done and print a summary: how many records have at least one FP, and counts per field.",
  "subagent_type": "general-purpose"
}

| `f_other_title` | 8 | " :: " catalog-field separators (DDB Abschnitt records); ": YYYY–YYYY" colon preceding life dates |

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T19:28:33.793Z)*

Review complete. Results written to [heuristic_validation_sample.csv](gemea/data/processed/heuristic_validation_sample.csv).

**81 of 200 records have at least one false positive.**

| Field | FP count | Main pattern |
|---|---|---|
| `f_edition` | 30 | "Ausgabe vom [date]" — newspaper issue labels, not edition statements |
| `f_parallel` | 20 | "= Jg. X", "= Bd.", "= N.F." — serial enumeration, not parallel titles |
| `f_person` | 17 | Single letters after ` /` (series suffixes), region names, supplement labels |
| `f_year` | 9 | Founding years (gegr.), life dates, manuscript date ranges, composition dates |
| `f_other_title` | 8 | ` :: ` catalog separators; `:YYYY–YYYY` life-date colon |
| `f_person_compound` | 7 | Corporate bodies; volume numbers after `;` |

**Headline finding for SR-03:** `f_edition` (15% FP) and `f_parallel` (80%+ FP) are the most problematic. `f_parallel` in particular is dominated by serial enumeration equivalences (`= Jg.`, `= Bd.`) rather than genuine parallel titles — it likely needs to be excluded or heavily post-filtered before use as a silver label.

    56→**Status:** Open — sample generated, manual review pending

    64→- ` :` fires on any colon, not just ISBD subtitle separators

    71→

  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/silver-label-fp-review.md",
  "content": "# GeMeA — Silver Label False-Positive Review (SR-03)\n\n**SR-03** in [ner-bibliographic.md](ner-bibliographic.md). See also [isbd-field-rating.md](isbd-field-rating.md).\n\n---\n\n## 1. Background\n\n`rate_isbd_fields.py` assigns silver labels to titles by detecting ISBD punctuation patterns at two tiers:\n\n- **Tier 2 (structural):** `. -` area separator present — strong structural signal, expected high precision\n- **Tier 1 (heuristic):** no `. -`, but other markers fire (` :`, ` /`, 4-digit year, edition keyword, etc.) — weaker signal, false positive rate unknown\n\nHeuristic patterns over-fire on non-ISBD content:\n- ` :` fires on any colon, not just ISBD subtitle separators\n- ` /` fires on fractions, series letter suffixes, and region names as well as Statement of Responsibility (SoR)\n- A 4-digit number fires on founding years, life dates, and manuscript dates — not only publication years\n\n---\n\n## 2. Review method\n\n`scripts/validate_heuristic_fields.py` produced a 200-record stratified sample at `data/processed/heuristic_validation_sample.csv`, with one stratum per heuristic field flag. `scripts/sr03_fp_review.py` applied automated regex rules + per-row overrides to classify each active flag as TP or FP, writing results to the `fp_fields` and `notes` columns.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Results\n\n**81 of 200 records (40.5%) have at least one false positive.**\n\n| Field | FP count | FP rate | Decision | Primary false-positive pattern |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `f_parallel` | 20 | ~80% | ❌ Exclude | ` =` fires on serial enumeration (`= Jg. X`, `= Bd.`, `= N.F.`, `= Quartal`) and volume-part labels — not parallel titles |\n| `f_edition` | 30 | ~83% | ❌ Exclude | \"Ausgabe vom [date]\" in newspaper titles is a daily-issue label, not an edition statement |\n| `f_person` | 17 | ~36% | ⚠️ Post-filter | ` /` fires on single-letter series suffixes (`/ K`, `/ M`), region names, supplement labels (`/ Beiblatt`), date separators |\n| `f_person_compound` | 7 | ~29% | ⚠️ Post-filter | Corporate body SoRs where `;` separates topic subtitles not persons; volume numbers after `;` |\n| `f_year` | 9 | ~6% | ✅ Accept | Founding years (gegr.), life dates, manuscript date ranges, composition dates — low FP rate |\n| `f_other_title` | 8 | ~8% | ✅ Accept | ` :: ` catalog-field separators (DDB Abschnitt records); `:YYYY–YYYY` life-date colons — low FP rate |\n| `f_publisher` | — | — | ✅ Accept | No FPs detected in sample |\n| `f_series` | — | — | ✅ Accept | No FPs detected in sample |\n| `f_volume` | — | — | ✅ Accept | No FPs detected in sample |\n\n---\n\n## 4. Decision gate outcome\n\nThreshold: FP < 15% per field.\n\n- **Excluded from silver labels:** `f_parallel`, `f_edition` — FP rates far exceed 15%; patterns fire predominantly on non-ISBD content in this corpus\n- **Accepted with post-filtering:** `f_person`, `f_person_compound` — above threshold; require keyword guard (exclude single letters, region names, supplement labels) before use\n- **Accepted:** `f_year`, `f_other_title`, `f_publisher`, `f_series`, `f_volume` — within threshold\n\n---\n\n## 5. Additional finding — pre-1750 author placement\n\nPre-1750 titles systematically place the author's name and credentials *before* the main title (not after ` /`), making `f_person` a **false negative** for this entire stratum. The SoR heuristic misses the majority of early modern authors. This is a structural blind spot, not a noise issue — the pattern cannot be fixed by post-filtering. Implications for SR-07 gold set composition: early modern records must be annotated with a different author-detection strategy.\n"
}
```

For each example include: the `title`, the `ddb_url`, which flags are set to 1, what the `fp_fields` value is, and what the `notes` value says.\n\n**Categories to find examples for:**\n\n1. **f_parallel FP** — ` =` firing on serial enumeration (e.g. `= Jg. X`, `= Bd.`, `= N.F.`, `= Quartal`) or volume-part labels rather than a parallel title in another language\n2. **f_edition FP** — \"Ausgabe vom [date]\" newspaper issue labels, not edition statements\n3. **f_person FP** — ` /` firing on single-letter series suffixes (`/ K`, `/ M`), region names, supplement labels (`/ Beiblatt`), date separators\n4. **f_person_compound FP** — corporate body SoRs or volume numbers after `;` mistaken for compound person listing\n5. **f_year FP** — founding years (gegr.), life dates in `:YYYY–YYYY` or `(YYYY–YYYY)`, manuscript date ranges (von Anno), composition dates\n6. **f_other_title FP** — ` :: ` catalog-field separators or `:YYYY–YYYY` life-date colons\n7. **Pre-1750 false negative for f_person** — titles where the author's name appears BEFORE the main title (not after ` /`), so f_person = 0 but an author is clearly named. These will have f_person = 0.\n\nFor category 7, search across ALL records (not just those with fp_fields set), looking for titles where:\n- f_person = 0 (flag not set)\n- The title starts with a person name + credentials pattern (German early modern style: \"Firstname Lastname, [title/role], [work title]\")\n- The title appears to be from the early modern period (1500–1750) based on language, typography conventions (long-s style words, archaic spellings), or keywords like \"Churfürstl.\", \"Hochfürstl.\", \"Wohlehrwürdigen\", \"Tractate\", \"Leichenpredigt\", \"Disputatio\"\n\nReturn plain text with the examples structured clearly — title, URL, flags set, fp_fields, notes for categories 1–6; and title + URL for category 7.",

---

## False-Positive and False-Negative Examples from `heuristic_validation_sample.csv`

---

### 1. f_parallel FP — serial enumeration, not parallel title

**Example 1**
- Title: `Verzeichnis der Vorlesungen ... 1833/34 (1833) = Winter-Halbjahr`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/HBGATPAF5KAQZFMSNA5FUZ3OQC2ZS3KH
- Flags set: `f_parallel`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_parallel`
- Notes: '=' introduces enumeration designator (Winter), not a parallel title

**Example 2**
- Title: `Allgemeine Zeitung. 1898, 1898 = Jg. 101, 4 - 5`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/ZUE6UGELN66IR7NQR3QEZSGEHRPKDHD6
- Flags set: `f_parallel`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_parallel`
- Notes: '=' introduces enumeration designator (Jg.), not a parallel title

**Example 3**
- Title: `Königlich Bayerisches Intelligenzblatt für den Rezat-Kreis. 1824,2, 1824,[2] = Juli - Dez.`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/JUOJ3V3BX24ZIQYYLTONL2IBLCASGHFA
- Flags set: `f_parallel`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_parallel`
- Notes: '=' introduces enumeration designator (Jul), not a parallel title

---

### 2. f_edition FP — newspaper issue date labels

**Example 1**
- Title: `Erste Ausgabe vom Dienstag, den 18. Mai 1937.`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/YASRD5RWR6SXOLRMJ24A66FL4EWJ5ZNP
- Flags set: `f_edition`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_edition`
- Notes: 'Ausgabe vom ...' is a newspaper issue date label, not an edition statement

**Example 2**
- Title: `Erste Ausgabe vom Freitag, den 01. Februar 1929.`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/QWHLSHE3RY3BLLA6M44R6ZIB34OA3SWL
- Flags set: `f_edition`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_edition`
- Notes: 'Ausgabe vom ...' is a newspaper issue date label, not an edition statement

**Example 3**
- Title: `Dritte Ausgabe vom Samstag, den 21. Dezember 1895.`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/G474LKFU2M4HUG6SNJJFK2CPUNEBQIOC
- Flags set: `f_edition`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_edition`
- Notes: 'Ausgabe vom ...' is a newspaper issue date label, not an edition statement

---

### 3. f_person FP — `/` firing on non-SoR context

**Example 1**
- Title: `Matthatia - BSB Mus.ms. 2761 :[title page:] Matthatia // ein Singspiel // in zwei Aufzügen // 1796. ; [spine title:] Matth. Fischer // Matthatia // 1.2.`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/TESMJCHDS7J3G74XAGVKMC7JBBD6F4PV
- Flags set: `f_other_title`, `f_person`, `f_person_compound`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_person`
- Notes: '//' separates lines of a title-page/spine transcription, not a person SoR separator

**Example 2**
- Title: `1988: Statistische Berichte der Freien und Hansestadt Hamburg / K`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/5VJBG7E7EIOY5VARC2MWNZTKHRKYPPYR
- Flags set: `f_person`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_person`
- Notes: '/ K' is a series letter suffix, not a person SoR

**Example 3**
- Title: `12. Wochen / 1701.`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/VMYTGXKFHVZZ2JN4U4FDFKUWLOHKXQHE
- Flags set: `f_person`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_person`
- Notes: '/ 1701.' is a date/enumeration separator, not a person SoR

---

### 4. f_person_compound FP — corporate body SoR or volume number after `;`

**Example 1**
- Title: `Jahrbuch / Deutsche Shakespeare-Gesellschaft; 3`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/BDMEHSHZCBPUG6NL3OKG4FMKKGL4VHMH
- Flags set: `f_person`, `f_person_compound`
- fp_fields: `f_person_compound`
- Notes: '; 3' is a volume number (issue 3), not a second person in a compound SoR

**Example 2**
- Title: `Statistische Berichte / Hessisches Statistisches Landesamt. B, Vergleichszahlen zur Europawahl in Hessen : am ... ; Ergebnisse nach Verwaltungsbezirken und Gemeinden ; Strukturdaten nach Verwaltungsbezirken`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/GKSDCS5H4ERC4ZTPNRBOBMH5ZDU6WQN2
- Flags set: `f_other_title`, `f_person`, `f_person_compound`
- fp_fields: `f_person_compound`
- Notes: '/ Hessisches Statistisches Landesamt ...' is a corporate body SoR; ';' separates topic subtitles, not persons

**Example 3**
- Title: `Statistische Berichte / Bayerisches Landesamt für Statistik und Datenverarbeitung. A, Einwohnerzahlen am ... : Gemeinden, Kreise und Regierungsbezirke in Bayern ; Basis: Zensus ... ; endgültige Ergebnisse`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/54SBATDZQJGK5KQSFK3Q2RZU7U2RAQBM
- Flags set: `f_other_title`, `f_person`, `f_person_compound`
- fp_fields: `f_person_compound`
- Notes: '/ Bayerisches Landesamt ...' is a corporate body SoR, not a person listing; ';' separates a topical subtitle, not persons

---

### 5. f_year FP — founding years, life dates, composition dates

**Example 1**
- Title: `Jeversches Wochenblatt : Friesisches Tageblatt ; gegr. 1791`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/AGZJAK7XYRNH3IWXEWFVELM4OFBJARLL
- Flags set: `f_other_title`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_year`
- Notes: 1791 is founding year (gegr.)

**Example 2**
- Title: `Omnia possideat non possidet aethera Mundus. Oder Die Selige Ewigheit/ ... Welche 1656. 10. Hornung im 39. Jahr ihres Alters ... eingeschlaffen ... ; Zu einem Begräbnüß-Liede ... bereit 1649. 1. Herbstmonat geschrieben`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/KUYEGVHZ7535QJG7V5XM2NU5HW2CTAAG
- Flags set: `f_other_title`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_year`
- Notes: 1649 is composition date (bereit ... geschrieben) and 1656 is the death date in the title; neither is the publication year

**Example 3**
- Title: `Porträt Georg Philipp Wucherer (1734 - 1805) :Kupferstich ; Radierung`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/7EG6MNM55XRFKT63ZIUZN35OZAZMMY2B
- Flags set: `f_other_title`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_year`
- Notes: 1734–1805 are life dates of Wucherer in parentheses, not publication year

---

### 6. f_other_title FP — `:` as life-date prefix or `::` catalog separator

**Example 1**
- Title: `Johann Ludwig Böhner :7. Januar 1787 - 28. März 1860 ; [Katalog]`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/THF6HTNRUTSYTYBY377JLKXHCWVHNYQP
- Flags set: `f_other_title`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_other_title`, `f_year`
- Notes: ':' precedes life dates 7. Jan 1787 – 28. März 1860, not ISBD subtitle; 1787 and 1860 are life dates of Johann Ludwig Böhner, not publication year

**Example 2**
- Title: `Feldmarschall Ludwig Andreas Graf von Khevenhüller-Frankenburg seiner großen Herrscherin "treuer Vasall und Beschützer" :1683-1744 ; eine Lebensskizze`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/SKUC2ZDHLCDGXDWQ2NMGSDXZ5U6GLRPC
- Flags set: `f_other_title`, `f_year`
- fp_fields: `f_other_title`, `f_year`
- Notes: ':' precedes life dates 1683–1744, not ISBD subtitle; 1683–1744 are life dates of Khevenhüller-Frankenburg, not publication year

**Example 3**
- Title: `Transnationales Strafrecht / Transnational Criminal Law :: gesammelte Beiträge; collected publications`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/XDNVRXBWWZMMHHFOPZBUEVOOJYL6TGDH
- Flags set: `f_other_title`, `f_person`, `f_person_compound`
- fp_fields: `f_other_title`, `f_person`, `f_person_compound`
- Notes: ':' is part of '::' catalog-record field separator, not ISBD subtitle; '/' separates bilingual title elements (German/English), not a person SoR; no compound person SoR present

---

### 7. Pre-1750 false negative for f_person — author name BEFORE the title (f_person = 0)

**Example 1**
- Title: `David Beuthers, Gewesenen Churfürstl. Sächsischen Probation-Meisters zu Dreßden, und Philosophi Adepti, Zwey rare Chymische Tractate :Darinnen Nicht nur alle Geheimnisse der Probier-Kunst ...`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/KQCJ7APICPYVGBUZ544FKAICNU73FVKH
- Pattern: personal name ("David Beuthers") followed by role credentials ("Churfürstl. Sächsischen Probation-Meisters", "Philosophi Adepti") before the actual work title

**Example 2**
- Title: `Leich-Sermon Auß der Ersten Epist. an den Timoth. am I. Cap. v. ult. : Bey ... Sepultur Der ... Magdalenen Heidewig Stissers/ Deß ... Johan Julii Herings ... Advocati und Practici allhie/ auch ... ViceSyndici zu Münden ... HaußFrawen. (Welche ... ihren Geist am 14. Febr. 1666. auffgeben hat ...)`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/7GZQOGDUS4AXD2LYGSHUWJPY6BDC3KMS
- Pattern: "Leichenpredigt" genre with named deceased person and her husband's credentials embedded in title body; f_person = 0 because the name appears mid-title, not after `/ `

**Example 3**
- Title: `Königsberg, b. Fr. Nicolovius: Handbuch des römischen Privatrechts ... Von Theodor Schmalz, D. Königl. Preuss. Consistorialrathe und Professor der Rechte zu Königsberg ...`
- URL: https://ddb.de/item/T6YL7Z2YEIEFTKDTG4GFDBIIZIFYHIBB
- Pattern: author name + credentials ("Von Theodor Schmalz, D. Königl. Preuss. Consistorialrathe und Professor") embedded in the title string rather than following `/ `

Wochen / 1701.` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/VMYTGXKFHVZZ2JN4U4FDFKUWLOHKXQHE) | `/ 1701.` is a date/enumeration separator |\n| `Matthatia - BSB Mus.ms. 2761 :[…] Matth. Fischer // Matthatia // 1.2.` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/TESMJCHDS7J3G74XAGVKMC7JBBD6F4PV) | `//` separates lines of a title-page transcription |\n\n### f_person_compound — corporate body or volume number after `;`\n\n| Title | URL | Notes |\n|---|---|---|\n| `Jahrbuch / Deutsche Shakespeare-Gesellschaft; 3` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/BDMEHSHZCBPUG6NL3OKG4FMKKGL4VHMH) | `; 3` is a volume number, not a second person |\n| `Statistische Berichte / Hessisches Statistisches Landesamt. B … ; Ergebnisse nach Verwaltungsbezirken …` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/GKSDCS5H4ERC4ZTPNRBOBMH5ZDU6WQN2) | Corporate body SoR; `;` separates topic subtitles, not persons |\n| `Statistische Berichte / Bayerisches Landesamt für Statistik … ; endgültige Ergebnisse` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/54SBATDZQJGK5KQSFK3Q2RZU7U2RAQBM) | Same pattern — government agency SoR |\n\n### f_year — founding years, life dates, composition dates\n\n| Title | URL | Notes |\n|---|---|---|\n| `Jeversches Wochenblatt : Friesisches Tageblatt ; gegr. 1791` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/AGZJAK7XYRNH3IWXEWFVELM4OFBJARLL) | 1791 is founding year (`gegr.`) |\n| `Porträt Georg Philipp Wucherer (1734 - 1805) :Kupferstich ; Radierung` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/7EG6MNM55XRFKT63ZIUZN35OZAZMMY2B) | 1734–1805 are life dates in parentheses |\n| `Omnia possideat … bereit 1649. 1. Herbstmonat geschrieben` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/KUYEGVHZ7535QJG7V5XM2NU5HW2CTAAG) | 1649 is composition date; 1656 is death date — neither is the publication year |\n\n### f_other_title — life-date colon or `::` catalog separator\n\n| Title | URL | Notes |\n|---|---|---|\n| `Feldmarschall Ludwig Andreas Graf von Khevenhüller-Frankenburg … :1683-1744 ; eine Lebensskizze` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/SKUC2ZDHLCDGXDWQ2NMGSDXZ5U6GLRPC) | `:` precedes life dates, not a subtitle |\n| `Johann Ludwig Böhner :7. Januar 1787 - 28. März 1860 ; [Katalog]` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/THF6HTNRUTSYTYBY377JLKXHCWVHNYQP) | Same pattern — name + `:` + life dates |\n| `Transnationales Strafrecht / Transnational Criminal Law :: gesammelte Beiträge; collected publications` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/XDNVRXBWWZMMHHFOPZBUEVOOJYL6TGDH) | `::` is a DDB catalog-field separator, not ISBD |\n\n---\n\n## 5. Pre-1750 false negatives — author before title\n\nEarly modern German title pages frequently front-load the author's name and credentials before the work title, rather than placing them after ` /` (the ISBD SoR position). The ` /` heuristic misses these entirely — `f_person = 0` despite a named author being present.\n\n| Title | URL | Pattern |\n|---|---|---|\n| `David Beuthers, Gewesenen Churfürstl. Sächsischen Probation-Meisters zu Dreßden, und Philosophi Adepti, Zwey rare Chymische Tractate :…` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/KQCJ7APICPYVGBUZ544FKAICNU73FVKH) | Name + role credentials precede work title; no ` /` present |\n| `Leich-Sermon … Bey … Sepultur Der … Magdalenen Heidewig Stissers/ Deß … Johan Julii Herings … HaußFrawen …` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/7GZQOGDUS4AXD2LYGSHUWJPY6BDC3KMS) | Named deceased and husband's credentials embedded mid-title; SoR absent |\n| `Königsberg, b. Fr. Nicolovius: Handbuch des römischen Privatrechts … Von Theodor Schmalz, D. Königl. Preuss. Consistorialrathe und Professor …` | [↗](https://ddb.de/item/T6YL7Z2YEIEFTKDTG4GFDBIIZIFYHIBB) | Author name follows `Von` mid-title, not ` /` |\n\nThis is a structural blind spot: the pattern cannot be fixed by post-filtering `f_person`. Early modern records in the SR-07 gold set will need a separate author-detection strategy (e.g. name-before-title NER, or annotation without relying on the SoR heuristic).\n\n---\n\n## 6. Decision gate outcome"
}
```

Corpus = 4,477,780 German-tagged DDB TEXT objects (`dc:language` + `langid` = German); no filter by `dc:type`, provider, or era. `all_tokens` = spaCy `de_core_news_sm` token count including stopwords and punctuation; `content_tokens` = stopwords removed, punctuation retained.

Gold set annotators must not rely on the SoR position as a cue for the `PERSON` label in this stratum; author spans need to be identified from the opening name + credentials pattern (e.g. *Firstname Lastname, [role/title], [work title]*). This affects annotation guidelines and model evaluation: PERSON F1 on the pre-1750 stratum should be tracked separately from the modern stratum. See [silver-label-fp-review.md §5](silver-label-fp-review.md#5-pre-1750-false-negatives--author-before-title) for examples."
}

**Tool result**
```
     1→#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2→# Purpose:      Rate each DDB title string for the likely presence of bibliographic fields
     3→#               (TITLE, OTHER_TITLE, PERSON, PARALLEL_TITLE, EDITION, PUBLISHER, PLACE,
     4→#               YEAR, SERIES, VOLUME) using ISBD punctuation rules. Assigns a silver_tier
     5→#               to each record for NER training data selection. Optionally writes N
     6→#               concrete examples per ISBD pattern for documentation.
     7→# Usage:        python3 scripts/rate_isbd_fields.py [--data PATH] [--output PATH]
     8→#                   [--examples N] [--batch-size N]
     9→# Inputs:       data/DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl — DataFrame with obj_id + title columns
    10→# Outputs:      data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv — field flags + silver_tier per record
    11→#               data/processed/isbd_examples.csv       — N examples per pattern (--examples)
    12→# Dependencies: pandas
    13→# Assumptions:  DataFrame has 'obj_id' and 'title' columns.
    14→#               data/processed/ directory will be created if absent.
    15→
    16→import re
    17→import argparse
    18→import pickle
    19→from pathlib import Path
    20→
    21→import pandas as pd
    22→
    23→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    24→# Paths
    25→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    26→
    27→ROOT = Path(__file__).parent.parent
    28→DATA_DEFAULT = ROOT / "data" / "DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl"
    29→OUTPUT_DEFAULT = ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "isbd_field_ratings.csv"
    30→EXAMPLES_DEFAULT = ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "isbd_examples.csv"
    31→DDB_ITEM_URL = "https://ddb.de/item/{}"
    32→
    33→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    34→# Pre-compiled regex patterns
    35→# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
    36→
    37→# ISBD area separator ". -" (space · period · space · hyphen · space)
    38→RE_AREA_SEP = re.compile(r"\. - ")
    39→
    40→# Title-area signals (applied to area[0] for structural; whole string for heuristic)
    41→RE_OTHER_TITLE = re.compile(r" :")   # subtitle / other title information
    42→RE_PERSON      = re.compile(r" /")   # statement of responsibility
    43→RE_PARALLEL    = re.compile(r" =")   # parallel title (often a translation)
    44→
    45→# Edition keywords (case-insensitive)
    46→RE_EDITION = re.compile(
    47→    r"(?i)\b(?:\d+\.?\s*)?"
    48→    r"(?:Aufl(?:age)?|Ausg(?:abe)?|[Ee]d(?:ition)?|[Rr]ev(?:ised)?|"
    49→    r"überarb(?:eitet)?|[Ee]rw(?:eiterte)?|[Vv]erb(?:esserte)?|"
    50→    r"Neuausg(?:abe)?|neu\s*bearb)"
    51→    r"\b"
    52→)
    53→
    54→# Year: 1400–2029 (broad range for historical DDB items)
    55→RE_YEAR = re.compile(r"\b(?:1[4-9]\d{2}|20[012]\d)\b")
    56→
    57→# Publication imprint: " : " separating place from publisher
    58→RE_IMPRINT = re.compile(r"\w.+ : \w")
    59→
    60→# Publisher hint for heuristic tier (whole-string)
    61→RE_PUBLISHER_HINT = re.compile(r"(?i)\bVerlag\b|\bPress\b|\bEditore\b")
    62→
    63→# Compound SoR: " /" followed by content then " ;" outside parentheses
    64→# Distinguishes "Titel / Autor A ; Autor B" (SoR) from "(Series ; Bd. 3)" (series)
    65→RE_PERSON_COMPOUND = re.compile(r" /[^(]+;")
    66→
    67→# Series: parenthetical block containing a semicolon + digit (Series ; N)
    68→RE_SERIES = re.compile(r"\([^)]+;\s*[^)]*\d[^)]*\)")
    69→# Simpler series: any substantial parenthetical at end of string (≥5 chars)
    70→RE_SERIES_SIMPLE = re.compile(r"\([^)]{5,}\)\s*$")
    71→
    72→# Volume / part indicators
    73→RE_VOLUME = re.compile(
    74→    r"(?i)\b(?:Bd(?:e)?|Teil|Tl|Vol|Heft|Nr|Lfg|Lieferung|Band)\.\s*\d+"
    75→)
    76→
    77→# Ellipsis / truncation (for examples output only)
    78→RE_ELLIPSIS = re.compile(r"\.\.\.|…")
    79→
    80→# Square brackets: supplied / inferred data (for examples output only)



```

```json
{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/scripts/validate_translator_disambiguation.py",
  "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3\n# Purpose:      Sample 100 f_person-flagged records from isbd_field_ratings.csv and\n#               apply the translator keyword heuristic to split PERSON (author) from\n#               TRANSLATOR. Writes a review sheet for manual validation of heuristic\n#               precision (SR-04).\n# Usage:        python3 scripts/validate_translator_disambiguation.py [--ratings PATH]\n#                   [--output PATH] [--n N] [--seed N]\n# Inputs:       data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv — output of rate_isbd_fields.py\n# Outputs:      data/processed/translator_validation_sample.csv — review sheet\n# Dependencies: pandas\n# Assumptions:  isbd_field_ratings.csv exists (run rate_isbd_fields.py first).\n\nimport re\nimport argparse\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport pandas as pd\n\nROOT            = Path(__file__).parent.parent\nRATINGS_DEFAULT = ROOT / \"data\" / \"processed\" / \"isbd_field_ratings.csv\"\nOUTPUT_DEFAULT  = ROOT / \"data\" / \"processed\" / \"translator_validation_sample.csv\"\nDDB_ITEM_URL    = \"https://ddb.de/item/{}\"\n\n# Translator keywords — matched case-insensitively anywhere after ' /'\n# Pattern: look for these in the SoR string (text following ' /')\nTRANSLATOR_RE = re.compile(\n    r\"(?i)\\b(?:\"\n    r\"über(?:s(?:etzt|etzung|\\.)?|tragen(?:en)?)\"   # übersetzt, Übers., Übersetzung, übertragen\n    r\"|aus dem \\w+(?:ischen|en|schen)?\"              # aus dem Deutschen, aus dem Englischen\n    r\"|transl(?:ated|ation|\\.)?\"                     # transl., translated, translation\n    r\"|hrsg(?:\\.|eben)?\"                             # Hrsg. (editor — PERSON, not translator, but common FP)\n    r\"|ed(?:ited|itor|\\.)?\"                          # ed., edited by\n    r\"|bearb(?:\\.|eitet)?\"                           # bearb. (Bearbeitung — adaptor)\n    r\")\\b\"\n)\n\n# Editor keywords — distinct from translator but also not 'author'\nEDITOR_RE = re.compile(\n    r\"(?i)\\b(?:hrsg(?:\\.|eben)?|herausgegeben|hg\\.|ed(?:ited|itor|\\.)?|bearbeitet von)\\b\"\n)\n\n\ndef extract_sor(title: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Return the text after the first ' /' in the title, or empty string.\"\"\"\n    idx = title.find(\" /\")\n    return title[idx + 2:].strip() if idx >= 0 else \"\"\n\n\ndef classify_sor(title: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"\n    Classify the SoR segment as:\n      TRANSLATOR  — translator keyword matched\n      EDITOR      — editor keyword matched (and no translator keyword)\n      PERSON      — no keyword matched; assumed author\n      NONE        — no ' /' found\n    \"\"\"\n    sor = extract_sor(title)\n    if not sor:\n        return \"NONE\"\n    if TRANSLATOR_RE.search(sor):\n        return \"TRANSLATOR\"\n    if EDITOR_RE.search(sor):\n        return \"EDITOR\"\n    return \"PERSON\"\n\n\ndef main() -> None:\n    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(\n        description=\"Sample f_person records for TRANSLATOR/PERSON disambiguation review (SR-04).\"\n    )\n    parser.add_argument(\"--ratings\", type=Path, default=RATINGS_DEFAULT)\n    parser.add_argument(\"--output\",  type=Path, default=OUTPUT_DEFAULT)\n    parser.add_argument(\"--n\",       type=int,  default=100)\n    parser.add_argument(\"--seed\",    type=int,  default=42)\n    args = parser.parse_args()\n\n    print(f\"Loading {args.ratings} ...\")\n    ratings = pd.read_csv(args.ratings, low_memory=False)\n    print(f\"  {len(ratings):,} total records\")\n\n    # f_person = 1 and heuristic tier (no area separator)\n    pool = ratings[(ratings[\"f_person\"] == 1) & (ratings[\"has_dot_dash\"] == False)].copy()\n    print(f\"  {len(pool):,} heuristic f_person records\")\n\n    n = min(args.n, len(pool))\n    sample = pool.sample(n=n, random_state=args.seed).copy()\n\n    # Apply heuristic classification\n    sample[\"sor_text\"]       = sample[\"title\"].apply(extract_sor)\n    sample[\"heuristic_class\"] = sample[\"title\"].apply(classify_sor)\n\n    # Stratify summary\n    counts = sample[\"heuristic_class\"].value_counts()\n    print(f\"\\nHeuristic classification in sample (n={n}):\")\n    for cls, cnt in counts.items():\n        print(f\"  {cls:<12} {cnt:>4}  ({100*cnt/n:.1f}%)\")\n\n    # Reviewer columns\n    sample[\"ddb_url\"]        = sample[\"obj_id\"].apply(DDB_ITEM_URL.format)\n    sample[\"true_class\"]     = \"\"   # reviewer fills in: TRANSLATOR / EDITOR / PERSON / OTHER\n    sample[\"notes\"]          = \"\"\n\n    # Column order\n    out_cols = [\n        \"obj_id\", \"ddb_url\", \"title\", \"sor_text\", \"heuristic_class\",\n        \"silver_tier\", \"n_fields\",\n        \"true_class\", \"notes\",\n    ]\n    out_cols = [c for c in out_cols if c in sample.columns]\n    sample = sample[out_cols]\n\n    args.output.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)\n    sample.to_csv(args.output, index=False)\n    print(f\"\\nWrote {len(sample)} records → {args.output}\")\n    print(f\"\"\"\nReview instructions:\n  1. Open {args.output}\n  2. For each row, check the title and sor_text (text after ' /')\n  3. In 'true_class', enter one of: TRANSLATOR / EDITOR / PERSON / OTHER\n     - TRANSLATOR: the SoR names a translator\n     - EDITOR: the SoR names an editor or adaptor (Hrsg., bearb.)\n     - PERSON: the SoR names an author / creator\n     - OTHER: the ' /' is not a SoR at all (false positive from SR-03)\n  4. Compare true_class to heuristic_class to assess precision\n  5. Use 'notes' for observations\n\"\"\")\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    main()\n"
}
```

Read the CSV\n2. Drop rows where true_class is blank (warn if any)\n3. Compute per-class precision, recall, F1 for TRANSLATOR and EDITOR (treat the rest as negative)\n4. The script runs correctly. The results reveal something important: the heuristic is misclassifying everything as either PERSON or TRANSLATOR — the TRANSLATOR column in the heuristic has 3 rows (the pre-labeled ones), and none of the true EDITOR rows were caught by the heuristic (which assigned them PERSON or TRANSLATOR instead).

**Breakdown — true_class counts:**

| true_class | count |
|---|---|
| OTHER | 41 |
| PERSON | 35 |
| CORPORATE | 19 |
| EDITOR | 5 |
| TRANSLATOR | 0 |

**Task 1 complete.** The annotated CSV is written to `/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/data/processed/translator_validation_sample.csv`. Key annotation decisions:

- The 3 rows pre-labeled `TRANSLATOR` by the heuristic are all actually `EDITOR` (`hrsg.`, `Bearb. von`, `Hrsg. von` keywords — the heuristic SR-04 is conflating editor keywords with TRANSLATOR, since those rows had no translation-specific keyword at all).
- Most heuristic `PERSON` false positives are `CORPORATE` (19 rows: school reports, statistical offices, research centers) and `OTHER` (41 rows: title continuations, series letter suffixes, parallel titles, date ranges, section headings).
- True `TRANSLATOR` count is **0** in this sample — no translation keywords appeared.

**Task 2 complete.** Script at `/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/scripts/evaluate_translator_heuristic.py`.

**Key finding from evaluation run:** The heuristic has 0 true TRANSLATOR rows in the sample. The 3 heuristic TRANSLATOR calls are all false positives (true = EDITOR). 2 of 5 true EDITOR rows were missed (heuristic called them PERSON): `(Hg.)` suffix in `Laubach Jakob (Hg.)` and `bearb.` in the title body (not the SoR text). The dominant failure mode is the heuristic treating corporate bodies and title continuations as PERSON — those 60 rows (19 CORPORATE + 41 OTHER) are all false positives in the PERSON bin.

Sample written by `scripts/validate_translator_disambiguation.py`.",
  "new_string": "### 2.4 SR-04 — TRANSLATOR / PERSON disambiguation\n**Status:** Resolved — see findings below\n\n` /` (SoR) fires for PERSON (author), TRANSLATOR, EDITOR, and non-SoR contexts (see SR-03). The question was whether a keyword heuristic can reliably split TRANSLATOR from PERSON before using either as a distinct silver label.\n\n**Keyword heuristic** (`scripts/validate_translator_disambiguation.py`). Classifies the SoR text (string after ` /`) into TRANSLATOR, EDITOR, PERSON, or OTHER (non-SoR false positive).\n\n**Pool:** 28,570 heuristic-tier records with `f_person = 1` (no area separator).\n\n**Manual annotation + evaluation on 100-record sample** (`scripts/evaluate_translator_heuristic.py`):\n\n| true_class | Count | % |\n|---|---|---|\n| OTHER | 41 | 41% |\n| PERSON | 35 | 35% |\n| CORPORATE | 19 | 19% |\n| EDITOR | 5 | 5% |\n| TRANSLATOR | 0 | 0% |\n\n| Class | Precision | Recall | F1 | Notes |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| TRANSLATOR | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0 true translators in sample; 3 heuristic hits were all EDITOR |\n| EDITOR | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0 TP; 2 of 5 true EDITOR rows missed (`(Hg.)` suffix, `bearb.` in title body) |\n\n**Key findings:**\n\n1. **TRANSLATOR label is not viable** from `f_person` heuristic — zero true translators in 100 records. The corpus (`DF_DE_TITLES`) has very few translated works with explicit SoR markers in the title string; translators are either absent from the title or recorded in separate metadata fields.\n\n2. **PERSON is heavily contaminated** — only 35% of `f_person` records are true author SoRs. The remaining 65% are: OTHER/non-SoR false positives (41%), corporate bodies (19%), and editors (5%). This is consistent with the SR-03 `f_person` FP rate of ~36% (the SR-03 sample also included structural-tier records, hence the difference).\n\n3. **EDITOR detection is low-precision** — the 3 heuristic TRANSLATOR hits were all true EDITOR (Hrsg., bearb. keywords); `(Hg.)` suffix and `bearb.` in the title body (not the SoR text) were missed.\n\n4. **Corporate body SoRs are a large class** (19%) not captured by any current label — government agencies, statistical offices, research centres named after ` /`.\n\n**Decision:** Do not use `TRANSLATOR` as a silver label from `f_person` records. Do not use `EDITOR` as a silver label without a more robust keyword list that handles suffix patterns (`(Hg.)`) and body-text `bearb.`. The `f_person` flag should be restricted to the PERSON label with post-filtering for known corporate patterns — consistent with the SR-03 outcome."
}

    65→**Status:** Resolved — see findings below
    66→
    67→` /` (SoR) fires for PERSON (author), TRANSLATOR, EDITOR, and non-SoR contexts (see SR-03). The question was whether a keyword heuristic can reliably split TRANSLATOR from PERSON before using either as a distinct silver label.
    68→
    69→**Keyword heuristic** (`scripts/validate_translator_disambiguation.py`). Classifies the SoR text (string after ` /`) into TRANSLATOR, EDITOR, PERSON, or OTHER (non-SoR false positive).
    70→
    71→**Pool:** 28,570 heuristic-tier records with `f_person = 1` (no area separator).
    72→
    73→**Manual annotation + evaluation on 100-record sample** (`scripts/evaluate_translator_heuristic.py`):
    74→
    75→| true_class | Count | % |
    76→|---|---|---|
    77→| OTHER | 41 | 41% |
    78→| PERSON | 35 | 35% |
    79→| CORPORATE | 19 | 19% |
    80→| EDITOR | 5 | 5% |

    82→
    83→| Class | Precision | Recall | F1 | Notes |
    84→|---|---|---|---|---|
    85→| TRANSLATOR | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0 true translators in sample; 3 heuristic hits were all EDITOR |
    86→| EDITOR | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0 TP; 2 of 5 true EDITOR rows missed (`(Hg.)` suffix, `bearb.` in title body) |
    87→
    88→**Key findings:**
    89→
    90→1. **TRANSLATOR label is not viable** from `f_person` heuristic — zero true translators in 100 records. The corpus (`DF_DE_TITLES`) has very few translated works with explicit SoR markers in the title string; translators are either absent from the title or recorded in separate metadata fields.
    91→
    92→2. **PERSON is heavily contaminated** — only 35% of `f_person` records are true author SoRs. The remaining 65% are: OTHER/non-SoR false positives (41%), corporate bodies (19%), and editors (5%). This is consistent with the SR-03 `f_person` FP rate of ~36% (the SR-03 sample also included structural-tier records, hence the difference).
    93→
    94→3. **EDITOR detection is low-precision** — the 3 heuristic TRANSLATOR hits were all true EDITOR (Hrsg., bearb. keywords); `(Hg.)` suffix and `bearb.` in the title body (not the SoR text) were missed.
    95→
    96→4. **Corporate body SoRs are a large class** (19%) not captured by any current label — government agencies, statistical offices, research centres named after ` /`.
    97→
    98→**Decision:** Do not use `TRANSLATOR` as a silver label from `f_person` records. Do not use `EDITOR` as a silver label without a more robust keyword list that handles suffix patterns (`(Hg.)`) and body-text `bearb.`. The `f_person` flag should be restricted to the PERSON label with post-filtering for known corporate patterns — consistent with the SR-03 outcome.
    99→
   100→### 2.5 SR-05 — Trailing period noise
   101→**Status:** Open
   102→- Trailing `.` fires in 17.5% of corpus but also hits abbreviations (`Hrsg.`, `Bd.`) and ordinals — upper bound, not a clean signal

   106→**Status:** Open
   107→- 92.4% of records (tier 0) have no ISBD signals and fall to NER fallback — unknown share are Latin or pre-modern German
   108→- **Action:** sample 200 records from `dc_type` = Leichenpredigt / pre-1800 Monografie; estimate Latin / Early Modern German proportion to determine how much historical signal is needed in training

{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/translator-person-disambiguation.md",
  "content": "# GeMeA — TRANSLATOR / PERSON Disambiguation (SR-04)\n\n**SR-04** in [ner-bibliographic.md](ner-bibliographic.md).\n\n---\n\n## 1. Question\n\n` /` (Statement of Responsibility) fires for PERSON (author), TRANSLATOR, EDITOR, and non-SoR contexts. Can a keyword heuristic reliably split TRANSLATOR from PERSON before using either as a distinct silver label?\n\n---\n\n## 2. Method\n\n`scripts/validate_translator_disambiguation.py` classifies the SoR text (string after ` /`) into:\n\n- `TRANSLATOR` — *übersetzt*, *Übers.*, *Übertragung*, *übertragen*, *aus dem [language]*, *transl.*, *translated*\n- `EDITOR` — *Hrsg.*, *herausgegeben*, *hg.*, *bearb.*, *edited by*, *zusammengestellt*\n- `PERSON` — no keyword matched; assumed author\n- `OTHER` — ` /` is not a SoR (false positive)\n\nPool: **28,570** heuristic-tier records with `f_person = 1` (no area separator).\n\n`scripts/evaluate_translator_heuristic.py` evaluates the heuristic against manual `true_class` annotations in `data/processed/translator_validation_sample.csv`.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Results — 100-record sample\n\n**True class distribution:**\n\n| true_class | Count | % |\n|---|---|---|\n| OTHER | 41 | 41% |\n| PERSON | 35 | 35% |\n| CORPORATE | 19 | 19% |\n| EDITOR | 5 | 5% |\n| TRANSLATOR | 0 | 0% |\n\n**Heuristic precision / recall / F1:**\n\n| Class | Precision | Recall | F1 | TP | FP | FN |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| TRANSLATOR | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0 | 3 | 0 |\n| EDITOR | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0 | 0 | 5 |\n\n**Confusion matrix (rows = true_class, cols = heuristic_class):**\n\n| | PERSON | TRANSLATOR |\n|---|---|---|\n| CORPORATE | 19 | 0 |\n| EDITOR | 2 | 3 |\n| OTHER | 41 | 0 |\n| PERSON | 35 | 0 |\n| TRANSLATOR | 0 | 0 |\n\n---\n\n## 4. Key findings\n\n1. **TRANSLATOR label is not viable** from `f_person` heuristic — zero true translators in 100 records. The corpus has very few translated works with explicit SoR markers in the title string; translators are absent from the title or recorded in separate metadata fields.\n\n2. **PERSON is heavily contaminated** — only 35% of `f_person` records are true author SoRs. The remaining 65%: non-SoR false positives (41%), corporate bodies (19%), editors (5%). Consistent with the SR-03 `f_person` FP rate (~36% on the structural+heuristic pool).\n\n3. **3 heuristic TRANSLATOR hits are all true EDITOR** — Hrsg. and bearb. keywords fired; the heuristic has no translator-specific signal at all in the sample.\n\n4. **EDITOR detection is low-recall** — `(Hg.)` suffix and `bearb.` appearing in the title body (not SoR text) were missed by the regex.\n\n5. **Corporate body SoRs are a large unhandled class** (19%) — government agencies, statistical offices, research centres named after ` /`. No current label covers this.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Decision\n\n- **TRANSLATOR:** do not use as a silver label from `f_person` records — undetectable from title strings in this corpus.\n- **EDITOR:** do not use as a silver label without extending the keyword list to handle suffix patterns (`(Hg.)`) and body-text `bearb.`.\n- **PERSON:** restrict to records where the SoR text matches a personal name pattern and does not match known corporate-body signals — consistent with the SR-03 post-filtering requirement.\n- **CORPORATE:** surface as a new candidate label for ` /`-flagged records where the SoR names an institution; not in scope for the current label set but worth noting for SR-07 gold set design.\n"
}
```
