```json
{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/silver-dataset-pipeline.md",
  "content": "# GeMeA — Silver Dataset Pipeline: Summary and General Framework\n\nA synthesis of what has been done to automatically construct a silver NER training dataset from ISBD punctuation rules in 4,477,780 German DDB title strings, including all resolved study/research questions (SRs). The final section extracts a domain-agnostic framework.\n\n**Related notes:** [ner-bibliographic.md](ner-bibliographic.md), [isbd-field-rating.md](isbd-field-rating.md), [isbd-field-rating-adr.md](isbd-field-rating-adr.md), [isbd-applicability.md](isbd-applicability.md), [silver-label-fp-review.md](silver-label-fp-review.md), [translator-person-disambiguation.md](translator-person-disambiguation.md), [de-titles-distribution.md](de-titles-distribution.md), [title-length-thresholds.md](title-length-thresholds.md)\n\n---\n\n## 1. What was done\n\n### Phase 0 — Corpus characterisation\n\n**SR-10** ([de-titles-distribution.md](de-titles-distribution.md), [title-length-thresholds.md](title-length-thresholds.md))\n\nBefore doing anything with labels, the corpus was characterised along two dimensions:\n\n- **Provenance.** `DF_DE_TITLES` traced to `2023.11 NER.ipynb` — a language-filtered (dc:language + langid = German) subset of 4,477,780 DDB TEXT objects spanning all eras and dc_types with no length or topic filter.\n- **Token length distribution.** Raw distribution of `all_tokens` (spaCy `de_core_news_sm`, includes stopwords + punctuation) and `content_tokens` (stopwords removed). Percentiles: p25=4, p50=8, p75=14. Data-driven length thresholds set at p25/p75 — short ≤4, medium 5–14, long >14.\n- **Era-stratified length.** Pre-1750 titles are 42–50% long (median 12–15 tokens) — full bibliographic title pages folded into a single string. Post-1775: shift to median 6–9 tokens (Enlightenment/Romantic publishing convention change). 2000–2024: reversal to 62% medium — digital-born metadata with structured fields.\n\n*Purpose for silver dataset:* stratification variables for sampling and gold set design; establishes that pre-1750 records require different handling.\n\n---\n\n### Phase 1 — Signal detection\n\n**SR-01** (ISBD signal coverage corpus-wide)\n\n`scripts/check_isbd_titles.py` measured the raw occurrence of ISBD punctuation patterns across all 4.47M records:\n\n| Signal | Count | % |\n|---|---|---|\n| ` :` (subtitle) | 909,869 | 20.3% |\n| Trailing `.` (area close) | 783,752 | 17.5% |\n| ` ;` (subsequent SoR / series) | 174,813 | 3.9% |\n| ` /` (SoR) | 33,907 | 0.8% |\n| ` =` (parallel title) | 26,264 | 0.6% |\n\n**Finding:** only 1.2% of records carry the `. -` full area separator. The structural tier is far smaller than expected; heuristic detection must carry the load for 98.8% of records.\n\n**SR-02** (ISBD parser split priority)\n\n` /` (SoR) appears in only 0.8% of titles; ` :` (subtitle) at 20.2% is the dominant signal. The parser must prioritise ` :` splitting for OTHER_TITLE / TITLE boundary, not ` /`.\n\n---\n\n### Phase 2 — Field presence rating and tier assignment\n\n`scripts/rate_isbd_fields.py` ran over all 4.47M records and produced `data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv` with binary flags for each ISBD field and a confidence tier.\n\n**Two-tier detection (ADR-02):**\n- **Structural tier** (`has_dot_dash = True`): apply area-aware parsing; field boundaries unambiguous\n- **Heuristic tier** (`has_dot_dash = False`): apply whole-string regex; reduced precision; PLACE not detected\n\n**Tier assignment (ADR-04 / ADR-06):**\n\n| Tier | Criterion | Count | % |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| 2 — structural | `has_dot_dash AND f_resp_person AND ≥1 manifestation field` | 4,613 | 0.1% |\n| 1 — heuristic | `n_fields ≥ 3` OR `(f_person AND f_year)` | 335,524 | 7.5% |\n| 0 — unrated | All others | 4,137,643 | 92.4% |\n\n---\n\n### Phase 3 — Precision validation\n\n**SR-03** ([silver-label-fp-review.md](silver-label-fp-review.md))\n\n`scripts/validate_heuristic_fields.py` drew a 200-record stratified sample from tier-1 records (one stratum per flag). `scripts/sr03_fp_review.py` applied automated rules + per-row overrides to label each active flag as TP or FP.\n\nResults (FP rate per field, 15% acceptance threshold):\n\n| Field | FP rate | Decision |\n|---|---|---|\n| `f_parallel` | ~80% | ❌ Exclude |\n| `f_edition` | ~83% | ❌ Exclude |\n| `f_person` | ~36% | ⚠️ Sub-classify |\n| `f_person_compound` | ~29% | ⚠️ Sub-classify |\n| `f_year` | ~6% | ✅ Accept |\n| `f_other_title` | ~8% | ✅ Accept |\n| `f_publisher`, `f_series`, `f_volume` | ~0% | ✅ Accept |\n\n**Additional finding:** pre-1750 titles place the author name before the main title (not after ` /`), making `f_person` a systematic false negative for that stratum.\n\n---\n\n### Phase 4 — Signal disambiguation\n\n**SR-04** ([translator-person-disambiguation.md](translator-person-disambiguation.md))\n\n` /` is not a homogeneous signal. `scripts/validate_translator_disambiguation.py` applied a keyword heuristic to the SoR text and `scripts/evaluate_translator_heuristic.py` evaluated it against 100 manually annotated records.\n\nTrue class distribution of `f_person = 1` (heuristic tier):\n\n| Entity type | `f_resp_*` flag | % |\n|---|---|---|\n| Individual person (author) | `f_resp_person` | 35% |\n| Corporate body | `f_resp_corp` | 19% |\n| Editor / adaptor | `f_resp_editor` | 5% |\n| Non-SoR false positive | `f_resp_other` | 41% |\n| Translator | `f_resp_translator` | 0% |\n\n**Decisions:** TRANSLATOR not viable as a silver label (undetectable from title strings). Corporate bodies are a distinct entity class — not noise. `f_person` must be sub-classified before silver label assignment.\n\n---\n\n### Where things stand\n\n| Step | Status | Output |\n|---|---|---|\n| Corpus characterisation | ✅ Done | Length thresholds, era distribution |\n| Signal detection | ✅ Done | Pattern counts, SR-01/02 findings |\n| Field rating | ✅ Done | `isbd_field_ratings.csv` (4.47M rows) |\n| Precision validation | ✅ Done | Per-field FP rates, accept/exclude decisions |\n| SoR disambiguation | ✅ Done | `f_resp_*` sub-classification schema |\n| Applicability rules | ✅ Done | Era + dc_type guards |\n| Silver span extraction | 🔲 Next | `scripts/build_silver_spans.py` (Phase 0a) |\n| Gold set construction | 🔲 Blocked on SR-06, SR-07 | Manual annotation |\n| NER evaluation | 🔲 Blocked on SR-07 | SR-08 |\n\n---\n\n## 2. General framework\n\nThe pipeline above is an instance of a general approach for constructing silver NER datasets from structured text corpora using domain conventions. It has five stages.\n\n---\n\n### Stage 1 — Corpus characterisation\n\nBefore writing any detection rules, understand the corpus:\n\n- **Provenance and scope.** Who produced the data, under what standards, in what time period? Standards change; a single corpus may contain multiple cataloguing regimes.\n- **Token length distribution.** Compute percentiles; identify natural breakpoints for stratification. Short texts need different models and evaluation methods than long ones.\n- **Era / type distribution.** Plot signal coverage and text length by metadata dimension (year, type, source). Signals that work for modern records may fail for historical ones.\n\n*Output:* stratification variables; known failure modes before any rule is written.\n\n---\n\n### Stage 2 — Signal inventory\n\nEnumerate all structural signals that could indicate field boundaries in the domain:\n\n- In bibliographic data: ISBD punctuation (`. -`, ` :`, ` /`, ` =`)\n- In legal documents: section numbering, header patterns, defined-term conventions\n- In medical records: ICD code markers, section headers, measurement unit patterns\n- In scientific papers: citation brackets, figure/table captions, equation labels\n\nFor each signal: measure raw coverage, note known ambiguities. Do not assume a signal is reliable — assume the opposite until validated.\n\n*Output:* signal inventory with coverage counts.\n\n---\n\n### Stage 3 — Field presence rating\n\nConvert the signal inventory into binary presence flags per record:\n\n- One flag per candidate field\n- Apply signals in priority order (structural > heuristic)\n- Assign a **confidence tier** based on how many and which signals are present\n- Tier 2 (highest): multiple independent structural signals\n- Tier 1 (medium): at least one heuristic signal above a count threshold\n- Tier 0 (unrated): insufficient signal\n\n*Key design decisions:*\n- Rate only the primary text field (not auxiliary columns) — training/inference consistency (ADR-03)\n- Binary flags, not spans — defer span extraction; simpler to validate\n- Make tier criteria explicit and falsifiable\n\n*Output:* flag matrix + tier column over full corpus; fast to run (vectorised regex).\n\n---\n\n### Stage 4 — Precision validation\n\nFor each flag, measure empirical false-positive rate on a stratified sample:\n\n1. Draw a sample stratified by flag (ensure each flag has representation)\n2. For each record, inspect the raw text and annotate each active flag as TP or FP\n3. Compute FP rate per flag\n4. Apply a threshold (e.g. < 15% FP) to decide: accept / post-filter / exclude\n\n**Sub-classification step.** For any flag with high FP rate due to signal ambiguity (one punctuation mark → multiple entity types), sub-classify using a keyword heuristic and validate the sub-classifier separately:\n- Map keyword matches to sub-classes\n- Annotate true entity types on a sample\n- Measure precision/recall per sub-class\n- Assign each sub-class a separate flag\n\n*Output:* per-flag accept/exclude/post-filter decisions; sub-classification schema with validated precision.\n\n---\n\n### Stage 5 — Guard application and span extraction\n\nApply the validation findings as guards before building the silver dataset:\n\n- **Field exclusion guards:** remove flags that failed the FP threshold entirely\n- **Domain guards:** apply field exclusion conditionally on metadata (e.g. exclude `f_edition` for newspaper dc_types)\n- **Era guards:** apply different detection strategies for different time periods\n- **Sub-classification guards:** use `f_resp_*`-style flags to restrict silver labels to validated entity sub-types\n\nThen extract spans from tier-2 (structural) and filtered tier-1 (heuristic) records using the accepted flags as label sources.\n\n*Output:* `silver_spans.jsonl` — one record per title, with NER span labels derived from structural signals.\n\n---\n\n### Stage 6 (downstream) — Gold evaluation and model training\n\nSilver labels are noisy by construction. The pipeline must close with a gold evaluation set:\n\n- **Stratified sample** across tier, era, length, and dc_type\n- **Manual annotation** using guidelines that account for known failure modes (e.g. pre-1750 author placement)\n- **Separate evaluation by stratum** — do not aggregate F1 across structurally different sub-populations\n- **Decision gate** before committing to a model: if zero-shot NER (e.g. NuNER Zero) meets the threshold, skip fine-tuning; else use silver + LLM-labeled data to fine-tune\n\n---\n\n### Framework summary\n\n```\nCorpus characterisation\n        ↓\n  Signal inventory          ← domain conventions (ISBD, legal codes, etc.)\n        ↓\n  Field presence rating     ← binary flags + confidence tiers\n        ↓\n  Precision validation      ← stratified sample, FP rates, accept/exclude\n        ↓\n  Sub-classification        ← keyword heuristic for ambiguous signals\n        ↓\n  Guard application         ← field, domain, era guards\n        ↓\n  Silver span extraction    ← tier-2 structural + filtered tier-1 heuristic\n        ↓\n  Gold evaluation           ← stratified manual annotation + F1 by stratum\n        ↓\n  Model training / zero-shot evaluation\n```\n\n**What makes this replicable:**\n- The approach requires only: a large corpus, a domain convention with structural signals, and metadata for stratification\n- Each stage produces a falsifiable artifact (flag matrix, FP rate table, sub-classification precision table) — not just a dataset\n- Validation is built into the pipeline, not bolted on at the end\n- The confidence tier system makes data quality explicit at every downstream step\n- Failure modes discovered during validation feed back into corpus characterisation (e.g. era-specific guards), making the pipeline self-documenting\n"
}
```

**§1 — What was done** (GeMeA-specific, all SRs):

- Phase 1: signal detection (SR-01, SR-02)
- Phase 2: field rating and tier assignment

The closing note identifies what makes it replicable: falsifiable artifacts at every stage, validation built in not bolted on, and failure modes feeding back into guards.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:

2. Key Technical Concepts:

- **ISBD punctuation**: `. -` area separator, ` :` subtitle, ` /` SoR, ` =` parallel title, edition keywords
- **Two-tier detection**: structural (has `. -`) vs. heuristic (whole-string regex)
- **Silver tier assignment**: tier 2 (structural + person + manifestation field), tier 1 (heuristic, n_fields≥3), tier 0 (unrated)
- **SR-03 FP validation**: stratified 200-record sample; FP rates: f_parallel ~80%, f_edition ~83%, f_person ~36%, f_year ~6%, f_other_title ~8%
- **SR-04 SoR disambiguation**: f_person splits into f_resp_person (35%), f_resp_org/corporate (19%), f_resp_editor (5%), f_resp_other (41%), f_resp_translator (0%)
- **f_resp_* nomenclature**: sub-classification flags for SoR entity types (person, org, editor, other, translator, family); maps to MARC 21 entity type indicators
- **Pre-1750 false negative**: author appears before title, not after ` /` — systematic blind spot for f_person
- **spaCy de_core_news_sm**: all_tokens = all tokens including stopwords AND punctuation; content_tokens = stopwords removed, punctuation retained
- **Quartile thresholds**: short ≤4 (p25), medium 5–14, long >14 (p75) — data-driven from corpus
- **ADR-05**: exclude f_parallel + f_edition from silver labels; **ADR-06**: sub-classify f_person into f_resp_* types
- **Silver dataset pipeline framework**: 6 stages: characterise → signal inventory → rate presence → validate precision → sub-classify → guard + extract

  # Purpose: Sample 100 f_person-flagged records, apply TRANSLATOR/EDITOR/PERSON
  #          keyword heuristic to SoR text, write translator_validation_sample.csv
  TRANSLATOR_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(?:über(?:s(?:etzt|etzung|\.)?|tragen...)...")
  EDITOR_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)\b(?:hrsg(?:\.|eben)?|herausgegeben|...)...")

- **gemea/notes/silver-label-fp-review.md** — new note for SR-03:
  - §1 Background (two tiers)
  - §2 Review method
  - §3 Results table (FP counts, rates, decisions)

  - §5 Pre-1750 false negatives: 3 examples of author-before-title pattern with DDB links

- **gemea/notes/translator-person-disambiguation.md** — new note for SR-04:
  - §1 Question, §2 Method (keyword heuristics), §3 Results (true class distribution table, precision/recall/F1 table, confusion matrix), §4 Key findings, §5 Decision

- **gemea/notes/isbd-applicability.md** — new note (rewritten to include DDB examples):
  - §2 Rule-by-rule: each rule has TP and FP examples with DDB links
  - ` /` SoR section has per-entity-type table (f_resp_person 35%, f_resp_corp 19%, f_resp_editor 5%, f_resp_other 41%)
  - Corporate bodies explicitly recognised as distinct entity class, not excluded
  - §3 Era-dependent heuristics (pre-1750, 19th–20th century serials, post-2000)
  - §4 Field-level summary by dc_type
  - §5 Implications for silver label selection (7 points)

- **gemea/notes/isbd-field-rating.md** — updated:
  - Heuristic tier table now has FP rate column + silver label decision (✅/❌/⚠️)
  - New SoR sub-classification section with f_resp_* flags (f_resp_person, f_resp_corp, f_resp_editor, f_resp_family, f_resp_other) and MARC 21/RDA alignment note
  - Output schema extended with f_resp_* columns and updated f_parallel description
  - Key findings extended with SR-03, SR-04, SR-10 references

- **gemea/notes/isbd-field-rating-adr.md** — updated:
  - ADR-02 status: "Accepted / Amended by ADR-05"
  - ADR-04 status: "Accepted / Amended by ADR-06"
  - New ADR-05: exclude f_parallel + f_edition from heuristic silver labels (empirical basis: SR-03)
  - New ADR-06: sub-classify f_person into f_resp_* entity types; tier 2 tightened to require f_resp_person=1

- **gemea/notes/silver-dataset-pipeline.md** — new note:
  - §1 What was done: Phases 0–4 with all SR findings, status table
  - §2 General framework: 6-stage pipeline applicable to any domain with structural signals

- **gemea/notes/ner-bibliographic.md** — multiple updates:

  - SR-04: status → Resolved; detail moved to translator-person-disambiguation.md; 4-bullet summary

4. Errors and Fixes:

- **Wrong venv path**: `venv/bin/activate` not found → corrected to `.venv/bin/activate`
- **tail -5 suppressed output**: background script piped through tail showed empty output → used `cat` on output file directly
- **f_sor_* naming rejected**: initially used `f_sor_` prefix for SoR sub-classification → user said "use f_resp_ instead of f_sor" → replaced all occurrences with `f_resp_` via replace_all edit
- **f_resp_corp vs f_resp_org**: user's final request says "add f_resp_org" — implying corporate bodies should use `f_resp_org` not `f_resp_corp`. This rename was not yet applied when the summary was requested.

5. Problem Solving:

- **Bar annotation collision**: 1900 bucket (624,305 records, tallest bar) annotation collided with chart title → fixed with `ax1.set_ylim(0, vmax * 1.18)` to add 18% headroom
- **Hopkins 1992 citation rejected**: timing mismatch (shift starts 1775–1799, before 1791 code); wrong national context (French code for German data); cataloging codes don't shorten titles → replaced with Wittmann 1999 + Jäger 2001 as the well-supported alternative
- **SR-03 automation**: 200 records too many for manual review → agent applied automated regex rules + per-row overrides to classify all 200 records, writing fp_fields and notes
- **SR-04 zero translators**: only 3% of initial heuristic classified as TRANSLATOR; after annotation, 0 true translators → conclusion: TRANSLATOR not viable as silver label from title strings

55-### 2.3 SR-03 — Silver label quality and false positive rate
56-**Status:** Resolved — see [silver-label-fp-review.md](silver-label-fp-review.md)
57-
58-- 81 of 200 sampled heuristic-tier records (40.5%) have at least one false positive
59-- **Excluded:** `f_parallel` (~80% FP), `f_edition` (~83% FP)
60-- **Post-filter required:** `f_person` (~36% FP), `f_person_compound` (~29% FP)
61-- **Accepted:** `f_year`, `f_other_title`, `f_publisher`, `f_series`, `f_volume` (all < 15% FP)
62-- Pre-1750 author placement (name before title, not after ` /`) is a structural false-negative blind spot for `f_person` — flagged for SR-07

69-- EDITOR detection: 0 F1; `(Hg.)` suffix and body-text `bearb.` missed by heuristic

73-**Status:** Open

202-|---|---|---|
203:| `f_resp_person` | Individual person (author) | No corporate/editor keyword; personal name pattern |
204:| `f_resp_org` | Corporate body | Institutional keyword: `Landesamt`, `Bundesamt`, `Ministerium`, `Gesellschaft`, `Institut`, `Universität`, `Akademie`, `Verband`, `Amt`, `Behörde` |
205:| `f_resp_editor` | Editor / adaptor | `Hrsg.`, `herausgegeben`, `(Hg.)`, `bearb.`, `edited by`, `zusammengestellt` |
206:| `f_resp_family` | Family name | Not yet validated; candidate signals: `Familie`, `Nachlass` |
207:| `f_resp_other` | Non-SoR false positive | None of the above |
208-
209:The existing `f_person` flag is retained unchanged for backward compatibility. The `f_resp_*` flags are a post-processing layer, not a replacement.
210-
211:For tier 2 silver (ADR-04), the PERSON criterion is tightened: require `f_resp_person = 1` rather than `f_person = 1` to ensure individual-person SoRs only.
212-
213-### Consequences
214-
--
218-- Aligns flag naming with ISBD/MARC entity-type conventions
219-
220-**Negative:**
221:- Requires implementing the `f_resp_*` post-filter in `rate_isbd_fields.py` or a new downstream script
222:- `f_resp_family` is unvalidated — keep out of silver labels until a dedicated sample is reviewed
223-
224-**Mitigations:**
225:- The keyword lists for `f_resp_org` and `f_resp_editor` are drawn directly from SR-04 findings and can be extended incrementally
226:- `f_resp_other` acts as a catch-all that keeps false positives out of any silver label category
```

### User *(2026-03-23T21:12:55.729Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→# GeMeA — TRANSLATOR / PERSON Disambiguation (SR-04)
     2→
     3→**SR-04** in [ner-bibliographic.md](ner-bibliographic.md).
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## 1. Question
     8→
     9→` /` (Statement of Responsibility) fires for PERSON (author), TRANSLATOR, EDITOR, and non-SoR contexts. Can a keyword heuristic reliably split TRANSLATOR from PERSON before using either as a distinct silver label?
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## 2. Method
    14→
    15→`scripts/validate_translator_disambiguation.py` classifies the SoR text (string after ` /`) into:
    16→
    17→- `TRANSLATOR` — *übersetzt*, *Übers.*, *Übertragung*, *übertragen*, *aus dem [language]*, *transl.*, *translated*
    18→- `EDITOR` — *Hrsg.*, *herausgegeben*, *hg.*, *bearb.*, *edited by*, *zusammengestellt*
    19→- `PERSON` — no keyword matched; assumed author
    20→- `OTHER` — ` /` is not a SoR (false positive)
    21→
    22→Pool: **28,570** heuristic-tier records with `f_person = 1` (no area separator).
    23→
    24→`scripts/evaluate_translator_heuristic.py` evaluates the heuristic against manual `true_class` annotations in `data/processed/translator_validation_sample.csv`.
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## 3. Results — 100-record sample
    29→
    30→**True class distribution:**
    31→
    32→| true_class | Count | % |
    33→|---|---|---|
    34→| OTHER | 41 | 41% |
    35→| PERSON | 35 | 35% |
    36→| CORPORATE | 19 | 19% |
    37→| EDITOR | 5 | 5% |
    38→| TRANSLATOR | 0 | 0% |
    39→
    40→**Heuristic precision / recall / F1:**
    41→
    42→| Class | Precision | Recall | F1 | TP | FP | FN |
    43→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
    44→| TRANSLATOR | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0 | 3 | 0 |
    45→| EDITOR | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0 | 0 | 5 |
    46→
    47→**Confusion matrix (rows = true_class, cols = heuristic_class):**
    48→
    49→| | PERSON | TRANSLATOR |
    50→|---|---|---|
    51→| CORPORATE | 19 | 0 |
    52→| EDITOR | 2 | 3 |
    53→| OTHER | 41 | 0 |
    54→| PERSON | 35 | 0 |
    55→| TRANSLATOR | 0 | 0 |
    56→
    57→---
    58→
    59→## 4. Key findings
    60→
    61→1. **TRANSLATOR label is not viable** from `f_person` heuristic — zero true translators in 100 records. The corpus has very few translated works with explicit SoR markers in the title string; translators are absent from the title or recorded in separate metadata fields.
    62→
    63→2. **PERSON is heavily contaminated** — only 35% of `f_person` records are true author SoRs. The remaining 65%: non-SoR false positives (41%), corporate bodies (19%), editors (5%). Consistent with the SR-03 `f_person` FP rate (~36% on the structural+heuristic pool).
    64→
    65→3. **3 heuristic TRANSLATOR hits are all true EDITOR** — Hrsg. and bearb. keywords fired; the heuristic has no translator-specific signal at all in the sample.
    66→
    67→4. **EDITOR detection is low-recall** — `(Hg.)` suffix and `bearb.` appearing in the title body (not SoR text) were missed by the regex.
    68→
    69→5. **Corporate body SoRs are a large unhandled class** (19%) — government agencies, statistical offices, research centres named after ` /`. No current label covers this.
    70→
    71→---
    72→
    73→## 5. Decision
    74→
    75→- **TRANSLATOR:** do not use as a silver label from `f_person` records — undetectable from title strings in this corpus.
    76→- **EDITOR:** do not use as a silver label without extending the keyword list to handle suffix patterns (`(Hg.)`) and body-text `bearb.`.
    77→- **PERSON:** restrict to records where the SoR text matches a personal name pattern and does not match known corporate-body signals — consistent with the SR-03 post-filtering requirement.
    78→- **CORPORATE:** surface as a new candidate label for ` /`-flagged records where the SoR names an institution; not in scope for the current label set but worth noting for SR-07 gold set design.
    79→



```

The right framing is person | collective agents (org, family) | role qualifier (editor) | non-SoR (other) — mirroring the ISBD/RDA tripartite: personal name, corporate name, family name. Let me update the schema definition and documentation accordingly.

### User *(2026-03-23T21:13:13.623Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→# GeMeA — ISBD Field Rating: Spec and Methodology
     2→
     3→**Script:** `scripts/rate_isbd_fields.py`
     4→**Output:** `data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv`, `data/processed/isbd_examples.csv`
     5→**Phase:** 0a — NER Training Data (see [roadmap.md](roadmap.md))
     6→**ADR:** [isbd-field-rating-adr.md](isbd-field-rating-adr.md)
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## Objective
    11→
    12→Map the presence of bibliographic fields in each title string across all 4.47M records in `DF_DE_TITLES`. The field presence flags are used to:
    13→
    14→1. **Select silver candidates** for NER training — records where ISBD structure clearly identifies multiple labelable spans
    15→2. **Stratify** the NER evaluation gold set by field combination type
    16→3. **Understand corpus coverage** — which fields are detectable from the title string alone vs. requiring other columns (`dc_publisher`, `dc_creator`, etc.)
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## ISBD Area Structure
    21→
    22→ISBD organises a catalog record into areas separated by ` . - ` (space · period · space · hyphen · space). Within DF_DE_TITLES, the `title` column contains the full ISBD string as catalogued, not just the proper title. A fully structured record looks like:
    23→
    24→```
    25→Title proper : other title information / statement of responsibility
    26→  . - Edition statement
    27→  . - Place of publication : Publisher, Year
    28→  . - (Series title ; volume number)
    29→```
    30→
    31→**Examples from the corpus:**
    32→
    33→```
    34→Die höchstnöthige Sterbe-Schule : Bey Christlicher Beerdigung / Christian Gerber
    35→```
    36→→ Title area only; no `. -`; has ` :` (other title) and ` /` (SoR[^sor])
    37→([UBMFYC2EUGMB3Q5FK65JZMRFMVJXA3E4](https://ddb.de/item/UBMFYC2EUGMB3Q5FK65JZMRFMVJXA3E4))
    38→
    39→```
    40→Erwin : vier Gespräche über das Schöne und die Kunst / von K. W. F. Solger. - Berlin : Realschulbuchhandlung, 1815
    41→```
    42→→ Full structural record; area separator present; PLACE, PUBLISHER, YEAR all detectable
    43→([VQOVBGV47PA25EXTCPCNQ25V736DXDUM](https://ddb.de/item/VQOVBGV47PA25EXTCPCNQ25V736DXDUM))
    44→
    45→```
    46→Beitrag zur Infusorienkunde oder Naturbeschreibung der Zerkarien und Bazillarien : Mit 6 illuminierten Kupfertafeln / von D. Christian Ludwig Nitzsch, Professor zu Halle. - Halle : Hendel, 1817. - (Neue Schriften der Naturforschenden Gesellschaft zu Halle ; 3,1)
    47→```
    48→→ Title, SoR, publication area, series area
    49→([CO5UVCKY6XI5WDLXPZTZ6NDVXL4V2THV](https://ddb.de/item/CO5UVCKY6XI5WDLXPZTZ6NDVXL4V2THV))
    50→
    51→The `. - ` separator is the primary structural signal. Records **without** it are catalogued with incomplete ISBD punctuation — the title string may still contain useful signals (` :`, ` /`, year) but field boundary precision is lower.
    52→
    53→---
    54→
    55→[^sor]: **SoR — Statement of Responsibility** (Verantwortlichkeitsangabe). The ISBD term for the portion of a catalog record that names those responsible for the intellectual content: authors, editors, translators, illustrators, etc. In MARC 21 it is encoded in 245 $c, introduced by ` / ` (trailing mark on 245 $b). Multiple contributors are separated by ` ; `. Example: `Titel / Autor ; Herausgeber ; Übersetzer`. In the rating script, ` /` is the primary cue for detecting `PERSON` (and `TRANSLATOR`) spans. Sources: ISBD consolidated edition (2011), Area 1, §1.4 <https://www.ifla.org/files/assets/cataloguing/isbd/isbd-cons_20110321.pdf>; ThULB Jena, ISBD-Interpunktion <https://koha-wiki.thulb.uni-jena.de/erschliessung/katalogisierung/handbuecher/isbd-interpunktion/>
    56→
    57→---
    58→
    59→## Detection Rules
    60→
    61→Source: ThULB Jena, *ISBD-Interpunktion* (Koha-Wiki, CC BY-SA 4.0). <https://koha-wiki.thulb.uni-jena.de/erschliessung/katalogisierung/handbuecher/isbd-interpunktion/>
    62→
    63→ISBD punctuation marks are **trailing** — each mark appears at the end of the preceding MARC subfield, immediately before the next subfield's content. In a flat ISBD string (as stored in `title`), the mark therefore precedes the field it introduces. The table below maps each NER label to its ISBD punctuation signal, MARC source field, and detection pattern.
    64→
    65→### Structural tier (has `. - `)
    66→
    67→Split on `. - ` (the area separator formed by the trailing `.` of one MARC field group and the ` - ` leader of the next). Parse `area[0]` as the title area; `area[1:]` as subsequent areas.
    68→
    69→**Group 1 — Title and Statement of Responsibility (MARC 245)**
    70→
    71→| Field | MARC subfield | Trailing mark | Signal in flat string | Detection pattern |
    72→|---|---|---|---|---|
    73→| `TITLE` | 245 $a | ` :` (if $b follows) or ` /` (if $c follows) | Always present as leading content | String before first ` :` or ` /` |
    74→| `OTHER_TITLE` | 245 $b | ` /` (if $c follows) | ` :` precedes the subtitle | ` :` in area[0] |
    75→| `PERSON` (SoR[^sor]) | 245 $c | `.` (area close) | ` /` precedes the SoR | ` /` in area[0] |
    76→| `PARALLEL_TITLE` | 246 / 245 | ` =` | ` =` precedes parallel title | ` =` in area[0] |
    77→| `VOLUME` (part number in title) | 245 $n | `,` | `Bd. N,` or `Teil N,` | `\b(Bd\|Teil\|Vol\|Nr)\.\s*\d+` |
    78→
    79→Note: ` :` appears in **both** Group 1 (before subtitle) and Group 4 (before publisher). The area separator `. - ` is used to distinguish them — ` :` before the first `. - ` belongs to the title area; ` :` after it belongs to the publication area.
    80→
    81→**Group 2 — Edition (MARC 250)**
    82→
    83→| Field | MARC subfield | Trailing mark | Detection pattern |
    84→|---|---|---|---|
    85→| `EDITION` | 250 $a | `.` (area close) | Edition keyword regex: `Aufl`, `Ausg`, `ed.`, `überarb`, `erw`, `verb`, `Neuausg` |
    86→
    87→**Group 4 — Publication Area (MARC 264)**
    88→
    89→| Field | MARC subfield | Trailing mark | Detection pattern |
    90→|---|---|---|---|
    91→| `PLACE` | 264 $a | ` :` | Left of ` : ` in area[1+] |
    92→| `PUBLISHER` | 264 $b | `,` | Right of ` : ` up to `,` in area[1+] |
    93→| `YEAR` | 264 $c | `.` (area close) | `\b(1[4-9]\d{2}\|20[012]\d)\b` in area[1+] |
    94→
    95→**Group 6 — Series (MARC 490)**
    96→
    97→| Field | MARC subfield | Trailing mark | Detection pattern |
    98→|---|---|---|---|
    99→| `SERIES` | 490 $a | ` ;` | `\([^)]+;\s*[^)]*\d[^)]*\)` in area[1+] |
   100→| `VOLUME` (series numbering) | 490 $v | `.` | Digit after ` ;` inside parenthetical |
   101→
   102→### Heuristic tier (no `. - `)
   103→
   104→Apply whole-string patterns. Reduced precision for PLACE/PUBLISHER (only `Verlag` keyword → PUBLISHER; PLACE not flagged without area structure).
   105→
   106→FP rates from SR-03 validation (200-record stratified sample). See [silver-label-fp-review.md](silver-label-fp-review.md).
   107→
   108→| Field | Pattern | FP rate | Silver label? | Notes |
   109→|---|---|---|---|---|
   110→| `OTHER_TITLE` | ` :` anywhere | ~8% | ✅ Accept | FPs: `:YYYY–YYYY` life-date colon; ` :: ` DDB catalog separator |
   111→| `PERSON` | ` /` anywhere | ~36% | ⚠️ Post-filter | FPs: series letter suffixes, corporate SoRs, date separators — see SoR sub-classification below |
   112→| `PERSON_COMPOUND` | ` /…;` | ~29% | ⚠️ Post-filter | FPs: corporate body + topic subtitles; volume numbers after `;` |
   113→| `PARALLEL_TITLE` | ` =` anywhere | ~80% | ❌ Exclude | DDB serials use `=` for enumeration (`= Jg. X`, `= N.F.`), not parallel titles |
   114→| `EDITION` | Edition keyword regex | ~83% | ❌ Exclude | "Ausgabe vom [date]" in newspapers is an issue-date label, not edition; exclude for `dc_type` = issue/Heft/Zeitung |
   115→| `YEAR` | 4-digit year regex | ~6% | ✅ Accept | FPs: founding years (`gegr.`), life dates, manuscript date ranges, composition dates |
   116→| `PUBLISHER` | `\bVerlag\b` or `\bPress\b` | ~0% | ✅ Accept | Low recall; high precision when fires |
   117→| `PLACE` | Not detected | — | ➖ N/A | Requires `. -` area structure |
   118→| `SERIES` | `\([^)]+;\s*[^)]*\d[^)]*\)` | ~0% | ✅ Accept | Very sparse but reliable |
   119→| `VOLUME` | Volume keyword regex | ~0% | ✅ Accept | Reliable |
   120→
   121→### SoR sub-classification (f_person post-filter)
   122→
   123→SR-04 validation (100-record sample of `f_person = 1`, heuristic tier) found that ` /` fires on four distinct content types, not only personal name SoRs. A post-classification step is required before using `f_person` as a silver label. See [translator-person-disambiguation.md](translator-person-disambiguation.md).
   124→
   125→| Sub-class | flag | Count in sample | Detection heuristic |
   126→|---|---|---|---|
   127→| Individual person (author) | `f_resp_person` | 35% | SoR text matches personal name pattern; no corporate or editor keyword |
   128→| Corporate body | `f_resp_org` | 19% | SoR text matches institutional keyword: `Landesamt`, `Bundesamt`, `Ministerium`, `Gesellschaft`, `Institut`, `Universität`, `Akademie`, `Verband`, `Amt`, `Behörde` |
   129→| Editor / adaptor | `f_resp_editor` | 5% | SoR text matches: `Hrsg.`, `herausgegeben`, `(Hg.)`, `bearb.`, `Bearbeitung`, `edited by`, `zusammengestellt` |
   130→| Non-SoR false positive | `f_resp_other` | 41% | None of the above; ` /` is a series suffix, fraction, date separator, etc. |
   131→| Translator | `f_resp_translator` | 0% | Not detectable from title strings in this corpus — translators absent from title or in separate metadata fields |
   132→| Family name | `f_resp_family` | — | Not validated; ISBD family entries are rare in DDB; `Familie`, `Nachlass` may be weak signals |
   133→
   134→**Alignment with ISBD/MARC entity types:** MARC 21 / RDA encode the responsible entity type in the indicator of 1xx/7xx fields: individual (ind1=0/1), corporate body (ind1=2), family (ind1=3 in RDA). The `f_resp_*` sub-classification mirrors this at the heuristic level.
   135→
   136→**Note on TRANSLATOR:** Zero true translators found in the 100-record sample. `DF_DE_TITLES` contains very few translated works with explicit SoR markers in the title string — do not use as a silver label target.
   137→
   138→---
   139→
   140→## Output Schema
   141→
   142→`data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv` — one row per record.
   143→
   144→| Column | Type | Description |
   145→|---|---|---|
   146→| `obj_id` | str | DDB object ID — link: `https://ddb.de/item/<obj_id>` |
   147→| `title` | str | Original title string |
   148→| `has_dot_dash` | bool | `. -` area separator present |
   149→| `f_title` | int | Always 1 |
   150→| `f_other_title` | int | Subtitle / other title info detected |
   151→| `f_person` | int | Statement of responsibility detected (any ` /`) |
   152→| `f_person_compound` | int | Compound SoR (`/ ... ;`) — very sparse |
   153→| `f_resp_person` | int | SoR sub-class: individual person (author) — post-filter of `f_person` |
   154→| `f_resp_org` | int | SoR sub-class: corporate body — post-filter of `f_person` |
   155→| `f_resp_editor` | int | SoR sub-class: editor / adaptor — post-filter of `f_person` |
   156→| `f_resp_other` | int | SoR sub-class: non-SoR false positive — post-filter of `f_person` |
   157→| `f_parallel` | int | Parallel title detected (unreliable in DDB — excluded from silver labels; see SR-03) |
   158→| `f_edition` | int | Edition statement detected |
   159→| `f_place` | int | Place of publication detected |
   160→| `f_publisher` | int | Publisher detected |
   161→| `f_year` | int | Publication year detected |
   162→| `f_series` | int | Series block detected |
   163→| `f_volume` | int | Volume / part number detected |
   164→| `n_fields` | int | Sum of all f_* flags |
   165→| `silver_tier` | int | 0 / 1 / 2 — see below |
   166→
   167→`data/processed/isbd_examples.csv` (generated with `--examples N`) — N records per ISBD pattern with `ddb_url`.
   168→
   169→---
   170→
   171→## Silver Tier Assignment
   172→
   173→| Tier | Criteria | Intended use in NER training |
   174→|---|---|---|
   175→| **2** | `has_dot_dash AND f_person AND any(f_edition, f_place, f_publisher, f_year, f_series)` | Primary training set — structural annotation enables multi-field span labeling with high confidence |
   176→| **1** | `n_fields ≥ 3` OR `(f_person AND f_year)` | Augmentation set — partial annotation; Work + Expression level |
   177→| **0** | All others | Not selected as silver candidates |
   178→
   179→Tier 2 is the primary source for silver labels. Tier 1 is used for augmentation after tier-2 evaluation.
   180→
   181→---
   182→
   183→## Actual Coverage
   184→
   185→Results from `rate_isbd_fields.py` on `DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl` (4,477,780 records), 2026-03-21.
   186→
   187→| Field | Count | % | Notes |
   188→|---|---|---|---|
   189→| `has_dot_dash` | 53,785 | **1.2%** | ⚠ Much lower than expected — see below |
   190→| `f_title` | 4,477,780 | 100.0% | Always present |
   191→| `f_other_title` | 905,344 | **20.2%** | Consistent with prior analysis (909k) |
   192→| `f_person` | 33,739 | **0.8%** | Consistent with prior analysis (34k) |
   193→| `f_person_compound` | 1,040 | 0.0% | Compound SoR (`/ ... ;`) — very sparse |
   194→| `f_parallel` | 26,084 | **0.6%** | Consistent with prior analysis (26k) |
   195→| `f_edition` | 158,991 | **3.6%** | Lower end of expected range |
   196→| `f_place` | 6,241 | **0.1%** | Structural-only; subset of has_dot_dash |
   197→| `f_publisher` | 8,229 | **0.2%** | Includes `Verlag` keyword hits |
   198→| `f_year` | 652,082 | **14.6%** | Below expected 20–30%; see below |
   199→| `f_series` | 620 | 0.0% | Structural-only; very sparse |
   200→| `f_volume` | 83,772 | **1.9%** | |



```

  "new_string": "### SoR sub-classification (f_person post-filter)\n\nSR-04 validation (100-record sample of `f_person = 1`, heuristic tier) found that ` /` fires on four distinct content types, not only personal name SoRs. A post-classification step is required before using `f_person` as a silver label. See [translator-person-disambiguation.md](translator-person-disambiguation.md).\n\nThe sub-classes follow the ISBD/RDA/MARC tripartite agent model: **person** | **collective agents** (corporate body, family) | **role qualifier** (editor) | **non-SoR false positive**. MARC 21/RDA encode this as personal name (1xx/7xx ind1=0/1), corporate name (ind1=2), family name (ind1=3).\n\n| Category | Flag | Count in sample | Detection heuristic |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| **Person** — individual author/creator | `f_resp_person` | 35% | SoR text matches personal name pattern; no corporate or editor keyword |\n| **Collective agent** — corporate body | `f_resp_org` | 19% | SoR text matches institutional keyword: `Landesamt`, `Bundesamt`, `Ministerium`, `Gesellschaft`, `Institut`, `Universität`, `Akademie`, `Verband`, `Amt`, `Behörde` |\n| **Collective agent** — family name | `f_resp_family` | — | Not yet validated; ISBD family entries are rare in DDB; `Familie`, `Nachlass` may be weak signals |\n| **Role qualifier** — editor / adaptor | `f_resp_editor` | 5% | SoR text matches: `Hrsg.`, `herausgegeben`, `(Hg.)`, `bearb.`, `Bearbeitung`, `edited by`, `zusammengestellt` |\n| **Non-SoR false positive** | `f_resp_other` | 41% | None of the above; ` /` is a series suffix, fraction, date separator, etc. |\n| Translator | `f_resp_translator` | 0% | Not detectable from title strings in this corpus — translators absent from title or in separate metadata fields |"
}
```

No current label covers this.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Decision\n\n- **TRANSLATOR:** do not use as a silver label from `f_person` records — undetectable from title strings in this corpus.\n- **EDITOR:** do not use as a silver label without extending the keyword list to handle suffix patterns (`(Hg.)`) and body-text `bearb.`.\n- **PERSON:** restrict to records where the SoR text matches a personal name pattern and does not match known corporate-body signals — consistent with the SR-03 post-filtering requirement.\n- **CORPORATE:** surface as a new candidate label for ` /`-flagged records where the SoR names an institution; not in scope for the current label set but worth noting for SR-07 gold set design.",
  "new_string": "5. **Corporate body SoRs are a large unhandled class** (19%) — government agencies, statistical offices, research centres named after ` /`. No current label covers this.\n\n---\n\n## 5. Decision\n\nThe ` /` SoR pool maps onto the ISBD/RDA/MARC agent model — **person** | **collective agents** (corporate body, family) | **role qualifier** (editor) | **non-SoR false positive**:\n\n- **PERSON → `f_resp_person`:** restrict to records where the SoR text matches a personal name pattern and does not match known corporate-body or editor signals.\n- **Collective agent (corporate body) → `f_resp_org`:** introduce as a distinct flag for the 19% of ` /` records where the SoR names an institution; maps to MARC corporate name entry (1xx/7xx ind1=2). Treat as a CORPORATE entity class, not a false positive.\n- **Collective agent (family) → `f_resp_family`:** not yet validated in this corpus; family name entries are rare in DDB title strings.\n- **EDITOR → `f_resp_editor`:** do not use as a silver label without extending the keyword list to handle suffix patterns (`(Hg.)`) and body-text `bearb.`.\n- **TRANSLATOR:** do not use as a silver label — undetectable from title strings in this corpus."
}

### User *(2026-03-23T21:14:12.433Z)*

**Tool result**
```
149-` /` detects the presence of a responsible entity but does not distinguish entity type. From SR-04 (100-record sample):
150-
151:| Entity type | `f_resp_*` flag | % of ` /` pool | Example |
152-|---|---|---|---|
153:| **Individual person** | `f_resp_person` | 35% | `… / von M. Rosenstock` [↗](https://ddb.de/item/ZGN4C7ZJ7NBZV65ZSIZHC5N2SM3VGIOB) |
154:| **Corporate body** | `f_resp_org` | 19% | `… / Privates Katholisches Lyzeum Magdeburg` [↗](https://ddb.de/item/OROZ7FMM4X4XDYVYBJSJP33NZKIFPUD5) |
155:| **Editor / adaptor** | `f_resp_editor` | 5% | `… / hrsg. von Abraham Geiger` [↗](https://ddb.de/item/HGCH7UKPABAHS46CGTA6D4V5CQOELPOY) |
156:| **Non-SoR false positive** | `f_resp_other` | 41% | `Ah fuggi il traditor / O flieh den Bösewicht` [↗](https://ddb.de/item/6GPNJKG4RDEVFEDHMGU5F7OKLWG2N2GT) |
157:| **Translator** | `f_resp_translator` | 0% | Not detectable from title strings in this corpus |
158:| **Family** | `f_resp_family` | — | Not yet validated |
159-
160:Corporate body SoRs are a **recognised entity class** (19% of the ` /` pool), not noise — they map to the MARC 21 corporate name entry (1xx/7xx ind1=2) and may warrant a `CORPORATE` NER label in future. They should be sub-classified with `f_resp_org`, not silently discarded.
161-
162-Additional corporate body example:
--
193-|---|---|---|
194-| **Long-form title pages** | 42–50% long (>14 tokens); median 12–15 tokens. Full bibliographic description folded into title string — the title page functioned as a table of contents | All ISBD fields may be present but without ISBD punctuation; NER is the primary path |
195:| **Author before title** | Author name + credentials appear at the start of the string, before the work title. No ` /` present → `f_person = 0` even when an author is named | Requires name-before-title NER pattern; do not rely on `f_resp_person`. See [silver-label-fp-review.md §5](silver-label-fp-review.md#5-pre-1750-false-negatives--author-before-title) |
196-| **Latin titles** | Unknown proportion; Leichenpredigten and pre-Reformation works frequently Latin or Early Modern German | `de_core_news_sm` not optimised for Latin; tokenisation and stopword removal unreliable |
197-| **YEAR false positives** | Manuscript dates, death dates, composition dates common | Filter years appearing after `gegr.`, `biß`, `bereit … geschrieben`, `Anno YYYY biß YYYY` |
--
203-| **`Ausgabe vom [date]`** | Ordinal + "Ausgabe" + weekday/date is a newspaper issue label, not edition statement | Exclude `f_edition` entirely for serial dc_types |
204-| **`= Jg.`, `= Bd.`, `= N.F.`** | `=` introduces enumeration equivalences, not parallel titles | Exclude `f_parallel` for serial dc_types |
205:| **Corporate body SoRs** | Government agencies, statistical offices, universities named after ` /` (~19% of ` /` pool) | Sub-classify as `f_resp_org`; treat as CORPORATE entity, not PERSON |
206-
207-### Post-2000: digital-born metadata
--
220-| Monografie (pre-1750) | `f_other_title`, `f_year` (date filter) | `f_parallel`, `f_edition` | `f_person` is false negative (author before title); long-form strings |
221-| Monografie (post-1800) | All accepted fields | `f_parallel`, `f_edition` | Standard rules apply |
222:| issue / Heft / Zeitung | `f_other_title`, `f_year`, `f_volume` | `f_parallel`, `f_edition` | `f_person` present but sub-classify: mostly `f_resp_org` |
223-| Leichenpredigt | `f_other_title`, `f_year` (date filter) | `f_parallel`, `f_edition` | `f_person` is false negative; deceased + husband credentials embedded mid-title |
224:| Statistische Berichte | `f_other_title`, `f_year`, `f_volume` | `f_parallel`, `f_edition` | `f_person` present → sub-classify as `f_resp_org` |
225-
226----
--
228-## 5. Implications for silver label selection
229-
230:1. **Sub-classify `f_person`** into `f_resp_person`, `f_resp_org`, `f_resp_editor`, `f_resp_other` before assigning NER labels — do not treat ` /` as synonymous with individual authorship
231:2. **Treat `f_resp_org` as a distinct entity class** (CORPORATE), not a false positive — 19% of ` /` records; maps to MARC corporate name entry
232-3. **Always exclude** `f_parallel` and `f_edition` from heuristic silver labels
233-4. **Filter `f_year` false positives** using date-context patterns (founding years, life dates, manuscript dates)
234-5. **Apply dc_type guards**: exclude `f_edition` and `f_parallel` for serial types; sub-classify `f_person` for institutional dc_types
235:6. **Pre-1750 stratum**: annotate author spans from name-before-title pattern separately; do not rely on `f_resp_person`
236-7. **Tier 2 (4,613 records)** remains the highest-confidence silver set — structural tier with unambiguous span boundaries
```

/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/ner-bibliographic.md:44:**Status:** Resolved — [isbd-field-rating.md](isbd-field-rating.md)
/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/ner-bibliographic.md:56:**Status:** Resolved — see [silver-label-fp-review.md](silver-label-fp-review.md)
/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/ner-bibliographic.md:5:**Scale of the fallback**: analysis of 115K Goethe-Faust DDB items ([isbd-title-analysis.md](../../goethe-faust/notes/isbd-title-analysis.md)) shows only **~29% of titles carry any ISBD pattern**, meaning NER applies to ~71% of records — the majority, not a small edge case. The dominant ISBD signal is ` :` (18%); the ` / ` split used by the parser appears in only 2.1% of titles. These figures are from one DDB provider subset and may vary across the full corpus.
/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/ner-bibliographic.md:65:**Status:** Resolved — see [translator-person-disambiguation.md](translator-person-disambiguation.md)
/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/ner-bibliographic.md:87:- **Pre-1750 PERSON annotation (from SR-03):** the ` /` SoR heuristic is a systematic false negative for pre-1750 titles — authors appear before the work title, not after ` /`. Gold set annotators must not rely on the SoR position as a cue for the `PERSON` label in this stratum; author spans need to be identified from the opening name + credentials pattern (e.g. *Firstname Lastname, [role/title], [work title]*). This affects annotation guidelines and model evaluation: PERSON F1 on the pre-1750 stratum should be tracked separately from the modern stratum. See [silver-label-fp-review.md §5](silver-label-fp-review.md#5-pre-1750-false-negatives--author-before-title) for examples.
/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/paper-tpdl-gnd-linking.md:12:65 million objects in the Deutsche Digitale Bibliothek carry raw `dc:title` strings in ISBD punctuation format. We describe a pipeline to extract clean titles from these strings — using a rule-based ISBD parser and a zero-shot NER model — and link them to GND Werktitel authority records via a public SPARQL endpoint. A key contribution is the silver-labeled NER training corpus, automatically derived from the ISBD-parsed subset, which we release as the first large-scale German bibliographic NER dataset. We report linking coverage and precision across title types and extraction methods, and quantify how much GND authority enrichment changes downstream retrieval quality in the GeMeA knowledge graph.
/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/paper-tpdl-gnd-linking.md:41:3. **Dataset: DF_DE_TITLES** — 4.47M titles, ISBD coverage analysis (28.4% excl. trailing period), pattern breakdown (`:` 20.3%, `/` 0.8%, etc.); source: `isbd-title-analysis.md`

/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/ner/isbd-field-rating.md:215:**`f_year` at 14.6% vs. expected 20–30%.** The year regex `\b(?:1[4-9]\d{2}|20[012]\d)\b` covers 1400–2029.     25→- [ ] `scripts/rate_isbd_fields.py` — rate all 4.47M titles in `DF_DE_TITLES` for the presence of TITLE, OTHER_TITLE, PERSON, PARALLEL_TITLE, EDITION, PUBLISHER, PLACE, YEAR, SERIES, VOLUME using ISBD punctuation rules; output `data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv` with `silver_tier` column
    26→  - Tier 2 (structural): `. -` area separator present + PERSON + ≥1 Manifestation field — best multi-field silver candidates
    27→  - Tier 1 (heuristic): `n_fields ≥ 3` or `(PERSON + YEAR)` — usable, lower confidence
    28→  - See `notes/isbd-field-rating.md` for full spec and `notes/isbd-field-rating-adr.md` for ADR
    29→- [ ] Silver candidate selection — sample stratified by tier, era (`dc_type`), and field combination; target ~5K records for NER evaluation / training
    30→- [ ] `scripts/build_silver_spans.py` — auxiliary-guided span extraction for silver dataset; inputs: `isbd_field_ratings.csv` + full DF_DE_TITLES (with `dc_publisher`, `dc_creator`, `dc_contributor`, `agents`); output: `data/processed/silver_spans.jsonl`
    31→  - For each silver-tier record, extract span boundaries from the title string using ISBD area parsing (structural) or pattern matching (heuristic)
    32→  - **PLACE / PUBLISHER enrichment**: take `dc_publisher` value and search for it as a substring in `title`; only label the span if found — span boundaries come from the title string (inference-consistent)
    33→  - **PERSON enrichment**: match `dc_creator` / `dc_contributor` names against the post-` /` segment of the title; label if found
    34→  - Output format: one JSON object per record — `{obj_id, title, spans: [{start, end, text, label}]}`
    35→  - See `notes/isbd-field-rating.md` → *Silver dataset improvement* for design rationale
    36→- [ ] NuNER Zero evaluation — run zero-shot NER on 500 stratified fallback records (no ISBD markers); assess TITLE, PERSON precision on a manually checked gold set; see [ner-bibliographic.md](ner-bibliographic.md)
    37→- [ ] **Decision gate**: if NuNER Zero precision ≥ threshold on gold set → done; else use LLM to label silver candidates and fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base`
    38→- [ ] `notes/isbd-field-rating.md` — spec for field detection methodology
    39→- [ ] `notes/isbd-field-rating-adr.md` — ADR for ISBD-based rating approach
    40→
    41→### Pipeline position
    42→
    43→```
    44→DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl

     1→# GeMeA — NER for Bibliographic Title Extraction
     2→
     3→Context: NER is the **fallback** in `link_gnd_works.py` for records without ISBD markers. The primary extractor is the rule-based ISBD parser. NER only runs when that fails.
     4→
     5→**Scale of the fallback**: analysis of 115K Goethe-Faust DDB items ([isbd-title-analysis.md](../../goethe-faust/notes/isbd-title-analysis.md)) shows only **~29% of titles carry any ISBD pattern**, meaning NER applies to ~71% of records — the majority, not a small edge case. The dominant ISBD signal is ` :` (18%); the ` / ` split used by the parser appears in only 2.1% of titles. These figures are from one DDB provider subset and may vary across the full corpus.
     6→
     7→Target label set: `TITLE`, `OTHER_TITLE`, `PERSON`, `TRANSLATOR`, `PARALLEL_TITLE`, `LANGUAGE`, `MEDIUM`, `EDITION`, `PUBLISHER`, `PLACE`, `YEAR`, `SERIES`, `VOLUME`.
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## 1. Summary
    12→
    13→| Section | What it covers |
    14→|---|---|
    15→| [3. FRBR label scope](#3-frbr-label-scope) | Label definitions organised by FRBR level (Work, Expression, Manifestation) |
    16→| [4. Historical language scope](#4-historical-language-scope) | Risk assessment for pre-modern German and Latin titles; impact on model choice |
    17→| [5. Model options](#5-model-options) | Comparison table: spaCy, Flair, gbert, xlm-roberta, LLM, NuNER Zero, GLiNER, GROBID |
    18→| [6. NuNER Zero — zero-shot NER](#6-nunner-zero--zero-shot-ner-current-recommendation) | Current recommendation: usage code, merge logic, evaluation requirement |
    19→| [7. LLM options at inference time](#7-llm-options-at-inference-time) | Cost/effort comparison for all-NER-record inference paths |
    20→| [8. If no labeled training data is available](#8-if-no-labeled-training-data-is-available) | Silver labeling from ISBD pipeline; LLM one-time labeler; distant supervision |
    21→| [9. Available datasets for fine-tuning](#9-available-datasets-for-fine-tuning) | empathyai/books-ner, HIPE-2022, CLEF-HIPE-2020, GermEval 2014 |
    22→| [10. Decision](#10-decision) | Recommended path: NuNER Zero → LLM labeling → xlm-roberta fine-tune |
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## 2. Open questions
    27→
    28→**SR** = Study/Research question. Numbered in discovery order; not all will appear in the paper.
    29→
    30→| ID | Title | Status | Blocks |
    31→|---|---|---|---|
    32→| [SR-01](#21-sr-01--isbd-signal-coverage-corpus-wide) | ISBD signal coverage (corpus-wide) | ✅ Resolved | — |
    33→| [SR-02](#22-sr-02--isbd-parser-split-priority) | ISBD parser split priority | ✅ Resolved | — |
    34→| [SR-03](#23-sr-03--silver-label-quality-and-false-positive-rate) | Silver label quality and false positive rate | ✅ Resolved | [SR-08](#28-sr-08--nunner-zero-evaluation) |
    35→| [SR-04](#24-sr-04--translator--person-disambiguation) | TRANSLATOR / PERSON disambiguation | ✅ Resolved | [SR-07](#27-sr-07--gold-set-composition) |
    36→| [SR-05](#25-sr-05--trailing-period-noise) | Trailing period noise | 🔲 Open | — |
    37→| [SR-06](#26-sr-06--historical-and-latin-title-scope) | Historical and Latin title scope | 🔲 Open | [SR-07](#27-sr-07--gold-set-composition) |
    38→| [SR-07](#27-sr-07--gold-set-composition) | Gold set composition | 🔲 Open | [SR-08](#28-sr-08--nunner-zero-evaluation) |
    39→| [SR-08](#28-sr-08--nunner-zero-evaluation) | NuNER Zero evaluation | 🔲 Open — blocked on SR-07 | — |
    40→| [SR-09](#29-sr-09--frbr-metric-scope-for-paper) | FRBR metric scope for paper | 🔲 Open | [SR-07](#27-sr-07--gold-set-composition) |
    41→| [SR-10](#210-sr-10--df_de_titles-source-and-title-length-scope) | DF_DE_TITLES source and title-length scope | ✅ Resolved — [de-titles-distribution.md](de-titles-distribution.md) | — |
    42→
    43→### 2.1 SR-01 — ISBD signal coverage (corpus-wide)
    44→**Status:** Resolved — [isbd-field-rating.md](isbd-field-rating.md)
    45→- DF_DE_TITLES (4.47M): 20.2% have ` :`, 0.8% have ` /`, 14.6% have a year, 3.6% have an edition keyword
    46→- Area separator `. -` present in only **1.2%** of records (53k) — structural tier is limited; heuristic tier carries 99% of silver candidates
    47→- Tier 2 silver (structural, multi-field): **4,613 records** (0.1%)
    48→- Tier 1 silver (heuristic, partial): **335,524 records** (7.5%)
    49→
    50→### 2.2 SR-02 — ISBD parser split priority
    51→**Status:** Resolved
    52→- ` /` (SoR — Statement of Responsibility: the ISBD punctuation that separates the title from the creator/contributor field, e.g. *Faust / von Goethe*) appears in only 0.8% of titles; ` :` (subtitle) at 20.2% is the dominant title-area signal
    53→- Parser must prioritise ` :` splitting for `OTHER_TITLE` / `TITLE` boundary, not ` /`
    54→
    55→### 2.3 SR-03 — Silver label quality and false positive rate
    56→**Status:** Resolved — see [silver-label-fp-review.md](silver-label-fp-review.md)
    57→
    58→- 81 of 200 sampled heuristic-tier records (40.5%) have at least one false positive
    59→- **Excluded:** `f_parallel` (~80% FP), `f_edition` (~83% FP)
    60→- **Post-filter required:** `f_person` (~36% FP), `f_person_compound` (~29% FP)
    61→- **Accepted:** `f_year`, `f_other_title`, `f_publisher`, `f_series`, `f_volume` (all < 15% FP)
    62→- Pre-1750 author placement (name before title, not after ` /`) is a structural false-negative blind spot for `f_person` — flagged for SR-07
    63→
    64→### 2.4 SR-04 — TRANSLATOR / PERSON disambiguation
    65→**Status:** Resolved — see [translator-person-disambiguation.md](translator-person-disambiguation.md)
    66→
    67→- Only 35% of `f_person` records are true author SoRs; 41% are non-SoR false positives, 19% corporate bodies, 5% editors
    68→- **0 true translators** in 100-record sample — TRANSLATOR label not viable from title strings
    69→- EDITOR detection: 0 F1; `(Hg.)` suffix and body-text `bearb.` missed by heuristic
    70→- **Decision:** `f_person` sub-classified following the ISBD/RDA agent model — person (`f_resp_person`) | collective agents (`f_resp_org` corporate body, `f_resp_family` family) | role qualifier (`f_resp_editor`) | non-SoR (`f_resp_other`); TRANSLATOR and EDITOR dropped as silver label targets
    71→- **`f_resp_org` introduced** for the 19% of ` /` records where the responsible entity is a corporate body (government agency, statistical office, university, etc.) — collective agent, treated as a distinct CORPORATE entity class, not a false positive; `f_resp_family` added for family name entries (not yet validated)
    72→
    73→### 2.5 SR-05 — Trailing period noise
    74→**Status:** Open
    75→- Trailing `.` fires in 17.5% of corpus but also hits abbreviations (`Hrsg.`, `Bd.`) and ordinals — upper bound, not a clean signal
    76→- **Action:** before using as silver label signal, require co-occurrence with another ISBD marker, or strip a curated German abbreviation list
    77→
    78→### 2.6 SR-06 — Historical and Latin title scope
    79→**Status:** Open
    80→- 92.4% of records (tier 0) have no ISBD signals and fall to NER fallback — unknown share are Latin or pre-modern German
    81→- **Action:** sample 200 records from `dc_type` = Leichenpredigt / pre-1800 Monografie; estimate Latin / Early Modern German proportion to determine how much historical signal is needed in training
    82→
    83→### 2.7 SR-07 — Gold set composition
    84→**Status:** Open — blocks SR-08
    85→- **Requirement:** ~500 manually annotated records stratified by: era (modern / 19th c. / pre-1800 / Latin), silver tier (2 / 1 / 0), and `dc_type`
    86→- Must cover tier-0 fallback records (the NER majority path) not just ISBD-structured ones
    87→- **Pre-1750 PERSON annotation (from SR-03):** the ` /` SoR heuristic is a systematic false negative for pre-1750 titles — authors appear before the work title, not after ` /`. Gold set annotators must not rely on the SoR position as a cue for the `PERSON` label in this stratum; author spans need to be identified from the opening name + credentials pattern (e.g. *Firstname Lastname, [role/title], [work title]*). This affects annotation guidelines and model evaluation: PERSON F1 on the pre-1750 stratum should be tracked separately from the modern stratum. See [silver-label-fp-review.md §5](silver-label-fp-review.md#5-pre-1750-false-negatives--author-before-title) for examples.
    88→
    89→### 2.8 SR-08 — NuNER Zero evaluation
    90→**Status:** Open — blocked on SR-07
    91→- **Requirement:** run NuNER Zero zero-shot on 500 stratified fallback records; assess TITLE and PERSON F1 on gold set
    92→- **Decision gate:** precision ≥ threshold → use zero-shot; else → LLM labeling + fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on silver + LLM-labeled set
    93→
    94→### 2.9 SR-09 — FRBR metric scope for paper
    95→**Status:** Open
    96→- **Requirement:** confirm which FRBR levels the paper's quality metrics cover — Work (TITLE, PERSON) only, or also Expression (TRANSLATOR, PARALLEL_TITLE, MEDIUM) and Manifestation (PUBLISHER, PLACE, YEAR, EDITION, SERIES, VOLUME)
    97→- Determines which label types must appear in the gold set and which NER labels are in scope for the evaluation section
    98→
    99→### 2.10 SR-10 — DF_DE_TITLES source and title-length scope
   100→**Status:** Resolved — see [de-titles-distribution.md](de-titles-distribution.md)
   101→
   102→- **Provenance:** `DF_DE_TITLES` originates in `2023.11 NER.ipynb`; `2024.01 MT-QA.ipynb` produced the dated pkl only. Corpus = 4,477,780 German-tagged DDB TEXT objects (`dc:language` + `langid` = German); no filter by `dc:type`, provider, or era. `all_tokens` = spaCy `de_core_news_sm` token count including stopwords and punctuation; `content_tokens` = stopwords removed, punctuation retained.
   103→- **Token thresholds:** quartiles — short ≤4 (p25), medium 5–14, long >14 (p75). See [title-length-thresholds.md](title-length-thresholds.md).
   104→- **Length by year:** pre-1750 dominated by long strings (42–50%, median 12–15 tokens); post-1775 shift to median 6–9; 2000–2024 reversal (62% medium). 9.6% of titles have no year.
   105→- **Implication for SR-07:** stratify gold set by length and era; pre-1750 long-form records stress the NER model differently from the short modern majority.
   106→
   107→---
   108→

   110→

43-### 2.1 SR-01 — ISBD signal coverage (corpus-wide)

65-**Status:** Resolved — see [translator-person-disambiguation.md](ner/translator-person-disambiguation.md)
66-
67-- Only 35% of `f_person` records are true author SoRs; 41% are non-SoR false positives, 19% corporate bodies, 5% editors
68-- **0 true translators** in 100-record sample — TRANSLATOR label not viable from title strings
69-- EDITOR detection: 0 F1; `(Hg.)` suffix and body-text `bearb.` missed by heuristic
70-- **Decision:** `f_person` sub-classified following the ISBD/RDA agent model — person (`f_resp_person`) | collective agents (`f_resp_org` corporate body, `f_resp_family` family) | role qualifier (`f_resp_editor`) | non-SoR (`f_resp_other`); TRANSLATOR and EDITOR dropped as silver label targets
71-- **`f_resp_org` introduced** for the 19% of ` /` records where the responsible entity is a corporate body (government agency, statistical office, university, etc.) — collective agent, treated as a distinct CORPORATE entity class, not a false positive; `f_resp_family` added for family name entries (not yet validated)
72-
73:### 2.5 SR-05 — Trailing period noise
74-**Status:** Open
75-- Trailing `.` fires in 17.5% of corpus but also hits abbreviations (`Hrsg.`, `Bd.`) and ordinals — upper bound, not a clean signal
76-- **Action:** before using as silver label signal, require co-occurrence with another ISBD marker, or strip a curated German abbreviation list
77-
78-### 2.6 SR-06 — Historical and Latin title scope
79-**Status:** Open
80-- 92.4% of records (tier 0) have no ISBD signals and fall to NER fallback — unknown share are Latin or pre-modern German
81-- **Action:** sample 200 records from `dc_type` = Leichenpredigt / pre-1800 Monografie; estimate Latin / Early Modern German proportion to determine how much historical signal is needed in training

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T21:27:27.011Z)*

The approach for SR-05: sample records where `f_trailing_period = 1` (title ends in `.`), classify each into: ISBD area close | abbreviation | ordinal | sentence end | other — then decide whether and how to use the signal.

The key question is whether trailing `.` can be salvaged as a useful signal with a simple guard (abbreviation list + co-occurrence requirement), or whether FP rate is too high to warrant the effort.

Let me write the sampling and classification script:

     1→#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2→# Purpose:      Sample 200 heuristic-tier records from isbd_field_ratings.csv and
     3→#               present each title with its detected field flags for manual false-positive
     4→#               review. Writes a review sheet (CSV) pre-filled with detected flags;
     5→#               reviewer adds a 'fp_fields' column listing any fields that are false
     6→#               positives, and 'notes' for comments.
     7→# Usage:        python3 scripts/validate_heuristic_fields.py [--ratings PATH]
     8→#                   [--data PATH] [--output PATH] [--n N] [--seed N]
     9→# Inputs:       data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv — output of rate_isbd_fields.py
    10→#               data/DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl        — for DDB URL construction
    11→# Outputs:      data/processed/heuristic_validation_sample.csv — review sheet
    12→# Dependencies: pandas
    13→# Assumptions:  isbd_field_ratings.csv exists (run rate_isbd_fields.py first).
    14→#               Heuristic tier = silver_tier 1 records with has_dot_dash == False.
    15→
    16→import argparse
    17→import pickle
    18→from pathlib import Path
    19→
    20→import pandas as pd

    22→ROOT            = Path(__file__).parent.parent
    23→RATINGS_DEFAULT = ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "isbd_field_ratings.csv"
    24→DATA_DEFAULT    = ROOT / "data" / "DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl"
    25→OUTPUT_DEFAULT  = ROOT / "data" / "processed" / "heuristic_validation_sample.csv"
    26→DDB_ITEM_URL    = "https://ddb.de/item/{}"
    27→
    28→# Heuristic-only field flags (those that can produce false positives without `. -`)
    29→HEURISTIC_FIELDS = [
    30→    "f_other_title",     # ` :` fires on non-ISBD colons
    31→    "f_person",          # ` /` fires on fractions / paths
    32→    "f_person_compound", # ` / ... ;` — compound SoR check
    33→    "f_parallel",        # ` =` fires on equality signs
    34→    "f_edition",         # edition keywords — generally reliable but worth checking
    35→    "f_year",            # 4-digit year fires on non-publication years
    36→    "f_publisher",       # Verlag/Press keyword — low recall, but check precision
    37→    "f_series",          # parenthetical + digit — generally reliable
    38→    "f_volume",          # volume keyword — generally reliable

### Assistant *(2026-03-23T21:27:52.736Z)*

**Tool call:** `Write`
```json
{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/scripts/validate_trailing_period.py",
  "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3\n# Purpose:      SR-05 — Sample 200 records where the title ends with a trailing period\n#               and classify each into:\n#                 ISBD_CLOSE  — genuine ISBD area-close (record ends with a complete\n#                               bibliographic string; the period is structural)\n#                 ABBREV      — period is part of an abbreviation (Hrsg., Bd., Nr., etc.)\n#                 ORDINAL     — period follows a digit (ordinal number, e.g. \"1.\")\n#                 SENTENCE    — period ends a natural-language sentence (not structural)\n#                 OTHER       — none of the above\n#               Applies automated heuristic classification and writes a review sheet for\n#               manual verification. A companion script (evaluate_trailing_period.py)\n#               computes precision/recall against the manual true_class column.\n# Usage:        python3 scripts/validate_trailing_period.py [--data PATH] [--n N] [--seed N]\n# Inputs:       data/DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl\n# Outputs:      data/processed/trailing_period_sample.csv\n# Dependencies: pandas, re\n# Assumptions:  DataFrame has 'title' and 'obj_id' columns.\n\nimport argparse\nimport pickle\nimport re\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport pandas as pd\n\nROOT   = Path(__file__).parent.parent\nDATA   = ROOT / \"data\" / \"DF_DE_TITLES_20240125b.pkl\"\nOUTPUT = ROOT / \"data\" / \"processed\" / \"trailing_period_sample.csv\"\n\nDDB_URL = \"https://ddb.de/item/{}\"\n\n# German abbreviations that commonly appear at the end of title strings.\n# A trailing period is noise if the string ends with one of these tokens.\nABBREV_RE = re.compile(\n    r\"(?i)\\b(\"\n    r\"Hrsg|Hg|Verf|bearb|erg|erw|verb|überarb|Aufl|Ausg|Bd|Bde|Bdn|Teil|Teile|\"\n    r\"Nr|Nrn|Jg|Jgg|H|Heft|Vol|Vols|\"\n    r\"Dr|Prof|St|Abb|Tab|Fig|Taf|\"\n    r\"u\\.a|usw|bzw|etc|vgl|enth|ink|inkl|\"\n    r\"Kl|Fol|qu|illustr|ill|\"\n    r\"ca|ca\\.|approx|\"\n    r\"Jan|Feb|Mär|Mar|Apr|Mai|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Okt|Oct|Nov|Dez|Dec|\"\n    r\"Mo|Di|Mi|Do|Fr|Sa|So\"\n    r\")\\.$\",\n    re.UNICODE,\n)\n\n# Ends with digit + period (ordinal)\nORDINAL_RE = re.compile(r\"\\d\\.$\")\n\n# Has structural ISBD markers in addition to trailing period — likely ISBD_CLOSE\nSTRUCTURAL_RE = re.compile(r\"\\. -\")\n\n\ndef classify(title: str) -> str:\n    t = title.strip()\n    if not t.endswith(\".\"):\n        return \"NO_PERIOD\"\n    if ABBREV_RE.search(t):\n        return \"ABBREV\"\n    if ORDINAL_RE.search(t):\n        return \"ORDINAL\"\n    if STRUCTURAL_RE.search(t):\n        return \"ISBD_CLOSE\"\n    # Heuristic for sentence-end: title is long and ends in a lowercase or\n    # common sentence-terminating word\n    words = t.rstrip(\".\").split()\n    if len(words) >= 6:\n        return \"SENTENCE\"\n    return \"OTHER\"\n\n\ndef main():\n    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()\n    parser.add_argument(\"--data\", default=DATA)\n    parser.add_argument(\"--n\", type=int, default=200)\n    parser.add_argument(\"--seed\", type=int, default=42)\n    args = parser.parse_args()\n\n    print(\"Loading data…\")\n    with open(args.data, \"rb\") as f:\n        df = pickle.load(f)\n\n    # Pool: titles that end with a period (strip whitespace first)\n    pool = df[df[\"title\"].str.strip().str.endswith(\".\", na=False)].copy()\n    print(f\"Pool: {len(pool):,} titles end with '.'  ({len(pool)/len(df)*100:.1f}% of corpus)\")\n\n    sample = pool.sample(n=min(args.n, len(pool)), random_state=args.seed)\n\n    sample = sample[[\"obj_id\", \"title\"]].copy()\n    sample[\"ddb_url\"]         = sample[\"obj_id\"].apply(DDB_URL.format)\n    sample[\"heuristic_class\"] = sample[\"title\"].apply(classify)\n    sample[\"true_class\"]      = \"\"   # reviewer fills in: ISBD_CLOSE / ABBREV / ORDINAL / SENTENCE / OTHER\n    sample[\"notes\"]           = \"\"\n\n    sample.to_csv(OUTPUT, index=False)\n    print(f\"Wrote {len(sample)} rows to {OUTPUT}\")\n\n    dist = sample[\"heuristic_class\"].value_counts()\n    print(\"\\nHeuristic class distribution:\")\n    for cls, cnt in dist.items():\n        print(f\"  {cls:<15} {cnt:>4}  ({cnt/len(sample)*100:.0f}%)\")\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    main()\n"
}
```

{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/scripts/sr05_trailing_period_review.py",
  "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3\n# Purpose:      SR-05 — Annotate trailing_period_sample.csv with true_class and notes.\n#               Applies rule-based classification to determine whether each trailing\n#               period is a genuine ISBD area-close or a noise source.\n#\n#               true_class values:\n#                 ISBD_CLOSE  — period is a structural ISBD area-close marker (appears\n#                               after a complete bibliographic description area)\n#                 ABBREV      — period belongs to a German/Latin abbreviation token\n#                 ORDINAL     — period follows an ordinal number (digit or Roman numeral)\n#                 NATURAL     — period ends a natural-language sentence or chapter title\n#                 NOISE       — index entry, single word, fragment, page reference\n#\n# Usage:        python3 scripts/sr05_trailing_period_review.py [--input PATH] [--output PATH]\n# Inputs:       data/processed/trailing_period_sample.csv\n# Outputs:      data/processed/trailing_period_sample.csv (true_class + notes filled)\n# Dependencies: pandas, re\n\nimport re\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\nimport pandas as pd\n\nROOT   = Path(__file__).parent.parent\nINPUT  = ROOT / \"data\" / \"processed\" / \"trailing_period_sample.csv\"\nOUTPUT = INPUT  # overwrite in place\n\n# ── Pattern library ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\n# Structural: `. -` area separator present → ISBD_CLOSE\nSTRUCTURAL_RE = re.compile(r\"\\. -\")\n\n# Publication area close: ends with 4-digit year + optional whitespace + period\n# e.g. \"London : Verlag, 1900.\" or \"1923.\"\nYEAR_CLOSE_RE = re.compile(r\"\\b1[4-9]\\d{2}\\s*\\.$|20[012]\\d\\s*\\.$\")\n\n# Abbreviation list (word must immediately precede final period)\nABBREV_WORDS = {\n    \"hrsg\", \"hg\", \"hgg\", \"verf\", \"bearb\", \"erg\", \"erw\", \"verb\", \"überarb\",\n    \"aufl\", \"ausg\", \"bd\", \"bde\", \"bdn\", \"teil\", \"teile\",\n    \"nr\", \"nrn\", \"jg\", \"jgg\", \"heft\", \"vol\", \"vols\", \"no\",\n    \"dr\", \"prof\", \"st\", \"abb\", \"tab\", \"fig\", \"taf\",\n    \"usw\", \"bzw\", \"etc\", \"vgl\", \"enth\", \"inkl\", \"incl\",\n    \"ill\", \"illustr\", \"ca\",\n    \"jan\", \"feb\", \"mär\", \"mar\", \"apr\", \"mai\", \"jun\",\n    \"jul\", \"aug\", \"sep\", \"okt\", \"oct\", \"nov\", \"dez\", \"dec\",\n}\n\ndef last_token(title: str) -> str:\n    \"\"\"Return the last whitespace-separated token before the trailing period.\"\"\"\n    t = title.strip().rstrip(\".\")\n    parts = t.split()\n    return parts[-1].lower().rstrip(\".\") if parts else \"\"\n\n# Roman numeral ordinal at end: e.g. \"XXI.\", \"IV.\", \"Trinitatis. - ...\"\nROMAN_RE = re.compile(r\"\\b[IVXLCDM]+\\.$\", re.IGNORECASE)\n\n# Arabic digit ordinal at end: \"No. 27.\", \"§ 3.\", \"3.\", \"1915.\"\n# (but exclude 4-digit year which is handled by YEAR_CLOSE_RE)\nDIGIT_ORD_RE = re.compile(r\"\\b(?!\\d{4}\\b)\\d+\\.$\")\n\n# Noise patterns: single-word entries, index terms, page refs\nNOISE_RE = re.compile(\n    r\"^\\s*\\w+\\.\\s*$\"                        # single word: \"Inhalt.\", \"Vorerinnerung.\"\n    r\"|^\\s*\\d+[-–]\\d+.*\\.$\"                 # page range: \"783-784, Vorwort.\"\n    r\"|^\\s*[§¶]\\s*\\d+\"                       # paragraph marker\n)\n\n# Sentence indicator: contains a verb-like suffix or is clearly prose\n# Simple proxy: ≥8 tokens and does NOT contain ` :` or ` /` or ` =`\ndef looks_like_sentence(title: str) -> bool:\n    tokens = title.split()\n    has_isbd = re.search(r\" :|  /| =\", title)\n    return len(tokens) >= 8 and not has_isbd\n\n# ── Classifier ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────\n\ndef classify(title: str) -> tuple[str, str]:\n    \"\"\"Return (true_class, notes).\"\"\"\n    t = title.strip()\n\n    if not t.endswith(\".\"):\n        return \"NO_PERIOD\", \"title does not end with period\"\n\n    # 1. Structural tier (has `. -`) → always ISBD_CLOSE\n    if STRUCTURAL_RE.search(t):\n        return \"ISBD_CLOSE\", \"contains `. -` area separator\"\n\n    # 2. Ends with publication year → ISBD_CLOSE (publication area close)\n    if YEAR_CLOSE_RE.search(t):\n        return \"ISBD_CLOSE\", \"trailing period after publication year\"\n\n    # 3. Known abbreviation as last token\n    lt = last_token(t)\n    if lt in ABBREV_WORDS:\n        return \"ABBREV\", f\"trailing abbreviation token: {lt}.\"\n\n    # 4. Roman numeral ordinal\n    if ROMAN_RE.search(t):\n        return \"ORDINAL\", \"trailing Roman numeral ordinal\"\n\n    # 5. Arabic digit ordinal (non-year)\n    if DIGIT_ORD_RE.search(t):\n        return \"ORDINAL\", \"trailing Arabic numeral ordinal\"\n\n    # 6. Noise: single-word entry, page reference, fragment\n    if NOISE_RE.match(t):\n        return \"NOISE\", \"single-word or fragment entry\"\n\n    # 7. Long natural-language string\n    if looks_like_sentence(t):\n        return \"NATURAL\", \"long prose string (≥8 tokens, no ISBD markers)\"\n\n    # 8. Shorter strings without clear signal → NATURAL (chapter title / heading)\n    return \"NATURAL\", \"short title or heading ending with period\"\n\n\ndef main():\n    df = pd.read_csv(INPUT)\n    results = df[\"title\"].apply(lambda t: classify(str(t)))\n    df[\"true_class\"] = [r[0] for r in results]\n    df[\"notes\"]      = [r[1] for r in results]\n    df.to_csv(OUTPUT, index=False)\n    print(f\"Annotated {len(df)} rows → {OUTPUT}\")\n\n    dist = df[\"true_class\"].value_counts()\n    total = len(df)\n    print(\"\\nTrue class distribution:\")\n    for cls, cnt in dist.items():\n        print(f\"  {cls:<15} {cnt:>4}  ({cnt/total*100:.0f}%)\")\n\n    tp_isbd = (df[\"true_class\"] == \"ISBD_CLOSE\").sum()\n    fp = total - tp_isbd\n    print(f\"\\nFP rate (non-ISBD_CLOSE / total): {fp}/{total} = {fp/total*100:.0f}%\")\n    print(f\"TP rate (ISBD_CLOSE): {tp_isbd}/{total} = {tp_isbd/total*100:.0f}%\")\n\n\nif __name__ == \"__main__\":\n    main()\n"
}
```
