    94→
    95→### 2.7 SR-07 — Gold set composition
    96→**Status:** Open — blocks SR-08
    97→- **Requirement:** ~500 manually annotated records stratified by: era (modern / 19th c. / 1700–1800 / pre-1700), silver tier (2 / 1 / 0), and `dc_type`
    98→- Must cover tier-0 fallback records (the NER majority path) not just ISBD-structured ones
    99→- **No Latin stratum (from SR-06):** true Latin prevalence is ~0.5% — too rare to stratify; identify manually if encountered. Pre-1700 stratum (Leichenpredigt + legal/administrative Monografie) is the historical register proxy.
   100→- **Pre-1750 PERSON annotation (from SR-03):** the ` /` SoR heuristic is a systematic false negative for pre-1750 titles — authors appear before the work title, not after ` /`. Gold set annotators must not rely on the SoR position as a cue for the `PERSON` label in this stratum; author spans need to be identified from the opening name + credentials pattern (e.g. *Firstname Lastname, [role/title], [work title]*). This affects annotation guidelines and model evaluation: PERSON F1 on the pre-1750 stratum should be tracked separately from the modern stratum. See [silver-label-fp-review.md §5](ner/sr03_silver-label-fp-review.md#5-pre-1750-false-negatives--author-before-title) for examples.
   101→
   102→### 2.8 SR-08 — NuNER Zero evaluation
   103→**Status:** Open — blocked on SR-07
   104→- **Requirement:** run NuNER Zero zero-shot on 500 stratified fallback records; assess TITLE and PERSON F1 on gold set
   105→- **Decision gate:** precision ≥ threshold → use zero-shot; else → LLM labeling + fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on silver + LLM-labeled set
   106→
   107→### 2.9 SR-09 — FRBR metric scope for paper
   108→**Status:** Open
   109→- **Requirement:** confirm which FRBR levels the paper's quality metrics cover — Work (TITLE, PERSON) only, or also Expression (TRANSLATOR, PARALLEL_TITLE, MEDIUM) and Manifestation (PUBLISHER, PLACE, YEAR, EDITION, SERIES, VOLUME)
   110→- Determines which label types must appear in the gold set and which NER labels are in scope for the evaluation section
   111→
   112→### 2.10 SR-10 — DF_DE_TITLES source and title-length scope
   113→**Status:** Resolved — see [de-titles-distribution.md](ner/sr10_de-titles-distribution.md)
   114→
   115→- **Provenance:** `DF_DE_TITLES` originates in `2023.11 NER.ipynb`; `2024.01 MT-QA.ipynb` produced the dated pkl only. Corpus = 4,477,780 German-tagged DDB TEXT objects (`dc:language` + `langid` = German); no filter by `dc:type`, provider, or era. `all_tokens` = spaCy `de_core_news_sm` token count including stopwords and punctuation; `content_tokens` = stopwords removed, punctuation retained.
   116→- **Token thresholds:** quartiles — short ≤4 (p25), medium 5–14, long >14 (p75). See [title-length-thresholds.md](ner/sr10_title-length-thresholds.md).
   117→- **Length by year:** pre-1750 dominated by long strings (42–50%, median 12–15 tokens); post-1775 shift to median 6–9; 2000–2024 reversal (62% medium). 9.6% of titles have no year.
   118→- **Implication for SR-07:** stratify gold set by length and era; pre-1750 long-form records stress the NER model differently from the short modern majority.

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T13:53:27.833Z)*

No SR-07 note file yet. Before starting, a few open questions that will shape the design:

**Stratification**

The current spec says ~500 records across era × tier × `dc_type`. That's at least 12 cells (4 eras × 3 tiers), and with `dc_type` added it multiplies further. Some cells will be near-empty by construction (e.g. tier 2 × pre-1700 — structural ISBD records are almost nonexistent pre-1750).

Two decisions needed before writing the sampling plan:

1. **Total n**: 500 is the working number. Is that a hard constraint, or a starting point? The HIPE-2022 gold sets ranged from ~1,000 (French) to ~7,000 (German) — 500 is lean but defensible for a single evaluation pass.

2. **SR-09 dependency**: the gold set label scope depends on SR-09 (which FRBR levels are in scope for the paper). If the paper only evaluates Work-level labels (TITLE, PERSON), the annotation task is much simpler than if Expression/Manifestation labels are also required. Do you want to resolve SR-09 first, or design the gold set around Work-level only and extend later?

3. **Annotation method**: three options for the actual labeling —
   - Manual (you + tool like Label Studio / doccano)
   - LLM-assisted (Claude labels, human reviews)
   - LLM-only with spot-check sample

   The pre-1750 stratum almost certainly needs manual review regardless, given the non-standard orthography and author-before-title structure.

Which of these should we settle first, or do you want me to draft the full SR-07 note with what's already specified and flag the open questions inline?

SR-09 should be resolved first. Want to work on that instead?

gemea/notes/ner/sr03_silver-label-fp-review.md:107:This is a structural blind spot: the pattern cannot be fixed by post-filtering `f_person`. Early modern records in the SR-07 gold set will need a separate author-detection strategy (e.g. name-before-title NER, or annotation without relying on the SoR heuristic).
gemea/notes/ner/sr03_silver-label-fp-review.md:123:Pre-1750 titles systematically place the author's name and credentials *before* the main title (not after ` /`), making `f_person` a **false negative** for this entire stratum. The SoR heuristic misses the majority of early modern authors. This is a structural blind spot, not a noise issue — the pattern cannot be fixed by post-filtering. Implications for SR-07 gold set composition: early modern records must be annotated with a different author-detection strategy.
gemea/notes/ner/sr10_de-titles-distribution.md:109:- **Implication for SR-07 (gold set):** stratify by length as well as era. Pre-1750 long-form records stress the NER model differently from the short modern majority — the TITLE boundary is structurally different. The 9.6% no-year group needs separate treatment — sample by `dc_type` or `silver_tier` instead.
gemea/notes/ner/sr06_historical-scope.md:59:**Conclusion:** n=200 is sufficient for the decision this study supports — determining whether a Latin stratum is needed in the gold set (SR-07) and confirming Early Modern German as the primary historical challenge. It is **not** sufficient for precise per-class prevalence estimates publishable as corpus statistics; a larger stratified sample (n≥500 per stratum) would be needed for that.
gemea/notes/ner/sr06_historical-scope.md:61:**Unsampled strata:** Leichenpredigt and pre-1800 Monografie represent ~186k records. Pre-1800 Periodika, Handschrift, and Einblattdruck are not covered. Their language distribution may skew differently (e.g. Handschrift may have higher Latin prevalence). Flag for SR-07 gold set design if these dc_types are included.
gemea/notes/ner/sr06_historical-scope.md:172:SR-07 gold set design does not need a dedicated Latin stratum. Early Modern German is the primary historical challenge. The gold set should include a **pre-1700** stratum (long-form Leichenpredigt and legal/administrative Monografie) as a proxy for the full historical register.
gemea/notes/ner/sr06_historical-scope.md:189:**What is needed:** the SR-07 gold set (pre-1700 stratum, human-annotated) or LLM-labeled pre-1750 records as an interim (see [ner-bibliographic.md §8.2](../ner-bibliographic.md)).
gemea/notes/ner/sr11_field-level-weighting.md:54:- **External**: once SR-07 gold set exists, measure NER F1 for models trained at different score cutoffs and select the elbow.
gemea/notes/ner/sr11_field-level-weighting.md:56:The external method is preferred — it directly measures annotation quality impact rather than proxy precision metrics. It requires SR-07 to be completed first.
gemea/notes/ner/sr11_field-level-weighting.md:96:- SR-07 completion (for external calibration of tier boundary)
gemea/notes/ner/sr10_title-length-thresholds.md:3:**Applies to:** `scripts/analyse_title_lengths.py`, gold set stratification (SR-07), NER evaluation (SR-08)

   100→- **Pre-1750 PERSON annotation (from SR-03):** the ` /` SoR heuristic is a systematic false negative for pre-1750 titles — authors appear before the work title, not after ` /`. Gold set annotators must not rely on the SoR position as a cue for the `PERSON` label in this stratum; author spans need to be identified from the opening name + credentials pattern (e.g. *Firstname Lastname, [role/title], [work title]*). This affects annotation guidelines and model evaluation: PERSON F1 on the pre-1750 stratum should be tracked separately from the modern stratum. See [silver-label-fp-review.md §5](ner/sr03_silver-label-fp-review.md#5-pre-1750-false-negatives--author-before-title) for examples.
   101→
   102→### 2.8 SR-08 — NuNER Zero evaluation
   103→**Status:** Open — blocked on SR-07
   104→- **Requirement:** run NuNER Zero zero-shot on 500 stratified fallback records; assess TITLE and PERSON F1 on gold set
   105→- **Decision gate:** precision ≥ threshold → use zero-shot; else → LLM labeling + fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on silver + LLM-labeled set
   106→
   107→### 2.9 SR-09 — FRBR metric scope for paper
   108→**Status:** Open
   109→- **Requirement:** confirm which FRBR levels the paper's quality metrics cover — Work (TITLE, PERSON) only, or also Expression (TRANSLATOR, PARALLEL_TITLE, MEDIUM) and Manifestation (PUBLISHER, PLACE, YEAR, EDITION, SERIES, VOLUME)
   110→- Determines which label types must appear in the gold set and which NER labels are in scope for the evaluation section
   111→
   112→### 2.10 SR-10 — DF_DE_TITLES source and title-length scope
   113→**Status:** Resolved — see [de-titles-distribution.md](ner/sr10_de-titles-distribution.md)



```

gemea/notes/ner/sr10_de-titles-distribution.md:109:- **Implication for SR-09 (gold set):** stratify by length as well as era. Pre-1750 long-form records stress the NER model differently from the short modern majority — the TITLE boundary is structurally different. The 9.6% no-year group needs separate treatment — sample by `dc_type` or `silver_tier` instead.
gemea/notes/ner/sr03_silver-label-fp-review.md:107:This is a structural blind spot: the pattern cannot be fixed by post-filtering `f_person`. Early modern records in the SR-09 gold set will need a separate author-detection strategy (e.g. name-before-title NER, or annotation without relying on the SoR heuristic).
gemea/notes/ner/sr03_silver-label-fp-review.md:123:Pre-1750 titles systematically place the author's name and credentials *before* the main title (not after ` /`), making `f_person` a **false negative** for this entire stratum. The SoR heuristic misses the majority of early modern authors. This is a structural blind spot, not a noise issue — the pattern cannot be fixed by post-filtering. Implications for SR-09 gold set composition: early modern records must be annotated with a different author-detection strategy.
gemea/notes/ner/sr06_historical-scope.md:59:**Conclusion:** n=200 is sufficient for the decision this study supports — determining whether a Latin stratum is needed in the gold set (SR-09) and confirming Early Modern German as the primary historical challenge. It is **not** sufficient for precise per-class prevalence estimates publishable as corpus statistics; a larger stratified sample (n≥500 per stratum) would be needed for that.
gemea/notes/ner/sr06_historical-scope.md:61:**Unsampled strata:** Leichenpredigt and pre-1800 Monografie represent ~186k records. Pre-1800 Periodika, Handschrift, and Einblattdruck are not covered. Their language distribution may skew differently (e.g. Handschrift may have higher Latin prevalence). Flag for SR-09 gold set design if these dc_types are included.
gemea/notes/ner/sr06_historical-scope.md:172:SR-09 gold set design does not need a dedicated Latin stratum. Early Modern German is the primary historical challenge. The gold set should include a **pre-1700** stratum (long-form Leichenpredigt and legal/administrative Monografie) as a proxy for the full historical register.
gemea/notes/ner/sr06_historical-scope.md:189:**What is needed:** the SR-09 gold set (pre-1700 stratum, human-annotated) or LLM-labeled pre-1750 records as an interim (see [ner-bibliographic.md §8.2](../ner-bibliographic.md)).
gemea/notes/ner/sr11_field-level-weighting.md:54:- **External**: once SR-09 gold set exists, measure NER F1 for models trained at different score cutoffs and select the elbow.
gemea/notes/ner/sr11_field-level-weighting.md:56:The external method is preferred — it directly measures annotation quality impact rather than proxy precision metrics. It requires SR-09 to be completed first.
gemea/notes/ner/sr11_field-level-weighting.md:96:- SR-09 completion (for external calibration of tier boundary)
```

  "new_string": "### 2.7 SR-07 — FRBR metric scope for paper\n**Status:** Resolved\n\n- **DDB objects are Manifestation-level.** All DDB bibliographic records represent Manifestations (a specific published item). FRBR Work and Expression are derived by grouping — they are not the unit of description in the source data.\n- **Phase 1 scope — Work:** initial NER evaluation covers Work-level labels only: `TITLE`, `OTHER_TITLE`, `PERSON`. This is the minimum viable label set for the paper's linking quality metrics.\n- **Phase 2 scope — Expression:** once Work-level extraction is validated, add Expression-level labels: `TRANSLATOR`, `PARALLEL_TITLE`, `MEDIUM`. These can be derived from the Manifestation record but require additional heuristics (language field, ` /` SoR role disambiguation, medium indicators).\n- **Manifestation labels deferred:** `PUBLISHER`, `PLACE`, `YEAR`, `EDITION`, `SERIES`, `VOLUME` are extractable from the Manifestation string and useful for deduplication, but are not in scope for the paper's NER evaluation.\n- **Implication for SR-09:** gold set Phase 1 annotation covers `TITLE`, `OTHER_TITLE`, `PERSON` only. Expression labels (`TRANSLATOR`, `PARALLEL_TITLE`) should be included in the annotation schema from the start (to avoid re-annotation) but are not required for the initial evaluation pass."
}

- DDB objects are Manifestation-level — Work/Expression are derived groupings, not source units

- Gold set annotation should include Expression labels in the schema from the start to avoid re-annotation, but Phase 1 evaluation only requires Work labels

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T14:29:53.903Z)*

**NuNER Zero is still relevant, but this paper shifts the landscape in two ways.**

**What the paper shows**

- Fine-tuned open-source LLMs (LLaMA3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B) with LoRA + Inline Bracketed/XML format reach F1 93–94 on CoNLL2003 — matching or exceeding BERT-MRC (93.04). Traditional encoder-based models are no longer clearly dominant.
- Zero-shot LLM NER (GPT-3 ZS) is materially weaker: F1 ~88–90 on flat NER, dropping to ~68 on nested. Zero-shot is a real step down.
- Output format is decisive: Inline Bracketed/XML ≈ 90–91 avg F1; JSON drops to 85–87; Offset-based JSON collapses entirely (~30 F1).
- Low-resource domains (GENIA biomedical) still show a gap — LLMs fall behind specialized fine-tuned models there.

**Relevance to NuNER Zero (SR-08)**

NuNER Zero is not evaluated in the paper — it's an encoder/span-extraction zero-shot model, architecturally distinct from the generative paradigm here. The paper doesn't invalidate it. The zero-shot gap it documents (GPT-3 ZS ~88 vs fine-tuned ~93) actually *supports* the SR-08 decision logic: evaluate zero-shot first, and only if it clears a threshold avoid the cost of fine-tuning.

NuNER Zero still makes sense as the zero-shot baseline. The main risk is that historical German bibliographic NER is further out-of-distribution than GPT-3's gap from CoNLL2003 — so the threshold for "good enough" may be harder to clear than it looks on general-domain benchmarks.

**What this paper does change**

The `xlm-roberta-large` fine-tune path in ner-bibliographic.md §10 should now note fine-tuned generative LLMs (Qwen3-1.7B or LLaMA3.2-1B with LoRA) as a legitimate comparison — they match encoder models on general NER at smaller scale, and the LoRA footprint is manageable. Worth adding as an SR-08 benchmark option alongside `mdeberta-v3-base`.

Also: if LLM labeling is used to generate the silver/gold set, this paper strongly recommends Inline Bracketed or Inline XML output format over any JSON variant.

Want me to update `ner-bibliographic.md` §10 and the SR-08 entry with these implications, and add this paper to §9?

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T14:32:40.957Z)*

**Tool call:** `Write`
```json
{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/ner/ref_zhan2026-generative-ner.md",
  "content": "# Zhan et al. (2026) — Assessment of Generative NER in the Era of LLMs\n\n**Citation:** Qi Zhan, Yile Wang, Hui Huang. *Assessment of Generative Named Entity Recognition in the Era of Large Language Models.* arXiv:2601.17898v1, 25 Jan 2026. Shenzhen University.\n\n**File:** `references/2026-zhan-assessment-nerllm.pdf`\n\n---\n\n## 1. Core claim\n\nFine-tuned open-source LLMs with parameter-efficient methods (LoRA) and structured output formats can match the performance of traditional encoder-based NER models on standard flat NER benchmarks, while maintaining general-purpose capabilities. Generative NER is a viable alternative to sequence-labeling approaches.\n\n---\n\n## 2. Method\n\n- **Models:** LLaMA3.1/3.2/3.3 (1B–8B) and Qwen2.5/3 (1.5B–7B), fine-tuned with LoRA (rank 256, α 512, all linear modules); 2 epochs, batch size 8, lr 2e-5\n- **Output formats tested:** Inline Bracketed, Inline XML, Category-grouped JSON, Occurrence-based JSON, Offset-based JSON (5 formats)\n- **Datasets:** CoNLL2003, OntoNotes5.0 (flat, general domain); ACE2005, GENIA (nested; general and biomedical)\n- **Baselines:** BERT-Tagger, BERT-MRC, W2NER, Biaffine+CNN (traditional encoder-based); GPT-3 zero-shot and few-shot (generative closed-source)\n\n---\n\n## 3. Key findings\n\n### 3.1 Output format is decisive\n\n| Format | Avg F1 (flat) | Avg F1 (nested) |\n|---|---|---|\n| Inline Bracketed | 90.69 | 77.22 |\n| Inline XML | 90.07 | 76.91 |\n| Category-grouped JSON | 85.60 | 74.92 |\n| Occurrence-based JSON | 87.52 | 72.81 |\n| Offset-based JSON | 29.66 | 14.51 |\n\nInline Bracketed and Inline XML are significantly better than all JSON formats. Offset-based JSON collapses completely — character-level position calculation is incompatible with autoregressive generation.\n\n### 3.2 Fine-tuned LLMs match encoder-based models on general-domain NER\n\nLLaMA3.1-8B with Inline Bracketed format: F1 93.83 on CoNLL2003, 91.28 on OntoNotes5.0 — comparable to BERT-MRC (93.04 / 91.11) and above GPT-3 fine-tuned (~90.62 / 69.01 on flat/nested).\n\n### 3.3 Zero-shot LLM NER is materially weaker\n\nGPT-3 zero-shot: F1 ~88.54 on flat, ~68.51 on nested — a real step down from fine-tuned performance. Zero-shot cannot substitute for fine-tuning on precision-sensitive tasks.\n\n### 3.4 Low-resource domain gap persists\n\nOn GENIA (biomedical), LLMs fall behind specialized fine-tuned models (LLaMA3.1-8B Inline Bracketed: 83.75 avg F1 vs BERT-MRC 85.32). Domain-specific knowledge gaps are not fully compensated by model scale or instruction following.\n\n### 3.5 LLMs genuinely learn entities, not memorize labels\n\nReplacing label names with arbitrary symbols causes only a marginal F1 drop (−0.62 on CoNLL2003-SE, −0.42 on CoNLL2003-SO). Performance is driven by instruction following and entity recognition, not by memorizing entity-label correlations.\n\n### 3.6 Fine-tuning preserves general capabilities\n\nLoRA fine-tuning for NER causes minor fluctuations (±3–4%) on general benchmarks (MMLU, HellaSwag, GSM8K, TruthfulQA). DROP improves significantly (+25 F1) because NER fine-tuning strengthens entity span extraction, which DROP requires.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Error analysis (§5)\n\nLLMs and encoder models make qualitatively different errors:\n\n| Error type | BERT-MRC | LLaMA3.1-8B |\n|---|---|---|\n| Wrong Types | 29.0% | 38.2% |\n| Omitted Mentions | 45.5% | 13.7% |\n| Completely-O | 0% | 25.2% |\n\n- **Encoder models** predominantly miss entities (Omitted Mentions 45.5%) — conservative extraction\n- **LLMs** predominantly misclassify types (38.2%) and over-extract (Completely-O 25.2%) — aggressive extraction driven by pre-training world knowledge conflicting with annotation guidelines\n\n---\n\n## 5. Implications for GeMeA\n\n### 5.1 NuNER Zero (SR-08) remains relevant\n\nNuNER Zero is not evaluated in this paper (it uses a GLiNER/encoder span-extraction architecture, not generative). The zero-shot gap documented here (GPT-3 ZS ~88 vs fine-tuned ~93 on general NER) supports the SR-08 decision logic: evaluate zero-shot first; only fine-tune if zero-shot falls below threshold. The risk is that historical Early Modern German bibliographic NER is further out-of-distribution than standard CoNLL2003, so NuNER Zero's zero-shot precision may be lower than the general-domain gap suggests.\n\n### 5.2 Fine-tuned generative LLMs should be added as SR-08 benchmark\n\nIf NuNER Zero is insufficient and fine-tuning is pursued, a small fine-tuned LLM (Qwen3-1.7B or LLaMA3.2-1B with LoRA) should be benchmarked alongside `xlm-roberta-large`. The paper shows 1B–4B models lag 7B–8B by ~10 F1 points, but the 7B–8B range matches encoder-based models — and LoRA fine-tuning footprint is manageable for the DDB use case.\n\n### 5.3 LLM labeling output format\n\nIf Claude or another LLM is used to generate silver labels or annotate the SR-09 gold set, use **Inline Bracketed** or **Inline XML** output format. JSON formats (especially offset-based) perform significantly worse. Category-grouped JSON also loses positional information.\n\n### 5.4 Low-resource domain caveat applies directly\n\nThe GENIA biomedical gap is structurally similar to GeMeA's situation: historical German bibliographic text is a low-resource domain where LLM pre-training provides minimal coverage. This supports prioritising the SR-09 gold set and fine-tuning over zero-shot-only deployment.\n\n### 5.5 Error mode expectation\n\nWhen evaluating LLM-based NER on DDB strings, expect: (1) type misclassification as the dominant error (TITLE vs OTHER_TITLE boundary, PERSON vs corporate body); (2) over-extraction on short, fragment-like records. Encoder-based models (NuNER Zero, xlm-roberta-large) will likely under-extract instead — missing entities in long Early Modern German strings.\n\n---\n\n## 6. Limitations of the paper (relevance to GeMeA)\n\n- Evaluates only LLaMA and Qwen families — does not cover encoder-only zero-shot models (GLiNER, NuNER Zero)\n- All datasets are modern general-domain or biomedical English/multilingual — no historical NER, no bibliographic structure\n- Does not evaluate multilingual performance on non-English text\n- LoRA fine-tuning assumes a labelled training set; does not address the zero/low-resource regime directly\n"
}
```

   250→# Merge adjacent tokens with the same label (token classifier output)
   251→def merge_entities(entities, text):

   255→    current = entities[0]

   261→            merged.append(current)
   262→            current = nxt
   263→    merged.append(current)
   264→    return merged
   265→
   266→entities = merge_entities(entities, text)
   267→# → [{"text": "Faust drittes Buch", "label": "title", "score": 0.91}, ...]
   268→```
   269→
   270→BERT-sized — runs on CPU, same deployment footprint as GLiNER. Zero-shot precision on DDB strings is unknown; **evaluate on ~500 stratified fallback records before committing** (see [SR-08](#28-sr-08--nunner-zero-evaluation)).
   271→
   272→---
   273→
   274→## 7. LLM options at inference time
   275→
   276→NER applies to ~71% of records (~3.5–7M unique pairs after deduplication). LLM API at inference is expensive at this scale; local options are strongly preferred.
   277→
   278→| Approach | Scale | Cost | Historical/Latin | Effort |
   279→|---|---|---|---|---|
   280→| **NuNER Zero** (local) | All NER records | Free | Moderate | Low — deploy and evaluate |
   281→| **Local LLM** (Llama 3.1 8B) | All NER records | GPU infra only | Moderate | Medium |
   282→| **LLM one-time labeler → fine-tune xlm-roberta** | Generate labels once | Low one-time API cost | Good | Medium |
   283→| **Fine-tuned xlm-roberta-base** | All NER records | Cheap at inference | Good (if trained on historical) | High upfront |
   284→| **LLM API at inference** (GPT-4o, Claude) | All NER records | ~$1,500–3,500 at full scale | Good | Low — but costly |
   285→
   286→**Recommended path**: start with NuNER Zero on 500 stratified NER records. If precision is acceptable, done. If not, use an LLM to label a few thousand records and fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on that output.
   287→
   288→---
   289→
   290→## 8. If no labeled training data is available
   291→
   292→**8.1 Silver labeling from the ISBD pipeline**
   293→
   294→The ~28% of records where ISBD area structure is present (`has_dot_dash`) become auto-labeled training examples. Silver candidate selection is handled by `scripts/rate_isbd_fields.py` → `data/processed/isbd_field_ratings.csv`:
   295→
   296→- **Silver tier 2** (primary): `has_dot_dash AND f_person AND ≥1 manifestation field` — structural annotation, multi-field spans; use as primary training set
   297→- **Silver tier 1** (augmentation): `n_fields ≥ 3` or `(f_person AND f_year)` — partial annotation; validate ~200 records before use
   298→
   299→See [isbd-field-rating.md](ner/sr01_isbd-field-rating.md) for the full detection spec and [isbd-field-rating-adr.md](ner/sr01_isbd-field-rating-adr.md) for design decisions.
   300→
   301→ISBD pattern → NER label mapping across FRBR levels:
   302→
   303→| ISBD pattern | Label(s) | Notes |
   304→|---|---|---|
   305→| Before ` :` or ` /` | `TITLE` | Primary title string |
   306→| After ` :` | `OTHER_TITLE` | Subtitle / other title info |
   307→| After ` =` | `PARALLEL_TITLE` | Expression-level; often a translation |
   308→| After ` /`; no "übersetzt" keyword | `PERSON` | Statement of responsibility |
   309→| After ` /`; "übersetzt von", "Übers.:", "transl. by" | `TRANSLATOR` | Expression-level |
   310→| Span matching "Aus dem …", "in … Übersetzung", "traduit de" | `TRANSLATION` | Expression-level; sparse |
   311→| Medium keyword ("Klavierauszug", "Partitur", "Textbuch", "Hörbuch") | `MEDIUM` | Expression-level; relevant for music/audio |
   312→| Edition keyword before `. -` block | `EDITION` | "Aufl.", "Neuausg.", "rev. ed." |
   313→| `. - Place :` block, first token(s) before `:` | `PLACE` | Manifestation-level |
   314→| `. - Place :` block, after `:` up to `,` | `PUBLISHER` | Manifestation-level |
   315→| Trailing 4-digit year or year after `,` | `YEAR` | Manifestation-level |
   316→| `( Series ; N )` block, title part | `SERIES` | Manifestation-level |
   317→| "Bd.", "Teil", "Vol.", "Heft", "Nr." + number | `VOLUME` | Manifestation-level |
   318→
   319→TRANSLATOR disambiguation is the main ambiguity: the ` /` cue fires for both `PERSON` and `TRANSLATOR`; keyword matching ("übersetzt", "Übers.", "transl.") is sufficient for a first-pass split. Language-agnostic structurally — works for Latin and historical German titles. Fine-tune `xlm-roberta-base` on these labels. Evaluate on a manually checked gold set of ~500 records including historical and Latin examples.
   320→
   321→**8.2 LLM as a one-time labeler**
   322→
   323→Use GPT-4o or Claude to label a few thousand records. Handles Latin and historical German well. Fine-tune a smaller model on those outputs. Expensive per call but a one-time cost.
   324→
   325→**8.3 Distant supervision (supplementary)**
   326→
   327→For records where a GND Werk URI was confirmed via the local GND instance, the extracted title string is a positive `TITLE` example. Linked person GND URIs supply `PERSON` labels. Useful to augment silver labels, not sufficient alone.
   328→
   329→---
   330→
   331→## 9. Available datasets for fine-tuning
   332→
   333→| Dataset | Labels | Language | Size | Relevance |
   334→|---|---|---|---|---|
   335→| [empathyai/books-ner-dataset](https://huggingface.co/datasets/empathyai/books-ner-dataset) | TITLE, AUTHOR | English | Project Gutenberg catalogues | Closest label match; English only — use for domain-transfer pretraining |
   336→| [HIPE-2022 (ajmc)](https://github.com/hipe-eval/HIPE-2022-data) | Fine-grained bibliographic refs | German, French, English | ~10K tokens | Historical document NER with bibliographic references; German included. Top systems used `xlm-roberta-large` as backbone. Ehrmann et al. (2022) ⚠️ *verify citation* |
   337→| [CLEF-HIPE-2020](https://zenodo.org/record/3836029) | PER, ORG, LOC, PROD (work titles) | German, French, English | Historical newspapers | PROD label covers work titles; 19th-century orthography. XLM-R large dominant in top submissions. Ehrmann et al. (2020) ⚠️ *verify citation* |
   338→| [GermEval 2014](https://sites.google.com/site/germeval2014ner/) | PER, ORG, LOC, OTH | German | ~31K sentences | Modern German only; useful only for PERSON transfer |
   339→
   340→**Recommended fine-tuning path**:
   341→1. Pretrain on `empathyai/books-ner-dataset` for TITLE/AUTHOR signal (domain transfer)
   342→2. Fine-tune on silver-labeled DDB records
   343→3. Supplement with HIPE-2022 ajmc and CLEF-HIPE-2020 for historical German and Latin signal
   344→4. Evaluate on a gold set stratified by era (modern, 19th c., 1700–1800, pre-1700) — no dedicated Latin stratum needed (SR-06: ~0.5% prevalence)
   345→
   346→---
   347→
   348→## 10. Decision
   349→



```

   348→## 10. Decision
   349→
   350→1. **Try NuNER Zero first** — current SOTA compact zero-shot NER, no training data, runs locally, handles arbitrarily long spans. Evaluate on 500 stratified fallback records (see [SR-08](#28-sr-08--nunner-zero-evaluation)).

### User *(2026-03-24T14:34:27.667Z)*

what other options are there aside from NuNER? I honestly have not heard of NuNER

---

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T14:35:04.671Z)*

NuNER Zero is relatively niche — it's a wrapper around **GLiNER**, which is the more established underlying architecture. Here's the actual landscape:

**Encoder-based zero-shot span extraction**

- **GLiNER** (Zaratiana et al., 2023) — the base architecture NuNER Zero uses. Takes free-text entity type descriptions as input, extracts spans without generating text. DeBERTa backbone. `urchade/gliner_multi-v2.1` is a multilingual variant directly relevant for German. Well-cited, actively maintained. This is the honest "production-ready" option in this family.
- **NuNER Zero** (`numind/NuNerZero`) — GLiNER fine-tuned by NuMind on a large synthetic dataset to improve zero-shot transfer. The marginal improvement over GLiNER multi is unverified on historical German specifically.

**Instruction-tuned LLM zero-shot NER**

- **UniversalNER** (Zhou et al., 2023) — instruction-tuned LLaMA for open-type NER. Handles arbitrary label definitions. Older (LLaMA-1 base), less relevant now that Qwen3/LLaMA3 are available.
- **GNER** (Ding et al., 2024) — generative NER fine-tuned across ~400 NER datasets for zero-shot type transfer. More systematic coverage than UniversalNER.
- **Direct prompting** — Claude/GPT-4o/Llama3 zero-shot with a structured prompt. This is what Zhan et al. test as GPT-3 ZS (~88 F1); newer models will do better. Simplest to implement, easiest to inspect.

**Practical recommendation for SR-08**

For GeMeA's zero-shot evaluation, the honest comparison is:

| Model | Type | Multilingual | Zero-shot | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `gliner_multi-v2.1` | Encoder/span | Yes | Yes | Most relevant baseline; same arch as NuNER Zero |
| `NuNerZero` | Encoder/span | Partial | Yes | Marginal improvement over GLiNER multi; less tested |
| Claude/GPT-4o few-shot | Generative | Yes | Yes (few-shot) | Flexible label definitions; costly at inference scale |
| GNER | Generative | Partial | Yes | Broad zero-shot transfer; English-dominant training |

GLiNER multilingual is probably the right zero-shot baseline — better-established than NuNER Zero, genuinely multilingual, same inference footprint. NuNER Zero is worth trying alongside it but shouldn't be the only option evaluated in SR-08.

Want me to update SR-08 in `ner-bibliographic.md` to replace "NuNER Zero" with "GLiNER multilingual (+ NuNER Zero)" as the zero-shot evaluation target, and add a brief GLiNER explanation to §6?

### User *(2026-03-24T14:46:25.339Z)*

**Tool result**
```
All three papers are fully read. Here is a structured extraction of all citable facts.

---

## Paper 1: GLiNER

**Full citation:** Urchade Zaratiana, Nadi Tomeh, Pierre Holat, Thierry Charnois. "GLiNER: Generalist Model for Named Entity Recognition using Bidirectional Transformer." arXiv:2311.08526. Published in *Proceedings of NAACL 2024* (North American Chapter of the ACL: Human Language Technologies, Volume 1: Long Papers), pp. 5364–5376, June 16–21, 2024.

**Note on arXiv ID vs. venue:** The paper's own reference list cites its arXiv preprint as `abs/2311.08526` (2023), but the published version is NAACL 2024. The file is named `2024-zaratiana-gliner.pdf`. Both the 2023 arXiv ID and 2024 venue are correct — they refer to the same paper.

---

### Architecture (Section 2, pp. 5365–5367)

- **Three-component encoder architecture:** (i) a pre-trained bidirectional language model (BiLM) backbone, defaulting to `deberta-v3`; (ii) a **span representation module** that computes span embeddings from token embeddings via a two-layer FFN over concatenated boundary token representations `S_ij = FFN(h_i ⊗ h_j)`; (iii) an **entity representation module** that refines the `[ENT]` token outputs via a two-layer FFN to produce entity type embeddings.
- **Input format:** A single unified sequence combining entity type names (each preceded by a special `[ENT]` token) and the input text, separated by `[SEP]`. Entity types are expressed in natural language. The `[ENT]` and `[SEP]` tokens are randomly initialized.
- **Matching score:** `φ(i,j,t) = σ(S_ij^T q_t)` — dot product between span and entity embeddings, passed through sigmoid. Scores > 0.5 are considered entity candidates (Section 2.1).
- **Training loss:** Binary cross-entropy over all span/entity-type pairs (Section 2.2).
- **Decoding:** Greedy span selection; supports both flat NER (no overlapping spans) and nested NER (fully nested spans allowed). Priority-queue implementation gives O(n log n) complexity (Section 2.3).
- **Span length cap:** Maximum span width fixed at K=12 tokens (Section 2.3 / Appendix A.1), stated as preserving linear complexity "without harming recall."
- **Key architectural distinction from NuNER:** GLiNER **merges** text and entity type encoders in a single BiLM pass. NuNER keeps them separate (noted in NuNER paper, Section 2, p. 11830).
- **Training data:** Pile-NER dataset (Zhou et al., 2023) — 44,889 passages, ~240k entity spans, 13k distinct entity types, derived from the Pile corpus and annotated by ChatGPT without specifying entity types in advance (Section 3.1).
- **Three model sizes:** GLiNER-S (50M params), GLiNER-M (90M), GLiNER-L (0.3B). All use deberta-v3 variants (Section 3.3 / Table 1).
- **Training hardware/time:** GLiNER-L trains in ~3 hours 50 minutes on A100 80G; trained for up to 30,000 steps, batch size 8 (Appendix A.1).
- **Negative entity type sampling:** 50% negative sampling ratio found optimal; 0% (only positives) yields lower precision and higher recall (Table 5). Randomly dropping entity types during training improves OOD performance by over 1.4 points (Section 5.3).

---

### Benchmark F1 Scores (Sections 4.1–4.3)

**Zero-shot OOD NER Benchmark** (Table 1) — 7 datasets from CrossNER and MIT. All scores are F1 (micro/entity-level exact match):

| Model | Params | Average F1 |
|---|---|---|
| ChatGPT | – | 47.5 |
| GoLLIE | 7B | 58.0 |
| UniNER-7B | 7B | 53.7 |
| UniNER-13B | 13B | 55.6 |
| GLiNER-S | 50M | 52.7 |
| GLiNER-M | 90M | 55.4 |
| **GLiNER-L** | **0.3B** | **60.9** |

GLiNER-L outperforms all baselines including GoLLIE (58.0) and UniNER-13B (55.6). GLiNER-M (90M) achieves comparable results to UniNER-13B (13B), described as "140 times smaller" (Section 4.1).

**Zero-shot 20 NER Benchmark** (Table 2) — 20 diverse datasets (biomedical, news, tweets, etc.):

| Model | Average F1 |
|---|---|
| ChatGPT | 36.5 |
| UniNER-7B | 45.7 |
| **GLiNER-L** | **47.8** |

GLiNER achieves highest performance on 13 of 20 datasets. Notable dataset scores for GLiNER-L: WikiNeural 71.8, WikiANN 58.9, MultiNERD 59.7, CoNLL03 64.6. Authors note GLiNER underperforms on tweet-based NER (TweetNER7: 41.4) (Section 4.1).

**In-domain Supervised Fine-tuning** (Table 4) — after fine-tuning on 20 NER datasets:

| Model | Average F1 |
|---|---|
| InstructUIE | 81.2 |
| UniNER-7B (w/ Pile-NER pretraining) | 84.8 |
| GLiNER-L (w/ Pile-NER pretraining) | 82.9 |
| GLiNER-L (w/o Pile-NER pretraining) | 82.1 |

Pretrained GLiNER-L outperforms InstructUIE (81.2 vs. 82.9) despite InstructUIE being ~30x larger (Section 5.2).

---

### Multilingual Capability (Section 4.2, Table 3)

Evaluated zero-shot on **MultiCONER** (11 languages). Two variants: GLiNER-En (deberta-v3-large backbone, English only) and GLiNER-Multi (mdeberta-v3-base backbone). Supervised baseline is XLM-R fine-tuned per language.

| Language | Supervised baseline | ChatGPT | GLiNER-En | GLiNER-Multi |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| German | 64.6 | 37.1 | 35.6 | **39.5** |
| English | 62.6 | 37.2 | **42.4** | 41.7 |
| Spanish | 58.7 | 34.7 | 38.7 | **42.1** |
| Dutch | 62.6 | 35.7 | 35.6 | **38.9** |
| Bengali | 39.7 | 23.3 | 0.89 | **25.9** |
| Persian | 52.3 | 25.9 | 14.9 | **30.2** |
| Hindi | 47.8 | 27.3 | 11.3 | **27.8** |
| **Average** | **54.9** | **29.9** | **23.6** | **32.9** |

**Key finding:** GLiNER-Multi surpasses ChatGPT in the majority of languages (Section 4.2). For Latin-script languages (German, Spanish, Dutch), GLiNER-Multi scores are competitive with or above ChatGPT. Performance is "substantially less effective in non-Latin languages," with Bengali reaching only 0.89 F1 for GLiNER-En (Section 4.2). Authors note the training data (Pile-NER) "comprises solely English examples."

**GLiNER outperforms ChatGPT in 8 out of 10 languages** not in training data (p. 5365).

---

### Computational Complexity

- Inference can run on CPU (stated in abstract and Fig. 1 caption).
- Decoding is O(n log n) in input length (Section 2.3).
- Max span width 12 keeps complexity linear in input size (Section 2.3).
- GLiNER-M (90M) achieves results comparable to UniNER-13B while being 140x smaller (Section 4.1).

---

### Limitations Stated by Authors (p. 5371, "Limitations" section)

1. **Cannot extract discontinuous entities** — "One notable limitation is the model's inability to extract discontinuous entities. This constraint sets GLiNER apart from some Large Language Models."
2. **Exact match metric** — "our evaluation methodology primarily relies on the exact matching metric. While this is a robust measure, it may not fully capture more nuanced aspects of the model's output. Subtleties such as partial matches or context-sensitive interpretations of entities are not adequately represented."
3. **Future work:** "aim to further improve GLiNER's design for enhanced performance and to better adapt it for low-resource languages."

---

## Paper 2: NuNER / NuNER Zero

**Full citation:** Sergei Bogdanov, Alexandre Constantin, Timothée Bernard, Benoit Crabbé, Etienne Bernard. "NuNER: Entity Recognition Encoder Pre-Training via LLM-Annotated Data." *Proceedings of EMNLP 2024* (Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing), pp. 11829–11841, November 12–16, 2024.

**Note:** The paper's own arXiv reference cites Zaratiana et al. 2023 as `abs/2311.08526` — confirming that is the GLiNER preprint. The NuNER paper does not self-cite an arXiv ID; your reference `arXiv:2402.15343` is not confirmed from the PDF itself. The file is `2024-bogdanov-nuner.pdf` and the venue is EMNLP 2024. **Flag: verify the arXiv ID 2402.15343 independently.**

---

### How NuNER Zero Differs from Base GLiNER (Section 2 / 3, pp. 11829–11832)

The paper introduces **NuNER** (a task-specific foundation model) and **NuNER Zero** is the zero-shot-capable variant. Key architectural differences from GLiNER:

- **Separate encoders:** NuNER keeps text encoder and concept encoder independent (unlike GLiNER's merged BiLM). "GLiNER merges the text and concept encoders, whereas in our approach, they remain independent" (p. 11830). This means NuNER can pre-compute text embeddings and function as a drop-in replacement for BERT/RoBERTa in standard token-classification pipelines.
- **Backbone:** RoBERTa-base (125M parameters) for both text and concept encoders; the concept encoder is discarded after training — only the text encoder (NuNER) is kept (Section 3.3).
- **Contrastive training:** Matrix-multiply text token vectors against concept vector, divided by temperature τ=5, through logistic sigmoid. Binary cross-entropy loss (Section 3.2).
- **Training details:** 10 epochs on 1M sentence dataset; lr=0.00003, batch size=48; bottom 6 layers of text encoder frozen (Section 3.3); AdamW optimizer, linear scheduler with 10% warmup.
- **NuNER Zero** is the variant evaluated in zero-shot settings by adapting NuNER into an open-vocabulary NER model (Section 6 / Appendix A.2). The paper explicitly states: "we only studied the Few-shot learning capabilities of the model, without experimenting with the potential Zero-shot modifications of NuNER" (Limitations, p. 11836). **This means NuNER Zero's zero-shot benchmarks are not reported in this paper** — the Limitations section explicitly flags this as unexplored.

---

### Training Data (Section 3.1, pp. 11831–11832)

- **Source corpus:** Random sample of **C4** (Raffel et al., 2020) — an English web crawl with blog posts, news articles, social media. Chosen for domain diversity.
- **Annotation model:** GPT-3.5-turbo-0301, with an unconstrained prompt allowing the LLM to assign any concept it identifies (not restricted to standard entity types). This produces "concepts" broader than traditional NER types (e.g., "wellness").
- **Scale:** 1.35M sentences annotated at a cost of less than $500. After filtering, final dataset contains **1M annotated sentences**, **4.38M annotations**, **200k unique concepts**.
- **Annotation quality (manual review of 100 examples):** >95% of assigned concepts are "completely sensible"; <5% are "questionable." However, the LLM has "high precision but low recall" — many entities are missed (Section 3.1).
- **Concept distribution:** Heavy-tailed — "person," "location," "organization" each appear in >1% of annotations; over 100k concepts appear only once (Fig. 4).

---

### Benchmark Results (Sections 4–6)

**Note on metric:** NuNER results use macro-averaged F1 of token classifications (not entity-level exact match), and evaluation is primarily few-shot (1–64 annotations per entity type). This makes direct numerical comparison with GLiNER's exact-match F1 inappropriate.

**Few-shot on 4 datasets** (OntoNotes 5.0, BioNLP 2004, MIT Restaurant, MIT Movie) — Figure 7 / Table 4, performance at k annotations per entity type:

| k | RoBERTa | RoBERTa w/ NER-BERT | NuNER |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 24.5 | 32.3 | **39.4** |
| 4 | 44.7 | 50.9 | **59.6** |
| 16 | 58.1 | 61.9 | **67.8** |
| 64 | 65.4 | 67.6 | **71.5** |

"NuNER substantially outperforms both models for all training sizes" (Section 4.1). The gain is largest at k=1 (single-shot): NuNER 39.4 vs. RoBERTa 24.5.

**Few-NERD benchmark** (Table 1) — using TadNER framework, NuNER-BERT vs. TadNER backbone:

| Setting | TadNER | NuNER-BERT |
|---|---|---|
| 5-WAY 1~2 INTRA | 60.78 | **62.48** |
| 10-WAY 1~2 INTRA | 55.44 | **57.63** |
| 5-WAY 1~2 INTER | 64.83 | **67.37** |
| 10-WAY 1~2 INTER | 64.06 | **66.54** |

"NuNER outperforms TadNER in all settings and training sizes, establishing it as the new SOTA for this benchmark" (Section 4.2).

**NuNER vs. UniversalNER — few-shot entity-level micro F1** (Table 2, k~2k setting):

| k range | UniversalNER | NuNER |
|---|---|---|
| 8~16 | 57.89 ± 4.34 | 58.75 ± 0.93 |
| 64~128 | 71.02 ± 1.53 | 70.30 ± 0.35 |

"NuNER and UniversalNER have similar performance" despite NuNER being **56 times smaller** (125M vs. 7B parameters) (Section 4.3).

**Inference cost comparison:** "one test example costs around 0.1$" for GPT-4, "while NuNER achieves similar performance while costing less than 0.0001$ per example" (Section 6, p. 11836).

---

### Ablation Study Findings (Section 5)

- **Concept diversity is the most important factor** for pre-training performance (Section 5.2 / Fig. 9). Dataset diversity (text source) matters less than the diversity of the annotation concepts.
- **Dataset size:** Performance improves monotonically from 1k to 1M examples for most datasets (Fig. 10 / Table 7). Exception: BioNLP2004 degrades after 100k examples (reason unclear).
- **Model size:** NuNER-large (355M) shows "an overall increase of the F1-score of a few percent" over NuNER-125M, more pronounced at k<10 (Appendix A.1 / Table 8). Combined with concept diversity and dataset size effects, "scaling up both models and data would lead to further performance improvements."
- **Text diversity:** C4 and Wikipedia (when both annotated by GPT-3.5) yield very similar performance — "the main reason for the performance gap is the LLM-annotation procedure rather than the underlying corpus" (Section 5.1).

---

### Multilingual Evaluation (Appendix A.2, Table 3)

A multilingual NuNER variant was created using multilingual BERT, with 16,000 text fragments per language from multilingual C4, annotated in English but instructed to output in the language of the fragment. Languages: English, French, Spanish, Portuguese, Dutch, Italian, Polish, Russian.

Evaluated on **MultiNERD** (Tedeschi and Navigli, 2022):

| k | mBERT | Multilingual NuNER |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10.84 | **27.71** |
| 2 | 19.19 | **39.55** |
| 4 | 31.60 | **49.77** |
| 8 | 43.84 | **56.58** |
| 16 | 52.69 | **61.16** |
| 32 | 58.65 | **65.38** |
| 64 | 62.20 | **67.68** |

"Multilingual NuNER confidently outperforms multilingual BERT for all k, even for the bigger k the gap between models stays respectable, despite mBERT also being pre-trained on the evaluated languages" (Appendix A.2). **No German-specific scores** are reported; MultiNERD is a multilingual dataset (languages not broken out in the table shown).

---

### Limitations Stated by Authors (p. 11836, "Limitations" section)

1. **Only few-shot regime studied:** "we only studied the Few-shot learning capabilities of the model, without experimenting with the potential Zero-shot modifications of NuNER." This is a direct flag: NuNER Zero's zero-shot performance on standard benchmarks is **not characterized in this paper**.
2. **No error analysis on zero-shot use.**
3. **English training data only** (for the base NuNER) — the multilingual variant is appendix-level work.

---

## Paper 3: Contextual Evaluation (Zhan et al., 2026)

**Full citation:** Qi Zhan, Yile Wang, Hui Huang. "Assessment of Generative Named Entity Recognition in the Era of Large Language Models." arXiv:2601.17898v1 [cs.CL], 25 January 2026. Shenzhen University.

This paper does **not** evaluate GLiNER or NuNER directly. It evaluates open-source generative LLMs (LLaMA3.1, LLaMA3.2, Qwen2.5, Qwen3) against traditional encoder-based NER models on CoNLL2003, OntoNotes5.0, ACE2005, GENIA. It is relevant as context for where encoder-based models stand relative to LLMs.

**Relevant findings for your GeMeA context:**

- Fine-tuned LLMs with structured output formats (Inline Bracketed or Inline XML) match traditional encoder NER models on general-domain datasets: LLaMA3.1-8B reaches F1 93.85 (CoNLL2003) and 91.28 (OntoNotes5.0) with Inline XML, comparable to BERT-MRC (93.04, 91.11) (Section 3.2).
- "LLMs lag behind traditional specialized models in low-resource domains" — on GENIA (biomedical nested NER), average LLM F1 is 79.18 vs. traditional models 83.75 (Section 3.2). This is directly relevant to historical/bibliographic text, which is also a low-resource specialized domain.
- "LLMs tend to generate wrong types … from a misalignment between the rich intrinsic knowledge and the detailed annotation guidelines" (Section 3.2). Primary error for LLMs is Wrong Types (38.2% of all errors), vs. Omitted Mentions for encoder models (45.5%) (Section 5 / Fig. 2).
- Offset-based JSON format collapses LLM performance to avg. F1 ~14–29 (Table 3) — character-level boundary prediction is not suited to autoregressive generation.

---

## Summary Table for Your Pros/Cons Analysis of NuNER Zero

| Dimension | GLiNER (Zaratiana et al., NAACL 2024) | NuNER / NuNER Zero (Bogdanov et al., EMNLP 2024) |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Merged BiLM; span + entity encoder in single pass | Separate text + concept encoders; concept encoder discarded at inference; drop-in for BERT/RoBERTa |
| Params | 50M–300M (deberta-v3) | 125M (RoBERTa-base); 355M large variant |
| Zero-shot | Yes — evaluated on OOD NER (avg F1 52.7–60.9) and 20 NER datasets (47.8) | **Not evaluated in this paper** (authors state this explicitly) |
| Few-shot | Pretrained weights benefit fine-tuning (avg +0.8 on 20 NER) | Core strength; at k=1 reaches 39.4 avg F1 vs. RoBERTa 24.5 |
| Multilingual | GLiNER-Multi (mdeberta-v3) surpasses ChatGPT in most MultiCONER languages; poor on non-Latin scripts | Multilingual variant (appendix only) outperforms mBERT on MultiNERD; no German-specific results |
| Training data | Pile-NER: 44,889 passages, 240k spans, 13k types (English) | C4 subset: 1M sentences, 4.38M annotations, 200k concepts (English; GPT-3.5) |
| Key limitation | Cannot extract discontinuous entities; exact match metric may miss partial matches | Zero-shot not studied; English pre-training only; NuNER Zero architecture uncharacterized in paper |
| Inference cost | CPU-capable; O(n log n) decode | \<$0.0001 per example vs. GPT-4 $0.10 |

---

**Flags and uncertainties:**

1. **arXiv ID 2402.15343 for NuNER** — not confirmed from the PDF. The venue (EMNLP 2024) is confirmed. Verify the arXiv ID independently before citing it.
2. **"NuNER Zero" as a distinct model** — the EMNLP 2024 paper introduces the NuNER encoder and notes zero-shot use as future work. The NuNER Zero HuggingFace model (available at `numind/NuNER_Zero`) is a released artifact but its specific benchmark evaluation in zero-shot NER is **not reported in the EMNLP 2024 paper**. Any zero-shot benchmark figures for NuNER Zero cited elsewhere originate from sources outside this paper.
3. **Historical/bibliographic NER** — none of the three papers evaluate on historical German text, bibliographic text, or ISBD-structured records. There is no published benchmark for NuNER Zero on such data in these PDFs. Any claims about NuNER Zero's suitability for your GeMeA use case require either your own evaluation or citation of a third-party study not in these files.
4. **Metric incompatibility:** GLiNER reports entity-level exact-match F1; NuNER reports macro-averaged token-classification F1. The numbers are not directly comparable across the two papers.

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T14:47:47.643Z)*

Created [ref_gliner-nunerzero-comparison.md](gemea/notes/ner/ref_gliner-nunerzero-comparison.md).

---

On your question about dataset size for fine-tuning:

**4k–5k is a reasonable working number, but the answer splits by stratum:**

- **Modern records** — you already have 335k silver tier-1 and 4,613 tier-2 records from the ISBD pipeline. No additional labeling needed; this is your fine-tuning corpus.
- **Pre-1750 records** — tier-0 by construction (no ISBD markers), so no silver labels exist. This is where the 4k–5k figure applies: LLM-labeled or gold-annotated records to cover Early Modern German. At this size, with xlm-roberta-large, you'd expect solid performance — standard NER fine-tuning papers converge well in the 1k–5k range for a focused label set (3–5 types).

On direct prompting specifically: yes, it's the most practical path for generating that 4k–5k historical stratum. Claude or GPT-4o with Inline Bracketed output format (per Zhan et al.) labels a batch of pre-1750 records, you spot-check ~200, and fine-tune on the rest. One-time cost, no annotation tool needed.

The main risk is systematic LLM errors on Early Modern German — the author-before-title pattern and non-standard orthography (`vnd`, `deß`) may trip up prompting without explicit guidance in the system prompt. Worth building that into the prompting spec.

{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/ner/labeling-strategy.md",
  "content": "# NER Labeling Strategy — Dataset Size and LLM Annotation\n\nRelated: [ner-bibliographic.md §8](../ner-bibliographic.md), [SR-09](../ner-bibliographic.md#29-sr-09--gold-set-composition)\n\n---\n\n## 1. Fine-tuning dataset size by FRBR scope\n\nRule of thumb for domain-specific NER fine-tuning from a strong multilingual checkpoint (xlm-roberta-large): **200–500 annotated spans per entity type** is the practical minimum for reliable boundary learning. Below ~200 per type, type confusion and boundary errors dominate; above ~500, returns diminish rapidly for a focused label set.\n\nTranslating to record counts (each bibliographic title typically yields 1–3 labeled spans):\n\n| Scope | Label types | Spans needed | Est. records needed | Notes |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| Work only | `TITLE`, `OTHER_TITLE`, `PERSON` | ~900–1,500 spans | **1k–2k records** | Most titles have all three; efficient labeling |\n| Work + Expression | + `TRANSLATOR`, `PARALLEL_TITLE`, `MEDIUM` | ~1,800–3,000 spans | **3k–5k records** | Expression types are sparse per record — need more records to reach span count |\n| Full (incl. Manifestation) | + `PUBLISHER`, `PLACE`, `YEAR`, `EDITION`, `SERIES`, `VOLUME` | ~3,000–6,000 spans | **5k–10k records** | Manifestation fields extractable from ISBD silver; less urgent to label manually |\n\n**Practical implication:** Work-only scope (Phase 1, per SR-07) is achievable with 1k–2k LLM-labeled records for the pre-1750 stratum. Extending to Expression adds 2k–3k more. Manifestation labels can be deferred — the ISBD silver set already covers these for the modern stratum.\n\nThe pre-1750 stratum is the binding constraint in all cases: it is entirely tier-0, so no silver labels exist regardless of scope. Modern records have 335k silver tier-1 and 4,613 tier-2 records — fine-tuning on the modern stratum is not the bottleneck.\n\n---\n\n## 2. LLM as annotator — using Claude\n\nUsing Claude (or another capable LLM) to generate the pre-1750 labeled dataset is the most practical path. This is architecturally identical to the approach used to build NuNER's training corpus (GPT-3.5 annotating 1M C4 sentences; Bogdanov et al., EMNLP 2024) and is consistent with the §8.2 recommendation in ner-bibliographic.md.\n\n### 2.1 Why it works\n\n- Claude handles Early Modern German well — Fraktur-adjacent orthography (`vnd`, `deß`, `seyn`) is within its training distribution from historical corpora\n- Bibliographic structure is well-represented in Claude's pretraining (library catalogs, digitized text)\n- Label definitions (TITLE, PERSON, TRANSLATOR) are semantically clear — low ambiguity for an LLM\n- Inline Bracketed output format works reliably for Claude (see Zhan et al. 2026 — same format family tops generative NER benchmarks)\n- Cost is negligible: 4k records × ~300 tokens average ≈ 1.2M tokens, a small fraction of API budget\n\n### 2.2 Risks and mitigations\n\n| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |\n|---|---|---|\n| Systematic errors on author-before-title (pre-1750) | High | Explicit annotation guideline in system prompt: \"In pre-1750 titles, the author's name and credentials appear before the main title, not after ` /`. Label the opening name+credentials span as PERSON.\" |\n| Type confusion: PERSON vs. TRANSLATOR | Medium | Include disambiguation rule: \"Label as TRANSLATOR only if a translation keyword is present (`übersetzt`, `Übers.`, `transl.`, `traduit`).\" |\n| Hallucinated spans (text not in input) | Low–Medium | Use Inline Bracketed format — spans are extracted inline from the input string, reducing hallucination compared to generative span prediction |\n| Systematic bias across similar records | Medium | Evaluate on the SR-09 gold set (human-annotated, independent); if LLM errors cluster on a dc_type or era, resample |\n| Early Modern abbreviations / title-page conventions | Medium | Include 3–5 annotated examples in the prompt (few-shot) covering the main patterns |\n\n### 2.3 Recommended workflow\n\n1. Sample 4k–5k pre-1750 tier-0 records stratified by `dc_type` (Leichenpredigt, Monografie, Einblattdruck) and decade\n2. Write annotation prompt with:\n   - Label definitions (FRBR Work scope: TITLE, OTHER_TITLE, PERSON)\n   - Inline Bracketed output format\n   - Pre-1750 author-before-title rule\n   - 5 few-shot examples drawn from the SR-09 gold set (once available) or manually annotated\n3. Run Claude API in batches; log model version and prompt hash for reproducibility\n4. Spot-check 200 records manually (~5%) — compute agreement rate; if below 85%, revise prompt and re-run\n5. Use as fine-tuning data; evaluate on SR-09 gold set (held out, human-annotated)\n\n### 2.4 Bootstrapping problem\n\nThe few-shot examples in the prompt should ideally come from the SR-09 gold set — but SR-09 requires annotation work first. Two options:\n\n- **Option A (sequential):** Annotate 50–100 records manually as the prompt seed; use these to generate 4k–5k LLM labels; use the LLM-labeled set for fine-tuning and a separate 200-record human sample as the evaluation set\n- **Option B (parallel):** Run Claude zero-shot first, spot-check 200 records, use the verified subset as few-shot examples for a second pass\n\nOption A is cleaner — the 50-record manual seed is also the start of the SR-09 gold set.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Modern stratum — no labeling needed\n\nSilver tier-1 (335,524 records) and tier-2 (4,613 records) cover the modern stratum with ISBD-derived labels. These are sufficient for fine-tuning the modern register without any additional annotation. The LLM labeling effort above is exclusively for the pre-1750 gap.\n"
}
```

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T14:51:43.648Z)*

Created [labeling-strategy.md](gemea/notes/ner/labeling-strategy.md). Key points:

- **Work only (3 types):** 1k–2k records — sufficient, efficient
- **Work + Expression (6 types):** 3k–5k records — Expression types are sparse per record, so you need more records to accumulate enough spans
- **Manifestation:** defer — ISBD silver already covers it for modern records

**On using Claude as annotator:** yes, it's a sound approach and effectively what NuNER's authors did with GPT-3.5. The note covers the workflow in detail. Two specific risks worth flagging for Early Modern German: the author-before-title pattern needs an explicit rule in the system prompt, and you'll want 5 few-shot examples to anchor the format — which means annotating ~50 records by hand first as a prompt seed (those 50 records also become the start of your SR-09 gold set). The spot-check threshold is ~85% agreement; below that, revise the prompt before running the full batch.

   331→For records where a GND Werk URI was confirmed via the local GND instance, the extracted title string is a positive `TITLE` example. Linked person GND URIs supply `PERSON` labels. Useful to augment silver labels, not sufficient alone.
   332→
   333→---
   334→
   335→## 9. Available datasets for fine-tuning
   336→
   337→| Dataset | Labels | Language | Size | Relevance |
   338→|---|---|---|---|---|
   339→| [empathyai/books-ner-dataset](https://huggingface.co/datasets/empathyai/books-ner-dataset) | TITLE, AUTHOR | English | Project Gutenberg catalogues | Closest label match; English only — use for domain-transfer pretraining |
   340→| [HIPE-2022 (ajmc)](https://github.com/hipe-eval/HIPE-2022-data) | Fine-grained bibliographic refs | German, French, English | ~10K tokens | Historical document NER with bibliographic references; German included. Top systems used `xlm-roberta-large` as backbone. Ehrmann et al. (2022) ⚠️ *verify citation* |
   341→| [CLEF-HIPE-2020](https://zenodo.org/record/3836029) | PER, ORG, LOC, PROD (work titles) | German, French, English | Historical newspapers | PROD label covers work titles; 19th-century orthography. XLM-R large dominant in top submissions. Ehrmann et al. (2020) ⚠️ *verify citation* |
   342→| [GermEval 2014](https://sites.google.com/site/germeval2014ner/) | PER, ORG, LOC, OTH | German | ~31K sentences | Modern German only; useful only for PERSON transfer |
   343→
   344→**Recommended fine-tuning path**:
   345→1. Pretrain on `empathyai/books-ner-dataset` for TITLE/AUTHOR signal (domain transfer)
   346→2. Fine-tune on silver-labeled DDB records
   347→3. Supplement with HIPE-2022 ajmc and CLEF-HIPE-2020 for historical German signal
   348→4. Evaluate on a gold set stratified by era (modern, 19th c., 1700–1800, pre-1700) — no dedicated Latin stratum needed (SR-06: ~0.5% prevalence)
   349→
   350→**Alternative generative path (from Zhan et al. 2026):** Fine-tuned open-source LLMs (Qwen3-1.7B or LLaMA3.2-1B) with LoRA + Inline Bracketed/XML format match encoder-based models on general-domain NER (CoNLL2003 F1 ≈ 90–94 for 7–8B models; 1–4B models lag by ~10 F1 points). Add a fine-tuned small LLM as a benchmark in SR-08 alongside `xlm-roberta-large` and `mdeberta-v3-base`. Note: Zhan et al. evaluate on modern English/general-domain benchmarks only — performance on historical German bibliographic NER is unknown and likely lower (cf. GENIA biomedical gap). See [ref_zhan2026-generative-ner.md](ner/ref_zhan2026-generative-ner.md).

### User *(2026-03-24T14:55:07.127Z)*

**Tool result**
```
     1→# NER Labeling Strategy — Dataset Size and LLM Annotation
     2→
     3→Related: [ner-bibliographic.md §8](../ner-bibliographic.md), [SR-09](../ner-bibliographic.md#29-sr-09--gold-set-composition)
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## 1. Fine-tuning dataset size by FRBR scope
     8→
     9→Rule of thumb for domain-specific NER fine-tuning from a strong multilingual checkpoint (xlm-roberta-large): **200–500 annotated spans per entity type** is the practical minimum for reliable boundary learning. Below ~200 per type, type confusion and boundary errors dominate; above ~500, returns diminish rapidly for a focused label set.
    10→
    11→Translating to record counts (each bibliographic title typically yields 1–3 labeled spans):
    12→
    13→| Scope | Label types | Spans needed | Est. records needed | Notes |
    14→|---|---|---|---|---|
    15→| Work only | `TITLE`, `OTHER_TITLE`, `PERSON` | ~900–1,500 spans | **1k–2k records** | Most titles have all three; efficient labeling |
    16→| Work + Expression | + `TRANSLATOR`, `PARALLEL_TITLE`, `MEDIUM` | ~1,800–3,000 spans | **3k–5k records** | Expression types are sparse per record — need more records to reach span count |
    17→| Full (incl. Manifestation) | + `PUBLISHER`, `PLACE`, `YEAR`, `EDITION`, `SERIES`, `VOLUME` | ~3,000–6,000 spans | **5k–10k records** | Manifestation fields extractable from ISBD silver; less urgent to label manually |
    18→
    19→**Practical implication:** Work-only scope (Phase 1, per SR-07) is achievable with 1k–2k LLM-labeled records for the pre-1750 stratum. Extending to Expression adds 2k–3k more. Manifestation labels can be deferred — the ISBD silver set already covers these for the modern stratum.
    20→
    21→The pre-1750 stratum is the binding constraint in all cases: it is entirely tier-0, so no silver labels exist regardless of scope. Modern records have 335k silver tier-1 and 4,613 tier-2 records — fine-tuning on the modern stratum is not the bottleneck.
    22→
    23→---
    24→
    25→## 2. LLM as annotator — using Claude
    26→
    27→Using Claude (or another capable LLM) to generate the pre-1750 labeled dataset is the most practical path. This is architecturally identical to the approach used to build NuNER's training corpus (GPT-3.5 annotating 1M C4 sentences; Bogdanov et al., EMNLP 2024) and is consistent with the §8.2 recommendation in ner-bibliographic.md.
    28→
    29→### 2.1 Why it works
    30→
    31→- Claude handles Early Modern German well — Fraktur-adjacent orthography (`vnd`, `deß`, `seyn`) is within its training distribution from historical corpora
    32→- Bibliographic structure is well-represented in Claude's pretraining (library catalogs, digitized text)
    33→- Label definitions (TITLE, PERSON, TRANSLATOR) are semantically clear — low ambiguity for an LLM
    34→- Inline Bracketed output format works reliably for Claude (see Zhan et al. 2026 — same format family tops generative NER benchmarks)
    35→- Cost is negligible: 4k records × ~300 tokens average ≈ 1.2M tokens, a small fraction of API budget
    36→
    37→### 2.2 Risks and mitigations
    38→
    39→| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |
    40→|---|---|---|
    41→| Systematic errors on author-before-title (pre-1750) | High | Explicit annotation guideline in system prompt: "In pre-1750 titles, the author's name and credentials appear before the main title, not after ` /`. Label the opening name+credentials span as PERSON." |
    42→| Type confusion: PERSON vs. TRANSLATOR | Medium | Include disambiguation rule: "Label as TRANSLATOR only if a translation keyword is present (`übersetzt`, `Übers.`, `transl.`, `traduit`)." |
    43→| Hallucinated spans (text not in input) | Low–Medium | Use Inline Bracketed format — spans are extracted inline from the input string, reducing hallucination compared to generative span prediction |
    44→| Systematic bias across similar records | Medium | Evaluate on the SR-09 gold set (human-annotated, independent); if LLM errors cluster on a dc_type or era, resample |
    45→| Early Modern abbreviations / title-page conventions | Medium | Include 3–5 annotated examples in the prompt (few-shot) covering the main patterns |
    46→
    47→### 2.3 Recommended workflow
    48→
    49→1. Sample 4k–5k pre-1750 tier-0 records stratified by `dc_type` (Leichenpredigt, Monografie, Einblattdruck) and decade
    50→2. Write annotation prompt with:
    51→   - Label definitions (FRBR Work scope: TITLE, OTHER_TITLE, PERSON)
    52→   - Inline Bracketed output format
    53→   - Pre-1750 author-before-title rule
    54→   - 5 few-shot examples drawn from the SR-09 gold set (once available) or manually annotated
    55→3. Run Claude API in batches; log model version and prompt hash for reproducibility
    56→4. Spot-check 200 records manually (~5%) — compute agreement rate; if below 85%, revise prompt and re-run
    57→5. Use as fine-tuning data; evaluate on SR-09 gold set (held out, human-annotated)
    58→
    59→### 2.4 Bootstrapping problem
    60→
    61→The few-shot examples in the prompt should ideally come from the SR-09 gold set — but SR-09 requires annotation work first. Two options:
    62→
    63→- **Option A (sequential):** Annotate 50–100 records manually as the prompt seed; use these to generate 4k–5k LLM labels; use the LLM-labeled set for fine-tuning and a separate 200-record human sample as the evaluation set
    64→- **Option B (parallel):** Run Claude zero-shot first, spot-check 200 records, use the verified subset as few-shot examples for a second pass
    65→
    66→Option A is cleaner — the 50-record manual seed is also the start of the SR-09 gold set.
    67→
    68→---
    69→
    70→## 3. Modern stratum — no labeling needed
    71→
    72→Silver tier-1 (335,524 records) and tier-2 (4,613 records) cover the modern stratum with ISBD-derived labels. These are sufficient for fine-tuning the modern register without any additional annotation. The LLM labeling effort above is exclusively for the pre-1750 gap.
    73→



```

   126→**Status:** Future — blocked on SR-03 extension, SR-04 completion, SR-09 completion

   128→See [sr11_field-level-weighting.md](ner/sr11_field-level-weighting.md) for full requirements.
   129→
   130→- **Goal:** replace binary `n_fields ≥ 3` threshold with a precision-weighted score (`score = Σ (1 − FP_rate_i)` over active fields), enabling principled tier-boundary calibration and a potential four-tier split for curriculum fine-tuning
   131→- **Missing FP data:** `f_volume` (1.9% coverage) and `f_publisher` (0.2%) not yet sampled — extend SR-03 with ~100 records each
   132→- **Calibration:** tier boundary requires SR-09 gold set to measure NER F1 at different score cutoffs (external calibration preferred over internal proxy)
   133→- **Conditional FP rates** (optional): field co-occurrence effects (e.g. `f_year AND f_other_title` vs `f_year` alone) need a joint sample if a four-tier split is pursued
   134→- **Primary motivation:** curriculum learning — train first on high-confidence tier-2 + high-score tier-1 records, then add lower-confidence augmentation
   135→

  "old_string": "### 2.11 SR-12 — Field-level weighting for silver tier assignment\n**Status:** Future — blocked on SR-03 extension, SR-04 completion, SR-09 completion\n\nSee [sr12_field-level-weighting.md](ner/sr12_field-level-weighting.md) for full requirements.\n\n- **Goal:** replace binary `n_fields ≥ 3` threshold with a precision-weighted score (`score = Σ (1 − FP_rate_i)` over active fields), enabling principled tier-boundary calibration and a potential four-tier split for curriculum fine-tuning\n- **Missing FP data:** `f_volume` (1.9% coverage) and `f_publisher` (0.2%) not yet sampled — extend SR-03 with ~100 records each\n- **Calibration:** tier boundary requires SR-09 gold set to measure NER F1 at different score cutoffs (external calibration preferred over internal proxy)\n- **Conditional FP rates** (optional): field co-occurrence effects (e.g. `f_year AND f_other_title` vs `f_year` alone) need a joint sample if a four-tier split is pursued\n- **Primary motivation:** curriculum learning — train first on high-confidence tier-2 + high-score tier-1 records, then add lower-confidence augmentation\n\n### 2.12 SR-11 — Labeling strategy and prompt design (pre-1750)\n**Status:** Open — blocked on SR-09 seed (50-record manual annotation)\n\nSee [sr11_labeling-strategy.md](ner/sr11_labeling-strategy.md) for full content.\n\n- **Dataset size:** Work-only (3 types) requires 1k–2k records; Work + Expression (6 types) requires 3k–5k; Manifestation deferred — ISBD silver covers modern stratum\n- **Annotator:** Claude API with Inline Bracketed output format; 4k–5k pre-1750 tier-0 records, stratified by `dc_type` and decade\n- **Bootstrapping:** annotate 50–100 records manually as prompt seed (these also seed SR-09 gold set); spot-check 200 LLM-labeled records (~5%); re-run if agreement < 85%\n- **Early Modern German prompt:** system prompt must encode author-before-title rule, credential sequence patterns, orthographic markers, and embedded-Latin disambiguation; spot-check 200 LLM-labeled records (~5%); re-run if agreement < 85%\n- **Early Modern German prompt:** system prompt must encode author-before-title rule, credential sequence patterns, orthographic markers, and embedded-Latin disambiguation;
