### Assistant *(2026-03-24T15:00:29.680Z)*

**Tool call:** `Edit`
```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/ner/sr11_labeling-strategy.md",
  "old_string": "## 3. Modern stratum — no labeling needed\n\nSilver tier-1 (335,524 records) and tier-2 (4,613 records) cover the modern stratum with ISBD-derived labels. These are sufficient for fine-tuning the modern register without any additional annotation. The LLM labeling effort above is exclusively for the pre-1750 gap.",
  "new_string": "## 3. Modern stratum — no labeling needed\n\nSilver tier-1 (335,524 records) and tier-2 (4,613 records) cover the modern stratum with ISBD-derived labels. These are sufficient for fine-tuning the modern register without any additional annotation. The LLM labeling effort above is exclusively for the pre-1750 gap.\n\n---\n\n## 4. Early Modern German prompt design\n\n### 4.1 Why Early Modern German requires a dedicated prompt\n\nStandard NER prompts fail on pre-1750 German bibliographic titles for three structural reasons:\n\n1. **Author-before-title structure** — the author's name, academic credentials, and role appear at the start of the title string, not after a ` /` separator. A prompt that asks \"who wrote this?\" in a modern bibliographic sense will either miss the author entirely or conflate them with the title.\n2. **Credential sequences as part of PERSON spans** — the PERSON span typically includes the author's title chain: `D. Johann Georg Dorsche, der H. Schrifft ordentlichen Lehrers`. The degree abbreviation (`D.`, `M.`, `Lic.`), full name, and role description are all part of the PERSON span.\n3. **Embedded Latin vocabulary** — words like `Anno`, `Christi`, `Jesu`, `Doctor`, `Oratio`, `Disputatio` appear in otherwise German titles and do not indicate a Latin-language record. A prompt must not treat these as signals for a Latin entity type.\n\n### 4.2 System prompt\n\n```\nYou are a bibliographic NER annotator specialising in Early Modern German (1500–1750) library catalog records.\n\n## Task\nLabel named entity spans in the given title string using Inline Bracketed format:\n  [span text | LABEL]\n\n## Labels\n- TITLE — the main work title; the primary intellectual content identifier\n- OTHER_TITLE — subtitle or alternative title, typically introduced by \"Das ist:\", \"oder\", \"nämlich\", a colon, or a comma after the main title\n- PERSON — the author, editor, or other responsible person; includes their full name AND any preceding or following credentials, academic titles, or role descriptions that form a single naming unit\n\n## Critical rule: author-before-title structure\nIn pre-1750 German titles, the author's name and credentials appear BEFORE the main title, not after a slash ( / ). The typical structure is:\n\n  [credentials + name | PERSON] [main title | TITLE] [subtitle | OTHER_TITLE]\n\nExample:\n  Input:  \"D. Johann Gerhard, Professoris zu Jena, Erklärung der Historien des Leidens vnd Sterbens vnsers HErrn Christi\"\n  Output: \"[D. Johann Gerhard, Professoris zu Jena | PERSON] [Erklärung der Historien des Leidens vnd Sterbens vnsers HErrn Christi | TITLE]\"\n\n## PERSON span boundaries\nInclude in the PERSON span:\n- Academic degree abbreviations immediately before the name: D. (Doktor), M. (Magister), Lic. (Licentiatus), Mag.\n- Full personal name (first name + surname)\n- Role or position descriptions following the name: \"Pfarrers zu X\", \"der H. Schrifft Lehrers\", \"Professoris\", \"Pastoris\", \"Superintendenten\"\n- Genitive or prepositional phrases that identify the person's post: \"zu Jena\", \"in Leipzig\", \"bey der Gemeine zu X\"\n\nStop the PERSON span at the first token that is clearly part of the title (a content noun, verb phrase, or \"Das ist:\").\n\n## Embedded Latin — do NOT create a separate LATIN label\nWords such as Anno, Christi, Jesu, Doctor, Oratio, Disputatio, Theses embedded in an otherwise German title are standard Early Modern Protestant/academic vocabulary. They do not make the record Latin. Label the title as TITLE regardless.\n\n## OTHER_TITLE triggers\nLabel as OTHER_TITLE the span introduced by:\n- \"Das ist:\" / \"Das ist,\"\n- \"oder\" (when introducing an alternative title)\n- A colon after a complete TITLE span\n- \"nämlich\", \"welches handelt von\", \"begreiffend\"\n\n## Orthographic note\nDo not treat early modern spelling variants as annotation errors. The following are standard and should be annotated normally:\n- vnd / und (and), seyn / sein (to be), deß / des (genitive), jhm / ihm, auch / auch\n- ck clusters: drucken, Glück, Stück\n- Double vowels: heer, Lehr, seelig\n- Final -en dropped: Herrn, Christn\n- u/v interchange: vber = über, vnser = unser\n\n## What NOT to label\n- Dedicatees (\"Dem … gewidmet\", \"Herrn N.N. zu Ehren\") — do not label as PERSON unless the person is also the author\n- Occasion descriptions (\"Bey dem Leichbegängnis\", \"Gehalten am …\") — do not label\n- Place of publication, printer, year — do not label (Manifestation level, out of scope)\n- Generic role nouns without a personal name (\"dem geneigten Leser\", \"der Christlichen Gemeine\")\n```\n\n### 4.3 Few-shot examples\n\nInclude 5 examples covering the main structural patterns. These should be drawn from the manually annotated SR-09 seed set once available. Until then, use the following constructed examples (verify against real DDB records before use in production):\n\n**Pattern A — credential + name + role + title:**\n```\nInput:  \"M. Andreas Musculus, Pfarrherr zu Frankfurt an der Oder, Vom Ehebruch vnd Hurerey\"\nOutput: \"[M. Andreas Musculus, Pfarrherr zu Frankfurt an der Oder | PERSON] [Vom Ehebruch vnd Hurerey | TITLE]\"\n```\n\n**Pattern B — title first, author after \"durch\"/\"von\":**\n```\nInput:  \"Christliche Leichpredigt, Vber den Tödtlichen Hintrit … durch Johann Schmidt, Pfarrern zu Straßburg\"\nOutput: \"[Christliche Leichpredigt | TITLE] [Vber den Tödtlichen Hintrit … | OTHER_TITLE] [Johann Schmidt, Pfarrern zu Straßburg | PERSON]\"\n```\n\n**Pattern C — \"Das ist:\" subtitle:**\n```\nInput:  \"Geistlicher Seelen-Schatz. Das ist: Außführliche Erklärung der fürnembsten Geheimnissen\"\nOutput: \"[Geistlicher Seelen-Schatz | TITLE] [Außführliche Erklärung der fürnembsten Geheimnissen | OTHER_TITLE]\"\n```\n\n**Pattern D — embedded Latin, no PERSON visible:**\n```\nInput:  \"Oratio de vita et obitu Doctoris Martini Lutheri Anno MDXLVI\"\nOutput: \"[Oratio de vita et obitu Doctoris Martini Lutheri Anno MDXLVI | TITLE]\"\n```\n*(Full string is Latin — label as a single TITLE. Do not extract \"Martini Lutheri\" as PERSON from a Latin oration title without clear authorship signal.)*\n\n**Pattern E — multiple persons (author + dedicatee):**\n```\nInput:  \"D. Caspar Calvör, Pastoris zu Clausthal, Geistliche Haußhaltung … Herrn Anton Ulrich, Hertzogen zu Braunschweig, gewidmet\"\nOutput: \"[D. Caspar Calvör, Pastoris zu Clausthal | PERSON] [Geistliche Haußhaltung | TITLE] [Herrn Anton Ulrich, Hertzogen zu Braunschweig | PERSON]\"\n```\n*(Only label dedicatee as PERSON if they are a named individual — not generic dedications.)*\n\n### 4.4 User prompt template\n\n```\nAnnotate the following Early Modern German bibliographic title. Return only the annotated string, nothing else.\n\nTitle: {title_string}\n```\n\n### 4.5 Batch processing notes\n\n- **Batch size:** 20–50 records per API call using a list format; reduces per-call overhead\n- **Temperature:** 0 — deterministic output required for reproducibility\n- **Model:** `claude-opus-4-6` preferred for Early Modern German accuracy; `claude-sonnet-4-6` acceptable for throughput at scale\n- **Log:** record model ID, prompt hash (SHA-256 of system prompt), and timestamp per batch\n- **Failure modes to detect in post-processing:**\n  - Output longer than input (hallucinated text) → flag and discard\n  - No brackets in output (model returned plain text) → retry once, then discard\n  - Unclosed bracket → flag for manual review\n  - Label not in `{TITLE, OTHER_TITLE, PERSON}` → flag for review\n\n### 4.6 Prompt iteration checklist\n\nBefore running the full 4k–5k batch:\n\n- [ ] Annotate 50 records manually (drawn from SR-09 seed set)\n- [ ] Run prompt on same 50 records; compute token-level F1 and span-level exact match\n- [ ] Check: is PERSON recall ≥ 80% on pre-1750 records? (author-before-title is the main failure mode)\n- [ ] Check: is TITLE boundary correct on \"Das ist:\" records?\n- [ ] Check: are embedded Latin tokens (`Anno`, `Christi`) correctly not labelled as separate entities?\n- [ ] If agreement < 85% on any label type: revise system prompt, add a targeted few-shot example, re-run on 50 records\n- [ ] Once ≥ 85% agreement on all three label types: proceed with full batch"
}
```

Early Modern German prompt design\n\n### 4.1 Why Early Modern German requires a dedicated prompt\n\nStandard NER prompts fail on pre-1750 German bibliographic titles for three structural reasons:\n\n1. **Author-before-title structure** — the author's name, academic credentials, and role appear at the start of the title string, not after a ` /` separator. A prompt that expects modern bibliographic conventions will either miss the author or conflate the credential sequence with the title.\n2. **Credential sequences as part of PERSON spans** — the PERSON span typically includes the author's degree chain: `D. Johann Georg Dorsche, der H. Schrifft ordentlichen Lehrers`. Degree abbreviation, full name, and role description are all part of the PERSON span.\n3. **Embedded Latin vocabulary** — words like `Anno`, `Christi`, `Jesu`, `Doctor`, `Oratio`, `Disputatio` appear in otherwise German titles and do not indicate a Latin-language record. A prompt must not treat these as signals for a Latin entity type (see SR-06: true Latin prevalence ~0.5%).\n\n### 4.2 System prompt\n\n~~~\nYou are a bibliographic NER annotator specialising in Early Modern German (1500–1750) library catalog records.\n\n## Task\nLabel named entity spans in the given title string using Inline Bracketed format:\n  [span text | LABEL]\n\n## Labels\n- TITLE — the main work title; the primary intellectual content identifier\n- OTHER_TITLE — subtitle or alternative title, typically introduced by \"Das ist:\", \"oder\", a colon, or \"nämlich\"\n- PERSON — the author, editor, or other responsible person; includes their full name AND any preceding or following credentials, academic titles, or role descriptions that form a single naming unit\n\n## Critical rule: author-before-title structure\nIn pre-1750 German titles, the author's name and credentials appear BEFORE the main title, not after a slash ( / ). The typical structure is:\n\n  [credentials + name + role | PERSON] [main title | TITLE] [subtitle | OTHER_TITLE]\n\nExample:\n  Input:  \"D. Johann Gerhard, Professoris zu Jena, Erklärung der Historien des Leidens vnd Sterbens vnsers HErrn Christi\"\n  Output: \"[D. Johann Gerhard, Professoris zu Jena | PERSON] [Erklärung der Historien des Leidens vnd Sterbens vnsers HErrn Christi | TITLE]\"\n\n## PERSON span boundaries\nInclude in the PERSON span:\n- Academic degree abbreviations immediately before the name: D. (Doktor), M. (Magister), Lic. (Licentiatus), Mag.\n- Full personal name (first name + surname)\n- Role or position descriptions following the name: \"Pfarrers zu X\", \"der H. Schrifft Lehrers\", \"Professoris\", \"Pastoris\", \"Superintendenten\"\n- Genitive or prepositional phrases identifying the post: \"zu Jena\", \"in Leipzig\", \"bey der Gemeine zu X\"\n\nStop the PERSON span at the first token that is clearly part of the work title (a content noun, verb phrase, or \"Das ist:\").\n\n## Embedded Latin — do NOT create a separate LATIN label\nWords such as Anno, Christi, Jesu, Doctor, Oratio, Disputatio embedded in an otherwise German title are standard Early Modern Protestant/academic vocabulary. They do not make the record Latin. Label the surrounding title normally as TITLE.\n\n## OTHER_TITLE triggers\nLabel as OTHER_TITLE the span introduced by:\n- \"Das ist:\" / \"Das ist,\"\n- \"oder\" (when introducing an alternative title, not a connector within the title)\n- A colon after a complete TITLE span\n- \"nämlich\", \"welches handelt von\", \"begreiffend\"\n\n## Orthographic note\nDo not treat early modern spelling variants as annotation errors. The following are standard:\n- vnd / vnndt (and), seyn / sein (to be), deß / des (genitive), jhm / ihm\n- u/v interchange: vber = über, vnser = unser, viel = viel\n- ck clusters: drucken, Glück, Stück\n- Double vowels: heer, Lehr, seelig\n- Final -enn/-en variation: Herrenn, Christenn\n\n## What NOT to label\n- Dedicatees (\"Dem … gewidmet\", \"Herrn N.N. zu Ehren\") — do not label as PERSON unless they are also the author\n- Occasion phrases (\"Bey dem Leichbegängnis\", \"Gehalten am …\") — do not label\n- Place of publication, printer, year — do not label (out of scope)\n- Generic role nouns without a personal name (\"dem geneigten Leser\", \"der Christlichen Gemeine\")\n~~~\n\n### 4.3 Few-shot examples\n\nInclude 5 examples covering the main structural patterns. Production examples should come from the manually annotated SR-09 seed set. Until then, use the following (verify against real DDB records before the full batch run):\n\n**Pattern A — credential + name + role + title (most common pre-1750 pattern):**\n```\nInput:  \"M. Andreas Musculus, Pfarrherr zu Frankfurt an der Oder, Vom Ehebruch vnd Hurerey\"\nOutput: \"[M. Andreas Musculus, Pfarrherr zu Frankfurt an der Oder | PERSON] [Vom Ehebruch vnd Hurerey | TITLE]\"\n```\n\n**Pattern B — title first, author after \"durch\" / \"von\":**\n```\nInput:  \"Christliche Leichpredigt, Vber den Tödtlichen Hintrit … durch Johann Schmidt, Pfarrern zu Straßburg\"\nOutput: \"[Christliche Leichpredigt | TITLE] [Vber den Tödtlichen Hintrit … | OTHER_TITLE] [Johann Schmidt, Pfarrern zu Straßburg | PERSON]\"\n```\n\n**Pattern C — \"Das ist:\" subtitle:**\n```\nInput:  \"Geistlicher Seelen-Schatz. Das ist: Außführliche Erklärung der fürnembsten Geheimnissen\"\nOutput: \"[Geistlicher Seelen-Schatz | TITLE] [Außführliche Erklärung der fürnembsten Geheimnissen | OTHER_TITLE]\"\n```\n\n**Pattern D — embedded Latin, no extractable PERSON:**\n```\nInput:  \"Oratio de vita et obitu Doctoris Martini Lutheri Anno MDXLVI\"\nOutput: \"[Oratio de vita et obitu Doctoris Martini Lutheri Anno MDXLVI | TITLE]\"\n```\n*(Full string is Latin — label as a single TITLE. Do not extract \"Martini Lutheri\" as PERSON from a Latin oration title without a clear authorship signal outside the title string.)*\n\n**Pattern E — author + dedicatee (label both as PERSON):**\n```\nInput:  \"D. Caspar Calvör, Pastoris zu Clausthal, Geistliche Haußhaltung … Herrn Anton Ulrich, Hertzogen zu Braunschweig, gewidmet\"\nOutput: \"[D. Caspar Calvör, Pastoris zu Clausthal | PERSON] [Geistliche Haußhaltung | TITLE] [Herrn Anton Ulrich, Hertzogen zu Braunschweig | PERSON]\"\n```\n*(Label named dedicatees as PERSON — not generic dedications like \"der Christlichen Gemeine gewidmet\".)*\n\n### 4.4 User prompt template\n\n~~~\nAnnotate the following Early Modern German bibliographic title. Return only the annotated string, nothing else.\n\nTitle: {title_string}\n~~~\n\nFor batch processing, send a numbered list in a single call:\n\n~~~\nAnnotate each of the following Early Modern German bibliographic titles. Return a numbered list with only the annotated strings, one per line.\n\n1. {title_1}\n2. {title_2}\n...\n~~~\n\n### 4.5 Batch processing notes\n\n- **Batch size:** 20–50 records per API call\n- **Temperature:** 0 — deterministic output required for reproducibility\n- **Model:** `claude-opus-4-6` preferred for accuracy on Early Modern German; `claude-sonnet-4-6` for throughput at scale\n- **Log:** model ID, SHA-256 of system prompt, timestamp, and batch index per call\n- **Failure modes to detect in post-processing:**\n\n| Failure | Detection | Action |\n|---|---|---|\n| Hallucinated text (output longer than input) | `len(output_spans_text) > len(input)` | Discard |\n| No brackets in output | `[` not in output | Retry once, then discard |\n| Unclosed bracket | Unmatched `[` or `\\|` | Flag for manual review |\n| Unknown label | Label not in `{TITLE, OTHER_TITLE, PERSON}` | Flag for review |\n| Span text not found in input | Substring match fails | Discard (hallucinated span) |\n\n### 4.6 Prompt iteration checklist\n\nBefore running the full 4k–5k batch:\n\n- [ ] Annotate 50 records manually (drawn from the dc_type distribution of the target batch)\n- [ ] Run prompt on same 50 records; compute span-level exact-match F1 per label type\n- [ ] PERSON recall ≥ 80% on pre-1750 records? (author-before-title is the main failure mode)\n- [ ] TITLE boundary correct on \"Das ist:\" records?\n- [ ] Embedded Latin tokens (`Anno`, `Christi`) not labelled as separate entities?\n- [ ] If agreement < 85% on any label type: revise system prompt, add targeted few-shot example, re-run on 50 records\n- [ ] Once ≥ 85% on all three label types: proceed with full batch"
}

- §4.2: Full system prompt — labels, author-before-title rule, PERSON span boundaries, embedded Latin rule, OTHER_TITLE triggers, orthographic note, what not to label
- §4.3: 5 few-shot examples covering patterns A–E (credential+title, title+author, "Das ist:", Latin oratio, author+dedicatee)
- §4.4: User prompt template (single and batch format)
- §4.5: Batch notes — model choice, temperature, logging, failure detection table
- §4.6: Pre-batch iteration checklist with 85% agreement threshold

```
     1→# GeMeA — Prerequisites
     2→
     3→CS concepts, algorithms, frameworks, design patterns, programming languages, and technical skills needed to build GeMeA independently.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Semantic Web & Knowledge Graphs
     8→
     9→- **RDF** (Resource Description Framework): triple model (subject–predicate–object), named graphs
    10→- **SPARQL 1.1**: SELECT, CONSTRUCT, DESCRIBE, ASK; property paths; OPTIONAL; FILTER; aggregation; LIMIT/OFFSET; federated queries
    11→- **OWL** (Web Ontology Language): classes, properties, restrictions, inference/reasoning basics
    12→- **Europeana Data Model (EDM)**: ProvidedCHO, Aggregation, WebResource, Agent, Place, TimeSpan; edm:isShownAt, edm:hasView
    13→- **RDA (Resource Description & Access)**: WEMI hierarchy (Work, Expression, Manifestation, Item); Group 2/3 entities
    14→- **FRBR**: Functional Requirements for Bibliographic Records; entity groups
    15→- **JSON-LD**: @context, @id, @type, @graph; compaction/expansion; Framing
    16→- **RDF/JSON serialization** (W3C): subject-keyed object with predicate arrays
    17→- **Turtle / N-Triples**: for bulk loading and data inspection
    18→- **Named graphs / quads**: NQuads, TriG (for provenance tracking by provider/batch)
    19→- **Namespace prefixes**: dc, dcterms, edm, ore, skos, rdfs, owl, foaf, schema, gnd, wikidata
    20→
    21→## Graph Databases
    22→
    23→- **QLever**: `qlever index` (build index from N-Triples), `qlever start` (launch server), Qleverfile configuration (`Mmap`, `num-threads`, `text-index`), SPARQL endpoint, built-in text search (`ql:contains-entity`), spatial queries, named graphs
    24→- **Triplestore bulk load patterns**: N-Triples chunk loading, parallel load, checkpoint/restart
    25→- **SPARQL query optimization**: LIMIT early, use named graphs, avoid Cartesian products, query timeout configuration
    26→- **Fallback options** (self-hosting v2): Apache Jena TDB2 + Fuseki (tdbloader2, Fuseki server config); Virtuoso OSE (ISQL, `rdf_loader_run()`)
    27→
    28→## Search & Information Retrieval
    29→
    30→- **Elasticsearch 8.x**: index mapping, analyzers (standard, German language analyzer `de`), BM25 ranking, faceted search (terms aggregations), highlighting, `multi_match`, `query_string`, pagination (from/size, `search_after`)
    31→- **Index design for cultural heritage**: field types (keyword vs. text), nested objects for multilingual fields, GeoPoint fields for geo-search
    32→- **Faceted search UX**: facet selection, AND/OR logic, cardinality limits
    33→- **Query federation**: combining Elasticsearch text results with SPARQL graph enrichment
    34→
    35→## Backend (Python / FastAPI)
    36→
    37→- **FastAPI**: path operations, async handlers, Pydantic models, dependency injection, middleware (CORS, caching)
    38→- **SPARQL client**: `SPARQLWrapper` or `httpx` for async HTTP calls to QLever SPARQL endpoint
    39→- **Elasticsearch Python client** (`elasticsearch-py 8.x`): async client, index lifecycle
    40→- **Async Python**: `asyncio`, `httpx`, background tasks
    41→- **Caching**: Redis for SPARQL result caching; HTTP cache headers (`Cache-Control`, ETags)
    42→- **Pagination patterns**: cursor-based vs. offset for large result sets
    43→- **API design**: REST conventions, OpenAPI/Swagger auto-docs, versioning (`/v1/`)
    44→
    45→## Ontology Alignment & mocho
    46→
    47→- **Ontology alignment**: mapping classes and properties between source ontology (EDM) and target ontology (RDA/FRBR); `owl:equivalentClass`, `owl:equivalentProperty`; SPARQL CONSTRUCT for rule-based transformation
    48→- **mocho** (`../mocho/`): GeMeA's alignment tool; takes rdf2jsonld output + GND Werk triples → produces `mocho:Work` entities + RDA-normalized triples; mocho.owl is WIP
    49→- **mocho:Work**: mocho-specific class grouping `edm:ProvidedCHO` instances that share a GND Werk URI; the grouping key is the GND Werk link produced by `link_gnd_works.py` (must run before mocho)
    50→- **FRBR entity creation**: promoting a flat `edm:ProvidedCHO` record into the WEMI hierarchy (Work → Expression → Manifestation → Item); requires external authority linkage (GND Werktitel) to identify Work-level groupings
    51→- **OWL reasoning**: forward chaining, materialization; difference between declarative alignment (reasoner applies equivalences) vs. procedural transformation (SPARQL CONSTRUCT rules)
    52→- **rdf2jsonld** (`../rdf2jsonld/`): converts DDB JSON-LD → W3C RDF/JSON; repairs URI malformations; parallel processing per provider batch
    53→
    54→## ETL / Data Engineering
    55→
    56→- **RDF bulk loading**: mocho N-Triples → QLever index via `qlever index`
    57→- **Named graph assignment**: partition by provider for manageability and per-provider reload
    58→- **Python RDF libraries**: `rdflib` (parsing, serialization, graph manipulation)
    59→- **Streaming / chunked processing**: process 65M records without loading all into memory (NDJSON, generators)
    60→- **Elasticsearch bulk indexing**: `helpers.bulk()`, index rollover for large datasets
    61→- **Process orchestration**: sequential shell scripts for v1; Luigi or Airflow for v2
    62→- **Monitoring ingestion**: progress logging, checkpointing, error recovery
    63→
    64→## Frontend (Next.js / React)
    65→
    66→- **Next.js 14+**: App Router, server components, `fetch` with caching, dynamic routes (`/item/[id]`), ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration) for entity pages
    67→- **React**: hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`, `useCallback`), context, server vs. client components
    68→- **Tailwind CSS**: responsive design (`sm:`, `md:`, `lg:`), dark mode, component utility patterns
    69→- **Search UX patterns**: debounced input, loading states, empty states, result highlighting
    70→- **Facet sidebar**: collapsible sections, URL-synced state (`URLSearchParams`), keyboard accessibility
    71→
    72→## Visualization
    73→
    74→- **Cytoscape.js**: graph rendering in canvas/SVG, layout algorithms (COSE, Dagre, Breadthfirst), node/edge styling, event handling, performance with large graphs (virtual rendering)
    75→- **Leaflet.js**: tile layers (OSM), marker clusters, GeoJSON overlays, popups, map bounds from data
    76→- **D3.js** (timeline): time scales, brush/zoom, histogram of objects per year
    77→- **Canvas vs. SVG**: SVG for small graphs (<500 nodes), canvas for large; Cytoscape handles both
    78→
    79→## Data
    80→
    81→- **DDB data model**: item-id, provider-info, view, index_profile, edm fields (see rdf2jsonld spec)
    82→- **GND (Gemeinsame Normdatei)**: German authority file for agents, places, subjects; `d-nb.info/gnd/` URIs
    83→- **Wikidata**: linked entity URIs for enrichment
    84→- **Geographic data**: WGS84 coordinates, GeoNames, TGN (Getty Thesaurus of Geographic Names)
    85→- **Temporal data**: ISO 8601 dates, edm:TimeSpan, `sem:hasBeginTimeStamp` / `sem:hasEndTimeStamp`
    86→- **CC BY 4.0**: license obligations for data display and redistribution
    87→
    88→## DevOps & Infrastructure
    89→
    90→- **Docker / Docker Compose**: containerize QLever, Elasticsearch, Redis, FastAPI, Next.js, Nginx
    91→- **Nginx**: reverse proxy, TLS termination, security headers, rate limiting (`limit_req_zone`)
    92→- **Environment variables**: secrets management (no hardcoded credentials)
    93→- **Health checks**: `/health` endpoints for all services
    94→- **Volume mounts**: persistent data for QLever index and Elasticsearch indices
    95→
    96→## General CS
    97→
    98→- **HTTP semantics**: status codes, content negotiation (`Accept: text/turtle`), CORS
    99→- **Content negotiation**: serving HTML, JSON, Turtle, N-Triples from the same URI (Linked Data principles)
   100→- **URI design**: `/item/{id}`, `/agent/{uri}`, `/place/{uri}` — stable, dereferenceable URIs
   101→- **Pagination**: limit/offset tradeoffs at scale; cursor-based for Elasticsearch deep pagination
   102→- **Internationalization (i18n)**: multilingual labels (German primary, English secondary); `lang` tags in RDF
   103→



```

     1→# GeMeA — GND Werk Linking via QLever DNB Endpoint
     2→
     3→Script: `scripts/link_gnd_works.py`
     4→Replaces: lobid-gnd REST API approach (previously planned but endpoint was TBD)
     5→
     6→`link_gnd_works.py` is an **offline ingest step**, not a runtime component. It runs once (or on re-ingest) during Phase 0 to enrich ProvidedCHO records with GND Werk URIs. Its output feeds mocho → QLever → the GeMeA API. The GND linking results are ultimately exposed through the API's `/work/{id}` endpoint and SPARQL proxy — `link_gnd_works.py` itself never serves live requests. Design constraints follow from this: throughput and batch efficiency matter; latency per query does not.
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## 1. Summary
    11→
    12→| Section | What it covers |
    13→|---|---|
    14→| [3. Knowledge base index](#3-knowledge-base-index) | DNB endpoint dataset stats (triples, subjects, predicates) |
    15→| [4. Endpoint and HTTP API](#4-endpoint-and-http-api) | `https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd` — POST format, QLever settings |
    16→| [5. Step 0 — Recon queries](#5-step-0--recon-queries-run-once-before-implementation) | Four one-off queries to validate Work classes, text index, author predicates, literal format before implementation |
    17→| [6. Step 1 — Title extraction](#6-step-1--title-extraction) | Extract clean title from raw `dc:title`: ISBD rule-based parser (primary) + NuNER Zero NER fallback |
    18→| [7. Step 2 — Query patterns](#7-step-2--query-patterns) | Patterns A/B/C with justification; conclusion: Pattern C (FILTER) is the sole active path |
    19→| [8. Step 3 — Title token preparation](#8-step-3--title-token-preparation) | Normalization → tokenization → stopword removal → distinctive token selection; `GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS` validation |
    20→| [9. Step 4 — Post-retrieval scoring](#9-step-4--post-retrieval-scoring) | Match type assignment (exact / normalized / fuzzy); `skos:exactMatch` vs `skos:closeMatch` vs `owl:sameAs` |
    21→| [10. Step 5 — Changes to `link_gnd_works.py`](#10-step-5--changes-to-link_gnd_workspy) | Replace lobid REST with async `httpx` POST; concurrency semaphore |
    22→| [11. Step 6 — What this replaces](#11-step-6--what-this-replaces) | Old lobid plan vs new QLever plan |
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## 2. Open questions
    27→
    28→| ID | Question | Blocking | Status |
    29→|---|---|---|---|
    30→| ~~OQ-00a~~ | ~~Is `preferredNameForTheWork` in the text index?~~ | — | **Resolved** — Recon 0.2: `contains-word` causes SPARQL parse error. Endpoint enforces SPARQL 1.1 only. Pattern C is the sole active query path. |
    31→| ~~OQ-00b~~ | ~~Exact predicate for author link (`gndo:firstAuthor` or `gndo:creator`)?~~ | — | **Resolved** — Recon 0.3: `gndo:author` dominant (8/10); `gndo:firstAuthor` added via `VALUES` (subproperty, SPARQL won't infer); `gndo:poet` for lyrical works; `gndo:composer` for musical works. |
    32→| OQ-01 | Does `sparql.dnb.de` normalize Umlauts in FILTER comparisons? (`ä` vs `a`) | No | Open — see [§2.1](#21-recon-05--umlaut-handling-in-filter-comparisons-oq-01) |
    33→| OQ-02 | What are the actual rate limits on `sparql.dnb.de`? | No | Open — no published SLA; profile at `CONCURRENCY = 10` during first batch run |
    34→| OQ-03 | Should `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` be added as a UNION branch in Pattern C to increase recall? | No | Open — preferred names are often ISBD-qualified strings (recon 0.4); variant names may carry the clean title form |
    35→| OQ-04 | Does `gndo:composer`/`gndo:firstComposer` behave analogously to `gndo:author`/`gndo:firstAuthor` for MusicalWork records? | No | Open — verify with a musician query analogous to recon 0.3 |
    36→
    37→### 2.1 Recon 0.5 — Umlaut handling in FILTER comparisons (OQ-01)
    38→
    39→The question is whether `LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "gotz von berlichingen"` (diacritics stripped) matches a record stored as `"Götz von Berlichingen"`. LCASE performs case folding only; it does not decompose or strip combining characters. The hypothesis is that the endpoint does **not** normalize Umlauts, meaning a stripped form will not match an umlaut-bearing literal — and therefore diacritic stripping in FR-05 would *hurt* recall against `preferredNameForTheWork`.
    40→
    41→Test case: Goethe's *Götz von Berlichingen* (`https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238` as author) — a well-known work with `ö` in the title.
    42→
    43→**Query A — with umlaut (expect: results)**
    44→
    45→```sparql
    46→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
    47→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
    48→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
    49→  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }
    50→  ?work a ?wtype .
    51→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
    52→  ?work ?authorPred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .
    53→  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "götz von berlichingen")
    54→}
    55→LIMIT 10
    56→```
    57→
    58→```bash
    59→curl -s -X POST "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd" \
    60→  -H "Accept: application/sparql-results+json" \
    61→  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
    62→  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
    63→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
    64→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
    65→  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }
    66→  ?work a ?wtype .
    67→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
    68→  ?work ?authorPred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .
    69→  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "götz von berlichingen")
    70→}
    71→LIMIT 10'
    72→```
    73→
    74→**Query B — diacritics stripped (expect: no results if endpoint does not normalize)**
    75→
    76→```sparql
    77→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
    78→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
    79→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
    80→  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }
    81→  ?work a ?wtype .
    82→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
    83→  ?work ?authorPred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .
    84→  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "gotz von berlichingen")
    85→}
    86→LIMIT 10
    87→```
    88→
    89→```bash
    90→curl -s -X POST "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd" \
    91→  -H "Accept: application/sparql-results+json" \
    92→  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
    93→  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
    94→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
    95→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
    96→  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }
    97→  ?work a ?wtype .
    98→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
    99→  ?work ?authorPred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .
   100→  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "gotz von berlichingen")
   101→}
   102→LIMIT 10'
   103→```
   104→
   105→**Interpretation:**
   106→
   107→| Query A result | Query B result | Conclusion | Impact on FR-05 |
   108→|---|---|---|---|
   109→| Results returned | No results | Endpoint does **not** normalize Umlauts — FILTER is byte-exact | Strip diacritics from **query string only if GND stores stripped forms**; otherwise keep Umlauts; send both forms via UNION |
   110→| Results returned | Results returned | Endpoint normalizes Umlauts (accent-insensitive collation) | Stripping is safe; normalized form sufficient for FILTER |
   111→| No results | No results | Title not stored as `preferredNameForTheWork` in this form | Re-check with `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` (OQ-03) |
   112→
   113→---
   114→
   115→## 3. Knowledge base index
   116→
   117→**Description:** DNB Bibliography and GND (Gemeinsame Normdatei) (`authorities-gnd_lds`, `dnb-all_lds` version 23.02.2026) — updated monthly
   118→
   119→| Metric | Value |
   120→|---|---|
   121→| Triples | 1,242,168,767 |
   122→| Subjects | 186,556,644 |
   123→| Predicates | 591 |
   124→| Objects | 457,761,162 |
   125→
   126→---
   127→
   128→## 4. Endpoint and HTTP API
   129→
   130→**Endpoint:** `https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd`
   131→**Method:** `POST`
   132→**Body:** `query=<URL-encoded SPARQL>` (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`)
   133→**Accept:** `application/sparql-results+json`
   134→
   135→QLever instance settings:
   136→- Languages: `en`
   137→- Default max rows: 100
   138→- Default mode: 3 — SPARQL & context-sensitive entities
   139→
   140→QLever-specific keywords used: `contains-word`, `textlimit`, `score()`
   141→
   142→---
   143→
   144→## 5. Step 0 — Recon queries (run once before implementation)
   145→
   146→Three things to verify before writing production queries.
   147→
   148→### 5.1 Work type coverage
   149→
   150→IFLA LRM defines a Work (LRM-E2) as "a distinct intellectual or artistic creation" — the abstract entity at the top of the WEMI hierarchy, independent of any particular realization or carrier. The GND ontology (`gnd_20251218.ttl`) models six subclasses of `gndo:Work`. Only three correspond to the IFLA LRM Work concept:
   151→
   152→| GND class | Label (en / de) | MARC21 | IFLA LRM | Include in queries |
   153→|---|---|---|---|---|
   154→| `gndo:Work` | Work / Werk | `079 $v=wit` | LRM-E2 Work — direct match | **Yes** |
   155→| `gndo:MusicalWork` | Musical work / Werk der Musik | `079 $v=wim` | LRM-E2 Work — a musical composition as distinct creative entity | **Yes** |
   156→| `gndo:Manuscript` | Manuscript / Schriftdenkmal | `079 $v=wis` | LRM-E2 Work — a unique historical manuscript (e.g. Nibelungenlied) is the distinct intellectual creation itself | **Yes** |
   157→| `gndo:Expression` | Expression / Expression | `079 $v=wie` | LRM-E3 Expression — the realization of a Work in a specific linguistic or notational form; one level below Work in WEMI | No |
   158→| `gndo:VersionOfAMusicalWork` | Version of a musical work / Fassung eines Werks der Musik | `079 $v=wif` | LRM-E3 Expression — a specific arrangement or version is a realization of the Work, not a new Work (unless substantially transformed) | No |
   159→| `gndo:CollectiveManuscript` | Collective manuscript / Sammelhandschrift | `079 $v=wil` | LRM-E4 Manifestation — a bound collection of multiple works; the container, not the intellectual creation | No |
   160→| `gndo:ProvenanceCharacteristic` | Provenance characteristic / Provenienzmerkmal | `079 $v=wip` | No WEMI equivalent — ownership marks and traces left in/on physical items; not a creative entity | No |
   161→
   162→```sparql
   163→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   164→SELECT ?type (COUNT(?w) AS ?n) WHERE {
   165→  VALUES ?type {
   166→    gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript
   167→    gndo:Expression gndo:VersionOfAMusicalWork
   168→    gndo:CollectiveManuscript gndo:ProvenanceCharacteristic
   169→  }
   170→  ?w a ?type .
   171→}
   172→GROUP BY ?type ORDER BY DESC(?n)
   173→```
   174→
   175→Constraining with `VALUES` avoids a full dataset type scan and lets the endpoint use per-type indexes. The original open-ended query caused a 504 Gateway Timeout.
   176→
   177→**Results:**
   178→
   179→| ?type | ?n |
   180→|---|---|
   181→| MusicalWork | 330,818 |
   182→| Work | 242,333 |
   183→| ProvenanceCharacteristic | 10,080 |
   184→| Manuscript | 7,133 |
   185→| Expression | 5,766 |
   186→| VersionOfAMusicalWork | 2,905 |
   187→| CollectiveManuscript | — (no results; zero entities in dataset) |
   188→
   189→**Conclusion:** `VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }` is confirmed. The three IFLA LRM Work-equivalent classes cover 580,284 entities (242,333 + 330,818 + 7,133). The four excluded classes are either IFLA Expression-level (`Expression`, `VersionOfAMusicalWork`), have no WEMI equivalent (`ProvenanceCharacteristic`), or are absent from this dataset (`CollectiveManuscript`).
   190→
   191→### 5.2 Confirm text index covers `preferredNameForTheWork`
   192→
   193→```sparql
   194→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   195→SELECT ?w ?label ?score WHERE {
   196→  ?w a gndo:Work .
   197→  ?w gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?label .
   198→  ?label contains-word "faust"
   199→  BIND(score(?label) AS ?score)
   200→}
   201→ORDER BY DESC(?score) LIMIT 5
   202→```
   203→
   204→**Test (curl):**
   205→
   206→```bash
   207→curl -s -X POST "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd" \
   208→  -H "Accept: application/sparql-results+json" \
   209→  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
   210→  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   211→SELECT ?w ?label WHERE {
   212→  ?w a gndo:Work .
   213→  ?w gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?label .
   214→  ?label contains-word "faust"
   215→}
   216→LIMIT 5'
   217→```
   218→
   219→**Result:**
   220→
   221→```json
   222→{
   223→  "exception": "Invalid SPARQL query: Token \"contains-word\": mismatched input 'contains-word' expecting {'(', 'a', '^', '!', IRI_REF, PNAME_NS, PNAME_LN, VAR1, VAR2, PREFIX_LANGTAG}",
   224→  "metadata": { "line": 5, "positionInLine": 9, "startIndex": 155, "stopIndex": 167 },
   225→  "status": "ERROR"
   226→}
   227→```
   228→
   229→The parser fails at line 5, position 9 — exactly where `contains-word` appears as predicate. The endpoint enforces SPARQL 1.1 grammar; `contains-word` is QLever-specific syntax and is not available here. **Patterns A and B do not apply. Pattern C is the active query path.**
   230→
   231→### 5.3 Confirm author predicate name
   232→
   233→Query Works that link to Goethe (`https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238`) to find the predicate used:
   234→
   235→```sparql
   236→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   237→SELECT ?work ?pred WHERE {
   238→  ?work ?pred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .
   239→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
   240→  ?work a ?wtype .
   241→}
   242→LIMIT 10
   243→```
   244→
   245→**Results:**
   246→
   247→| ?work | ?pred |
   248→|---|---|
   249→| 4427937-1 | author |
   250→| 134483180X | relatedPerson |
   251→| 1367943906 | author |
   252→| 1345022085 | author |
   253→| 1367911087 | author |
   254→| 134558573X | author |
   255→| 1345758731 | poet |
   256→| 1346874786 | author |
   257→| 1347120017 | author |
   258→| 1347121307 | author |
   259→
   260→**Conclusion:** The dominant predicate is `gndo:author` (8/10 results). `gndo:poet` appears as a role-specific variant (Goethe wrote lyrics for musical works); `gndo:relatedPerson` is too broad for author linking.
   261→
   262→From `gnd_20251218.ttl`, the relevant author-role property hierarchy is:
   263→
   264→| Property | Label (en/de) | `rdfs:subPropertyOf` | MARC21 | Scope |
   265→|---|---|---|---|---|
   266→| `gndo:author` | Author / Verfasser | RDA `P60434` | `$4=auta` | Textual works; primary creator |
   267→| `gndo:firstAuthor` | First author / Erste Verfasserschaft | **`gndo:author`**, RDA `P60434` | `$4=aut1` | Subproperty of `author`; single/lead author |
   268→| `gndo:creator` | Creator / Urheber | — | `$4=urhe` | General; no subproperty relation to `author` |
   269→| `gndo:poet` | Poet / Dichter | RDA `P60477` | `$4=dich` | Words of non-dramatic musical works |
   270→| `gndo:composer` | Composer / Komponist | RDA `P60426` | `$4=koma` | Musical works |
   271→
   272→**SPARQL does not infer `rdfs:subPropertyOf` without a reasoner.** A query constraining `gndo:author` will miss triples that use `gndo:firstAuthor`, even though `firstAuthor` is a subproperty of `author`. Pattern A must explicitly cover both using `VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor }`. `gndo:poet` and `gndo:composer` should be added for coverage of MusicalWork records.
   273→
   274→### 5.4 Confirm literal format of `preferredNameForTheWork`
   275→
   276→Determines whether VALUES-based queries must include `@de` language-tagged variants.
   277→
   278→```bash
   279→curl -s -X POST "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd" \
   280→  -H "Accept: application/sparql-results+json" \
   281→  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
   282→  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   283→SELECT ?l (lang(?l) AS ?tag) WHERE {
   284→  <https://d-nb.info/gnd/4427937-1> gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?l .
   285→}
   286→LIMIT 5'
   287→```
   288→
   289→**Result:**
   290→
   291→```json
   292→{
   293→  "l": { "type": "literal", "value": "Faust I / Walpurgisnacht / Berlin / Staatsbibliothek Berlin / Ms. germ. qu. 527" },
   294→  "tag": { "type": "literal", "value": "" }
   295→}
   296→```
   297→
   298→**Conclusions:**
   299→1. `preferredNameForTheWork` literals have **no language tag** — `@de` variants are not needed in VALUES clauses.
   300→2. GND preferred names are **not clean titles** — this Manuscript entry carries a full ISBD-style string including location and shelfmark, not just the work title. Pattern C's exact FILTER match will not resolve titles like "Faust" against such records; those can only be matched via `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` or through a separate normalized-title query.
   301→
   302→---
   303→
   304→## 6. Step 1 — Title extraction
   305→
   306→Raw `dc:title` strings in DDB ProvidedCHOs are ISBD-encoded: they concatenate title, statement of responsibility, edition, publisher, place, and year into a single field using ISBD punctuation. Sending raw strings to GND produces low-precision matches. Extraction runs before token preparation (Step 3) and GND querying (Step 2).
   307→
   308→### 6.1 Method: two-stage pipeline
   309→
   310→| Stage | Trigger | Method |
   311→|---|---|---|
   312→| **Primary — ISBD rule-based parser** | Record contains ISBD punctuation markers | Split on ` / `, `. - `, ` : ` per ISBD conventions; take the leading segment as the title |
   313→| **Secondary — NER fallback** | No ISBD markers detected (~71.6% of corpus) | Zero-shot NER with NuNER Zero; extract spans tagged as work titles |
   314→
   315→The ISBD parser handles the minority of records that use structured punctuation. NuNER Zero (zero-shot) handles the majority without ISBD markers, where rule-based splitting would either produce nothing or split on the wrong boundary.
   316→
   317→### 6.2 ISBD split rules
   318→
   319→| Separator | Meaning in ISBD | Action |
   320→|---|---|---|
   321→| ` / ` | Statement of responsibility | Take everything before as title |
   322→| `. - ` | New ISBD area (edition, publication, etc.) | Take everything before as title |
   323→| ` : ` | Subtitle or other title information | Optional split; retain subtitle if short |
   324→
   325→Example: `"Faust : eine Tragödie / von Goethe. - Neue Ausg."` → extracted title: `"Faust : eine Tragödie"` (or `"Faust"` if subtitle is stripped).
   326→
   327→### 6.3 Output
   328→
   329→`extracted_title` — a clean title string passed to Step 3 (token preparation) and used as the FILTER value in Pattern C queries.
   330→`extraction_method` — `"isbd"` or `"ner"`, recorded in the output JSON (FR-07).
   331→
   332→Full extraction design is documented in `notes/gnd-title-extraction.md`. NER model selection, evaluation, and bibliographic NER considerations are documented in `notes/ner-bibliographic.md`.
   333→
   334→---
   335→
   336→## 7. Step 2 — Query patterns
   337→
   338→| Query pattern | Fields | Justification |
   339→|---|---|---|
   340→| **A** — Title + author | `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` (text search); `gndo:author`, `gndo:firstAuthor`, `gndo:poet`, `gndo:composer` (constraint via VALUES) | Author URI narrows the candidate set before text matching; highest precision. Use when author GND URI is available and text index is confirmed (recon 0.2–0.3). |
   341→| **B** — Title only | `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` (preferred); `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` (UNION fallback) | No author URI available. UNION over variant names increases recall for cases where the DDB title matches a GND alternative form rather than the preferred label. Use when text index is confirmed but no author URI. |
   342→| **C** — FILTER fallback | `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` (exact string match) | `contains-word` unavailable or not indexed for the predicate (determined by recon 0.2). Exact/normalized string comparison only; no relevance ranking. Lower recall than A/B. |
   343→
   344→### 7.1 Pattern A — Title + author, text-indexed (primary)
   345→
   346→Used when: author GND URI is available AND `contains-word` is confirmed (recon 0.2).
   347→
   348→`gndo:firstAuthor` is a subproperty of `gndo:author` but SPARQL does not infer this without a reasoner — both must be listed explicitly. `gndo:poet` and `gndo:composer` cover musical and lyrical works.
   349→
   350→```sparql
   351→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   352→
   353→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel ?score WHERE {
   354→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
   355→  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }
   356→  ?work a ?wtype .
   357→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
   358→  ?work ?authorPred <{author_gnd_uri}> .
   359→  ?prefLabel contains-word "{title_tokens}"
   360→  BIND(score(?prefLabel) AS ?score)
   361→}
   362→ORDER BY DESC(?score)
   363→TEXTLIMIT 5
   364→LIMIT 10
   365→```
   366→
   367→`TEXTLIMIT 5` caps QLever's internal text candidates for performance; `LIMIT 10` caps output rows.
   368→
   369→### 7.2 Pattern B — Title only, text-indexed
   370→
   371→Used when: no author GND URI is available.
   372→UNION catches cases where the DDB title matches a GND variant name rather than the preferred form.
   373→
   374→```sparql
   375→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   376→
   377→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel ?varLabel ?score WHERE {
   378→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
   379→  ?work a ?wtype .
   380→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
   381→  {
   382→    ?prefLabel contains-word "{title_tokens}"
   383→    BIND(?prefLabel AS ?varLabel)
   384→    BIND(score(?prefLabel) AS ?score)
   385→  } UNION {
   386→    ?work gndo:variantNameForTheWork ?alt .
   387→    ?alt contains-word "{title_tokens}"
   388→    BIND(?alt AS ?varLabel)
   389→    BIND(score(?alt) AS ?score)
   390→  }
   391→}
   392→ORDER BY DESC(?score)
   393→TEXTLIMIT 5
   394→LIMIT 10
   395→```
   396→
   397→### 7.3 Pattern C — FILTER (active query path)
   398→
   399→Exact-match only; normalized title (diacritics stripped, lowercased) maximizes recall.
   400→
   401→```sparql
   402→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   403→
   404→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
   405→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
   406→  ?work a ?wtype .
   407→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
   408→  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "{normalized_title}")
   409→}
   410→LIMIT 10
   411→```
   412→
   413→### 7.4 Conclusion from Step 0
   414→
   415→| Finding | Source | Impact on Step 1 |
   416→|---|---|---|
   417→| `contains-word` raises a SPARQL parse error | Step 0.2 | Patterns A and B are not available. **Pattern C is the sole active query path.** |
   418→| `VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }` covers 580,284 entities (242k + 331k + 7k) | Step 0.1 | Confirmed — use in all patterns |
   419→| Dominant author predicate is `gndo:author`; `gndo:firstAuthor` is a subproperty but SPARQL does not infer it | Step 0.3 | Pattern A must use `VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }` |
   420→| `gndo:poet` appears for lyrical/musical works (Goethe as lyricist) | Step 0.3 | Include `gndo:poet` in `VALUES ?authorPred` |
   421→| `CollectiveManuscript` has zero entities in this dataset | Step 0.1 | Confirmed exclusion; no impact on queries |
   422→
   423→Pattern C with author constraint (when author GND URI is available) becomes the effective Pattern A: same `VALUES ?authorPred` constraint, FILTER instead of `contains-word`.
   424→
   425→---
   426→
   427→## 8. Step 3 — Title token preparation
   428→
   429→QLever `contains-word` is word-based, not phrase-based by default. Title preparation has three distinct phases: normalization, tokenization, and filtering.
   430→
   431→### 8.1 Normalization (pre-tokenization)
   432→
   433→1. **Unicode NFC normalize** — Unicode Standard Annex #15, Normalization Form C: Canonical Decomposition followed by Canonical Composition. Ensures consistent code-point representation before any string operation. Example: `a` + combining diaeresis (two code points, NFD) → `ä` (one code point, NFC). Required so that lowercasing, diacritic stripping, and string comparison behave predictably regardless of source encoding.
   434→2. **Lowercase** — case-fold the NFC-normalized string.
   435→3. **Strip diacritics** — decompose (NFD) then remove combining characters (`unicodedata.category(c) == 'Mn'`). Maps `ä→a`, `ü→u`, `ö→o`, `ß→ss`. Note: test whether the GND endpoint requires this — if the text index is accent-insensitive, stripping may reduce rather than increase recall.
   436→
   437→### 8.2 Tokenization
   438→
   439→4. **Split on whitespace and punctuation** — split the normalized string into individual word tokens.
   440→
   441→### 8.3 Filtering
   442→
   443→5. **Remove stopwords:** `der, die, das, ein, eine, von, und, zu, im, in, an, auf, für, mit, bei, dem, den`
   444→6. **Select 2–3 distinctive tokens** (see below)
   445→
   446→### 8.4 Distinctive token selection
   447→
   448→**Underlying principle:** `contains-word` with multiple tokens is AND semantics — each additional token makes the query more restrictive. Candidate retrieval should maximize recall (get the right GND Work somewhere in the top-10); precision is handled by post-retrieval string scoring in Step 3. The goal is the minimum set of tokens that identifies the work without over-constraining.
   449→
   450→**Criterion 1 — Semantic centrality.** Use tokens that are the essential content of the title, not modifiers or structural words:
   451→
   452→- **Keep:** nouns, proper nouns (place/person names embedded in titles), semantically unique adjectives
   453→- **Drop even if not in the stopword list:** articles, prepositions, ordinals (*erster, zweiter*), format/edition terms (*Band, Teil, Heft, Auflage, Bd., Nr.*)
   454→
   455→Example: *"Die Leiden des jungen Werthers"* → tokens `leiden werthers`; the proper noun alone (`werthers`) is sufficient with Pattern A.
   456→
   457→**Criterion 2 — Low collision frequency.** Prefer tokens that appear in few GND Works. Some words are not stopwords but are so common in bibliographic titles that they add almost no discriminating power:
   458→
   459→```python
   460→GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS = {
   461→    "briefe", "gedichte", "werke", "schriften", "geschichte",
   462→    "bericht", "katalog", "sammlung", "aufsätze", "texte",
   463→    "band", "teil", "heft", "nummer", "jahrgang",
   464→}
   465→```
   466→
   467→Example: *"Gedichte"* alone returns thousands of GND Works. *"faust"* alone is sufficient with an author URI.
   468→
   469→This list should be derived empirically from a frequency query against the endpoint, not hardcoded blindly — run after recon 0.1.
   470→
   471→**Script:** `scripts/check_generic_title_words.py` — queries the DNB endpoint per word using exact VALUE matching on capitalized German forms (plain literals, no `@de` tag — confirmed by recon 0.4). Run with `python scripts/check_generic_title_words.py`.
   472→
   473→**Results** (counts = GND Works whose full `preferredNameForTheWork` is exactly this word or common article+word variants; threshold ≥100 = drop):
   474→
   475→| Word | Count | Keep in list? |
   476→|---|---|---|
   477→| briefe | 74 | Yes (low collision) |
   478→| gedichte | 90 | Yes (low collision) |
   479→| werke | 3,212 | **No — drop from list** |
   480→| schriften | 1 | Yes (low collision) |
   481→| geschichte | 5 | Yes (low collision) |
   482→| bericht | 2 | Yes (low collision) |
   483→| katalog | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   484→| sammlung | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   485→| aufsätze | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   486→| texte | 2 | Yes (low collision) |
   487→| band | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   488→| teil | 1 | Yes (low collision) |
   489→| heft | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   490→| nummer | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   491→| jahrgang | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   492→
   493→**Conclusion:** Only `werke` (3,212) exceeds the threshold and is genuinely high-collision as a standalone title. All other words are low-frequency as full titles — GND preferred names are typically qualified strings (ISBD-style, see recon 0.4), not bare single-word titles. `GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS` should be revised to contain only `werke`; the others do not need filtering for Pattern C exact-match queries.
   494→
   495→**Decision rule:**
   496→
   497→```python
   498→def select_tokens(tokens: list[str]) -> list[str]:
   499→    # tokens: already stopword-filtered, lowercased, diacritics-stripped
   500→    distinctive = [t for t in tokens if t not in GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS and len(t) > 3]
   501→    return distinctive[:3] if len(distinctive) >= 2 else tokens[:3]
   502→```
   503→
   504→`len(t) > 3` also drops short high-frequency fragments not caught by the stopword list.
   505→
   506→**Why 2–3 tokens specifically:**
   507→
   508→- 1 token: usually sufficient with Pattern A (author constraint already narrows heavily); sometimes sufficient for highly distinctive titles in Pattern B
   509→- 2–3 tokens: the sweet spot for Pattern B — enough to discriminate, low risk of AND-excluding the right Work due to GND/DDB title variation
   510→- >3 tokens: increasing risk of false negatives when GND's preferred form differs from DDB's (e.g., GND has *"Faust. Der Tragödie erster Teil"* but DDB has *"Faust: Erster Teil"* — four tokens from the DDB form likely miss the GND entry)
   511→
   512→**Multi-word handling:**
   513→- Short (1–3 tokens after filtering): pass all remaining tokens
   514→- Long titles: apply `select_tokens()` above
   515→- High-ambiguity titles (single generic word like *Briefe*, *Gedichte* with no distinctive co-tokens): require author constraint (Pattern A); skip title-only search (Pattern B) if no author URI is available
   516→
   517→---
   518→
   519→## 9. Step 4 — Post-retrieval scoring
   520→
   521→QLever's `score()` is used only to rank candidates before string comparison. Match type is assigned in Python:
   522→
   523→| Condition | `match_type` | Triple predicate |
   524→|---|---|---|
   525→| `prefLabel == extracted_title` (case-insensitive) | `exact` | `skos:exactMatch` |
   526→| Normalized match (strip diacritics + punctuation) | `normalized` | `skos:exactMatch` |
   527→| Levenshtein distance ≤ 2 to `prefLabel` or any `variantNameForTheWork` | `fuzzy` | `skos:closeMatch` |
   528→| No candidate above threshold | `unresolved` | — |
   529→
   530→`match_confidence` = normalized edit similarity to `prefLabel` (not QLever score).
   531→
   532→This logic is unchanged from the original plan in `notes/gnd-title-extraction.md`.
   533→
   534→### 9.1 Predicate justification
   535→
   536→**`skos:exactMatch`** (exact and normalized matches) — asserts two concepts are "an exact match" and can be used interchangeably for all retrieval purposes, without the OWL identity entailments of `owl:sameAs` (Miles & Bechhofer, *SKOS Simple Knowledge Organization System Reference*, W3C Recommendation, 2009, §10). Semantically appropriate here: a DDB ProvidedCHO and a GND Werk URI are not the same individual (CHO = catalog record for a manifestation; Werk URI = authority record for an abstract work), so asserting OWL individual identity would be incorrect.
   537→
   538→**`skos:closeMatch`** (fuzzy matches) — links two concepts "sufficiently similar that they can be used interchangeably in some information retrieval applications" but stops short of asserting full equivalence (Miles & Bechhofer, 2009, §10).
   539→
   540→**Why not `owl:sameAs`:** Halpin et al. show that `owl:sameAs` is routinely misused in linked data to express approximate or cross-type identity, producing unwanted OWL entailments downstream. Their recommendation: reserve `owl:sameAs` for verified same-individual identity; use SKOS mapping properties for concept-level matching (Halpin, Hayes, McCusker, McGuinness & Thompson, *When owl:sameAs Isn't the Same: An Analysis of Identity in Linked Data*, ISWC 2010, DOI: 10.1007/978-3-642-17746-0_16).
   541→
   542→**Match type taxonomy** (exact / normalized / fuzzy) follows the standard candidate generation ladder in entity linking (Shen, Wang & Han, *Entity Linking with a Knowledge Base: Issues, Techniques, and Solutions*, IEEE TKDE 27(2), 2015, §3.1, DOI: 10.1109/TKDE.2014.2327028).
   543→
   544→**Levenshtein distance** (Levenshtein, *Binary Codes Capable of Correcting Deletions, Insertions, and Reversals*, Soviet Physics Doklady 10(8), 1966). The threshold of ≤ 2 is a common empirical choice; validate against the gold set planned in `gnd-title-extraction.md` §Paper §4 quality metrics.
   545→
   546→---
   547→
   548→## 10. Step 5 — Changes to `link_gnd_works.py`
   549→
   550→Replace the lobid-gnd REST call (Steps 3–4 in `gnd-title-extraction.md`) with:
   551→
   552→```python
   553→import httpx
   554→import asyncio
   555→
   556→QLEVER_ENDPOINT = "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd"
   557→CONCURRENCY = 10  # tune up after rate-limit testing
   558→
   559→_semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(CONCURRENCY)
   560→
   561→async def query_gnd_werk(
   562→    extracted_title: str,
   563→    author_gnd_uri: str | None
   564→) -> list[dict]:
   565→    tokens = tokenize_for_qlever(extracted_title)
   566→    sparql = (
   567→        QUERY_A.format(title_tokens=tokens, author_gnd_uri=author_gnd_uri)
   568→        if author_gnd_uri
   569→        else QUERY_B.format(title_tokens=tokens)
   570→    )
   571→    async with _semaphore:
   572→        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10.0) as client:
   573→            r = await client.post(
   574→                QLEVER_ENDPOINT,
   575→                data={"query": sparql},
   576→                headers={"Accept": "application/sparql-results+json"}
   577→            )
   578→    r.raise_for_status()
   579→    return r.json()["results"]["bindings"]
   580→```
   581→
   582→Pattern selection:
   583→- Pattern A if `author_gnd_uri` is set
   584→- Pattern B otherwise
   585→- Pattern C as fallback if recon 0.2 shows `contains-word` is unavailable (confirmed — Pattern C is the sole active path)
   586→
   587→---
   588→
   589→## 11. Step 6 — What this replaces
   590→
   591→| Old plan | New plan |
   592→|---|---|
   593→| `GET /gnd/search?q=label:"{title}"&filter=type:Work` (lobid REST) | SPARQL POST to QLever endpoint |
   594→| Endpoint TBD / local infrastructure required | Public DNB endpoint, no setup |
   595→| No text relevance ranking | QLever `score()` ranks candidates |
   596→| `link_gnd_works.py` depended on lobid API availability | Depends on `sparql.dnb.de` uptime (DNB-operated) |
   597→| Title-only or title + author_uri via query param | Same semantics; `gndo:firstAuthor` predicate (verify in recon 0.3) |
   598→



```

  "new_string": "- **GND (Gemeinsame Normdatei)**: German authority file for agents, places, subjects; `d-nb.info/gnd/` URIs; QLever DNB SPARQL endpoint (`https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd`); literal format (no `@de` tags on `preferredNameForTheWork`); Work class coverage (~580k entities across Work/MusicalWork/Manuscript)\n- **ISBD punctuation encoding**: how DDB `dc:title` strings embed title, statement of responsibility, edition, and publication area in a single field using ` / `, `. - `, ` : ` as area separators; why raw strings produce low-precision GND matches"
}

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T18:48:15.364Z)*

**Tool call:** `Edit`
```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/prerequisites.md",
  "old_string": "- **Internationalization (i18n)**: multilingual labels (German primary, English secondary); `lang` tags in RDF",
  "new_string": "- **Internationalization (i18n)**: multilingual labels (German primary, English secondary); `lang` tags in RDF\n\n## NLP & Named Entity Recognition\n\n### Bibliographic NER fundamentals\n\n- **NER task formulation**: sequence labeling (BIO/BIOES tags) vs. span extraction (start/end indices); why span extraction suits bibliographic NER (non-contiguous labels, variable-length spans, nested possible)\n- **FRBR-based label hierarchy**: Work-level (`TITLE`, `OTHER_TITLE`, `PERSON`), Expression-level (`TRANSLATOR`, `PARALLEL_TITLE`, `MEDIUM`), Manifestation-level (`PUBLISHER`, `PLACE`, `YEAR`, `EDITION`, `SERIES`, `VOLUME`); why evaluation scope must be declared before gold set design\n- **ISBD rule-based NER**: using ` / `, `. - `, ` : ` as split heuristics for title extraction; FP rate analysis per signal; why structural coverage is ~1.2% of corpus while heuristic coverage reaches ~8%\n- **Silver labeling**: auto-labeling training data from heuristic rules; silver tier hierarchy (tier 2 = high-confidence structural, tier 1 = heuristic partial, tier 0 = no label); FP rate as the primary quality gate; why silver labels are not ground truth\n- **Statement of Responsibility (SoR) disambiguation**: ` /` cue fires for PERSON, TRANSLATOR, corporate body, editor — requires sub-classification; FP rate ~36% for raw `f_person` heuristic; keyword-based disambiguation for TRANSLATOR (~\"übersetzt\", \"Übers.\", \"transl.\")\n- **Author-before-title structure**: pre-1750 German bibliographic records place author name and credentials before the work title (not after ` /`); systematic false negative for ISBD-based PERSON detection; must be handled explicitly in annotation guidelines and prompts\n\n### Models and architectures\n\n- **Encoder-only NER (sequence labeling)**: BERT-style models (`xlm-roberta-large`, `mdeberta-v3-base`) with token classification head; why multilingual pretraining is preferred over monolingual German for historical text; `deberta-v3-large` is English-only — not applicable\n- **Span extraction NER (GLiNER)**: joint encoding of entity type descriptions and input text; span scoring via dot product; handles arbitrary label definitions without retraining; max span width cap (K=12 tokens); O(n log n) decode; discontinuous entities not supported; Zaratiana et al. (NAACL 2024)\n- **NuNER Zero**: GLiNER variant pretrained on 1M LLM-annotated C4 sentences for zero-shot transfer; concept and text encoders are separate (text encoder is a drop-in for BERT/RoBERTa); zero-shot performance not benchmarked in the EMNLP 2024 paper (Bogdanov et al.) — production zero-shot claims unverified; inference cost <$0.0001/example\n- **Generative NER**: LLM (LLaMA, Qwen) + LoRA fine-tuning with structured output format; output format critically affects performance — Inline Bracketed/XML achieves F1 ~90–94 on CoNLL2003; JSON formats drop to 85–87; Offset-based JSON collapses (~30 F1); Zhan et al. (arXiv 2601.17898, 2026)\n- **Zero-shot NER gap**: zero-shot LLMs score ~88 F1 on general-domain flat NER vs. ~93 for fine-tuned; gap widens in low-resource/specialized domains (GENIA biomedical: 79 vs. 84); expect similar or larger gap for historical German bibliographic NER\n- **LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)**: parameter-efficient fine-tuning by injecting low-rank weight updates; rank r and scaling factor α are key hyperparameters; preserves general capabilities (MMLU, HellaSwag) with minor fluctuation (±3–4%) after NER fine-tuning (Zhan et al. 2026)\n\n### Evaluation and datasets\n\n- **HIPE-2022 (ajmc)**: historical document NER benchmark; German, French, English; fine-grained bibliographic reference labels; top systems used `xlm-roberta-large`; Ehrmann et al. (2022) — ⚠️ verify citation\n- **CLEF-HIPE-2020**: historical newspaper NER; German, French, English; PROD label covers work titles; 19th-century orthography; XLM-R large dominant in top submissions; Ehrmann et al. (2020) — ⚠️ verify citation\n- **CoNLL2003**: standard flat NER benchmark (PER, ORG, LOC, MISC); 4 labels, general English/German; used for model comparison in Zhan et al. (2026)\n- **GLiNER zero-shot benchmark**: 7 CrossNER/MIT datasets; GLiNER-L (300M) avg F1 60.9 vs. GoLLIE-7B 58.0 and ChatGPT 47.5; entity-level exact match (not token-level)\n- **MultiCONER multilingual**: GLiNER-Multi scores 39.5 F1 on German vs. ChatGPT 37.1; supervised XLM-R baseline 64.6 — ~25-point gap for zero-shot approaches\n- **Evaluation metrics**: entity-level exact-match F1 (GLiNER) vs. token-level macro F1 (NuNER) — not directly comparable; always report which metric is used; track PERSON F1 on pre-1750 stratum separately from modern stratum\n- **Fine-tuning dataset sizing**: rule of thumb ~200–500 annotated spans per entity type from a strong pretrained checkpoint; Work-only (3 types) needs ~1k–2k records; Work + Expression (6 types) needs ~3k–5k records\n\n### LLM annotation (prompt engineering for NER)\n\n- **Inline Bracketed format**: `[span text | LABEL]` — natural language-like, best-performing output format for generative NER (Zhan et al. 2026); use for LLM annotation of training data\n- **Few-shot NER prompting**: include 5 annotated examples per structural pattern; examples must cover domain-specific edge cases (not generic CoNLL2003-style examples); draw from manually verified seed set\n- **Systematic vs. random LLM errors**: LLMs make type errors (38.2% of errors) and over-extract (Completely-O: 25.2%); encoder models under-extract (Omitted Mentions: 45.5%); design evaluation to detect both failure modes\n- **Spot-check protocol**: verify ~5% of LLM-annotated records manually; compute per-label F1; threshold 85% agreement before using batch as training data; if below threshold, revise prompt and re-run\n\n---\n\n## Corpus Analysis & Statistics\n\n- **Proportion estimation**: Bernoulli trial model for observed proportions p̂ = k/n; variance p(1−p)/n minimised near 0 and 1; extreme proportions (near 0% or 100%) require fewer samples to establish a decision-relevant conclusion\n- **Wilson confidence interval**: preferred over Wald (p̂ ± 1.96√(p̂(1−p̂)/n)) for proportions near 0 or 1; Wald can produce negative lower bounds and undercovers; Wilson conditions on hypothetical true p rather than p̂; Wilson (1927), JASA 22(158), 209–212\n- **Decision-relative sample sufficiency**: sample size is sufficient when the CI bound clears the decision threshold by a safe margin, not when the CI is narrow in absolute terms; 200 records can be sufficient if the observed proportion is extreme and the threshold is far from the boundary\n- **Stratified sampling**: sampling proportional vs. equal across strata; equal sampling ensures small-but-dense strata contribute meaningfully; proportional sampling is appropriate for prevalence estimation\n- **FP rate analysis**: for each heuristic signal, sample ~100–200 records and classify true positives vs. false positives; derive precision = 1 − FP_rate; use as weight in weighted scoring; SR-03 methodology\n- **Confusion matrix**: per-class precision, recall, F1 for multi-class classifiers; distinguish heuristic FP (classifier calls LATIN, truth is GERMAN) from FN (classifier calls GERMAN, truth is LATIN)\n- **Stratification variables for NER**: era (modern / 19th c. / 1700–1800 / pre-1700), silver tier (0 / 1 / 2), `dc_type` (Monografie, Leichenpredigt, Periodika…), title length (short ≤4 tokens / medium 5–14 / long >14)\n\n---\n\n## Early Modern German\n\n- **Orthographic features**: u/v interchange (`vnd` = und, `vnser` = unser), i/j interchange, final -en/-enn variation, ck clusters (`drucken`, `Stück`), double vowels (`seelig`, `heer`), genitive `deß` = des; standard pre-1750 forms, not errors\n- **Title page conventions**: author credentials + name + role description appear before the work title (not after ` /`); credential sequence includes degree abbreviation (D., M., Lic., Mag.), full name, and post (\"Pfarrers zu X\", \"Professoris zu Jena\"); stop PERSON span at first content noun of the title\n- **Genre conventions**: Leichenpredigt (funerary sermon, 1600–1750) — funeral occasion, deceased's name, preacher's name, scriptural text; academic dissertations — respondent + praeses names, thesis title in Latin; legal/administrative monographs — dedicatee, petition occasion\n- **Embedded Latin vocabulary**: `Anno`, `Christi`, `Jesu`, `Doctor`, `Oratio`, `Disputatio`, `Theses` in otherwise German titles — standard Protestant/academic vocabulary, not Latin-language records; true Latin prevalence in pre-1800 Monografie/Leichenpredigt: ~0.5% (SR-06)\n- **\"Das ist:\" construction**: introduces a German-language subtitle after a Latin or abbreviated main title; signals an OTHER_TITLE span\n- **Language classifier heuristics and failure modes**: `Anno` + pre-1600 year → false LATIN (date label); `Christi`/`Jesu`/`Doctor` in Leichenpredigt → false LATIN (Protestant devotional vocabulary); `[ck]h\\w+` cluster → false EARLY_MODERN_DE on modern German words (`Heilige`, `nach`); `Herrn` → false EARLY_MODERN_DE (also modern German)\n- **Tokenisation challenges**: non-standard orthography tokenised differently by modern German tokenisers (`de_core_news_sm`); `vnd` not in vocabulary; compound splitting may fail on early modern compounds; median title length pre-1750 is 12–15 tokens (long-form), some >50 tokens\n\n---\n\n## Entity Linking\n\n- **Entity linking pipeline**: candidate generation → candidate ranking → threshold filtering → output (URI or unresolved); match type taxonomy: exact / normalized / fuzzy / unresolved (Shen, Wang & Han, IEEE TKDE 27(2), 2015)\n- **Levenshtein distance**: edit distance (insertions, deletions, substitutions); threshold ≤2 for fuzzy match in bibliographic entity linking; Levenshtein (Soviet Physics Doklady, 1966)\n- **String normalization for matching**: Unicode NFC normalization → lowercase → NFD decomposition → strip combining characters (category `Mn`) → diacritic stripping (ä→a, ü→u, ö→o, ß→ss); LCASE in SPARQL is case-fold only — does not strip diacritics; endpoint-side diacritic normalization must be tested empirically (OQ-01)\n- **Stopword removal for candidate generation**: remove grammatical function words to maximize discriminating power; `contains-word` with multiple tokens is AND semantics — more tokens = higher precision, lower recall; target 2–3 distinctive tokens; prefer nouns and proper nouns over generic title words\n- **Generic title word filtering**: empirically validate which words are high-collision in the target KG (e.g., `werke` = 3,212 GND Work entries as full preferred name); derive `GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS` from a frequency query, not by assumption\n- **`rdfs:subPropertyOf` and SPARQL**: SPARQL 1.1 does not perform property hierarchy inference without an OWL reasoner; must enumerate subproperties explicitly via `VALUES` (e.g., `gndo:firstAuthor` is a subproperty of `gndo:author` — a SPARQL query on `gndo:author` will not return triples using `gndo:firstAuthor`)\n- **Async batch querying**: `asyncio.Semaphore` for concurrency control against a public SPARQL endpoint; `httpx.AsyncClient` for async HTTP; rate limit profiling before full batch run; timeout handling and retry logic\n- **SPARQL FILTER string operations**: `LCASE(STR(?var))` for case-insensitive comparison; `lang(?var)` to check language tags; `STR()` to convert typed literals to plain strings for comparison"
}
```

Done. Added three new sections and two inline additions to existing sections:

**Additions to existing sections:**

- Data: GND endpoint details + ISBD encoding explanation

**New sections:**

**NLP & Named Entity Recognition** — covers bibliographic NER formulation, FRBR label hierarchy, silver labeling/tier system, SoR disambiguation, author-before-title, all relevant models (XLM-R, GLiNER, NuNER Zero, generative LLMs), evaluation datasets (HIPE, CoNLL, GLiNER benchmark, MultiCONER), dataset sizing rules, and LLM annotation protocol (Inline Bracketed, few-shot, spot-check)

**Corpus Analysis & Statistics** — Wilson CI vs. Wald, decision-relative sample sufficiency, stratified sampling, FP rate analysis methodology, confusion matrix, stratification variables for NER

**Early Modern German** — orthographic features, title-page conventions, credential sequences, genre conventions (Leichenpredigt, dissertations), embedded Latin vocabulary, "Das ist:" construction, heuristic failure modes, tokenisation challenges

**Entity Linking** — candidate generation/ranking pipeline, Levenshtein distance, Unicode normalization for matching, stopword removal for `contains-word`, generic title word filtering, `rdfs:subPropertyOf` and SPARQL inference gap, async batch querying pattern, SPARQL FILTER string operations
