### Assistant *(2026-03-24T18:52:17.583Z)*

**Tool call:** `Edit`
```json
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/prerequisites.md",
  "old_string": "## Corpus Analysis & Statistics\n\n- **Proportion estimation**: Bernoulli trial model for observed proportions p̂ = k/n; variance p(1−p)/n minimised near 0 and 1; extreme proportions (near 0% or 100%) require fewer samples to establish a decision-relevant conclusion\n- **Wilson confidence interval**: preferred over Wald (p̂ ± 1.96√(p̂(1−p̂)/n)) for proportions near 0 or 1; Wald can produce negative lower bounds and undercovers; Wilson conditions on hypothetical true p rather than p̂; Wilson (1927), JASA 22(158), 209–212\n- **Decision-relative sample sufficiency**: sample size is sufficient when the CI bound clears the decision threshold by a safe margin, not when the CI is narrow in absolute terms; 200 records can be sufficient if the observed proportion is extreme and the threshold is far from the boundary\n- **Stratified sampling**: sampling proportional vs. equal across strata; equal sampling ensures small-but-dense strata contribute meaningfully; proportional sampling is appropriate for prevalence estimation\n- **FP rate analysis**: for each heuristic signal, sample ~100–200 records and classify true positives vs. false positives; derive precision = 1 − FP_rate; use as weight in weighted scoring; SR-03 methodology\n- **Confusion matrix**: per-class precision, recall, F1 for multi-class classifiers; distinguish heuristic FP (classifier calls LATIN, truth is GERMAN) from FN (classifier calls GERMAN, truth is LATIN)\n- **Stratification variables for NER**: era (modern / 19th c. / 1700–1800 / pre-1700), silver tier (0 / 1 / 2), `dc_type` (Monografie, Leichenpredigt, Periodika…), title length (short ≤4 tokens / medium 5–14 / long >14)",
  "new_string": "## Corpus Analysis & Statistics\n\n### Proportion estimation and confidence intervals\n\n- **Bernoulli trial model**: observed proportion p̂ = k/n is a point estimate of the true population proportion p; variance of the estimator is p(1−p)/n, maximised at p=0.5 and approaching zero near 0 or 1; this is why extreme proportions can be established with small samples\n- **Wald interval**: p̂ ± z·√(p̂(1−p̂)/n); simple but unreliable when p̂ is near 0 or 1 — produces negative lower bounds, systematically undercovers the true proportion at the 95% level; do not use for FP rates or rare-class prevalence estimates\n- **Wilson interval**: preferred for all proportion estimates in this project; conditions on a hypothetical true p rather than p̂; formula: (p̂ + z²/2n ± z·√(p̂(1−p̂)/n + z²/4n²)) / (1 + z²/n); always produces bounds in [0,1]; Wilson (1927), JASA 22(158), 209–212; use `statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint(k, n, method='wilson')` in Python\n- **One-sided vs. two-sided intervals**: use two-sided (default) when estimating a proportion with unknown direction; use one-sided upper bound when the decision is \"is this proportion below a threshold?\" (e.g., \"is FP rate below 15%?\")\n\n### Sample size determination\n\nSample size is not a universal constant — it depends on the decision being made, the expected proportion, and the acceptable error margin.\n\n**For proportion estimation (given target margin of error e and confidence level 1−α):**\n\n```\nn = z² · p(1−p) / e²\n```\n\nwhere z = 1.96 for 95% CI. Because p is unknown, use p = 0.5 for the most conservative estimate (maximum variance). Examples:\n\n| Target margin e | p = 0.5 (worst case) | p = 0.1 (rare class) | p = 0.9 (dominant class) |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| ±10% | n = 97 | n = 35 | n = 35 |\n| ±5% | n = 385 | n = 139 | n = 139 |\n| ±3% | n = 1,068 | n = 384 | n = 384 |\n| ±1% | n = 9,604 | n = 3,458 | n = 3,458 |\n\nRule of thumb for FP rate studies: **n = 100–200 per heuristic signal** gives ±7–10% margin, sufficient for deciding whether to include or exclude a signal (the decision threshold is usually far from the observed rate).\n\n**For rare-class detection (is prevalence below threshold T?):**\n\nThe decision is one-sided: does the upper CI bound fall below T? With k=1 positive in n=200 (p̂=0.5%), the Wilson upper bound is 2.8% — sufficient to rule out a \"5% Latin stratum\" threshold. With k=0, the upper bound is 3·1/n (rule of three); for n=100 this gives an upper bound of 3%.\n\n**Decision-relative sufficiency:** n is sufficient when the CI bound clears the decision threshold by a safe margin, regardless of CI width. The key question is not \"is the CI narrow?\" but \"does the CI exclude the decision-relevant range?\" Had SR-06 observed LATIN = 8% (near the 5% threshold), n=200 would not have been sufficient — wider CI would have straddled the threshold.\n\n**For FP rate comparison between two signals:**\n\nUse Fisher's exact test (small n) or chi-square test (n > 30 per cell) to test whether two signals have significantly different FP rates. In practice, if one signal is 6% FP and another is 83% FP, no formal test is needed — the difference is decision-relevant regardless of statistical significance.\n\n**For inter-annotator agreement:**\n\n- **Raw agreement rate**: proportion of records where two annotators agree; simple but ignores chance agreement\n- **Cohen's κ**: κ = (P_o − P_e) / (1 − P_e), where P_o = observed agreement, P_e = expected agreement by chance; κ > 0.8 = strong agreement; κ 0.6–0.8 = moderate; κ < 0.6 = weak; preferred over raw agreement for annotation quality assessment\n- **Practical threshold for LLM annotation spot-check**: raw span-level F1 ≥ 85% (per SR-11 protocol) is a reasonable proxy; Cohen's κ is more principled but requires a two-annotator setup\n\n### Threshold definition\n\nThresholds in this project are decision thresholds, not statistical significance thresholds. They must be set before observing the data and justified by the decision consequences.\n\n**FP rate acceptance threshold (heuristic signals, SR-03):**\n\nThe acceptance threshold of ~15% FP was chosen based on training data economics: a silver label with ≤15% FP contributes more correct signal than noise; above 15%, the false labels begin to dominate learning. This is a practical engineering heuristic, not a theoretically derived value. It implies that for every 100 labels with 15% FP, the model sees 85 correct and 15 incorrect training examples — analogous to label smoothing in the range 0.1–0.2, which is empirically acceptable.\n\n**Exclusion threshold (SR-05 trailing period):** set at 15% FP — same reasoning as above. Trailing period showed 93% FP, far beyond the threshold.\n\n**Agreement threshold for LLM annotation (SR-11):** 85% span-level F1 against a manually annotated reference set before running the full batch. Rationale: below 85%, LLM errors are likely systematic (not random noise) — a prompt revision will improve the whole batch, not just a few records.\n\n**NuNER Zero decision gate (SR-08):** threshold TBD — to be set after reviewing SR-09 gold set F1 distribution; candidate values are precision ≥ 0.80 on TITLE and PERSON (Work-level), or F1 ≥ 0.75 on the pre-1750 stratum.\n\n**Stratification cutoffs (SR-10, title length):** p25 = 4 tokens (short), p75 = 14 tokens (long); quartile-based, not domain-specific — chosen to ensure each stratum contains ~25% of the corpus, balancing stratum sizes for gold set sampling.\n\n### Stratified sampling design\n\n- **Proportional allocation**: sample from each stratum in proportion to its size in the population; appropriate when estimating corpus-wide prevalence; gives large strata more influence\n- **Equal allocation**: sample the same n from each stratum regardless of stratum size; appropriate when each stratum needs independent analysis and a small stratum must contribute meaningfully (SR-06: Leichenpredigt n=100 despite being 6% of the combined sample vs. proportional ~12 records)\n- **Optimal (Neyman) allocation**: sample proportional to stratum size × stratum standard deviation; minimises overall variance for fixed total n; requires prior estimate of within-stratum variance — impractical for first-pass corpus studies\n- **Effective sample size with stratification**: stratified samples are more precise than simple random samples when within-stratum variance < total variance; the design effect (DEFF) = variance(stratified) / variance(SRS) is typically < 1 for well-chosen strata\n- **Seed for reproducibility**: always set `random_state=42` (or equivalent) in `df.sample()`; log the seed alongside the results\n\n### Script patterns for corpus analysis\n\n- **Sampling**: `df.sample(n=100, random_state=42)` for uniform random; `df.groupby('stratum').apply(lambda x: x.sample(min(n, len(x)), random_state=42))` for stratified equal\n- **Wilson CI in Python**: `from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint; low, high = proportion_confint(k, n, alpha=0.05, method='wilson')`\n- **Confusion matrix**: `sklearn.metrics.confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred, labels=[...])` + `classification_report` for per-class precision/recall/F1\n- **Cohen's κ**: `sklearn.metrics.cohen_kappa_score(annotator_a, annotator_b)`\n- **FP rate per signal**: group by signal flag, compute `(true_class != signal_intended_class).mean()` per group"
}
```

**Sample size determination:**

- Rule of thumb for FP rate studies: 100–200 records gives ±7–10%, sufficient for include/exclude decisions
- Rare-class detection via one-sided Wilson upper bound and the rule of three (k=0 case)

- Design effect concept

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/prerequisites.md",
  "old_string": "### Proportion estimation and confidence intervals\n\n- **Bernoulli trial model**: observed proportion p̂ = k/n is a point estimate of the true population proportion p; variance of the estimator is p(1−p)/n, maximised at p=0.5 and approaching zero near 0 or 1; this is why extreme proportions can be established with small samples\n- **Wald interval**: p̂ ± z·√(p̂(1−p̂)/n); simple but unreliable when p̂ is near 0 or 1 — produces negative lower bounds, systematically undercovers the true proportion at the 95% level; do not use for FP rates or rare-class prevalence estimates\n- **Wilson interval**: preferred for all proportion estimates in this project; conditions on a hypothetical true p rather than p̂; formula: (p̂ + z²/2n ± z·√(p̂(1−p̂)/n + z²/4n²)) / (1 + z²/n); always produces bounds in [0,1]; Wilson (1927), JASA 22(158), 209–212; use `statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint(k, n, method='wilson')` in Python\n- **One-sided vs. two-sided intervals**: use two-sided (default) when estimating a proportion with unknown direction; use one-sided upper bound when the decision is \"is this proportion below a threshold?\" (e.g., \"is FP rate below 15%?\")",
  "new_string": "### Foundational probability and distributions (prerequisites for CI formulas)\n\nThese topics underpin everything in this section. Study them first if the CI formulas below are unclear.\n\n- **Binomial distribution**: models the number of successes k in n independent Bernoulli trials, each with success probability p; `B(n, p)`; mean = np, variance = np(1−p); the FP rate study (k FPs in n sampled records) is a binomial experiment. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §3.3 (free at openintro.org/book/os/)*\n- **Variance of a Bernoulli random variable**: for a single trial with success probability p, variance = p(1−p); this expression is maximised at p=0.5 (take derivative d/dp[p(1−p)] = 1−2p = 0, solve: p=0.5); at p=0.5 variance = 0.25; at p=0 or p=1 variance = 0; this is why p=0.5 is used as the worst-case assumption when p is unknown. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §3.4*\n- **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**: for large n, the sampling distribution of p̂ = k/n is approximately normal with mean p and standard deviation √(p(1−p)/n), regardless of the underlying distribution; this is what justifies using z-scores in the CI formula. Typically considered adequate for n·p ≥ 10 and n·(1−p) ≥ 10. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.1*\n- **Standard normal distribution N(0,1)**: a normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1; z-scores measure how many standard deviations a value is from the mean; the value z=1.96 is the 97.5th percentile of N(0,1), meaning P(Z ≤ 1.96) = 0.975 — equivalently, P(−1.96 ≤ Z ≤ 1.96) = 0.95; this is where the \"1.96\" in all 95% CI formulas comes from. Look up the value in a z-table or compute `scipy.stats.norm.ppf(0.975)`. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.1*\n- **Confidence level and α**: a 95% CI (α=0.05) means: if the experiment were repeated many times, 95% of the constructed intervals would contain the true p; α is split equally between the two tails for a two-sided interval (α/2 = 0.025 per tail), which is why z=Φ⁻¹(1−0.025)=1.96. For 99% CI use z=2.576. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.2*\n\n### Proportion estimation and confidence intervals\n\n- **Bernoulli trial model**: observed proportion p̂ = k/n is a point estimate of the true population proportion p; variance of the estimator is p(1−p)/n, maximised at p=0.5 and approaching zero near 0 or 1; this is why extreme proportions can be established with small samples\n- **Wald interval**: p̂ ± z·√(p̂(1−p̂)/n); simple but unreliable when p̂ is near 0 or 1 — produces negative lower bounds, systematically undercovers the true proportion at the 95% level; do not use for FP rates or rare-class prevalence estimates\n- **Wilson interval**: preferred for all proportion estimates in this project; conditions on a hypothetical true p rather than p̂; formula: (p̂ + z²/2n ± z·√(p̂(1−p̂)/n + z²/4n²)) / (1 + z²/n); always produces bounds in [0,1]; Wilson (1927), JASA 22(158), 209–212; use `statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint(k, n, method='wilson')` in Python\n- **One-sided vs. two-sided intervals**: use two-sided (default) when estimating a proportion with unknown direction; use one-sided upper bound when the decision is \"is this proportion below a threshold?\" (e.g., \"is FP rate below 15%?\")"
}
```

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T19:06:52.776Z)*

Done. The two missing topics are now explicit entries:

- **Variance of Bernoulli** — shows why p=0.5 is the worst case, via the derivative d/dp[p(1−p)]=0
- **Standard normal distribution** — shows where z=1.96 comes from: it's the 97.5th percentile of N(0,1), i.e. `scipy.stats.norm.ppf(0.975)`

Both reference OpenIntro Statistics (free), with specific sections.

---

On the 97.5th percentile: this comes directly from the confidence level. A **95% CI** means you want 95% of the probability mass to fall *inside* the interval, leaving 5% outside. For a **two-sided** interval, that 5% is split equally between the left tail and the right tail — 2.5% each. So you need the z-value that cuts off the top 2.5% of N(0,1), which is the **97.5th percentile** (100% − 2.5% = 97.5%). That value is 1.96.

Visually:

```
  2.5%  |←——— 95% ———→|  2.5%
 ───────+───────────────+───────
      -1.96            +1.96
```

If you wanted a 99% CI instead, you'd leave 0.5% in each tail, so you'd take the 99.5th percentile (z=2.576).

The topic to study: **OpenIntro Statistics §4.2** — "Confidence intervals" — explains exactly this tail-splitting logic.

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/prerequisites.md",
  "old_string": "- **Standard normal distribution N(0,1)**: a normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 1; z-scores measure how many standard deviations a value is from the mean; the value z=1.96 is the 97.5th percentile of N(0,1), meaning P(Z ≤ 1.96) = 0.975 — equivalently, P(−1.96 ≤ Z ≤ 1.96) = 0.95; this is where the \"1.96\" in all 95% CI formulas comes from. Look up the value in a z-table or compute `scipy.stats.norm.ppf(0.975)`. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.1*\n- **Confidence level and α**: a 95% CI (α=0.05) means: if the experiment were repeated many times, 95% of the constructed intervals would contain the true p; α is split equally between the two tails for a two-sided interval (α/2 = 0.025 per tail), which is why z=Φ⁻¹(1−0.025)=1.96. For 99% CI use z=2.576. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.2*",
  "new_string": "- **Normal distribution N(μ, σ²)**: a symmetric bell-shaped probability distribution fully described by its mean μ (centre) and variance σ² (spread); 68% of values fall within ±1σ, 95% within ±2σ, 99.7% within ±3σ. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §3.1*\n- **Standardisation and z-scores**: any normal random variable X ~ N(μ, σ²) can be converted to a standard score z = (X − μ) / σ; z measures how many standard deviations X is above or below the mean; z=0 is the mean, z=1 is one standard deviation above, z=−1.96 is 1.96 standard deviations below. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §3.1*\n- **Standard normal distribution N(0,1)**: a normal distribution with mean=0 and standard deviation=1; all z-scores live on this scale; the cumulative distribution function Φ(z) = P(Z ≤ z) gives the probability that a standard normal variable falls at or below z; look up in a z-table or compute `scipy.stats.norm.cdf(z)`. The inverse Φ⁻¹(p) gives the z-score at the p-th percentile: `scipy.stats.norm.ppf(p)`. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §3.1*\n- **Two-sided 95% CI — what it means step by step**:\n  1. You want an interval that captures 95% of the probability mass of the sampling distribution of p̂\n  2. The remaining 5% (called α = 0.05) falls *outside* the interval\n  3. \"Two-sided\" means you split that 5% equally between the left tail and the right tail: 2.5% each\n  4. You need the z-value that marks the boundary of the upper 2.5% tail: Φ⁻¹(1 − 0.025) = Φ⁻¹(0.975) = **1.96**\n  5. By symmetry, −1.96 marks the lower tail boundary\n  6. So P(−1.96 ≤ Z ≤ 1.96) = 0.95 — exactly 95% of the standard normal distribution lies between −1.96 and +1.96\n  7. The CI formula p̂ ± 1.96·SE then says: \"the true p lies within 1.96 standard errors of our estimate, with 95% confidence\"\n  - *\"Two-sided\"* means you allow the true value to be either above or below your estimate — appropriate when you don't know in advance which direction the error goes\n  - *\"95% confidence\"* means: if you repeated the sampling and CI construction many times, 95% of those intervals would contain the true p — it is a statement about the procedure, not about this specific interval\n  - For a **one-sided** upper bound (e.g., \"is FP rate below 15%?\"), all 5% goes into one tail: z = Φ⁻¹(0.95) = 1.645\n  - *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.2*\n- **Confidence level and α**: α = 1 − confidence level; α=0.05 for 95%, α=0.01 for 99%; for two-sided intervals use z = Φ⁻¹(1 − α/2); for 99% CI: z = Φ⁻¹(0.995) = 2.576. *Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.2*"
}
```

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T19:07:37.029Z)*

Here it is without the jargon.

Imagine you're trying to figure out how tall the average person in a city is, but you can only measure 200 people. You measure them, compute the average, and get 170cm. Your question is: how confident are you that the *true* city average is also close to 170cm?

A **confidence interval** gives you a range — say, 168–172cm — and says: "I'm 95% confident the true average is somewhere in here."

The **95%** part means: if you repeated this experiment 100 times (each time measuring 200 different people), about 95 of those 100 experiments would produce an interval that actually contains the true city average. 5 of them would get unlucky and miss.

The **z-score of 1.96** is just the width knob. The bell curve (normal distribution) has a property: 95% of all values fall within ±1.96 standard deviations of the centre. So the formula says: "build an interval that stretches 1.96 standard deviations either side of your estimate." That's the 95% interval. If you wanted to be 99% confident instead, you'd need to stretch further — to ±2.576 standard deviations.

**Two-sided** just means you're guarding against being wrong in either direction — the true value could be above or below your estimate, and you don't know which. If you already knew it could only be above (like "is the FP rate definitely below 15%?"), you'd use a one-sided interval and the width shrinks slightly (z=1.645 instead of 1.96).

**p=0.5** is the worst case because the width of the interval also depends on how spread out your measurements are. For a yes/no question (is this record a false positive?), the spread is p×(1−p). That expression is largest when p=0.5 (spread = 0.25) and smaller when p is near 0 or 1. Since you don't know the true FP rate before measuring, you plug in p=0.5 to get the *widest possible* interval — a safe upper bound on how many samples you need.

The key reference to study all of this properly: **OpenIntro Statistics, chapters 3–4** (free at openintro.org). Chapter 3 covers the normal distribution and z-scores; chapter 4 covers confidence intervals. It's written at an introductory level with no calculus required.

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T19:09:05.631Z)*

**Tool call:** `Write`
```json
{
  "file_path": "/Users/mta/Documents/claude/gemea/notes/prerequisites-abitur.md",
  "content": "# GeMeA — Prerequisites (Abitur Level)\n\nPlain-language explanations of mathematical and statistical concepts used in the project. No calculus required. For the full technical reference see [prerequisites.md](prerequisites.md).\n\n**Primary reference for all topics in this file:** OpenIntro Statistics (free at [openintro.org/book/os](https://openintro.org/book/os/)), Chapters 3–4.\n\n---\n\n## 1. The normal distribution (bell curve)\n\nIf you measure something that varies randomly — the height of people, the length of book titles, the number of errors a classifier makes — and you plot how often each value occurs, you often get a bell-shaped curve. Most values cluster around the middle (the average), and fewer and fewer values appear the further you get from the middle.\n\nThis shape is called the **normal distribution**. It has two properties that fully describe it:\n- **Mean (μ)**: the centre of the bell — where the peak is\n- **Standard deviation (σ)**: how wide or narrow the bell is — a large σ means values are spread out far from the mean; a small σ means they cluster tightly\n\nA useful rule to memorise:\n- 68% of all values fall within ±1σ of the mean\n- 95% of all values fall within ±2σ of the mean (more precisely: ±1.96σ)\n- 99.7% of all values fall within ±3σ of the mean\n\n*Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §3.1*\n\n---\n\n## 2. Z-scores\n\nA **z-score** answers the question: how many standard deviations away from the mean is this value?\n\nFormula: `z = (value − mean) / standard deviation`\n\nExamples:\n- A value exactly at the mean → z = 0\n- A value 1 standard deviation above the mean → z = 1\n- A value 2 standard deviations below the mean → z = −2\n\nZ-scores let you compare values from different scales. Instead of asking \"is 170cm tall?\", you ask \"is this person's height 1.5 standard deviations above average?\" — which means the same thing regardless of whether you measure in cm or inches.\n\nThe **standard normal distribution** is just a normal distribution with mean=0 and standard deviation=1. All z-scores live on this scale. When you look up a z-score in a table (or compute `scipy.stats.norm.ppf(p)` in Python), you're working with the standard normal.\n\n*Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §3.1*\n\n---\n\n## 3. What is a confidence interval?\n\nImagine you want to know what fraction of a library's records contain a false label (the FP rate). The library has 4 million records — you can't check all of them. So you pick 200 at random, check those, and find that 15 out of 200 are false positives.\n\nYour best guess for the true FP rate is 15/200 = 7.5%. But this is just an estimate from a sample. If you had picked a different 200 records, you might have got 14 or 17 false positives. The **confidence interval** expresses this uncertainty as a range.\n\nA **95% confidence interval** of, say, [4.3%, 12.0%] means:\n\n> \"If I repeated this experiment many times — each time sampling 200 records and computing an interval — then 95% of those intervals would contain the true FP rate.\"\n\nIt does **not** mean \"there is a 95% probability the true rate is in this specific interval.\" The true rate is a fixed number; it either is or isn't in the interval. The 95% is a property of the *procedure*, not this specific interval.\n\n**Two-sided vs. one-sided:**\n- **Two-sided**: you don't know in advance whether the true value is above or below your estimate — so you build an interval stretching in both directions. Use this by default.\n- **One-sided**: you only care about one direction. For example: \"is the FP rate definitely below 15%?\" Here you only need an upper bound. A one-sided interval is slightly narrower than a two-sided one because all the uncertainty budget goes in one direction.\n\n*Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.2*\n\n---\n\n## 4. Where does 1.96 come from?\n\nFrom the rule in §1: 95% of values in a normal distribution fall within ±1.96 standard deviations of the mean.\n\nMore precisely: if you want the middle 95%, you leave out 5% total — 2.5% in the left tail and 2.5% in the right tail. The z-score that cuts off the top 2.5% is 1.96. That's it.\n\n```\n   2.5%  |←————— 95% —————→|  2.5%\n─────────+───────────────────+─────────\n       −1.96                +1.96\n```\n\nSo when the CI formula says `p̂ ± 1.96 × SE`, it means: \"stretch the interval 1.96 standard errors either side of the estimate, which captures 95% of the sampling distribution.\"\n\nDifferent confidence levels use different z-scores:\n| Confidence level | α (left out) | Each tail | z-score |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| 90% | 10% | 5% | 1.645 |\n| 95% | 5% | 2.5% | 1.96 |\n| 99% | 1% | 0.5% | 2.576 |\n\n*Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.1–4.2*\n\n---\n\n## 5. Why p=0.5 is the worst case\n\nWhen estimating a yes/no proportion (e.g., \"is this record a false positive?\"), the width of the confidence interval depends on `p × (1−p)`, where p is the true proportion.\n\nThis product is largest when p = 0.5:\n- p = 0.5 → p(1−p) = 0.25 (widest interval)\n- p = 0.1 → p(1−p) = 0.09 (narrower)\n- p = 0.9 → p(1−p) = 0.09 (narrower)\n- p = 0.01 → p(1−p) = 0.0099 (very narrow)\n\nSince you don't know the true p before you measure, you can plug in p=0.5 to get the widest possible interval — a conservative upper bound on how many samples you need. If the true p turns out to be far from 0.5, your actual interval will be narrower than planned, which is fine.\n\nIn other words: p=0.5 answers the question \"how many samples do I need in the *worst case*?\"\n\n*Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §3.3–3.4*\n\n---\n\n## 6. Bernoulli trials and the binomial distribution\n\nA **Bernoulli trial** is a single yes/no experiment: flip a coin, check if a record is a false positive, ask if a title contains a person name. Each trial has two outcomes — success (probability p) or failure (probability 1−p).\n\nA **binomial distribution** counts how many successes you get in n independent Bernoulli trials. If you check 200 records and each has a 7.5% FP rate independently, the number of false positives follows B(200, 0.075).\n\nProperties:\n- Mean = n × p (expected number of successes)\n- Variance = n × p × (1−p)\n- For large n (typically n×p ≥ 10 and n×(1−p) ≥ 10), the binomial is well approximated by a normal distribution — this is why the normal-based CI formula works for proportions\n\n*Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §3.3–3.4*\n\n---\n\n## 7. The Central Limit Theorem (CLT)\n\nThe **Central Limit Theorem** is the reason all the CI formulas above work.\n\nIt says: if you take a large enough random sample and compute the sample mean (or proportion), that estimate will be approximately normally distributed — regardless of what the underlying data looks like. The distribution of the estimate has:\n- Mean = the true population value (p)\n- Standard deviation = √(p(1−p)/n), called the **standard error (SE)**\n\nThis is remarkable: even if the underlying data is not normally distributed, the *estimate* from a large sample behaves normally. The CLT is what allows you to use z=1.96 and the normal distribution formulas for proportion CIs.\n\n\"Large enough\" in practice means n×p ≥ 10 and n×(1−p) ≥ 10. For FP rates around 5–95% and n=100–200, this condition is met.\n\n*Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.1*\n\n---\n\n## 8. Putting it all together: reading a CI formula\n\nThe Wald confidence interval for a proportion:\n\n```\np̂  ±  z  ×  √(p̂(1−p̂) / n)\n```\n\nIn plain language:\n- **p̂** = your observed proportion (e.g., 15/200 = 0.075)\n- **z** = the z-score for your chosen confidence level (1.96 for 95%)\n- **√(p̂(1−p̂)/n)** = the standard error — how much your estimate would vary across repeated samples; gets smaller as n grows\n- **±** = the interval stretches equally in both directions (two-sided)\n\nThe whole expression says: \"my estimate is p̂, and I'm 95% confident the true value is within 1.96 standard errors of that.\"\n\nThe **Wilson interval** (preferred when p̂ is near 0 or 1) uses the same ingredients but arranges them differently to avoid the Wald interval's tendency to produce impossible negative lower bounds for rare events. Use `statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint(k, n, method='wilson')` in Python rather than computing it by hand.\n\n*Reference: OpenIntro Statistics §4.2; Wilson (1927), JASA 22(158), 209–212*\n"
}
```

2. Key Technical Concepts:

**NER / Silver labeling:**
- Three-tier silver system: tier 2 (structural, has_dot_dash + multi-field, 4,613 records), tier 1 (heuristic, n_fields≥3 or f_person∧f_year, 335,524 records), tier 0 (no labels, 92.4%)
- Three-tier justification: has_dot_dash is categorical (not a degree), convergent-evidence logic for tier 1/0 boundary, four tiers require field-level weighting not yet supported
- Field-level weighting: weight_i = 1 − FP_rate_i; composite score = Σ weight_i; requires FP rates for f_volume and f_publisher (unsampled); calibration requires SR-09 gold set

**FRBR scope (SR-07, resolved):**
- DDB objects are all at Manifestation level — Work and Expression are derived groupings
- Phase 1 (paper): TITLE, OTHER_TITLE, PERSON
- Phase 2: TRANSLATOR, PARALLEL_TITLE, MEDIUM
- Manifestation labels deferred; gold set annotation schema should include Expression labels from the start to avoid re-annotation

**SR renumbering:**
- SR-07 = FRBR metric scope (was SR-09) — now Resolved
- SR-08 = NuNER Zero evaluation — Open, blocked on SR-09
- SR-09 = Gold set composition (was SR-07) — Open, unblocked
- SR-11 = Labeling strategy and prompt design (was SR-12)
- SR-12 = Field-level weighting (was SR-11, future)

**Zhan et al. (2026) key findings:**
- Fine-tuned open-source LLMs (LLaMA3/Qwen + LoRA + Inline Bracketed/XML) match encoder-based NER (F1 ~93 on CoNLL2003)
- Output format decisive: Inline Bracketed/XML ~90–91 avg F1; Offset-based JSON collapses to ~30 F1
- Zero-shot LLM NER ~88 F1 — material step down from fine-tuned
- Low-resource domain gap persists (GENIA biomedical: 79 vs. 84)
- NuNER Zero not evaluated in this paper — it's encoder/span-extraction, not generative

**GLiNER vs NuNER Zero:**
- GLiNER (Zaratiana et al., NAACL 2024): merged BiLM encoder; span extraction; zero-shot OOD avg F1 60.9 (beats GoLLIE-7B); GLiNER-Multi (mdeberta-v3-base) surpasses ChatGPT on German (39.5 vs 37.1); max span width K=12; cannot extract discontinuous entities
- NuNER (Bogdanov et al., EMNLP 2024): separate text+concept encoders; few-shot SOTA (k=1: 39.4 vs RoBERTa 24.5); zero-shot performance explicitly NOT evaluated in paper; English-only training data; multilingual variant appendix-only
- Metric incompatibility: GLiNER = entity-level exact match; NuNER = token-level macro F1

**Dataset size for fine-tuning:**
- Rule of thumb: 200–500 spans per entity type from strong pretrained checkpoint
- Work only (3 types): 1k–2k records
- Work + Expression (6 types): 3k–5k records
- Modern stratum: already covered by 335k silver tier-1 + 4,613 tier-2; no additional labeling needed

**Early Modern German prompt design:**
- Three structural failure modes: author-before-title, credential sequences in PERSON span, embedded Latin vocabulary
- System prompt: label definitions, critical author-before-title rule, PERSON span boundaries, embedded Latin rule, OTHER_TITLE triggers, orthographic note, what not to label
- 5 few-shot patterns: A (credential+name+role+title), B (title+author after "durch"), C ("Das ist:" subtitle), D (embedded Latin, no PERSON), E (author+dedicatee)
- Batch: temperature=0, model=claude-opus-4-6 preferred, SHA-256 prompt hash for reproducibility, 85% F1 agreement threshold before full batch

**Statistics:**
- Wilson CI preferred over Wald for near-0/1 proportions; `proportion_confint(k, n, method='wilson')`
- p=0.5 is worst case because it maximises p(1−p) = 0.25 (derivative = 0 at p=0.5)
- z=1.96 is Φ⁻¹(0.975) — 97.5th percentile of N(0,1) because two-sided 95% CI leaves 2.5% in each tail
- n=100: ±9.8% margin (derived from 1.96×√(0.25/100)); n=200: ±6.9%
- Rule of thumb 100–200 has no separate citation — derived from formula, not empirical NLP convention

     1→# GeMeA — GND Werk Linking via QLever DNB Endpoint
     2→
     3→Script: `scripts/link_gnd_works.py`
     4→Replaces: lobid-gnd REST API approach (previously planned but endpoint was TBD)
     5→
     6→`link_gnd_works.py` is an **offline ingest step**, not a runtime component. It runs once (or on re-ingest) during Phase 0 to enrich ProvidedCHO records with GND Werk URIs. Its output feeds mocho → QLever → the GeMeA API. The GND linking results are ultimately exposed through the API's `/work/{id}` endpoint and SPARQL proxy — `link_gnd_works.py` itself never serves live requests. Design constraints follow from this: throughput and batch efficiency matter; latency per query does not.
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## 1. Summary
    11→

    25→
    26→## 2. Open questions
    27→
    28→| ID | Question | Blocking | Status |
    29→|---|---|---|---|
    30→| ~~OQ-00a~~ | ~~Is `preferredNameForTheWork` in the text index?~~ | — | **Resolved** — Recon 0.2: `contains-word` causes SPARQL parse error. Endpoint enforces SPARQL 1.1 only. Pattern C is the sole active query path. |
    31→| ~~OQ-00b~~ | ~~Exact predicate for author link (`gndo:firstAuthor` or `gndo:creator`)?~~ | — | **Resolved** — Recon 0.3: `gndo:author` dominant (8/10); `gndo:firstAuthor` added via `VALUES` (subproperty, SPARQL won't infer); `gndo:poet` for lyrical works; `gndo:composer` for musical works. |
    32→| OQ-01 | Does `sparql.dnb.de` normalize Umlauts in FILTER comparisons? (`ä` vs `a`) | No | Open — see [§2.1](#21-recon-05--umlaut-handling-in-filter-comparisons-oq-01) |
    33→| OQ-02 | What are the actual rate limits on `sparql.dnb.de`? | No | Open — no published SLA; profile at `CONCURRENCY = 10` during first batch run |
    34→| OQ-03 | Should `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` be added as a UNION branch in Pattern C to increase recall? | No | Open — preferred names are often ISBD-qualified strings (recon 0.4); variant names may carry the clean title form |
    35→| OQ-04 | Does `gndo:composer`/`gndo:firstComposer` behave analogously to `gndo:author`/`gndo:firstAuthor` for MusicalWork records? | No | Open — verify with a musician query analogous to recon 0.3 |
    36→
    37→### 2.1 Recon 0.5 — Umlaut handling in FILTER comparisons (OQ-01)
    38→
    39→The question is whether `LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "gotz von berlichingen"` (diacritics stripped) matches a record stored as `"Götz von Berlichingen"`. LCASE performs case folding only; it does not decompose or strip combining characters. The hypothesis is that the endpoint does **not** normalize Umlauts, meaning a stripped form will not match an umlaut-bearing literal — and therefore diacritic stripping in FR-05 would *hurt* recall against `preferredNameForTheWork`.
    40→
    41→Test case: Goethe's *Götz von Berlichingen* (`https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238` as author) — a well-known work with `ö` in the title.
    42→
    43→**Query A — with umlaut (expect: results)**
    44→
    45→```sparql
    46→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
    47→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
    48→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
    49→  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }
    50→  ?work a ?wtype .
    51→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
    52→  ?work ?authorPred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .
    53→  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "götz von berlichingen")
    54→}
    55→LIMIT 10
    56→```
    57→
    58→```bash
    59→curl -s -X POST "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd" \
    60→  -H "Accept: application/sparql-results+json" \
    61→  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
    62→  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
    63→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
    64→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
    65→  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }
    66→  ?work a ?wtype .
    67→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
    68→  ?work ?authorPred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .
    69→  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "götz von berlichingen")
    70→}
    71→LIMIT 10'
    72→```
    73→
    74→**Query B — diacritics stripped (expect: no results if endpoint does not normalize)**
    75→
    76→```sparql
    77→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
    78→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
    79→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
    80→  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }
    81→  ?work a ?wtype .
    82→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
    83→  ?work ?authorPred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .
    84→  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "gotz von berlichingen")
    85→}
    86→LIMIT 10
    87→```
    88→
    89→```bash
    90→curl -s -X POST "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd" \
    91→  -H "Accept: application/sparql-results+json" \
    92→  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
    93→  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
    94→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
    95→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
    96→  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }
    97→  ?work a ?wtype .
    98→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
    99→  ?work ?authorPred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .
   100→  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "gotz von berlichingen")
   101→}
   102→LIMIT 10'
   103→```
   104→
   105→**Interpretation:**
   106→
   107→| Query A result | Query B result | Conclusion | Impact on FR-05 |
   108→|---|---|---|---|
   109→| Results returned | No results | Endpoint does **not** normalize Umlauts — FILTER is byte-exact | Strip diacritics from **query string only if GND stores stripped forms**; otherwise keep Umlauts; send both forms via UNION |
   110→| Results returned | Results returned | Endpoint normalizes Umlauts (accent-insensitive collation) | Stripping is safe; normalized form sufficient for FILTER |
   111→| No results | No results | Title not stored as `preferredNameForTheWork` in this form | Re-check with `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` (OQ-03) |
   112→
   113→---
   114→
   115→## 3. Knowledge base index
   116→
   117→**Description:** DNB Bibliography and GND (Gemeinsame Normdatei) (`authorities-gnd_lds`, `dnb-all_lds` version 23.02.2026) — updated monthly
   118→
   119→| Metric | Value |
   120→|---|---|
   121→| Triples | 1,242,168,767 |
   122→| Subjects | 186,556,644 |
   123→| Predicates | 591 |
   124→| Objects | 457,761,162 |
   125→
   126→---
   127→
   128→## 4. Endpoint and HTTP API
   129→
   130→**Endpoint:** `https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd`
   131→**Method:** `POST`
   132→**Body:** `query=<URL-encoded SPARQL>` (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`)
   133→**Accept:** `application/sparql-results+json`
   134→
   135→QLever instance settings:
   136→- Languages: `en`
   137→- Default max rows: 100
   138→- Default mode: 3 — SPARQL & context-sensitive entities
   139→
   140→QLever-specific keywords used: `contains-word`, `textlimit`, `score()`
   141→
   142→---
   143→
   144→## 5. Step 0 — Recon queries (run once before implementation)
   145→
   146→Three things to verify before writing production queries.
   147→
   148→### 5.1 Work type coverage
   149→
   150→IFLA LRM defines a Work (LRM-E2) as "a distinct intellectual or artistic creation" — the abstract entity at the top of the WEMI hierarchy, independent of any particular realization or carrier. The GND ontology (`gnd_20251218.ttl`) models six subclasses of `gndo:Work`. Only three correspond to the IFLA LRM Work concept:
   151→
   152→| GND class | Label (en / de) | MARC21 | IFLA LRM | Include in queries |
   153→|---|---|---|---|---|
   154→| `gndo:Work` | Work / Werk | `079 $v=wit` | LRM-E2 Work — direct match | **Yes** |
   155→| `gndo:MusicalWork` | Musical work / Werk der Musik | `079 $v=wim` | LRM-E2 Work — a musical composition as distinct creative entity | **Yes** |
   156→| `gndo:Manuscript` | Manuscript / Schriftdenkmal | `079 $v=wis` | LRM-E2 Work — a unique historical manuscript (e.g. Nibelungenlied) is the distinct intellectual creation itself | **Yes** |
   157→| `gndo:Expression` | Expression / Expression | `079 $v=wie` | LRM-E3 Expression — the realization of a Work in a specific linguistic or notational form; one level below Work in WEMI | No |
   158→| `gndo:VersionOfAMusicalWork` | Version of a musical work / Fassung eines Werks der Musik | `079 $v=wif` | LRM-E3 Expression — a specific arrangement or version is a realization of the Work, not a new Work (unless substantially transformed) | No |
   159→| `gndo:CollectiveManuscript` | Collective manuscript / Sammelhandschrift | `079 $v=wil` | LRM-E4 Manifestation — a bound collection of multiple works; the container, not the intellectual creation | No |
   160→| `gndo:ProvenanceCharacteristic` | Provenance characteristic / Provenienzmerkmal | `079 $v=wip` | No WEMI equivalent — ownership marks and traces left in/on physical items; not a creative entity | No |
   161→
   162→```sparql
   163→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   164→SELECT ?type (COUNT(?w) AS ?n) WHERE {
   165→  VALUES ?type {
   166→    gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript
   167→    gndo:Expression gndo:VersionOfAMusicalWork
   168→    gndo:CollectiveManuscript gndo:ProvenanceCharacteristic
   169→  }
   170→  ?w a ?type .
   171→}
   172→GROUP BY ?type ORDER BY DESC(?n)
   173→```
   174→
   175→Constraining with `VALUES` avoids a full dataset type scan and lets the endpoint use per-type indexes. The original open-ended query caused a 504 Gateway Timeout.
   176→
   177→**Results:**
   178→
   179→| ?type | ?n |
   180→|---|---|
   181→| MusicalWork | 330,818 |
   182→| Work | 242,333 |
   183→| ProvenanceCharacteristic | 10,080 |
   184→| Manuscript | 7,133 |
   185→| Expression | 5,766 |
   186→| VersionOfAMusicalWork | 2,905 |
   187→| CollectiveManuscript | — (no results; zero entities in dataset) |
   188→
   189→**Conclusion:** `VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }` is confirmed. The three IFLA LRM Work-equivalent classes cover 580,284 entities (242,333 + 330,818 + 7,133). The four excluded classes are either IFLA Expression-level (`Expression`, `VersionOfAMusicalWork`), have no WEMI equivalent (`ProvenanceCharacteristic`), or are absent from this dataset (`CollectiveManuscript`).
   190→
   191→### 5.2 Confirm text index covers `preferredNameForTheWork`
   192→
   193→```sparql
   194→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   195→SELECT ?w ?label ?score WHERE {
   196→  ?w a gndo:Work .
   197→  ?w gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?label .
   198→  ?label contains-word "faust"
   199→  BIND(score(?label) AS ?score)
   200→}
   201→ORDER BY DESC(?score) LIMIT 5
   202→```
   203→
   204→**Test (curl):**
   205→
   206→```bash
   207→curl -s -X POST "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd" \
   208→  -H "Accept: application/sparql-results+json" \
   209→  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
   210→  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   211→SELECT ?w ?label WHERE {
   212→  ?w a gndo:Work .
   213→  ?w gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?label .
   214→  ?label contains-word "faust"
   215→}
   216→LIMIT 5'
   217→```
   218→
   219→**Result:**
   220→
   221→```json
   222→{
   223→  "exception": "Invalid SPARQL query: Token \"contains-word\": mismatched input 'contains-word' expecting {'(', 'a', '^', '!', IRI_REF, PNAME_NS, PNAME_LN, VAR1, VAR2, PREFIX_LANGTAG}",
   224→  "metadata": { "line": 5, "positionInLine": 9, "startIndex": 155, "stopIndex": 167 },
   225→  "status": "ERROR"
   226→}
   227→```
   228→
   229→The parser fails at line 5, position 9 — exactly where `contains-word` appears as predicate. The endpoint enforces SPARQL 1.1 grammar; `contains-word` is QLever-specific syntax and is not available here. **Patterns A and B do not apply. Pattern C is the active query path.**
   230→
   231→### 5.3 Confirm author predicate name
   232→
   233→Query Works that link to Goethe (`https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238`) to find the predicate used:
   234→
   235→```sparql
   236→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   237→SELECT ?work ?pred WHERE {
   238→  ?work ?pred <https://d-nb.info/gnd/118540238> .
   239→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
   240→  ?work a ?wtype .
   241→}
   242→LIMIT 10
   243→```
   244→
   245→**Results:**
   246→
   247→| ?work | ?pred |
   248→|---|---|
   249→| 4427937-1 | author |
   250→| 134483180X | relatedPerson |
   251→| 1367943906 | author |
   252→| 1345022085 | author |
   253→| 1367911087 | author |
   254→| 134558573X | author |
   255→| 1345758731 | poet |
   256→| 1346874786 | author |
   257→| 1347120017 | author |
   258→| 1347121307 | author |
   259→
   260→**Conclusion:** The dominant predicate is `gndo:author` (8/10 results). `gndo:poet` appears as a role-specific variant (Goethe wrote lyrics for musical works); `gndo:relatedPerson` is too broad for author linking.
   261→
   262→From `gnd_20251218.ttl`, the relevant author-role property hierarchy is:
   263→
   264→| Property | Label (en/de) | `rdfs:subPropertyOf` | MARC21 | Scope |
   265→|---|---|---|---|---|
   266→| `gndo:author` | Author / Verfasser | RDA `P60434` | `$4=auta` | Textual works; primary creator |
   267→| `gndo:firstAuthor` | First author / Erste Verfasserschaft | **`gndo:author`**, RDA `P60434` | `$4=aut1` | Subproperty of `author`; single/lead author |
   268→| `gndo:creator` | Creator / Urheber | — | `$4=urhe` | General; no subproperty relation to `author` |
   269→| `gndo:poet` | Poet / Dichter | RDA `P60477` | `$4=dich` | Words of non-dramatic musical works |
   270→| `gndo:composer` | Composer / Komponist | RDA `P60426` | `$4=koma` | Musical works |
   271→
   272→**SPARQL does not infer `rdfs:subPropertyOf` without a reasoner.** A query constraining `gndo:author` will miss triples that use `gndo:firstAuthor`, even though `firstAuthor` is a subproperty of `author`. Pattern A must explicitly cover both using `VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor }`. `gndo:poet` and `gndo:composer` should be added for coverage of MusicalWork records.
   273→
   274→### 5.4 Confirm literal format of `preferredNameForTheWork`
   275→
   276→Determines whether VALUES-based queries must include `@de` language-tagged variants.
   277→
   278→```bash
   279→curl -s -X POST "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd" \
   280→  -H "Accept: application/sparql-results+json" \
   281→  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
   282→  --data-urlencode 'query=PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   283→SELECT ?l (lang(?l) AS ?tag) WHERE {
   284→  <https://d-nb.info/gnd/4427937-1> gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?l .
   285→}
   286→LIMIT 5'
   287→```
   288→
   289→**Result:**
   290→
   291→```json
   292→{
   293→  "l": { "type": "literal", "value": "Faust I / Walpurgisnacht / Berlin / Staatsbibliothek Berlin / Ms. germ. qu. 527" },
   294→  "tag": { "type": "literal", "value": "" }
   295→}
   296→```
   297→
   298→**Conclusions:**
   299→1. `preferredNameForTheWork` literals have **no language tag** — `@de` variants are not needed in VALUES clauses.
   300→2. GND preferred names are **not clean titles** — this Manuscript entry carries a full ISBD-style string including location and shelfmark, not just the work title. Pattern C's exact FILTER match will not resolve titles like "Faust" against such records; those can only be matched via `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` or through a separate normalized-title query.
   301→
   302→---
   303→
   304→## 6. Step 1 — Title extraction
   305→
   306→Raw `dc:title` strings in DDB ProvidedCHOs are ISBD-encoded: they concatenate title, statement of responsibility, edition, publisher, place, and year into a single field using ISBD punctuation. Sending raw strings to GND produces low-precision matches. Extraction runs before token preparation (Step 3) and GND querying (Step 2).
   307→
   308→### 6.1 Method: two-stage pipeline
   309→
   310→| Stage | Trigger | Method |
   311→|---|---|---|
   312→| **Primary — ISBD rule-based parser** | Record contains ISBD punctuation markers | Split on ` / `, `. - `, ` : ` per ISBD conventions; take the leading segment as the title |
   313→| **Secondary — NER fallback** | No ISBD markers detected (~71.6% of corpus) | Zero-shot NER with NuNER Zero; extract spans tagged as work titles |
   314→
   315→The ISBD parser handles the minority of records that use structured punctuation. NuNER Zero (zero-shot) handles the majority without ISBD markers, where rule-based splitting would either produce nothing or split on the wrong boundary.
   316→
   317→### 6.2 ISBD split rules
   318→
   319→| Separator | Meaning in ISBD | Action |
   320→|---|---|---|
   321→| ` / ` | Statement of responsibility | Take everything before as title |
   322→| `. - ` | New ISBD area (edition, publication, etc.) | Take everything before as title |
   323→| ` : ` | Subtitle or other title information | Optional split; retain subtitle if short |
   324→
   325→Example: `"Faust : eine Tragödie / von Goethe. - Neue Ausg."` → extracted title: `"Faust : eine Tragödie"` (or `"Faust"` if subtitle is stripped).
   326→
   327→### 6.3 Output
   328→
   329→`extracted_title` — a clean title string passed to Step 3 (token preparation) and used as the FILTER value in Pattern C queries.
   330→`extraction_method` — `"isbd"` or `"ner"`, recorded in the output JSON (FR-07).
   331→
   332→Full extraction design is documented in `notes/gnd-title-extraction.md`. NER model selection, evaluation, and bibliographic NER considerations are documented in `notes/ner-bibliographic.md`.
   333→
   334→---
   335→
   336→## 7. Step 2 — Query patterns
   337→
   338→| Query pattern | Fields | Justification |
   339→|---|---|---|
   340→| **A** — Title + author | `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` (text search); `gndo:author`, `gndo:firstAuthor`, `gndo:poet`, `gndo:composer` (constraint via VALUES) | Author URI narrows the candidate set before text matching; highest precision. Use when author GND URI is available and text index is confirmed (recon 0.2–0.3). |
   341→| **B** — Title only | `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` (preferred); `gndo:variantNameForTheWork` (UNION fallback) | No author URI available. UNION over variant names increases recall for cases where the DDB title matches a GND alternative form rather than the preferred label. Use when text index is confirmed but no author URI. |
   342→| **C** — FILTER fallback | `gndo:preferredNameForTheWork` (exact string match) | `contains-word` unavailable or not indexed for the predicate (determined by recon 0.2). Exact/normalized string comparison only; no relevance ranking. Lower recall than A/B. |
   343→
   344→### 7.1 Pattern A — Title + author, text-indexed (primary)
   345→
   346→Used when: author GND URI is available AND `contains-word` is confirmed (recon 0.2).
   347→
   348→`gndo:firstAuthor` is a subproperty of `gndo:author` but SPARQL does not infer this without a reasoner — both must be listed explicitly. `gndo:poet` and `gndo:composer` cover musical and lyrical works.
   349→
   350→```sparql
   351→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   352→
   353→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel ?score WHERE {
   354→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
   355→  VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }
   356→  ?work a ?wtype .
   357→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
   358→  ?work ?authorPred <{author_gnd_uri}> .
   359→  ?prefLabel contains-word "{title_tokens}"
   360→  BIND(score(?prefLabel) AS ?score)
   361→}
   362→ORDER BY DESC(?score)
   363→TEXTLIMIT 5
   364→LIMIT 10
   365→```
   366→
   367→`TEXTLIMIT 5` caps QLever's internal text candidates for performance; `LIMIT 10` caps output rows.
   368→
   369→### 7.2 Pattern B — Title only, text-indexed
   370→
   371→Used when: no author GND URI is available.
   372→UNION catches cases where the DDB title matches a GND variant name rather than the preferred form.
   373→
   374→```sparql
   375→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   376→
   377→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel ?varLabel ?score WHERE {
   378→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
   379→  ?work a ?wtype .
   380→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
   381→  {
   382→    ?prefLabel contains-word "{title_tokens}"
   383→    BIND(?prefLabel AS ?varLabel)
   384→    BIND(score(?prefLabel) AS ?score)
   385→  } UNION {
   386→    ?work gndo:variantNameForTheWork ?alt .
   387→    ?alt contains-word "{title_tokens}"
   388→    BIND(?alt AS ?varLabel)
   389→    BIND(score(?alt) AS ?score)
   390→  }
   391→}
   392→ORDER BY DESC(?score)
   393→TEXTLIMIT 5
   394→LIMIT 10
   395→```
   396→
   397→### 7.3 Pattern C — FILTER (active query path)
   398→
   399→Exact-match only; normalized title (diacritics stripped, lowercased) maximizes recall.
   400→
   401→```sparql
   402→PREFIX gndo: <https://d-nb.info/standards/elementset/gnd#>
   403→
   404→SELECT ?work ?prefLabel WHERE {
   405→  VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }
   406→  ?work a ?wtype .
   407→  ?work gndo:preferredNameForTheWork ?prefLabel .
   408→  FILTER(LCASE(STR(?prefLabel)) = "{normalized_title}")
   409→}
   410→LIMIT 10
   411→```
   412→
   413→### 7.4 Conclusion from Step 0
   414→
   415→| Finding | Source | Impact on Step 1 |
   416→|---|---|---|
   417→| `contains-word` raises a SPARQL parse error | Step 0.2 | Patterns A and B are not available. **Pattern C is the sole active query path.** |
   418→| `VALUES ?wtype { gndo:Work gndo:MusicalWork gndo:Manuscript }` covers 580,284 entities (242k + 331k + 7k) | Step 0.1 | Confirmed — use in all patterns |
   419→| Dominant author predicate is `gndo:author`; `gndo:firstAuthor` is a subproperty but SPARQL does not infer it | Step 0.3 | Pattern A must use `VALUES ?authorPred { gndo:author gndo:firstAuthor gndo:poet gndo:composer }` |
   420→| `gndo:poet` appears for lyrical/musical works (Goethe as lyricist) | Step 0.3 | Include `gndo:poet` in `VALUES ?authorPred` |
   421→| `CollectiveManuscript` has zero entities in this dataset | Step 0.1 | Confirmed exclusion; no impact on queries |
   422→
   423→Pattern C with author constraint (when author GND URI is available) becomes the effective Pattern A: same `VALUES ?authorPred` constraint, FILTER instead of `contains-word`.
   424→
   425→---
   426→
   427→## 8. Step 3 — Title token preparation
   428→
   429→QLever `contains-word` is word-based, not phrase-based by default. Title preparation has three distinct phases: normalization, tokenization, and filtering.
   430→
   431→### 8.1 Normalization (pre-tokenization)
   432→
   433→1. **Unicode NFC normalize** — Unicode Standard Annex #15, Normalization Form C: Canonical Decomposition followed by Canonical Composition. Ensures consistent code-point representation before any string operation. Example: `a` + combining diaeresis (two code points, NFD) → `ä` (one code point, NFC). Required so that lowercasing, diacritic stripping, and string comparison behave predictably regardless of source encoding.
   434→2. **Lowercase** — case-fold the NFC-normalized string.
   435→3. **Strip diacritics** — decompose (NFD) then remove combining characters (`unicodedata.category(c) == 'Mn'`). Maps `ä→a`, `ü→u`, `ö→o`, `ß→ss`. Note: test whether the GND endpoint requires this — if the text index is accent-insensitive, stripping may reduce rather than increase recall.
   436→
   437→### 8.2 Tokenization
   438→
   439→4. **Split on whitespace and punctuation** — split the normalized string into individual word tokens.
   440→
   441→### 8.3 Filtering
   442→
   443→5. **Remove stopwords:** `der, die, das, ein, eine, von, und, zu, im, in, an, auf, für, mit, bei, dem, den`
   444→6. **Select 2–3 distinctive tokens** (see below)
   445→
   446→### 8.4 Distinctive token selection
   447→
   448→**Underlying principle:** `contains-word` with multiple tokens is AND semantics — each additional token makes the query more restrictive. Candidate retrieval should maximize recall (get the right GND Work somewhere in the top-10); precision is handled by post-retrieval string scoring in Step 3. The goal is the minimum set of tokens that identifies the work without over-constraining.
   449→
   450→**Criterion 1 — Semantic centrality.** Use tokens that are the essential content of the title, not modifiers or structural words:
   451→
   452→- **Keep:** nouns, proper nouns (place/person names embedded in titles), semantically unique adjectives
   453→- **Drop even if not in the stopword list:** articles, prepositions, ordinals (*erster, zweiter*), format/edition terms (*Band, Teil, Heft, Auflage, Bd., Nr.*)
   454→
   455→Example: *"Die Leiden des jungen Werthers"* → tokens `leiden werthers`; the proper noun alone (`werthers`) is sufficient with Pattern A.
   456→
   457→**Criterion 2 — Low collision frequency.** Prefer tokens that appear in few GND Works. Some words are not stopwords but are so common in bibliographic titles that they add almost no discriminating power:
   458→
   459→```python
   460→GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS = {
   461→    "briefe", "gedichte", "werke", "schriften", "geschichte",
   462→    "bericht", "katalog", "sammlung", "aufsätze", "texte",
   463→    "band", "teil", "heft", "nummer", "jahrgang",
   464→}
   465→```
   466→
   467→Example: *"Gedichte"* alone returns thousands of GND Works. *"faust"* alone is sufficient with an author URI.
   468→
   469→This list should be derived empirically from a frequency query against the endpoint, not hardcoded blindly — run after recon 0.1.
   470→
   471→**Script:** `scripts/check_generic_title_words.py` — queries the DNB endpoint per word using exact VALUE matching on capitalized German forms (plain literals, no `@de` tag — confirmed by recon 0.4). Run with `python scripts/check_generic_title_words.py`.
   472→
   473→**Results** (counts = GND Works whose full `preferredNameForTheWork` is exactly this word or common article+word variants; threshold ≥100 = drop):
   474→
   475→| Word | Count | Keep in list? |
   476→|---|---|---|
   477→| briefe | 74 | Yes (low collision) |
   478→| gedichte | 90 | Yes (low collision) |
   479→| werke | 3,212 | **No — drop from list** |
   480→| schriften | 1 | Yes (low collision) |
   481→| geschichte | 5 | Yes (low collision) |
   482→| bericht | 2 | Yes (low collision) |
   483→| katalog | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   484→| sammlung | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   485→| aufsätze | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   486→| texte | 2 | Yes (low collision) |
   487→| band | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   488→| teil | 1 | Yes (low collision) |
   489→| heft | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   490→| nummer | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   491→| jahrgang | 0 | Yes (low collision) |
   492→
   493→**Conclusion:** Only `werke` (3,212) exceeds the threshold and is genuinely high-collision as a standalone title. All other words are low-frequency as full titles — GND preferred names are typically qualified strings (ISBD-style, see recon 0.4), not bare single-word titles. `GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS` should be revised to contain only `werke`; the others do not need filtering for Pattern C exact-match queries.
   494→
   495→**Decision rule:**
   496→
   497→```python
   498→def select_tokens(tokens: list[str]) -> list[str]:
   499→    # tokens: already stopword-filtered, lowercased, diacritics-stripped
   500→    distinctive = [t for t in tokens if t not in GENERIC_TITLE_WORDS and len(t) > 3]
   501→    return distinctive[:3] if len(distinctive) >= 2 else tokens[:3]
   502→```
   503→
   504→`len(t) > 3` also drops short high-frequency fragments not caught by the stopword list.
   505→
   506→**Why 2–3 tokens specifically:**
   507→
   508→- 1 token: usually sufficient with Pattern A (author constraint already narrows heavily); sometimes sufficient for highly distinctive titles in Pattern B
   509→- 2–3 tokens: the sweet spot for Pattern B — enough to discriminate, low risk of AND-excluding the right Work due to GND/DDB title variation
   510→- >3 tokens: increasing risk of false negatives when GND's preferred form differs from DDB's (e.g., GND has *"Faust. Der Tragödie erster Teil"* but DDB has *"Faust: Erster Teil"* — four tokens from the DDB form likely miss the GND entry)
   511→
   512→**Multi-word handling:**
   513→- Short (1–3 tokens after filtering): pass all remaining tokens
   514→- Long titles: apply `select_tokens()` above
   515→- High-ambiguity titles (single generic word like *Briefe*, *Gedichte* with no distinctive co-tokens): require author constraint (Pattern A); skip title-only search (Pattern B) if no author URI is available
   516→
   517→---
   518→
   519→## 9. Step 4 — Post-retrieval scoring
   520→
   521→QLever's `score()` is used only to rank candidates before string comparison. Match type is assigned in Python:
   522→
   523→| Condition | `match_type` | Triple predicate |
   524→|---|---|---|
   525→| `prefLabel == extracted_title` (case-insensitive) | `exact` | `skos:exactMatch` |
   526→| Normalized match (strip diacritics + punctuation) | `normalized` | `skos:exactMatch` |
   527→| Levenshtein distance ≤ 2 to `prefLabel` or any `variantNameForTheWork` | `fuzzy` | `skos:closeMatch` |
   528→| No candidate above threshold | `unresolved` | — |
   529→
   530→`match_confidence` = normalized edit similarity to `prefLabel` (not QLever score).
   531→
   532→This logic is unchanged from the original plan in `notes/gnd-title-extraction.md`.
   533→
   534→### 9.1 Predicate justification
   535→
   536→**`skos:exactMatch`** (exact and normalized matches) — asserts two concepts are "an exact match" and can be used interchangeably for all retrieval purposes, without the OWL identity entailments of `owl:sameAs` (Miles & Bechhofer, *SKOS Simple Knowledge Organization System Reference*, W3C Recommendation, 2009, §10). Semantically appropriate here: a DDB ProvidedCHO and a GND Werk URI are not the same individual (CHO = catalog record for a manifestation; Werk URI = authority record for an abstract work), so asserting OWL individual identity would be incorrect.
   537→
   538→**`skos:closeMatch`** (fuzzy matches) — links two concepts "sufficiently similar that they can be used interchangeably in some information retrieval applications" but stops short of asserting full equivalence (Miles & Bechhofer, 2009, §10).
   539→
   540→**Why not `owl:sameAs`:** Halpin et al. show that `owl:sameAs` is routinely misused in linked data to express approximate or cross-type identity, producing unwanted OWL entailments downstream. Their recommendation: reserve `owl:sameAs` for verified same-individual identity; use SKOS mapping properties for concept-level matching (Halpin, Hayes, McCusker, McGuinness & Thompson, *When owl:sameAs Isn't the Same: An Analysis of Identity in Linked Data*, ISWC 2010, DOI: 10.1007/978-3-642-17746-0_16).
   541→
   542→**Match type taxonomy** (exact / normalized / fuzzy) follows the standard candidate generation ladder in entity linking (Shen, Wang & Han, *Entity Linking with a Knowledge Base: Issues, Techniques, and Solutions*, IEEE TKDE 27(2), 2015, §3.1, DOI: 10.1109/TKDE.2014.2327028).
   543→
   544→**Levenshtein distance** (Levenshtein, *Binary Codes Capable of Correcting Deletions, Insertions, and Reversals*, Soviet Physics Doklady 10(8), 1966). The threshold of ≤ 2 is a common empirical choice; validate against the gold set planned in `gnd-title-extraction.md` §Paper §4 quality metrics.
   545→
   546→---
   547→
   548→## 10. Step 5 — Changes to `link_gnd_works.py`
   549→
   550→Replace the lobid-gnd REST call (Steps 3–4 in `gnd-title-extraction.md`) with:
   551→
   552→```python
   553→import httpx
   554→import asyncio
   555→
   556→QLEVER_ENDPOINT = "https://sparql.dnb.de/api/dnbgnd"
   557→CONCURRENCY = 10  # tune up after rate-limit testing
   558→
   559→_semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(CONCURRENCY)
   560→
   561→async def query_gnd_werk(
   562→    extracted_title: str,
   563→    author_gnd_uri: str | None
   564→) -> list[dict]:
   565→    tokens = tokenize_for_qlever(extracted_title)
   566→    sparql = (
   567→        QUERY_A.format(title_tokens=tokens, author_gnd_uri=author_gnd_uri)
   568→        if author_gnd_uri
   569→        else QUERY_B.format(title_tokens=tokens)
   570→    )
   571→    async with _semaphore:
   572→        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10.0) as client:
   573→            r = await client.post(
   574→                QLEVER_ENDPOINT,
   575→                data={"query": sparql},
   576→                headers={"Accept": "application/sparql-results+json"}
   577→            )
   578→    r.raise_for_status()
   579→    return r.json()["results"]["bindings"]
   580→```
   581→
   582→Pattern selection:
   583→- Pattern A if `author_gnd_uri` is set
   584→- Pattern B otherwise
   585→- Pattern C as fallback if recon 0.2 shows `contains-word` is unavailable (confirmed — Pattern C is the sole active path)
   586→
   587→---
   588→
   589→## 11. Step 6 — What this replaces
   590→
   591→| Old plan | New plan |
   592→|---|---|
   593→| `GET /gnd/search?q=label:"{title}"&filter=type:Work` (lobid REST) | SPARQL POST to QLever endpoint |
   594→| Endpoint TBD / local infrastructure required | Public DNB endpoint, no setup |
   595→| No text relevance ranking | QLever `score()` ranks candidates |
   596→| `link_gnd_works.py` depended on lobid API availability | Depends on `sparql.dnb.de` uptime (DNB-operated) |
   597→| Title-only or title + author_uri via query param | Same semantics; `gndo:firstAuthor` predicate (verify in recon 0.3) |
   598→



```

   110→### 2.9 SR-09 — Gold set composition
   111→**Status:** Open — blocks SR-08
   112→- **Requirement:** ~500 manually annotated records stratified by: era (modern / 19th c. / 1700–1800 / pre-1700), silver tier (2 / 1 / 0), and `dc_type`

   115→- **Pre-1750 PERSON annotation (from SR-03):** the ` /` SoR heuristic is a systematic false negative for pre-1750 titles — authors appear before the work title, not after ` /`. Gold set annotators must not rely on the SoR position as a cue for the `PERSON` label in this stratum; author spans need to be identified from the opening name + credentials pattern (e.g. *Firstname Lastname, [role/title], [work title]*). This affects annotation guidelines and model evaluation: PERSON F1 on the pre-1750 stratum should be tracked separately from the modern stratum. See [silver-label-fp-review.md §5](ner/sr03_silver-label-fp-review.md#5-pre-1750-false-negatives--author-before-title) for examples.

   122→- **Length by year:** pre-1750 dominated by long strings (42–50%, median 12–15 tokens); post-1775 shift to median 6–9; 2000–2024 reversal (62% medium). 9.6% of titles have no year.
   123→- **Implication for SR-09:** stratify gold set by length and era; pre-1750 long-form records stress the NER model differently from the short modern majority.

   139→

   144→- **Primary motivation:** curriculum learning — train first on high-confidence tier-2 + high-score tier-1 records, then add lower-confidence augmentation
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→## 3. FRBR label scope
   149→
   150→Labels are organised by FRBR level. The goal is to extract enough structure to distinguish Works, Expressions, and Manifestations in the KG.
   151→
   152→**Work** — the abstract intellectual creation
   153→
   154→| Label | What it marks | Typical ISBD / catalog cue |
   155→|---|---|---|
   156→| `TITLE` | Proper title | Before ` :` or ` /`; primary string |
   157→| `OTHER_TITLE` | Subtitle / other title information | After ` :` |

     1→# GeMeA — Silver Label False-Positive Review (SR-03)
     2→
     3→**SR-03** in [ner-bibliographic.md](../ner-bibliographic.md). See also [sr01_isbd-field-rating.md](sr01_isbd-field-rating.md).
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## 1. Background
     8→
     9→`rate_isbd_fields.py` assigns silver labels to titles by detecting ISBD punctuation patterns at two tiers:
    10→
    11→- **Tier 2 (structural):** `. -` area separator present — strong structural signal, expected high precision
    12→- **Tier 1 (heuristic):** no `. -`, but other markers fire (` :`, ` /`, 4-digit year, edition keyword, etc.) — weaker signal, false positive rate unknown
    13→
    14→Heuristic patterns over-fire on non-ISBD content:
    15→- ` :` fires on any colon, not just ISBD subtitle separators
    16→- ` /` fires on fractions, series letter suffixes, and region names as well as Statement of Responsibility (SoR)
    17→- A 4-digit number fires on founding years, life dates, and manuscript dates — not only publication years
    18→
    19→---
    20→
    21→## 2. Review method
    22→
    23→`scripts/validate_heuristic_fields.py` produced a 200-record stratified sample at `data/processed/heuristic_validation_sample.csv`, with one stratum per heuristic field flag. `scripts/sr03_fp_review.py` applied automated regex rules + per-row overrides to classify each active flag as TP or FP, writing results to the `fp_fields` and `notes` columns.
    24→
    25→---
    26→
    27→## 3. Results
    28→
    29→**81 of 200 records (40.5%) have at least one false positive.**
    30→



```

     1→# GeMeA — Historical and Latin Title Scope (SR-06)
     2→
     3→**SR-06** in [ner-bibliographic.md](../ner-bibliographic.md).
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## 1. Background
     8→
     9→NER fallback applies to ~71% of DF_DE_TITLES records (the share with no ISBD markers; see SR-01). An unknown proportion of these are Latin or Early Modern German — both require different tokenisation, different model capabilities, and potentially different annotation strategies than modern German. This study estimates that proportion for two high-prevalence historical strata, and evaluates the accuracy of a heuristic language classifier.
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## 2. Method
    14→
    15→### 2.1 Stratum selection
    16→
    17→Two strata were selected as high-density proxies for historical records in DF_DE_TITLES. Script: [sr06_historical_scope.py](../../scripts/sr06_historical_scope.py).
    18→
    19→| Stratum | Filter | Total records | Tier-0 records | Rationale |
    20→|---|---|---|---|---|
    21→| A — Leichenpredigt | `dc_type` contains "Leichenpredigt" | 11,255 | 4,809 (43%) | Funerary sermons; nearly all pre-1750; known to contain Latin scripture and Early Modern German prose; the most historically homogeneous dc_type in DF_DE_TITLES |
    22→| B — Monografie pre-1800 | `dc_type == "Monografie"` AND `year_num < 1800` | 175,094 | 145,268 (83%) | Broad historical monograph category; spans 1500–1799; includes legal texts, academic dissertations, religious tracts, administrative documents — the widest coverage of pre-modern production types |
    23→
    24→Strata were chosen to span the two dominant historical production contexts: devotional/funerary (Leichenpredigt, mostly 1600–1750) and scholarly/administrative (Monografie, 1500–1799). Other potentially relevant dc_types (e.g. pre-1800 Periodika, Handschrift, Einblattdruck) were excluded from this study; their language distributions may differ.
    25→
    26→**Year filter for Stratum B:** `year_num` is derived from `pd.to_numeric(df["dates"], errors="coerce")` — a best-effort parse of the free-text `dates` field. Records with non-numeric or missing dates are excluded from Stratum B; they would not be in the tier-0 historical fallback in any case.
    27→
    28→**No tier filter applied:** both strata include tier-0, tier-1, and tier-2 records. The study targets language scope, not silver label quality; all records are valid regardless of tier.
    29→
    30→### 2.2 Sampling



```

### Assistant *(2026-03-24T19:13:47.589Z)*
